Conclusion: R2H-Diff achieves a favorable balance between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency, demonstrating that diffusion-based frameworks can be effectively adapted for hyperspectral reconstruction with minimal denoising steps and low model complexity.

Abstract: RGB-to-hyperspectral image reconstruction is a highly ill-posed inverse problem, since multiple plausible spectral distributions may correspond to the same RGB observation. Existing regression-based methods usually learn a deterministic mapping, which limits their ability to model reconstruction uncertainty and often leads to over-smoothed spectral responses. Although diffusion models provide strong distribution modeling capability, their direct application to hyperspectral reconstruction remains challenging due to the high spectral dimensionality, strong inter-band correlations, and strict requirement for spectral fidelity. To this end, we propose R2H-Diff, an efficient diffusion-based framework tailored for RGB-to-HSI reconstruction. Specifically, R2H-Diff formulates spectral recovery as a conditional iterative refinement process, enabling progressive reconstruction under RGB guidance. We proposed a Guided Spectral Refinement Module for RGB-conditioned feature fusion and a Hyperspectral-Adaptive Transposed Attention module for efficient spatial--spectral dependency modeling. Furthermore, a normalization-free denoising backbone is adopted to preserve spectral amplitude consistency, while a task-adapted linear noise schedule enables high-quality reconstruction with only five denoising steps. Extensive experiments on NTIRE2022, CAVE, and Harvard demonstrate that R2H-Diff achieves a favorable balance between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency. Notably, on NTIRE2022, R2H-Diff obtains 35.37 dB PSNR with a sub-million-parameter model of 0.58M parameters and 12.25G FLOPs, achieving the lowest model complexity among the evaluated methods while maintaining strong reconstruction fidelity.

</details>


### [28] [CFE-PPAR: Compression-friendly encryption for privacy-preserving action recognition leveraging video transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05692)
*Haiwei Lin,Shoko Imaizumi,Hitoshi Kiya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes CFE-PPAR, the first compression-friendly encryption method for privacy-preserving action recognition that maintains recognition performance and visual quality even after video compression, outperforming previous methods on UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing encryption-based PPAR methods suffer catastrophic performance drops and visual quality degradation when encrypted videos are compressed, making them not compression-friendly.

Method: CFE-PPAR encrypts videos with secret keys and uses a video transformer with parameters transformed by the same keys, enabling direct recognition of compressed encrypted videos.

Result: Outperforms previous methods on UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets under Motion-JPEG and H.264 compression.

Conclusion: CFE-PPAR is the first compression-friendly encryption method for PPAR, successfully addressing the trade-off between privacy protection and compression robustness.

Abstract: Privacy-preserving action recognition (PPAR) enables machines to understand human activities in videos without revealing sensitive visual content. Among the various strategies for PPAR, encryption-based methods achieve strong privacy protection while maintaining high recognition performance. However, these methods lead to a catastrophic decrease in recognition performance and visual quality when the encrypted videos are compressed. That is, the previous methods are not compression-friendly. To address these issues, in this paper, we propose the first compression-friendly encryption method for PPAR, called CFE-PPAR. In CFE-PPAR, videos encrypted with secret keys can be directly recognized by a video transformer, which uses parameters transformed by the same keys as those used for video encryption. In experiments, it is verified that CFE-PPAR outperforms previous methods on the UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets under Motion-JPEG and H.264 compression.

</details>


### [29] [Adaptive Physical-Facial Representation Fusion via Subject-Invariant Cross-Modal Prompt Tuning for Video-Based Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05694)
*Xiwen Luo,Jia Li,Rencheng Song,Yu Liu,Juan Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a subject-invariant cross-modal prompt-tuning framework that uses noise-robust rPPG time-frequency representations to generate prompts for a frozen Vision Transformer, along with decoupled shared-specific adapters, achieving state-of-the-art emotion recognition on MAHNOB-HCI and DEAP benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Facial expressions alone cannot fully reflect intrinsic affective states; rPPG provides complementary physiological cues but suffers from noise and inter-subject variability; existing multimodal fusion methods disrupt pretrained facial representations and lack explicit mechanisms to suppress subject-specific variations.

Method: (1) Transform rPPG waveforms into noise-robust time-frequency representations (TFRs); (2) Generate modality-complementary prompts from TFRs to modulate facial tokens in a frozen Vision Transformer (ViT); (3) Introduce decoupled shared-specific adapters (DSSA) in each ViT layer to separate subject-shared and subject-specific components.

Result: Outperforms strong baselines on MAHNOB-HCI and DEAP benchmarks in both recognition accuracy and cross-subject generalization ability.

Conclusion: The proposed subject-invariant cross-modal prompt-tuning framework effectively combines facial and rPPG modalities while preserving pretrained representations and mitigating subject-specific variations, demonstrating robust performance for video-based emotion recognition.

Abstract: Emotion recognition from facial videos enables non-contact inference of human emotional states. Although facial expressions are widely used cues, they cannot fully reflect intrinsic affective states. Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) provides complementary physiological information, but it is highly susceptible to noise and inter-subject variability, limiting generalization to unseen individuals. Existing multimodal methods combine facial and rPPG features, yet their fusion strategies often disrupt pretrained facial representations and lack explicit mechanisms to suppress subject-specific variations. To address these issues, we propose a subject-invariant cross-modal prompt-tuning framework for video-based emotion recognition. Specifically, rPPG waveforms are transformed into noise-robust time-frequency representations (TFRs), from which modality-complementary prompts are generated to modulate facial tokens within a frozen Vision Transformer (ViT). This design enables effective cross-modal interaction while preserving the generalizable facial representations learned by the pretrained backbone. In addition, we introduce a decoupled shared-specific adapter (DSSA) into each ViT layer to explicitly separate subject-shared and subject-specific components, thereby improving cross-subject generalization. Experiments on the MAHNOB-HCI and DEAP benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines in both recognition accuracy and generalization ability, highlighting its effectiveness for video-based emotion recognition.

</details>


### [30] [Closing the Loop: Unified 3D Scene Generation and Immersive Interaction via LLM-RL Coupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05711)
*Anh H. Vo,Sungyo Lee,Phil-Joong Kim,Soo-Mi Choi,Yong-Guk Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified closed-loop framework for language-driven 3D scene generation and immersive user interaction, combining LLMs, reinforcement learning, and VR-based HRI feedback. It achieves SOTA on ALFRED benchmark with improved immersion and task efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches treat 3D scene generation and user interaction as separate processes, limiting adaptability and immersive potential in interactive multimedia systems. The paper aims to close this gap.

Method: The framework uses LLMs to construct structured scene representations from natural language, optimizes spatial layouts via reinforcement learning under geometric/semantic constraints, and deploys scenes in VR for HRI-in-the-loop with continuous user feedback.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on ALFRED benchmark; user studies show improvements in immersion, interaction quality, and task efficiency.

Conclusion: Closed-loop integration of generation and interaction is crucial for next-generation adaptive and realistic multimedia systems.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have significantly improved language-driven 3D content generation, but most existing approaches still treat scene generation and user interaction as separate processes, limiting the adaptability and immersive potential of interactive multimedia systems. This paper presents a unified framework that closes the loop between language-driven 3D scene generation and immersive user interaction. Given natural language instructions, the system first constructs structured scene representations using LLMs, and then optimizes spatial layouts via reinforcement learning under geometric and semantic constraints. The generated environments are deployed in a virtual reality setting to facilitate HRI-in-the-loop, where user interactions provide continuous feedback to align generated content with human perception and usability. By tightly coupling generation and interaction, the proposed framework enables more responsive, adaptive, and realistic multimedia experiences. Experiments on the ALFRED benchmark demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in task-based scene generation. Furthermore, qualitative results and user studies show consistent improvements in immersion, interaction quality, and task efficiency, highlighting the importance of closed-loop integration of generation and interaction for next-generation multimedia systems. Our project page can be found at https://proj-showcase.github.io/h3ds/.

</details>


### [31] [EgoEMG: A Multimodal Egocentric Dataset with Bilateral EMG and Vision for Hand Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05712)
*Ziheng Xi,Jiayi Yu,Yitao Wang,Yanbo Duan,Jianjiang Feng,Jie Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EgoEMG is a new multimodal dataset synchronizing EMG and egocentric vision for bimanual hand pose estimation, covering 41 participants, 60 gestures, and 10+ hours of data, with a benchmark for EMG-to-pose, vision-to-pose, and fusion tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EMG and egocentric vision are complementary for hand sensing, but no existing dataset synchronizes both modalities.

Method: The dataset includes bilateral wristband EMG (16 channels, 2 kHz), IMU, egocentric RGB video, external RGB-D video, and mocap data. A benchmark with three tasks (EMG-to-pose, vision-to-pose, fusion) and cross-gesture/user/combin ed splits is introduced.

Result: EMGFormer and ResNet/ViT baselines are evaluated. A residual fusion architecture improves over lightweight vision-only baselines.

Conclusion: EgoEMG and its benchmark establish a foundation for future research on multimodal hand pose estimation with EMG and vision.

Abstract: Surface electromyography (sEMG) records muscle activity during hand movement and can be decoded to recover detailed hand articulation. EMG and egocentric vision are complementary for hand sensing: EMG captures fine-grained finger articulation even under occlusion and poor lighting, while vision provides global hand configuration. However, no existing dataset synchronizes both modalities. We present EgoEMG, a multimodal egocentric dataset for bimanual hand pose estimation. EgoEMG includes bilateral wristband EMG with 16 total channels (8 per wrist) sampled at 2 kHz, 120 Hz IMU, egocentric wide-angle RGB video, external RGB-D video, and mocap-derived hand motion with wrist articulation angles. The dataset covers 41 participants performing 60 gesture classes, including 30 single-hand gestures and 30 bimanual gestures, totaling more than 10 hours of recording. We also introduce a benchmark with three tasks -- EMG-to-pose, vision-to-pose, and EMG+vision fusion -- under a shared joint-angle prediction target and common generalization split axes (cross-gesture, cross-user, and combined). As baselines, we evaluate EMGFormer for EMG-to-pose and generic ResNet/ViT backbones for vision-to-pose. We further study a residual fusion architecture that improves over matched lightweight vision-only baselines. Together, EgoEMG and its benchmark establish a foundation for future research on multimodal hand pose estimation with EMG and vision.

</details>


### [32] [TriRelVLA: Triadic Relational Structure for Generalizable Embodied Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05714)
*Hanyu Zhou,Chuanhao Ma,Gim Hee Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TriRelVLA proposes a triadic relational VLA framework that explicitly models object-hand-task relations to improve generalization across unseen scenes, objects, and task compositions in robotic manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models struggle to generalize due to implicit visual representations that entangle appearance, background, and layout, making policies sensitive to visual variations. Prior structured representations capture scene semantics but miss action-relevant relations.

Method: Constructs explicit object-hand-task triadic representations, builds a task-grounded relational graph with cross-attention and relation-aware graph transformer, and performs relation-conditioned action generation via a triadic relational bottleneck into the LLM.

Result: Strong performance on fine-tuned tasks and clear gains in cross-scene, cross-object, and cross-task generalization.

Conclusion: Modeling object-hand-task relational structure reduces reliance on appearance statistics and enables effective transfer across varied environments and task compositions.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models perform well on training-seen robotic tasks but struggle to generalize to unseen scenes and objects. A key limitation lies in their implicit visual representations, which entangle object appearance, background, and scene layout. This makes policies sensitive to visual variations. Prior work improves transferability through structured intermediate representations that objectify visual content. However, these representations mainly capture scene semantics instead of action-relevant relations. As a result, action prediction remains tied to appearance statistics. We observe that manipulation actions depend on the object-hand-task relational structure, which governs interactions among task requirements, robot states, and object properties. Based on this observation, we propose TriRelVLA, a triadic relational VLA framework for generalizable embodied manipulation. Our approach consists of three components: 1) We construct explicit object-hand-task triadic representations from multimodal inputs as relational primitives. 2) We build a task-grounded relational graph. Task-guided cross-attention forms nodes, and a relation-aware graph transformer models interactions among them. 3) We perform relation-conditioned action generation. The relational structure is compressed into a bottleneck space and projected into the LLM for action prediction. This triadic relational bottleneck reduces reliance on appearance statistics and enables transfer across scenes, objects, and task compositions. We further introduce a real-world robotic dataset for fine-tuning. Experiments show strong performance on fine-tuned tasks and clear gains in cross-scene, cross-object, and cross-task generalization.

</details>


### [33] [$\mathcal{B}^{3}$-Net: Controlled Posterior Bridge Learning for Multi-Task Dense Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05722)
*Meihua Zhou,Li Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: B³-Net proposes a controlled posterior bridge learning framework for multi-task dense prediction that explicitly models evidence reliability via precision estimation, posterior bridge construction, and bounded redistribution, outperforming CNN, Transformer, diffusion, Mamba, and bridge-feature methods on NYUD-v2, PASCAL-Context, and Cityscapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing decoder-side interaction methods (attention, prompts, routing, diffusion, Mamba, bridge features) exchange task evidence implicitly without modeling that evidence reliability varies across tasks and spatial locations, leading to unreliable evidence contaminating shared representations and intensifying negative transfer.

Method: B³-Net decomposes decoder-side interaction into three components: (1) Precision Field Estimator estimates patch-wise evidence precision from task-reference alignment and local variation; (2) Posterior Bridge Operator builds a precision-weighted posterior bridge via heteroscedastic evidence fusion; (3) Contractive Dispatch Operator redistributes the bridge to each task branch through bounded updates.

Result: Achieves competitive or superior trade-offs over representative CNN, Transformer, diffusion, Mamba, and bridge-feature-based methods on NYUD-v2, PASCAL-Context, and Cityscapes. Backbone-matched comparisons confirm gains come from controlled posterior bridge learning, not backbone capacity or decoder scale.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling evidence reliability and controlling information flow through bounded redistribution improves multi-task dense prediction by reducing negative transfer and contamination from unreliable evidence.

Abstract: Multi-task dense prediction solves complementary pixel-level tasks in a unified model, such as semantic segmentation, depth estimation, surface normal estimation, and edge detection. Existing decoder-side interactions use attention, prompts, routing, diffusion, Mamba, or bridge features to exchange task evidence, but most of them organize this evidence implicitly. They usually fuse task features by similarity or affinity, without explicitly modeling that evidence reliability varies across tasks and spatial locations. As a result, unreliable evidence may contaminate the shared representation and intensify negative transfer. We propose $\mathcal{B}^{3}$-Net, a controlled posterior bridge learning framework for multi-task dense prediction. Our method decomposes decoder-side interaction into reliability estimation, posterior bridge construction, and bounded redistribution. The Precision Field Estimator estimates patch-wise evidence precision from task-reference alignment and local variation. The Posterior Bridge Operator builds a precision-weighted posterior bridge through heteroscedastic evidence fusion, yielding a shared state more reliable than uniform or heuristic mixtures. The Contractive Dispatch Operator redistributes the bridge to each task branch through a bounded update, reducing uncontrolled feature injection. Experiments on NYUD-v2, PASCAL-Context, and Cityscapes show that $\mathcal{B}^{3}$-Net achieves competitive or superior trade-offs over representative CNN-, Transformer-, diffusion-, Mamba-, and bridge-feature-based methods. Backbone-matched comparisons and extensive analyses further verify that the gains arise from controlled posterior bridge learning rather than backbone capacity or decoder scale.

</details>


### [34] [Ray-Aware Pointer Memory with Adaptive Updates for Streaming 3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05749)
*Feifei Li,Qi Song,Chi Zhang,Rui Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a ray-aware pointer memory for streaming 3D reconstruction that models spatial location and viewing direction jointly, using a retain-or-replace mechanism to manage memory efficiently and trigger pose refinement for loop closures, improving long-term stability and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing feed-forward reconstruction frameworks update memory based on appearance similarity, leading to redundant observations and unstable geometry under viewpoint changes. The authors aim to address these limitations by incorporating viewing direction into memory management.

Method: The method introduces a ray-aware pointer memory where each pointer stores 3D position, ray direction, and feature embedding. It uses an adaptive retain-or-replace update strategy instead of fusion-based compression, and triggers pose refinement upon detecting loop candidates via joint spatial-direction reasoning.

Result: Extensive experiments show the approach significantly improves long-term reconstruction stability and camera pose accuracy while maintaining efficient streaming inference.

Conclusion: The ray-aware memory design provides a principled, scalable, and drift-resistant framework for online 3D reconstruction from continuous image streams.

Abstract: Dense 3D reconstruction from continuous image streams requires both accurate geometric aggregation and stable long-term memory management. Recent feed-forward reconstruction frameworks integrate observations through persistent memory representations, yet most rely primarily on appearance-based similarity when updating memory. Such appearance-driven integration often leads to redundant accumulation of observations and unstable geometry when viewpoint changes occur. In this work, we propose a ray-aware pointer memory for streaming 3D reconstruction that explicitly models both spatial location and viewing direction within a unified memory representation. Each memory pointer stores its 3D position, associated ray direction, and feature embedding, allowing the system to reason jointly about geometric proximity and viewpoint consistency. Based on this representation, we introduce an adaptive pointer update strategy that replaces traditional fusion-based memory compression with a retain-or-replace mechanism. Instead of averaging nearby observations, the system selectively retains informative pointers while discarding redundant ones, preserving distinctive geometric structures while maintaining bounded memory growth. Furthermore, the joint reasoning over spatial distance and ray-direction discrepancy enables the system to distinguish between local redundancy, novel observations, and potential loop revisits in a unified manner. When loop candidates are detected, pose refinement is triggered to enforce global geometric consistency across the reconstruction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed ray-aware memory design significantly improves long-term reconstruction stability and camera pose accuracy while maintaining efficient streaming inference. Our approach provides a principled framework for scalable and drift-resistant online 3D reconstruction from image streams.

</details>


### [35] [Jointly Learning Structured Representations and Stabilized Affinity for Human Motion Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05753)
*Xianghan Meng,Zhiyuan Huang,Zhengyu Tong,Chun-Guang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TDSC is a temporal deep self-expressive subspace clustering method for Human Motion Segmentation that jointly learns temporally consistent structured representations and stabilized affinity, outperforming existing methods on five benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HMS methods rely on subspace clustering assuming high-dimensional features align with a Union-of-Subspaces (UoS), but real-world raw frame-level features often violate this assumption, leading to poor segmentation performance.

Method: TDSC alternately learns structured representations and self-expressive coefficients via a regularized self-expressive model with: (1) coding-rate maximization to avoid representation collapse and enforce UoS distribution, (2) temporal constraints to group adjacent frames, (3) temporal momentum averaging for stable affinity evolution, and (4) reparameterization for efficient optimization.

Result: Extensive experiments on five benchmark HMS datasets using both conventional (HoG) and deep features (CLIP, DINOv2) validate the effectiveness of TDSC.

Conclusion: TDSC addresses the UoS assumption violation in real-world videos by jointly learning temporally consistent representations and stabilized affinity, achieving accurate and robust human motion segmentation.

Abstract: Human Motion Segmentation (HMS), which aims to partition a video into non-overlapping segments corresponding to different human motions, has recently attracted increasing research attention. Existing HMS approaches are predominantly based on subspace clustering, which are grounded on the assumption that the distribution of high-dimensional temporal features well aligns with a Union-of-Subspaces (UoS). For videos in the real world, however, the raw frame-level features often violate the UoS assumption and yield unsatisfactory segmentation performance. To address this issue, we propose an efficient and effective approach for HMS, named Temporal Deep Self-expressive subspace Clustering (TDSC), which jointly learns temporally consistent structured representations and stabilized affinity for accurate and robust HMS. Specifically, in TDSC, we alternately learn structured representations of the input frame features and self-expressive coefficients via a properly regularized self-expressive model, in which a coding-rate maximization regularizer is incorporated to avoid representation collapse and conform the learned representations to span a desired UoS distribution, and meanwhile, temporal constraints are incorporated to promote temporally adjacent frames to be partitioned into the same groups. Moreover, we develop a temporal momentum averaging mechanism to stabilize affinity evolution and design a reparameterization strategy to enable efficient optimization. We conduct extensive experiments on five benchmark HMS datasets using both conventional (HoG) and up-to-date deep features (i.e., CLIP, DINOv2) to validate the effectiveness of our approach.

</details>


### [36] [iTRIALSPACE: Programmable Virtual Lesion Trials for Controlled Evaluation of Lung CT Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05761)
*Fakrul Islam Tushar,Umme Hafsa Momy,Joseph Y. Lo,Geoffrey D. Rubin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: iTRIALSPACE is a programmable evaluation framework that creates controlled virtual lesion trials from real CT scans and lesion profiles, enabling systematic assessment of lung CT models beyond static benchmarks, validated across 55,469 samples with high performance ranking transfer (ρ=0.93) to real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard benchmarks are static retrospective collections that entangle multiple factors (lesion size, lobe prevalence, anatomy, acquisition context), making it impossible to determine what structurally drives model accuracy. iTRIALSPACE addresses this limitation by enabling controlled, decomposable evaluation.

Method: Four-stage pipeline: (1) multidataset nodule profiling, (2) explicit trial specification, (3) anatomy-aware mask insertion, and (4) ControlNet-conditioned CT synthesis. Built on a 54-attribute nodule-profile dataset (13,140 nodules from 7 sources) with 13 trial modes.

Result: 55,469-sample Virtual Lesion Study across 3 VLMs, 4 guidance conditions, and 3 tasks. Synthetic quality within real-to-real FID baseline. Performance rankings transfer to real data (ρ=0.93, p<10⁻¹⁵). Revealed findings like size prediction collapse under lobe-equalized sampling and host-to-donor variance ratios of 8.9x and 3.3x.

Conclusion: iTRIALSPACE provides an auditable, falsifiable evaluation infrastructure for controlled testing beyond static retrospective benchmarks, enabling systematic identification of model failure modes and shortcut-driven behaviors.

Abstract: We introduce iTRIALSPACE, a programmable evaluation framework for controlled assessment of lung CT models. Standard benchmarks are static retrospective collections that entangle lesion size, lobe prevalence, anatomy, and acquisition context, making it difficult to determine what structurally drives model accuracy. iTRIALSPACE addresses this limitation by composing real clinical CTs and lesion profiles into controlled virtual lesion trials through a four-stage pipeline: multidataset nodule profiling, explicit trial specification, anatomy-aware mask insertion, and ControlNet-conditioned CT synthesis. The framework is built on a unified 54-attribute nodule-profile dataset spanning 13,140 annotated nodules from seven public CT sources and instantiated as 13 trial modes. We evaluate iTRIALSPACE in a 55,469-sample Virtual Lesion Study spanning three medical VLMs, four spatialguidance conditions, and three clinical tasks. Across all 13 modes, the synthetic substrate remains within the real-to-real FID baseline, and synthetic performance rankings transfer strongly to real clinical data ($ρ$ = 0.93, p < 10$^{-15}$). Controlled trial modes expose findings unavailable to fixed-distribution benchmarks, including shortcut-driven size prediction collapse under lobe-equalized sampling and hostto-donor variance ratios of 8.9x and 3.3x in twin-cross analysis. These results position iTRIALSPACE as an auditable evaluation infrastructure for controlled, falsifiable testing beyond static retrospective benchmarks.

</details>


### [37] [X-OmniClaw Technical Report: A Unified Mobile Agent for Multimodal Understanding and Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05765)
*Xiaoming Ren,Ru Zhen,Chao Li,Yang Song,Qiuxia Hou,Yanhao Zhang,Peng Liu,Qi Qi,Quanlong Zheng,Qi Wu,Zhenyi Liao,Binqiang Pan,Haobo Ji,Haonan Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: X-OmniClaw is a unified mobile agent for Android that integrates perception, memory, and action modules (Omni Perception, Omni Memory, Omni Action) to enable multimodal understanding and complex task execution, demonstrating improved interaction efficiency and task reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Driven by the demand for mobile-based personal agents capable of handling complex and intuitive interactions, inspired by OpenClaw.

Method: The system features three modules: (1) Omni Perception - a unified multimodal ingress pipeline integrating UI states, real-world visuals, and speech with temporal alignment; (2) Omni Memory - combines runtime working memory with long-term personal memory for personalized intelligence; (3) Omni Action - hybrid grounding using XML metadata and visual perception with Behavior Cloning and Trajectory Replay for reusable skills.

Result: X-OmniClaw effectively enhances interaction efficiency and task reliability across diverse scenarios.

Conclusion: Provides a practical architectural blueprint for next-generation mobile-native personal assistants.

Abstract: Inspired by the development of OpenClaw, there is a growing demand for mobile-based personal agents capable of handling complex and intuitive interactions. In this technical report, we introduce X-OmniClaw, a unified mobile agent designed for multimodal understanding and interaction in the Android ecosystem. This unified architecture of perception, memory, and action enables the agent to handle complex mobile tasks with high contextual awareness. Specifically, Omni Perception provides a unified multimodal ingress pipeline that integrates UI states, real-world visual contexts, and speech inputs, leveraging a temporal alignment module to decompose raw data into structured multimodal intent representations. Omni Memory leverages multimodal memory optimization to enhance personalized intelligence by integrating runtime working memory for task continuity with long-term personal memory distilled from local data, enabling highly context-aware and personalized interactions. Finally, Omni Action employs a hybrid grounding strategy that combines structural XML metadata with visual perception for robust interaction. Through Behavior Cloning and Trajectory Replay, the system captures user navigation as reusable skills, enabling precise direct-access execution. Demonstrations across diverse scenarios show that X-OmniClaw effectively enhances interaction efficiency and task reliability, providing a practical architectural blueprint for the next generation of mobile-native personal assistants.

</details>


### [38] [The autoPET3 Challenge -- Automated Lesion Segmentation in Whole-Body PET/CT - Multitracer Multicenter Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05775)
*Jakob Dexl,Katharina Jeblick,Andreas Mittermeier,Balthasar Schachtner,Anna Theresa Stüber,Johanna Topalis,Maximilian Rokuss,Fabian Isensee,Klaus H. Maier-Hein,Hamza Kalisch,Jens Kleesiek,Constantin M. Seibold,Hussain Alasmawi,Lap Yan Lennon Chan,Yixuan Yuan,Alexander Jaus,Rainer Stiefelhagen,Pauline Ornela Megne Choudja,Konstantin Nikolaou,Christian La Fougère,Sergios Gatidis,Matthias P. Fabritius,Maurice Heimer,Gizem Abaci,Lalith Kumar Shiyam Sundar,Rudolf A. Werner,Jens Ricke,Clemens C. Cyran,Thomas Küstner,Michael Ingrisch*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The third autoPET challenge (MICCAI 2024) benchmarked automated lesion segmentation in whole-body PET/CT under compositional generalization using 1,611 training studies and 200 test studies covering unseen tracer-center combinations. The best algorithm (nnU-Net-based) achieved mean DSC 0.66, improving 8% over baseline. Key findings: in-domain segmentation nears human agreement; generalization to unseen combinations remains challenging due to volume overestimation; case difficulty matters more than algorithm choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To benchmark automated lesion segmentation in whole-body PET/CT under compositional generalization (unseen tracer-center combinations) and to establish the largest publicly available annotated PSMA PET/CT dataset.

Method: A benchmark challenge with 1,014 [18F]-FDG and 597 PSMA PET/CT studies for training; 200 held-out test studies covering four tracer-center combinations (two unseen); 17 teams submitted 27 algorithms (mostly nnU-Net 3D with PET/CT concatenation); included a data-centric award with fixed baseline model.

Result: Top algorithm achieved mean DSC 0.66, FNV 3.18 mL, FPV 2.78 mL; 8% DSC improvement and 5 mL reduction in false-negative volume vs. baseline; ranking stable for top tier across resampling.

Conclusion: (1) In-domain multitracer segmentation approaches reader agreement; (2) compositional generalization to unseen tracer-center combos is still challenging due to systematic volume overestimation; (3) case heterogeneity/difficulty affects performance more than algorithm choice among top teams.

Abstract: We report the design and results of the third autoPET challenge (MICCAI 2024), which benchmarked automated lesion segmentation in whole-body PET/CT under a compositional generalization setting. Training data comprised 1,014 [18F]-FDG PET/CT studies from the University Hospital Tübingen and 597 [18F]/[68Ga]-PSMA PET/CT studies from the LMU University Hospital Munich, constituting the largest publicly available annotated PSMA PET/CT dataset to date. The held-out test set of 200 studies covered four tracer-center combinations, two of which represented unseen compositional pairings. A complementary data-centric award category isolated the contribution of data handling strategies by restricting participants to a fixed baseline model. Seventeen teams submitted 27 algorithms, predominantly nnU-Net-based 3D networks with PET/CT channel concatenation. The top-ranked algorithm achieved a mean DSC of 0.66, FNV of 3.18 mL, and FPV of 2.78 mL across all four test conditions, improving DSC by 8% and reducing the false-negative volume by 5 mL relative to the provided baseline. Ranking was stable across bootstrap resampling and alternative ranking schemes for the top tier. Beyond the benchmark, we provide an in-depth analysis of segmentation performance at the patient and lesion level. Three main conclusions can be drawn: (1) in-domain multitracer PET/CT segmentation is sufficient and probably approaching reader agreement; (2) compositional generalization to unseen tracer-center combinations remains an open problem mainly driven by systematic volume overestimation; (3) heterogeneity and case difficulty drive performance variation substantially more than the choice of algorithm among top-ranked teams.

</details>


### [39] [EA-WM: Event-Aware Generative World Model with Structured Kinematic-to-Visual Action Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06192)
*Zhaoyang Yang,Yurun Jin,Lizhe Qi,Cong Huang,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EA-WM is an event-aware generative world model that uses Structured Kinematic-to-Visual Action Fields and bidirectional fusion to improve video synthesis for robotic world models, achieving SOTA on WorldArena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing world-action models treat video generation as merely auxiliary for policy learning, neglecting the inverse problem of using action signals to guide video synthesis, leading to poor preservation of spatial geometry and interaction dynamics.

Method: Projects actions/kinematic states into target camera view as Structured Kinematic-to-Visual Action Fields, and uses event-aware bidirectional fusion blocks to modulate cross-branch attention for capturing object state changes and interaction dynamics.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the WorldArena benchmark, significantly outperforming existing baselines.

Conclusion: Bridging kinematic control and visual perception via geometrically grounded action representations and event-aware fusion improves video synthesis quality in robotic world models.

Abstract: Pretrained video diffusion models provide powerful spatiotemporal generative priors, making them a natural foundation for robotic world models. While recent world-action models jointly optimize future videos and actions, they predominantly treat video generation as an auxiliary representation for policy learning. Consequently, they insufficiently explore the inverse problem: leveraging action signals to guide video synthesis, thereby often failing to preserve precise robot spatial geometry and fine-grained robot-object interaction dynamics in the generated rollouts. To bridge this gap, we present EA-WM, an Event-Aware Generative World Model that effectively closes the loop between kinematic control and visual perception. Rather than injecting joint or end-effector actions as abstract, low-dimensional tokens, EA-WM projects actions and kinematic states directly into the target camera view as Structured Kinematic-to-Visual Action Fields. To fully exploit this geometrically grounded representation, we introduce event-aware bidirectional fusion blocks that modulate cross-branch attention, capturing object state changes and interaction dynamics. Evaluated on the comprehensive WorldArena benchmark, EA-WM achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing baselines by a significant margin.

</details>


### [40] [Steering Visual Generation in Unified Multimodal Models with Understanding Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05781)
*Zeyu Liu,Zanlin Ni,Yang Yue,Cheng Da,Huan Yang,Di Zhang,Kun Gai,Gao Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UNO is a lightweight framework that uses understanding tasks (captioning, visual regression) as direct supervision to improve generative models, demonstrating synergy between understanding and generation in multimodal models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current state-of-the-art multimodal models decouple understanding and generation components for competitive performance, but this weakens the connection needed for mutual enhancement, leaving synergy empirically uncertain.

Method: UNO (Understanding-Oriented Post-Training) — a lightweight framework that treats understanding as a direct supervisory signal for generation, incorporating captioning (semantic abstraction) and visual regression (structural details) objectives to enable gradient flow from understanding to generation.

Result: Extensive experiments on image generation and editing demonstrate that understanding can serve as an effective catalyst for generation.

Conclusion: Understanding-oriented supervision can restore synergy between understanding and generation, improving generative performance without fully decoupling the two components.

Abstract: Unified multimodal models are envisioned to bridge the gap between understanding and generation. Yet, to achieve competitive performance, state-of-the-art models adopt largely decoupled understanding and generation components. This design, while effective for individual tasks, weakens the connection required for mutual enhancement, leaving the potential synergy empirically uncertain. We propose to explicitly restore this synergy by introducing Understanding-Oriented Post-Training (UNO), a lightweight framework that treats understanding not only as a distinct task, but also a direct supervisory signal to steer generative representations. By incorporating objectives that encode semantic abstraction (captioning) and structural details (visual regression), we enable effective gradient flow from understanding to generation. Extensive experiments on image generation and editing demonstrate that understanding can serve as an effective catalyst for generation.

</details>


### [41] [Na-IRSTD: Enhancing Infrared Small Target Detection via Native-Resolution Feature Selection and Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05804)
*Qian Xu,Chi Zhang,Qiming Zhang,Xi Li,Haojuan Yuan,Mingjin Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Na-IRSTD is a native-resolution feature extraction and fusion framework for infrared small target detection that preserves fine details through native-resolution processing and a token reduction/selection strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results on four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing IRSTD methods downsample features and discard small targets' details, leading to suboptimal localization performance for dim targets in complex backgrounds.

Method: Na-IRSTD preserves native-resolution features to retain subtle target cues, and introduces a token reduction and selection strategy that selects target patches with high accuracy/confidence to boost low-level details while reducing computational burden.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on four public benchmarks, with robust and effective token reduction demonstrated across multiple datasets.

Conclusion: Native-resolution processing with intelligent token selection effectively overcomes resolution limitations of existing infrared approaches, significantly improving small target localization without imposing unbearable computational cost.

Abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces the inherent challenge of precisely localizing dim targets amid complex background clutter. While progress has been made, existing methods usually follow conventional strategies to downsample features and discard small targets' details, resulting in suboptimal performance. In this paper, we present Na-IRSTD, a native-resolution feature extraction and fusion framework for IRSTD. This framework elegantly incorporates native-resolution features to preserve subtle target cues, overcoming the resolution limitations of existing infrared approaches and significantly improving the model's ability to localize small targets. We also introduce an effective token reduction and selection strategy, which selects target patches with high accuracy and confidence, boosting the low-level details of the feature while effectively reducing native-resolution patch tokens compared to dense processing, thereby avoiding imposing an unbearable computational burden. Extensive experiments demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of our token reduction and selection strategy across multiple public datasets. Ultimately, our Na-IRSTD model achieves state-of-the-art performance on four benchmarks.

</details>


### [42] [CXR-ContraBench: Benchmarking Negated-Option Attraction in Medical VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05810)
*Zhengru Fang,Yanan Ma,Yu Guo,Senkang Hu,Yixian Zhang,Hangcheng Cao,Wenbo Ding,Yuguang Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Medical vision-language models frequently exhibit "negated-option attraction"—selecting "No X" even when X is visible on chest X-rays. The authors introduce CXR-ContraBench to benchmark this failure, find it severe and persistent (models select negated options >62% on presence questions), and propose QCCV-Neg, a verifier that repairs these polarity reversals, boosting accuracy from ~30% to >95%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) fail in a clinically dangerous way: when a chest X-ray shows consolidation, they answer "No consolidation"—a polarity reversal contradicting both visual evidence and the question. This failure (negated-option attraction) is poorly captured by standard accuracy metrics, motivating the need for targeted benchmarking and mitigation.

Method: The authors construct CXR-ContraBench using internal ReXVQA slices and external OpenI and CheXpert datasets. They probe model behavior on present-finding (e.g., "Is consolidation present?") and absent-finding questions. They evaluate MedGemma and Qwen2.5-VL, test chain-of-thought prompting, and propose QCCV-Neg—a deterministic consistency verifier that repairs polarity-confused outputs without retraining.

Result: On a direct presence probe, MedGemma and Qwen2.5-VL achieve only 31.49% and 30.21% accuracy. On a matched 135,754-record CheXpert protocol, both models select negated options on >62% of presence questions. Chain-of-thought reduces some reversals but can amplify others. QCCV-Neg raises accuracy to 96.60% (MedGemma) and 95.32% (Qwen2.5-VL) without retraining.

Conclusion: Standard accuracy metrics can conceal clinically meaningful polarity-reversal failures in medical VLMs. Negated-option attraction is a persistent problem across models and datasets. QCCV-Neg provides a simple, effective plug-in repair. The work calls for negation-aware evaluation in medical AI systems.

Abstract: When a chest X-ray shows consolidation but the question asks which finding is present, a medical vision-language model may answer "No consolidation." This is more than an incorrect choice: it is a polarity reversal that emits a clinical statement contradicting the image. We study this failure as negated-option attraction, where a model is drawn to a negated answer option even when it conflicts with both the visual evidence and the question. We introduce CXR-ContraBench (Chest X-Ray Contradiction Benchmark), a diagnostic benchmark spanning internal ReXVQA slices and external OpenI and CheXpert protocols. The benchmark centers on present-finding questions, where selecting "No X" despite visible X creates the main clinical risk, and uses absent-finding questions as secondary tests of whether models copy negated wording. Across CheXpert protocols, the failure is substantial and persistent. On a strict direct presence probe, MedGemma and Qwen2.5-VL reach only 31.49% and 30.21% accuracy, respectively; on a matched 135,754-record CheXpert training-split protocol, both models select negated options on over 62% of presence questions. Chain-of-thought prompting reduces some presence-side reversals but does not eliminate them and can amplify absence-side contradictions. Finally, QCCV-Neg (Question-Conditioned Consistency Verifier for Negation) deterministically repairs the measured polarity-confused subset without retraining, raising MedGemma and Qwen2.5-VL to 96.60% and 95.32% accuracy on the direct presence probe. These results show that standard accuracy can hide a clinically meaningful inference-time polarity failure. Source code and benchmark construction scripts are available at https://github.com/fangzr/cxr-contrabench-code.

</details>


### [43] [Reconstruction or Semantics? What Makes a Latent Space Useful for Robotic World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06388)
*Nilaksh,Saurav Jha,Artem Zholus,Sarath Chandar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates different latent spaces (reconstruction vs. semantic encoders) for action-conditioned video diffusion world models in robotics, finding that semantic latent spaces like V-JEPA 2.1 outperform reconstruction-based ones (VAE, Cosmos) on planning and policy performance, despite lower pixel-level fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World model-based policy evaluation using video diffusion models needs effective latent spaces, but the choice between autoencoding (reconstruction-focused) and semantic (representation-aligned) latent spaces is unclear and lacks systematic evaluation for robotic applications.

Method: The authors systematically compared six reconstruction and semantic encoders (VAE, Cosmos, V-JEPA 2.1, Web-DINO, SigLIP 2) to train world model variants under a fixed protocol on BridgeV2 dataset, assessing performance across three axes: visual fidelity, planning/policy performance, and latent representation quality.

Result: Visual fidelity alone is insufficient for world model selection. Reconstruction encoders (VAE, Cosmos) achieved strong pixel-level scores, but semantic encoders (V-JEPA 2.1 strongest overall on policy, Web-DINO, SigLIP 2) generally excelled on planning and policy performance at all model scales.

Conclusion: The study advocates for semantic latent spaces as a stronger foundation for policy-relevant robotics diffusion world models, suggesting that representation quality matters more than pixel reconstruction fidelity for downstream robot control tasks.

Abstract: World model-based policy evaluation is a practical proxy for testing real-world robot control by rolling out candidate actions in action-conditioned video diffusion models. As these models increasingly adopt latent diffusion modeling (LDM), choosing the right latent space becomes critical. While the status quo uses autoencoding latent spaces like VAEs that are primarily trained for pixel reconstruction, recent work suggests benefits from pretrained encoders with representation-aligned semantic latent spaces. We systematically evaluate these latent spaces for action-conditioned LDM by comparing six reconstruction and semantic encoders to train world model variants under a fixed protocol on BridgeV2 dataset, and show effective world model training in high-dimensional representation spaces with and without dimension compression. We then propose three axes to assess robotic world model performance: visual fidelity, planning and downstream policy performance, and latent representation quality. Our results show visual fidelity alone is insufficient for world model selection. While reconstruction encoders like VAE and Cosmos achieve strong pixel-level scores, semantic encoders such as V-JEPA 2.1 (strongest overall on policy), Web-DINO, and SigLIP 2 generally excel across the other two axes at all model scales. Our study advocates semantic latent space as stronger foundation for policy-relevant robotics diffusion world models.

</details>


### [44] [ChartZero: Synthetic Priors Enable Zero Shot Chart Data Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05820)
*Md Touhidul Islam,Yasir Mahmud,Sujan Kumar Saha,Mark Tehranipoor,Farimah Farahmandi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ChartZero is a zero-shot chart data extraction framework that trains exclusively on synthetic data, uses a Global Orthogonal Instance loss to prevent curve fragmentation, and employs VLM-guided legend matching to replace brittle spatial heuristics, achieving robust generalization without real-world annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated chart data extraction is bottlenecked by extreme stylistic diversity, scarcity of annotated real-world datasets, and high cost of manual annotations. Existing pipelines suffer from curve fragmentation on thin intersecting lines and error-prone semantic association due to rigid spatial heuristics for legend matching.

Method: ChartZero trains exclusively on a purely synthetic dataset of simple mathematical functions, bypassing real-world annotation needs. It introduces a Global Orthogonal Instance (GOI) loss to mitigate curve fragmentation, and replaces spatial heuristics with an open-vocabulary Vision-Language Model (VLM)-guided legend matching strategy.

Result: Evaluated on a new end-to-end reconstruction benchmark and metric, ChartZero significantly advances generalized plot digitization without requiring real-world supervision.

Conclusion: ChartZero demonstrates that synthetic priors alone can enable robust zero-shot chart data extraction, overcoming key failure modes of existing pipelines.

Abstract: Automated data extraction from line charts remains fundamentally bottlenecked by extreme stylistic diversity and a severe scarcity of comprehensively annotated, real-world datasets. Current end-to-end pipelines depend heavily on costly manual annotations, crippling their ability to generalize across arbitrary aesthetics and grid layouts. Furthermore, existing models suffer from two critical failure modes during reconstruction. First, extracting thin, intersecting curves frequently causes structural fragmentation and the erasure of fine visual details, as standard architectures struggle against complex backgrounds. Second, semantic association is notoriously error-prone; current pipelines rely on rigid spatial heuristics that easily break down against the unpredictable legend placements of in-the-wild charts. Finally, measuring true progress is hindered by evaluation protocols that assess isolated sub-tasks rather than holistic, end-to-end data reconstruction. To address these foundational issues, we introduce ChartZero, a parsing framework that leverages synthetic priors to enable robust zero-shot chart data extraction. By training exclusively on a purely synthetic dataset of simple mathematical functions, our model completely bypasses the real-world annotation bottleneck. We overcome curve fragmentation via a novel Global Orthogonal Instance (GOI) loss, and replace brittle spatial rules with an open-vocabulary, Vision-Language Model (VLM)-guided legend matching strategy. Accompanied by a new metric and benchmark specifically designed for full end-to-end reconstruction, our evaluations demonstrate that ChartZero significantly advances generalized plot digitization without requiring real-world supervision. Code and dataset will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [45] [Unifying Scientific Communication: Fine-Grained Correspondence Across Scientific Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05831)
*Megha Mariam K. M,Vineeth N. Balasubramanian,C. V. Jawahar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Multimodal Conference Dataset (MCD), the first benchmark connecting research papers, slides, videos, and presentations from the same scientific works, and evaluates embedding-based and vision-language models on their ability to align fine-grained cross-format correspondences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific communication is increasingly multimodal (text, visuals, speech) across papers, slides, and videos, but these materials lack structured connections, making it difficult to trace how concepts correspond across formats and limiting unified analysis.

Method: The authors curate the Multimodal Conference Dataset (MCD) linking research papers, presentation videos, explanatory videos, and slides from the same works, and evaluate embedding-based and vision-language models on fine-grained cross-format correspondence discovery.

Result: Vision-language models show robustness but struggle with fine-grained alignment; embedding-based models capture text-visual correspondences well, but equations and symbolic content form isolated clusters in embedding space.

Conclusion: Current approaches have complementary strengths and limitations, highlighting key directions for future multimodal scientific understanding research; resources are released for reproducibility.

Abstract: The communication of scientific knowledge has become increasingly multimodal, spanning text, visuals, and speech through materials such as research papers, slides, and recorded presentations. These different representations collectively convey a study's reasoning, results, and insights, offering complementary perspectives that enrich understanding. However, despite their shared purpose, such materials are rarely connected in a structured way. The absence of explicit links across formats makes it difficult to trace how concepts, visuals, and explanations correspond, limiting unified exploration and analysis of research content. To address this gap, we introduce the Multimodal Conference Dataset (MCD), the first benchmark that integrates research papers, presentation videos, explanatory videos, and slides from the same works. We evaluate a range of embedding-based and vision-language models to assess their ability to discover fine-grained cross-format correspondences, establishing the first systematic benchmark for this task. Our results show that vision-language models are robust but struggle with fine-grained alignment, while embedding-based models capture text-visual correspondences well but equations and symbolic content form distinct clusters in the embedding space. These findings highlight both the strengths and limitations of current approaches and point to key directions for future research in multimodal scientific understanding. To ensure reproducibility, we release the resources for MCD at https://github.com/meghamariamkm2002/MCD

</details>


### [46] [VideoRouter: Query-Adaptive Dual Routing for Efficient Long-Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05848)
*Kuanwei Lin,Wenhao Zhang,Ge Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VideoRouter is a query-adaptive dual-router framework that reduces visual token length in long video processing by dynamically allocating compression policies, achieving up to 67.9% token reduction while maintaining or improving performance on video benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long videos produce excessively long visual-token sequences, causing memory and latency bottlenecks during inference. Existing compression methods are either weakly query-aware or apply fixed policies across frames, which is suboptimal when visual evidence is unevenly distributed over time.

Method: VideoRouter introduces a dual-router framework: (1) Semantic Router predicts dominant allocation policy (broad temporal coverage vs. adaptive high-resolution preservation); (2) Image Router uses early LLM layers to score frame relevance for aggressive compression on less relevant frames. Training uses two datasets: Video-QTR-10K for allocation-policy supervision and Video-FLR-200K for frame-relevance supervision.

Result: Experiments on VideoMME, MLVU, and LongVideoBench show VideoRouter consistently improves over InternVL baseline under comparable or lower budgets, achieving up to 67.9% token reduction.

Conclusion: Query-adaptive dual-router compression effectively addresses the scalability bottleneck of long video LMMs by dynamically allocating visual evidence budgets, outperforming fixed-policy compression methods.

Abstract: Video large multimodal models increasingly face a scalability bottleneck: long videos produce excessively long visual-token sequences, which sharply increase memory and latency during inference. While existing compression methods are effective in specific settings, most are either weakly query-aware or apply a fixed compression policy across frames, proving suboptimal when visual evidence is unevenly distributed over time. To address this, we present VideoRouter, a query-adaptive dual-router framework built on InternVL for budgeted evidence allocation. The Semantic Router predicts the dominant allocation policy, choosing between broad temporal coverage and adaptive high-resolution preservation, while the Image Router uses early LLM layers to score frame relevance. This enables aggressive compression on less relevant frames while preserving detail on critical evidence frames. To train both routers, we build Video-QTR-10K for allocation-policy supervision and Video-FLR-200K for frame-relevance supervision. Experiments on VideoMME, MLVU, and LongVideoBench show that VideoRouter consistently improves over the InternVL baseline under comparable or lower budgets, achieving up to a 67.9% token reduction.

</details>


### [47] [Align3D-AD: Cross-Modal Feature Alignment and Dual-Prompt Learning for Zero-shot 3D Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05850)
*Letian Bai,Xuanming Cao,Juan Du,Chengyu Tao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Align3D-AD is a unified two-stage framework for zero-shot 3D anomaly detection that bridges the domain gap between 3D rendering features and RGB-semantic space via cross-modal feature alignment and modality-aware prompt learning, outperforming existing methods on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing zero-shot 3D anomaly detection methods project 3D observations into multi-view representations that capture mostly geometric cues rather than realistic visual semantics, creating a domain gap when processed with RGB-pretrained vision encoders. The authors aim to resolve this by leveraging RGB modality from auxiliary categories as cross-modal guidance.

Method: The framework has two stages: (1) Cross-modal feature alignment that maps rendering features into RGB semantic space via semantic consistency reweighting for local region refinement; (2) Modality-aware prompt learning with dual-prompt contrastive alignment, assigning independent prompts to RGB-aligned and rendering features to capture complementary cross-modal semantics.

Result: Extensive experiments on MVTec3D-AD, Eyecandies, and Real3D-AD show that Align3D-AD consistently outperforms existing zero-shot methods under both one-vs-rest and cross-dataset settings, demonstrating superior generalization and robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed cross-modal alignment and modality-aware prompt learning effectively bridge the domain gap in zero-shot 3D anomaly detection, achieving state-of-the-art performance and highlighting strong generalization capability across diverse datasets.

Abstract: Zero-shot 3D anomaly detection aims to identify anomalies without access to training data from target categories. However, existing methods mainly rely on projecting 3D observations into multi-view representations that primarily capture geometric cues rather than realistic visual semantics and process them with vision encoders pretrained on RGB data, leading to a significant domain gap between the encoder and the projected representations. To address this issue, we propose Align3D-AD, a unified two-stage framework that leverages the RGB modality from auxiliary categories as cross-modal guidance for zero-shot 3D anomaly detection. First, we introduce a cross-modal feature alignment paradigm that maps rendering features into the RGB semantic space. Unlike prior works that implicitly rely on pretrained encoders, our method enables direct semantic transfer from RGB observations. A semantic consistency reweighting strategy is further introduced to refine feature alignment by reweighting local regions according to holistic semantic consistency. Second, we propose a modality-aware prompt learning framework with dual-prompt contrastive alignment. By assigning independent prompts to RGB-aligned and rendering features, our method captures complementary semantics across modalities, while the contrastive alignment further enhances prompt representations to improve discriminability. Extensive experiments on MVTec3D-AD, Eyecandies, and Real3D-AD demonstrate that Align3D-AD consistently outperforms existing zero-shot methods under both one-vs-rest and cross-dataset settings, highlighting its generalization capability and robustness. Code and the dataset will be made available once our paper is accepted.

</details>


### [48] [InkDiffuser: High-Fidelity One-shot Chinese Calligraphy via Differentiable Morphological Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05865)
*Kunchong Shi,Jing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InkDiffuser is a diffusion-based framework for one-shot Chinese calligraphy generation that uses a high-frequency enhancement mechanism and a Differentiable Ink Structure (DIS) loss to produce realistic ink morphology and stroke rendering from a single reference glyph.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Chinese calligraphy generation methods suffer from poor stroke rendering, unrealistic ink morphology, limited visual fidelity, and lack of artistic fluidity.

Method: The proposed InkDiffuser uses: (1) a high-frequency enhancement mechanism that fuses high-frequency information to capture contour details and improve font structure, and (2) a Differentiable Ink Structure (DIS) loss that integrates differentiable morphological operations into the diffusion process for explicit decomposition and refinement of ink-trace structures.

Result: InkDiffuser outperforms existing few-shot font generation methods in structural consistency, detail fidelity, and visual authenticity, generating superior calligraphy fonts with realistic ink rendering from a single reference glyph across various styles and complex characters.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses stroke rendering and ink morphology issues in calligraphy generation, achieving high-fidelity one-shot synthesis with realistic visual outcomes.

Abstract: Current Chinese calligraphy generation methods suffer from poor stroke rendering and unrealistic ink morphology, resulting in outputs with limited visual fidelity and artistic fluidity. To address this problem, we propose \textbf{InkDiffuser}, a diffusion-based generative framework for one-shot Chinese calligraphy synthesis. To guarantee high-fidelity rendering, we introduce two core contributions: a high-frequency enhancement mechanism and a Differentiable Ink Structure (DIS) loss that explicitly regularizes ink morphology. Inspired by the observation that high-frequency information in individual samples typically carries contour details, we enhance content extraction by explicitly fusing high-frequency representations for more accurate font structure. Furthermore, we propose a differentiable ink structure loss that integrates differentiable morphological operations into the diffusion process. By allowing the model to learn an explicit decomposition of ink-trace structures, DIS facilitates fine-grained refinement of stroke contours and delivers significantly improved visual realism in the generated calligraphy. Extensive experiments on various calligraphic styles and complex characters demonstrate that InkDiffuser can generate superior calligraphy fonts with realistic ink rendering effects from only a single reference glyph and outperform existing few-shot font generation approaches in structural consistency, detail fidelity, and visual authenticity. The code is available at the following address: https://github.com/JingVIPLab/InkDiffuser.

</details>


### [49] [Training-Free Dense Hand Contact Estimation with Multi-Modal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05886)
*Daniel Sungho Jung,Kyoung Mu Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ContactPrompt is a training-free, zero-shot dense hand contact estimation method using MLLMs. It introduces hand-part segmentation, part-wise vertex-grid representation, and multi-stage structured reasoning to bridge global semantics with fine-grained geometry, outperforming supervised methods without any training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs have strong visual semantic understanding but are underexplored for dense hand contact estimation due to challenges in encoding 3D hand geometry and capturing fine-grained vertex-level contact.

Method: ContactPrompt uses: (1) detailed hand-part segmentation + part-wise vertex-grid representation for 3D geometry encoding, and (2) multi-stage structured contact reasoning with part conditioning to progressively bridge global semantics and fine-grained geometry.

Result: Outperforms previous supervised methods trained on large-scale dense contact datasets, despite being a zero-shot approach requiring no training.

Conclusion: MLLMs can be effectively leveraged for dense hand contact estimation through proper geometric encoding and structured reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art results without training.

Abstract: Dense hand contact estimation requires both high-level semantic understanding and fine-grained geometric reasoning of human interaction to accurately localize contact regions. Recently, multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in understanding visual semantics, enabled by vision-language priors learned from large-scale data. However, leveraging MLLMs for dense hand contact estimation remains underexplored. There are two major challenges in applying MLLMs to dense hand contact estimation. First, encoding explicit 3D hand geometry is difficult, as MLLMs primarily operate on vision and language modalities. Second, capturing fine-grained vertex-level contact remains challenging, as MLLMs tend to focus on high-level semantics rather than detailed geometric reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose ContactPrompt, a training-free and zero-shot approach for dense hand contact estimation using MLLMs. To effectively encode 3D hand geometry, we introduce a detailed hand-part segmentation and a part-wise vertex-grid representation that provides structured, localized geometric information. To enable accurate and efficient dense contact prediction, we develop a multi-stage structured contact reasoning with part conditioning, progressively bridging global semantics and fine-grained geometry. Therefore, our method effectively leverages the reasoning capabilities of MLLMs while enabling precise dense hand contact estimation. Surprisingly, the proposed approach outperforms previous supervised methods trained on large-scale dense contact datasets without requiring any training. The codes will be released.

</details>


### [50] [DBMSolver: A Training-free Diffusion Bridge Sampler for High-Quality Image-to-Image Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05889)
*Sankarshana Venugopal,Mohammad Mostafavi,Jonghyun Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DBMSolver is a training-free sampler for Diffusion Bridge Models that uses exponential integrators to exploit semi-linear structures, reducing function evaluations by up to 5x while improving image quality (e.g., 53% FID drop on DIODE). It achieves state-of-the-art efficiency-quality tradeoffs across various I2I tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion Bridge Models (DBMs) achieve high-quality image-to-image translation but suffer from slow sampling, requiring dozens of function evaluations (NFEs), which limits real-world applicability.

Method: DBMSolver is a training-free sampler that leverages exponential integrators to exploit the semi-linear structure of DBM's underlying SDE and ODE, providing 1st- and 2nd-order efficient solutions.

Result: Reduces NFEs by up to 5x while improving quality (e.g., 53% FID drop on DIODE at 20 NFEs vs. 2nd-order baseline). Achieves new SOTA efficiency-quality tradeoffs on inpainting, stylization, and semantics-to-image tasks up to 256x256 resolution.

Conclusion: DBMSolver enables real-world applicability of DBMs by drastically accelerating sampling without sacrificing quality, setting new state-of-the-art benchmarks in diffusion-based I2I translation.

Abstract: Diffusion-based image-to-image (I2I) translation excels in high-fidelity generation but suffers from slow sampling in state-of-the-art Diffusion Bridge Models (DBMs), often requiring dozens of function evaluations (NFEs). We introduce DBMSolver, a training-free sampler that exploits the semi-linear structure of DBM's underlying SDE and ODE via exponential integrators, yielding highly-efficient 1st- and 2nd-order solutions. This reduces NFEs by up to 5x while boosting quality (e.g., FID drops 53% on DIODE at 20 NFEs vs. 2nd-order baseline). Experiments on inpainting, stylization, and semantics-to-image tasks across resolutions up to 256x256 show DBMSolver sets new SOTA efficiency-quality tradeoffs, enabling real-world applicability. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/snumprlab/dbmsolver.

</details>


### [51] [MTL-MAD: Multi-Task Learners are Effective Medical Anomaly Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05891)
*Bogdan Alexandru Bercean,Florinel Alin Croitoru,Vlad Hondru,Ciprian Mihai Ceausescu,Andreea Iuliana Ionescu,Radu Tudor Ionescu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-task learning approach using Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) combines multiple self-supervised and pseudo-labeling tasks from scratch to detect anomalies in medical images, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on the BMAD benchmark while producing interpretable anomaly maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anomaly detection in medical images is challenging because anomalies are unavailable during training. Existing methods rely on single pretext tasks with large pre-trained models, which may limit representation learning of normal anatomical structures.

Method: The authors propose learning multiple self-supervised and pseudo-labeling tasks from scratch using a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) joint model. Multiple proxy tasks are integrated to learn robust representations of normal anatomy, and anomaly scores are derived from how well the multi-task learner solves each task during inference.

Result: Comprehensive experiments on the BMAD benchmark, covering diverse medical image modalities, show the multi-task learner outperforms all state-of-the-art competitors. The model also produces interpretable anomaly maps.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-task learning with MoE is an effective anomaly detector for medical images, achieving superior performance and providing interpretability that could assist physicians in diagnosis.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in medical images is a challenging task, since anomalies are not typically available during training. Recent methods leverage a single pretext task coupled with a large-scale pre-trained model to reach state-of-the-art performance. Instead, we propose to learn multiple self-supervised and pseudo-labeling tasks from scratch, using a joint model based on Mixture-of-Experts (MoE). By carefully integrating multiple proxy tasks, the joint model effectively learns a robust representation of normal anatomical structures, so that anomaly scores can be derived based on how well the multi-task learner (MTL) solves each task during inference. We perform comprehensive experiments on BMAD, a recent benchmark that comprises a broad range of medical image modalities. The empirical results indicate that our multi-task learner is an effective anomaly detector, outperforming all state-of-the-art competitors on BMAD. Moreover, our model produces interpretable anomaly maps, potentially helping physicians in providing more accurate diagnoses.

</details>


### [52] [Detecting AI-Generated Videos with Spiking Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05895)
*Minsuk Jang,Yujin Yang,Heeseon Kim,Minseok Son,Younghun Kim,Changick Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAST uses Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) to detect AI-generated videos by exploiting temporal smoothness gaps at pixel and semantic levels, achieving 93.14% accuracy across unseen generators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI-generated video detectors rely on ANN-based temporal analysis (full sequences, fixed descriptors, or statistical metrics), which degrade sharply under cross-generator evaluation due to varying artifact types and timescales. Detectors fail to jointly exploit multi-level temporal smoothness gaps.

Method: MAST processes multi-channel temporal residuals with a spike-driven temporal branch (using SNNs) alongside a frozen semantic encoder. SNNs naturally align with sparse, asynchronous temporal artifacts concentrated at object/motion boundaries.

Result: 93.14% mean accuracy across 10 unseen generators on GenVideo benchmark under strict cross-generator evaluation, matching/surpassing strong ANN-based detectors.

Conclusion: SNNs are practically applicable to AI-generated video detection, offering event-driven, sparsely-activated dynamics that align with temporal residual signals better than dense ANN backbones.

Abstract: Modern AI-generated videos are photorealistic at the single-frame level, leaving inter-frame dynamics as the main remaining axis for detection. Existing detectors typically handle this temporal evidence in three ways: feeding the full frame sequence to a generic temporal backbone, reducing one dominant temporal cue to fixed video-level descriptors, or comparing temporal features to real-video statistics through a detection metric. These strategies degrade sharply under cross-generator evaluation, where artifact type and timescale vary across generators. On caption-paired benchmark, GenVidBench, we identify two signatures that prior detectors do not jointly exploit: AI-generated videos exhibit smoother frame-to-frame temporal residuals at the pixel level, and more compact trajectories in the semantic feature space, indicating a temporal smoothness gap at both levels. We further observe that, when raw video is fed into a Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), fake clips elicit firing predominantly at object and motion boundaries, unlike real clips, suggesting that the SNN responds to temporal artifacts localized at edges. These cues are sparse, asynchronous, and concentrated at moments of change, which makes SNNs a natural choice for this task: their event-driven, sparsely-activated dynamics align with the structure of the residual signal in a way that dense ANN backbones do not. Building on this observation, we propose MAST, a detector that processes multi-channel temporal residuals with a spike-driven temporal branch alongside a frozen semantic encoder for cross-generator generalization. On the GenVideo benchmark, MAST achieves 93.14\% mean accuracy across 10 unseen generators under strict cross-generator evaluation, matching or surpassing the strongest ANN-based detectors and demonstrating the practical applicability of SNNs to AI-generated video detection.

</details>


### [53] [Understanding Cross-Language Transfer Improvements in Low-Resource HTR: The Role of Sequence Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05900)
*Sana Al-azzawi,Chang Liu,Nudrat Habib,Elisa Barney,Marcus Liwicki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether cross-language transfer benefits in Arabic-script HTR come from shared visual features or sequence-level modeling, finding that contextual/sequence-level dependencies drive improvements, not just shared character shapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether cross-language joint training improvements in Arabic-script HTR stem from shared visual representations or sequence-level dependencies, since prior work showed benefits but not why.
