If certain stability criteria are met, the update is guaranteed to be safe; otherwise, it remains no less safe than standard approximate policy iteration. Applying SV-API to PPO yields Stable Value PPO (SV-PPO), which matches or improves performance on high-dimensional discrete (Atari) and continuous control benchmarks while executing substantially larger target policy updates. These results demonstrate the viability of ANPS as a new solution to this classic challenge in RL.

</details>


### [160] [A Robust Foundation Model for Conservation Laws: Injecting Context into Flux Neural Operators via Recurrent Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05488)
*Taeyoung Kim,Joon-Hyuk Ko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes CONTEXT-FLUX-NO, a hypernetwork architecture that augments Flux Neural Operator with a recurrent Vision Transformer to infer and solve conservation laws from observed dynamics without needing explicit PDE coefficients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable neural operators to solve conservation laws without explicit access to governing equations or PDE coefficients, overcoming the limitation of standard Flux NO that requires known flux functions.

Method: A hypernetwork architecture combining Flux Neural Operator with a recurrent Vision Transformer (ViT) that extracts solution dynamics over a temporal window, encodes them, and generates parameters for a context-conditioned neural operator.

Result: The method preserves robustness, generalization, and long-time prediction advantages of Flux NO over standard neural operators, and delivers reliable numerical solutions across diverse conservative systems including unseen fluxes.

Conclusion: Context-conditioned Flux NO successfully infers conservation laws from observed dynamics alone, expanding applicability to systems where PDE coefficients are unknown.

Abstract: We propose an architecture that augments the Flux Neural Operator (Flux NO), which combines the classical finite volume method (FVM) with neural operators, with ViT-based context injection. Our model is formulated as a hypernetwork: it extracts solution dynamics over a finite temporal window, encodes them with a recurrent Vision Transformer, and generates the parameters of a context-conditioned neural operator. This enables the model to infer and solve conservation laws without explicit access to the governing equation or PDE coefficients. Experimentally, we show that the proposed method preserves the robustness, generalization ability, and long-time prediction advantages of Flux NO over standard neural operators, while delivering reliable numerical solutions across a broad range of conservative systems, including previously unseen fluxes. Our code is available at https://github.com/xx257xx/CONTEXT_FLUX_NO.

</details>


### [161] [MEMOA: Massive Mixtures of Online Agents via Mean-Field Decentralized Nash Equilibria](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05492)
*Xuwei Yang,David B. Emerson,Fatemeh Tavakoli,Anastasis Kratsios*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper derives a closed-form optimal decentralized policy for federated learning using mean-field theory and a minimax criterion, showing it converges to the scalable centralized Nash-optimal policy in large populations, and improves predictions via online weighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning faces scalability issues in computational and communication costs as the number of agents grows large, motivating the need for efficient decentralized agentic strategies.

Method: The authors derive the unique optimal decentralized policy in closed form using a worst-client/minimax criterion (minimizing under-performer regret) and employ an online weighting mechanism to optimize server-computed mixtures of client predictions.

Result: The decentralized policy asymptotically converges to the Nash-optimal centralized policy in the large-population limit, and numerical experiments show it outperforms natural greedy decentralized baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed decentralized policy is both optimal under the minimax criterion and scalable, offering a principled alternative to centralized federated learning in large agent populations.

Abstract: In the modern age of large-scale AI, federated learning has become an increasingly important tool for training large populations of AI agents; however, its computational and communication costs can rapidly fail to scale with the number of agents. This is precisely where decentralized agentic strategies shine: each agent acts autonomously, using only its own state together with a minimal summary of the ensemble, namely the mean-field. We derive the unique optimal decentralized policy in closed form. Optimality is characterized through a worst-client/minimax criterion: minimizing the under-performer regret, namely the maximal online cost incurred by the weakest agent in the ensemble. We further prove that the resulting decentralized policy asymptotically converges, in the large-population limit, to the Nash-optimal centralized policy, whose direct computation is not scalable. We use an online weighting mechanism to optimize the server-computed mixture of client predictions, thereby improving the mean prediction in addition to the previously optimized weakest-client prediction. Numerical experiments verify our theoretical guarantees and demonstrate that our decentralized policy typically outperforms natural greedy decentralized baselines.

</details>


### [162] [Shortcut Solutions Learned by Transformers Impair Continual Compositional Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05495)
*William T. Redman,Erik C. Johnson,Brian Robinson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends the LEGO framework to continual learning (continual LEGO) to study how Transformer models handle composition across experiences, finding that BERT learns shortcut solutions limiting generalization while ALBERT (a recurrent variant) exhibits better inductive bias for continual learning, though both fail at cross-experience composition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically understand how well Transformer neural network models can leverage their representations to learn new, related experiences in a continual learning setting.

Method: The authors expand the Learning Equality and Group Operations (LEGO) framework to a continual learning setting ("continual LEGO") and study feedforward (BERT) and recurrent (ALBERT) Transformer models.

Result: BERT learns shortcut solutions limiting generalization and forward transfer; ALBERT learns a For loop-esque solution leading to better CL performance. Both fail at cross-experience composition. ALBERT's performance can be rescued by combining data across experiences, but BERT's shortcut solution becomes entrenched.

Conclusion: The recurrent ALBERT model may have an inductive bias better suited for continual learning, motivating further investigation of the interplay between Transformer architecture and computational solutions in modern models.

Abstract: Identifying and exploiting common features across domains is at the heart of the human ability to make analogies, and is believed to be crucial for the ability to continually learn. To do this successfully, general and flexible computational strategies must be developed. While the extent to which Transformer neural network models can perform compositional reasoning has been the subject of intensive recent investigation, little work has been done to systematically understand how well these models can leverage their representations to learn new, related experiences. To address this gap, we expand the previously developed Learning Equality and Group Operations (LEGO) framework to a continual learning (CL) setting ("continual LEGO"). Using this continual LEGO experimental paradigm, we study the capability of feedforward and recurrent Transformer models to perform CL. We find that BERT, a canonical feedforward Transformer model, learns shortcut solutions that limits its ability to generalize and prevents strong forward transfer to new experiences. In contrast, we find evidence supporting the hypothesis that ALBERT, a recurrent version of BERT, learns a For loop-esque solution, which leads to better CL performance. When applying BERT and ALBERT models to a CL setting that requires composition across experiences, we find that both model families fail. Our investigation suggests that ALBERT models can have their performance drop rescued by use of training strategies that combine data across experiences, but this is not true for BERT models, where a detrimental shortcut solution becomes entrenched with initial training. Our results demonstrate that the recurrent ALBERT model may have an inductive bias better suited for CL and motivate future investigation of the interplay between Transformer architecture and computational solutions that emerge in modern models and tasks.

</details>


### [163] [Online Localized Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05497)
*Yuheng Lai,Garvesh Raskutti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OLCP and OLCP-Hedge combine online conformal prediction with covariate-dependent localization to achieve valid long-run coverage with narrower prediction sets than existing methods like ACI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing online conformal methods (e.g., ACI) achieve long-run validity but remain inefficient under covariate heterogeneity because they rely on global calibration.

Method: OLCP combines online adaptation with covariate-dependent localization; OLCP-Hedge performs bandwidth selection via online expert aggregation using constrained online convex optimization.

Result: Both algorithms achieve valid long-run coverage with narrower prediction sets than existing baselines in simulations and real-data experiments.

Conclusion: OLCP and OLCP-Hedge improve efficiency under covariate heterogeneity while maintaining coverage guarantees in non-exchangeable online settings.

Abstract: Conformal prediction is a framework that provides valid uncertainty quantification for general models with exchangeable data. However, in the online learning and time-series settings, exchangeability is not satisfied. Existing online conformal methods, such as adaptive conformal inference (ACI), can achieve long-run validity, yet they remain inefficient under covariate heterogeneity because they rely on global calibration. We propose \emph{Online Localized Conformal Prediction (OLCP)}, which combines online adaptation with covariate-dependent localization to better reflect heterogeneity. To reduce sensitivity to the localization bandwidth, we further develop \emph{OLCP-Hedge}, which performs bandwidth selection as an online expert aggregation problem using a constrained online convex optimization framework. Importantly, we provide coverage guarantees for both algorithms and demonstrate through simulations and real-data experiments that the proposed methods attain valid long-run coverage with narrower prediction sets than existing baselines.

</details>


### [164] [Non-Myopic Active Feature Acquisition via Pathwise Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05511)
*Linus Aronsson,Morteza Haghir Chehreghani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NM-PPG introduces non-myopic pathwise policy gradients for active feature acquisition, using continuous relaxation and straight-through rollout to optimize acquisition policies end-to-end, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive models often assume all features are freely available, but in practice, acquiring features incurs costs. The goal is to adaptively decide which features to acquire for each instance and when to stop, balancing cost and accuracy.

Method: Formulates AFA as a POMDP; uses continuous relaxation of the acquisition process to enable pathwise gradients through the full trajectory; employs a straight-through rollout scheme with hard acquisitions in forward pass and soft relaxation in backward pass; stabilizes with entropy regularization and staged temperature sharpening.

Result: NM-PPG achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art AFA baselines on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed non-myopic pathwise policy gradient method effectively addresses the high variance issue of standard policy gradients and enables end-to-end optimization, leading to better acquisition policies in AFA problems.

Abstract: Active feature acquisition (AFA) considers prediction problems in which features are costly to obtain and the learner adaptively decides which feature values to acquire for each instance and when to stop and predict. AFA can be formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), which naturally admits a sequential decision-making perspective. In this paper, we present non-myopic pathwise policy gradients (NM-PPG), a new AFA method built around this formulation. We introduce a continuous relaxation of the acquisition process that enables pathwise gradients through the full acquisition trajectory, avoiding the high variance of standard score-function policy gradients while allowing end-to-end optimization of a non-myopic acquisition policy. To better align training with deployment, we further develop a straight-through rollout scheme that follows hard feature acquisitions in the forward pass while backpropagating through the corresponding soft relaxation in the backward pass. We stabilize optimization with entropy regularization and staged temperature sharpening. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that NM-PPG yields superior performance relative to state-of-the-art AFA baselines.

</details>


### [165] [Structure-Preserving Gaussian Processes Via Discrete Euler-Lagrange Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06246)
*Jan-Hendrik Ewering,Kathrin Flaßkamp,Niklas Wahlström,Thomas B. Schön,Thomas Seel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) that learn dynamics from discrete position snapshots by preserving the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle, enabling physically consistent, data-efficient, and stable long-term predictions with uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome erroneous drift in system energy and provide stable long-term predictions when learning dynamics, especially in scenarios where only position measurements (not velocities/momenta) are available, such as motion capture or visual servoing.

Method: LGPs leverage linear operators for Gaussian process conditioning constructed from discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization schemes, preserving the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle.

Result: Demonstrated data-efficiency and generalization in synthetic and real-world case studies, including a soft robot with hysteresis, showing physically consistent dynamics with uncertainty quantification from sparse positional data and stable long-term predictions.

Conclusion: LGPs learn physically consistent dynamics solely from sparse positional data while providing uncertainty quantification, overcoming energy drift issues present in prior methods.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) for probabilistic and data-efficient learning of dynamics via discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations. Importantly, the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle, which governs the motion of dynamical systems, is preserved by construction in the absence of external forces. This allows learning physically consistent models that overcome erroneous drift in the system's energy, thereby providing stable long-term predictions. At the core of our approach lie linear operators for Gaussian process conditioning, constructed from discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization schemes. Thereby and unlike prior work, the method enables learning dynamics from discrete position snapshots, i.e., without access to a system's velocities or momenta. This is particularly relevant for a large class of practical scenarios where only position measurements are available, for instance, in motion capture or visual servoing applications. We demonstrate the data-efficiency and generalization capabilities of the LGPs in various synthetic and real-world case studies, including a real-world soft robot with hysteresis. The experimental results underscore that the LGPs learn physically consistent dynamics with uncertainty quantification solely from sparse positional data and enable stable long-term predictions.

</details>


### [166] [OpenG2G: A Simulation Platform for AI Datacenter-Grid Runtime Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05519)
*Jae-Won Chung,Zhirui Liang,Yanyong Mao,Jiasi Chen,Mosharaf Chowdhury,Vladimir Dvorkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpenG2G is a modular simulation platform for AI datacenter-grid runtime coordination that enables testing various control paradigms and quantifying how AI model choices affect grid flexibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI's growing compute demand and new datacenter buildouts create major capacity and reliability challenges for the electricity grid, causing multi-year interconnection delays that bottleneck AI growth.

Method: OpenG2G features a modular architecture with: a datacenter backend driven by real production-grade AI service measurements, a grid backend built on high-fidelity grid simulators, and a generic controller interface enabling classic, optimization, and learning-based control paradigms.

Result: The platform can answer a wide range of coordination questions, allowing users to implement and compare various control paradigms and quantify how AI model/deployment choices affect datacenter flexibility and coordination outcomes.

Conclusion: OpenG2G is demonstrated to be useful through realistic grid scenarios and AI workloads, providing a versatile tool for designing effective datacenter-grid coordination strategies.

Abstract: AI's growing compute demand and new datacenter buildouts present major capacity and reliability challenges for the electricity grid, leading to multi-year interconnection delays for new datacenters and bottlenecking AI growth. To ease this strain, datacenters increasingly offer rapid power flexibility in response to grid signals, where the datacenter can increase or decrease its power consumption by adapting its workload in real time.
  In order to understand the impact of large datacenters on the grid and to facilitate the design of effective coordination strategies, we build OpenG2G, a simulation platform for AI datacenter-grid runtime coordination. We show that OpenG2G is capable of answering a wide range of coordination questions by allowing users to implement and compare various control paradigms (including classic, optimization, and learning-based controllers), and quantify how AI model and deployment choices affect datacenter flexibility and coordination outcomes. This versatility is enabled by OpenG2G's modular and extensible architecture: a datacenter backend driven by real measurements of production-grade AI services, a grid backend built on high-fidelity grid simulators, and a generic controller interface that closes the loop between them. We describe the design of OpenG2G and demonstrate its usefulness through realistic grid scenarios and AI workloads.

</details>


### [167] [Bayesian Rain Field Reconstruction using Commercial Microwave Links and Diffusion Model Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05520)
*Badr Moufad,Albina Ilina,Hai Victor Habi,Salem Lahlou,Yazid Janati,Hagit Messer,Eric Moulines*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper uses Diffusion Models as spatial priors for Bayesian inverse reconstruction of rainfall fields from Commercial Microwave Links, outperforming traditional methods on both synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods oversimplify CMLs as point sensors and neglect line integration between rainfall and signal attenuation, leading to poor performance under heterogeneous precipitation.

Method: View rain field reconstruction as a Bayesian inverse problem using Diffusion Models as high-fidelity spatial priors, enabling training-free posterior sampling via Plug-and-Play, Sequential Monte Carlo, and Replica Exchange methods.

Result: Diffusion models better preserve key rainfall statistics than censored Gaussian processes, and consistently outperform established CML-based reconstruction baselines on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Diffusion Model priors provide a flexible, training-free Bayesian framework that significantly improves rain field reconstruction from path-integrated microwave link measurements.

Abstract: Commercial Microwave Links (CMLs) offer dense spatial coverage for rainfall sensing but produce path-integrated measurements that make accurate ground-level reconstruction challenging. Existing methods typically oversimplify CMLs as point sensors and neglect line integration relating rainfall to signal attenuation, resulting in degraded performance under heterogeneous precipitation. In this work, we view rain field reconstruction as a Bayesian inverse problem with Diffusion Models (DMs) as high-fidelity spatial priors. We show that diffusion models better preserve key rainfall statistics compared to censored Gaussian processes. Framing rainfall estimation as a Bayesian inverse problem with a DM prior enables training-free posterior sampling using a broad family of methods, including Plug-and-Play, Sequential Monte Carlo, and Replica Exchange methods. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over established CML-based reconstruction baselines.

</details>


### [168] [MOSAIC: Module Discovery via Sparse Additive Identifiable Causal Learning for Scientific Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05524)
*Shicheng Fan,Nour Elhendawy,Jianle Sun,Ke Fang,Kun Zhang,Yihang Wang,Lu Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MOSAIC is a sparse temporal VAE that transfers interpretability from observed variables to identifiable latent representations in scientific time series, recovering module-level structure with theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifiability in CRL does not guarantee interpretability, especially in scientific time series where latent mechanisms are unknown. The authors aim to bridge this gap by transferring the inherent semantic meaning of observations to the latent space.

Method: MOSAIC integrates temporal CRL identifiability (via regime-conditioned temporal variation) with a sparse additive decoder that recovers for each latent variable a sparse set of associated observations, enabling module-level interpretability.

Result: ANOVA main-effect supports are identifiable under general smooth mixing functions with finite-sample recovery guarantees. Empirically, MOSAIC recovers domain-consistent variable groups across RNA dynamics, solar wind, ENSO climate, Tennessee Eastman process, and a synthetic tokamak benchmark.

Conclusion: By leveraging the inherent semantics of scientific observations, MOSAIC achieves both identifiability and interpretability, enabling discovery of latent mechanisms in scientific time series without requiring ground-truth labels.

Abstract: Causal representation learning (CRL) seeks to recover latent variables with identifiability guarantees, typically up to permutation and component-wise reparameterization under appropriate assumptions. However, identifiability does not imply interpretability: latent semantics are typically assigned post hoc by alignment with known ground-truth factors. This limitation is particularly acute in scientific time series, where underlying mechanisms are unknown and discovering interpretable structure is a primary goal. In contrast, scientific observations (such as residue-pair distances, climate indices, or process sensors) are inherently semantic, as they correspond to named physical quantities. This raises a key question: can the interpretability of observations be transferred to the identifiable latent space? We propose MOSAIC (Module discovery via Sparse Additive Identifiable Causal learning), a sparse temporal VAE that integrates temporal CRL identifiability with support recovery over observed variables. MOSAIC identifies latent variables via regime-conditioned temporal variation, and recovers for each latent a sparse set of associated observations through an additive decoder, yielding module-level interpretability. We show that ANOVA main-effect supports are identifiable under general smooth mixing functions, and provide finite-sample recovery guarantees for a tractable sparse-additive variant. Empirically, MOSAIC recovers domain-consistent variable groups across RNA molecular dynamics, solar wind, ENSO climate, the Tennessee Eastman process, and a synthetic tokamak benchmark, enabling interpretable discovery of latent mechanisms in scientific time series.

</details>


### [169] [Energy Generative Modeling: A Lyapunov-based Energy Matching Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05530)
*Yixuan Wang,Wenqian Xue,Warren E. Dixon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper unifies training and sampling of static scalar energy generative models as a single density transport problem on Wasserstein space, viewed as a nonlinear control problem with KL divergence as Lyapunov function, deriving finite-step stopping criteria for Langevin sampling and compositional properties of trained energies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge static scalar energy generative models with nonlinear control theory, unifying training and sampling into a single framework and providing theoretical guarantees (finite-step stopping criteria, Lyapunov certificates, compositional properties) that were previously lacking.

Method: The authors reformulate training and sampling as instances of the same master dynamics (density transport on Wasserstein space) using KL divergence as a Lyapunov function, then apply nonlinear control theory tools to derive theoretical results including finite-step stopping criteria for Langevin sampling and additive composition of trained energies with explicit Gibbs invariant measures.

Result: Derived finite-step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling; proved no Lyapunov certificate exists for deterministic gradient flow; showed additive composition of trained scalar energies retains explicit Gibbs invariant measure and inherits Lyapunov certificates; validated on synthetic distributions.

Conclusion: The reformulation opens static scalar energy models to nonlinear control theory tools such as barrier functions for constrained generation and contraction metrics for accelerated sampling, bridging a gap between generative modeling and control theory.

Abstract: Generative models based on static scalar energy functions represent an emerging paradigm in which a single time independent potential drives sample generation through its gradient field, eliminating the need for time conditioning entirely. We unify the training and sampling phases of this paradigm, conventionally treated as separate procedures, within a single framework: density transport on the Wasserstein space, cast as a nonlinear control problem in which the Kullback Leibler (KL) divergence serves as a Lyapunov function. Training and sampling are then two instances of this same master dynamics, differing only in initial condition. Within this autonomous framework we develop two analytic results. First, since the Lyapunov certificate is asymptotic, we derive a finite step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling and prove that no Lyapunov certificate exists for the deterministic gradient flow on the same energy landscape. Second, the reformulation brings the toolkit of nonlinear control theory to bear on static scalar energy generative modeling, that is, we show that additive composition of trained scalar energies retains an explicit Gibbs invariant measure and inherits the closed-loop Lyapunov certificate. Beyond these immediate results, this reformulation bridges static scalar energy generative models with the full toolkit of nonlinear control theory, opening the door to barrier functions for constrained generation and contraction metrics for accelerated sampling. Experiments on synthetic distributions validate the theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [170] [Adversarial Graph Neural Network Benchmarks: Towards Practical and Fair Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05534)
*Tran Gia Bao Ngo,Zulfikar Alom,Federico Errica,Murat Kantarcioglu,Cuneyt Gurcan Akcora*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a large-scale benchmark re-evaluating 7 adversarial attacks and 8 defenses on GNNs across 6 datasets (453,000+ experiments), showing that inconsistent experimental settings in prior work lead to misleading conclusions, and identifying overlooked factors like target node selection and model training that significantly distort performance insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many adversarial attack and defense works on GNNs use different experimental settings, leading to ambiguous and contradictory scientific conclusions. The authors aim to demonstrate the need for fair, robust, and standardized evaluation protocols in adversarial GNN research.

Method: The authors perform a large-scale comprehensive re-evaluation of 7 widely used attacks and 8 recent defenses under both poisoning and evasion scenarios across 6 popular graph datasets, conducting over 453,000 experiments within a unified framework to ensure fair comparison.

Result: Substantial differences in adversarial attack performance are observed under fair evaluation. Previously overlooked factors such as target node selection and the training process of the attacked model have a profound impact on attack effectiveness, to the extent of completely distorting performance insights.

Conclusion: The results underscore the urgent need for standardized evaluations in adversarial graph machine learning to ensure reliable and reproducible conclusions.

Abstract: Adversarial learning and the robustness of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are topics of widespread interest in the machine learning community, as documented by the number of adversarial attacks and defenses designed for these purposes. While a rigorous evaluation of these adversarial methods is necessary to understand the robustness of GNNs in real-world applications, we posit that many works in the literature do not share the same experimental settings, leading to ambiguous and potentially contradictory scientific conclusions. In this benchmark, we demonstrate the importance of adopting fair, robust, and standardized evaluation protocols in adversarial GNN research. We perform a comprehensive re-evaluation of seven widely used attacks and eight recent defenses under both poisoning and evasion scenarios, across six popular graph datasets. Our study spans over 453,000 experiments conducted within a unified framework. We observe substantial differences in adversarial attack performance when evaluated under a fair and robust procedure. Our findings reveal that previously overlooked factors, such as target node selection and the training process of the attacked model, have a profound impact on attack effectiveness, to the extent of completely distorting performance insights. These results underscore the urgent need for standardized evaluations in adversarial graph machine learning.

</details>


### [171] [Towards Scalable One-Step Generative Modeling for Autoregressive Dynamical System Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05540)
*Tianyue Yang,Xiao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MeLISA is a latent-free autoregressive generative surrogate model for high-dimensional physical dynamics that uses pixel-space MeanFlow with blockwise stochastic transitions, achieving fast inference and strong long-horizon statistical accuracy on turbulent flow benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing surrogate models struggle with high-dimensional turbulent dynamics: neural operators drift in long rollouts, while diffusion-based models are computationally expensive due to iterative denoising and auxiliary compression. The goal is a model that combines fast autoregressive inference with statistically accurate long-horizon trajectories.

Method: MeLISA uses pixel-space MeanFlow with a blockwise stochastic transition kernel (single evaluation per block, no latent encoder or iterative diffusion). It employs two losses: Window-Consistency MeanFlow (learning from partially observed temporal windows) and Time Increment Consistency (constraining multi-lag increments for temporal correlation). Evaluated with UNet (3.7-5.7M params) and DiT (up to 150M) backbones.

Result: On 2D Kolmogorov flow (256x256) and turbulent channel flow (192x192), MeLISA outperforms neural operators in short-term accuracy and long-horizon statistical metrics (energy spectra, turbulent kinetic energy, mixing rates). Inference speed matches or exceeds neural operators. Compact models show strong parameter efficiency.

Conclusion: MeLISA demonstrates that latent-free autoregressive generative modeling can achieve both rollout efficiency and long-horizon statistical accuracy for high-dimensional turbulent dynamics, offering a practical and scalable alternative to operator-based and diffusion-based surrogates.

Abstract: Fast surrogate modeling for high-dimensional physical dynamics requires more than low short-term error: useful models must roll out efficiently while preserving the statistical structure of long trajectories. Neural operators provide inexpensive autoregressive forecasts but can drift in turbulent regimes, whereas rolling diffusion and latent generative surrogates can represent stochastic transitions at the cost of multi-step denoising, noise-schedule design, or auxiliary compression models. We propose MeanFlow Long-term Invariant Spatiotemporal Consistency Autoregressive Models (MeLISA), a latent-free autoregressive generative surrogate built on pixel-space MeanFlow. MeLISA defines a blockwise stochastic transition kernel that generates each forecast block with a single model evaluation, avoiding latent encoders and iterative diffusion solvers at inference time. To stabilize long-horizon rollouts, MeLISA combines a Window-Consistency MeanFlow objective that learns conditional spatiotemporal generation from partially observed temporal windows with a Time Increment Consistency loss that constrains multi-lag finite increments and targets temporal-correlation structure. We evaluate MeLISA with compact UNet and scalable DiT backbones on two high-resolution benchmarks, extended 2D Kolmogorov flow at $256 \times 256$ and turbulent channel-flow slice at $192 \times 192$. MeLISA outperforms neural-operator baselines on short-term forecasting accuracy and long-horizon statistical metrics, including energy spectra, turbulent kinetic energy, and mixing-rate-related dynamics, while achieving inference speeds comparable to, and in some cases faster than, neural operators. Compact 3.7-5.7M-parameter variants already deliver strong parameter efficiency, and DiT variants provide a scalable path up to 150M parameters. Overall, MeLISA benefits both rollout efficiency and long-horizon statistical accuracy.

</details>


### [172] [FedeKD: Energy-Based Gating for Robust Federated Knowledge Distillation under Heterogeneous Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05553)
*Quang-Huy Nguyen,Jiaqi Wang,Wei-shinn Ku*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedeKD proposes a reliability-aware federated knowledge distillation framework that uses an energy-based gating mechanism to estimate sample-wise trust weights for knowledge transfer without requiring public datasets, significantly reducing negative transfer in heterogeneous FL environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Negative transfer in federated learning due to heterogeneous data distributions and asymmetric model design; existing FKD methods lack sample-wise reliability estimation and often rely on public datasets.

Method: FedeKD uses dual models (private & proxy) per client, aggregates proxy models on the server, and applies an energy-based gating mechanism to compute sample-wise trust weights based on private-proxy disagreement for weighted backward distillation.

Result: Experiments on six real-world datasets show FedeKD significantly reduces negative transfer under heterogeneous settings while maintaining strong predictive performance.

Conclusion: Making sample-wise trust estimation explicit in knowledge transfer improves FL robustness in heterogeneous environments without relying on public data.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) operates in heterogeneous environments, where variations in data distributions and asymmetric model design often result in negative transfer. While federated knowledge distillation (FKD) avoids direct model parameter sharing, existing methods typically rely on public datasets or assume that transferred knowledge is uniformly reliable, which limits their robustness in practice. This paper presents FedeKD, a reliability-aware FKD framework that makes sample-wise trust estimation an explicit component of knowledge transfer, without relying on additional public data. Each client maintains a high-capacity private model for local learning and a lightweight shared proxy model for cross-client knowledge exchange. During training, proxy models are aggregated on the server to form a global proxy, which is then used to guide updates of the private models. At the core of FedeKD is an energy-based gating mechanism that converts task-specific private-proxy disagreement into sample-wise trust weights for backward distillation. This mechanism enables sample-wise weighting of knowledge transfer, where the proxy model contributes more to reliable samples while down-weighting unreliable ones. Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that FedeKD significantly reduces negative transfer under heterogeneous settings while maintaining strong predictive performance.

</details>


### [173] [Accelerating LMO-Based Optimization via Implicit Gradient Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05577)
*Won-Jun Jang,Si-Hyeon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes LMO-IGT, a new class of stochastic LMO-based optimizers that uses implicit gradient transport to accelerate convergence while maintaining single-gradient-per-iteration efficiency, achieving O(ε^{-3.5}) complexity. It also introduces a unified optimization framework and the regularized support function (RSF) stationarity measure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LMO-based optimizers (Lion, Muon) lack theoretical unification and suffer from either computational overhead (variance reduction requires extra gradient evaluations) or fragmented theoretical understanding across constrained/unconstrained settings.

Method: The paper introduces LMO-IGT (Implicit Gradient Transport), which evaluates stochastic gradients at transported points rather than current iterates, enabling acceleration without extra gradient evaluations. It also proposes a unified framework with the Regularized Support Function (RSF) as a stationarity measure.

Result: LMO-IGT achieves O(ε^{-3.5}) iteration complexity using only a single stochastic gradient per iteration, compared to O(ε^{-4}) for standard stochastic LMO and O(ε^{-3}) for variance-reduced LMO (which requires extra gradients). Empirically, Muon-IGT achieves the strongest overall performance.

Conclusion: Implicit gradient transport provides an effective and practical acceleration mechanism for modern LMO-based optimization, with Muon-IGT being the strongest instantiation across evaluated settings.

Abstract: Recent optimizers such as Lion and Muon have demonstrated strong empirical performance by normalizing gradient momentum via linear minimization oracles (LMOs). While variance reduction has been explored to accelerate LMO-based methods, it typically incurs substantial computational overhead due to additional gradient evaluations. At the same time, the theoretical understanding of LMO-based methods remains fragmented across unconstrained and constrained formulations. Motivated by these limitations, we propose \emph{LMO-IGT}, a new class of stochastic LMO-based methods leveraging implicit gradient transport (IGT). We further introduce a unified framework for stochastic LMO-based optimization together with a new stationarity measure, the \emph{regularized support function} (RSF), which bridges gradient-norm and Frank--Wolfe-gap notions within a common framework. By evaluating stochastic gradients at transported points, LMO-IGT accelerates convergence while retaining the single-gradient-per-iteration structure of standard stochastic LMO. Our analysis establishes that stochastic LMO achieves an iteration complexity of $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-4})$, variance-reduced LMO achieves $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3})$ at the cost of additional gradient evaluations, and LMO-IGT achieves $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3.5})$ using only a single stochastic gradient per iteration. Empirically, LMO-IGT consistently improves over stochastic LMO counterparts with negligible overhead. Among its instantiations, Muon-IGT achieves the strongest overall performance across evaluated settings, demonstrating that IGT provides an effective and practical acceleration mechanism for modern LMO-based optimization.

</details>


### [174] [AeroJEPA: Learning Semantic Latent Representations for Scalable 3D Aerodynamic Field Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05586)
*Francisco Giral,Abhijeet Vishwasrao,Andrea Arroyo Ramo,Mahmoud Golestanian,Federica Tonti,Adrian Lozano-Duran,Steven L. Brunton,Sergio Hoyas,Hector Gomez,Soledad Le Clainche,Ricardo Vinuesa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AeroJEPA uses a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture to learn meaningful latent representations of aerodynamic flow fields, achieving scalable surrogate modeling that supports interpolation, probing, and design optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current aerodynamic surrogate models struggle with scaling to large 3D flow fields and fail to produce latent representations useful for analysis and design tasks.

Method: AeroJEPA predicts target latent representations of flow from context latent representations of geometry/conditions, decoupling prediction from field resolution, with optional reconstruction via a continuous implicit decoder.

Result: Competitive as a continuous surrogate; scales to high-resolution outputs; latents encode geometry/aerodynamics without direct supervision; supports interpolation, linear probing, concept-vector arithmetic, and design optimization.

Conclusion: Predictive latent learning is a promising direction for scalable and design-meaningful aerodynamic surrogate modeling.

Abstract: Aerodynamic surrogate models are increasingly used to replace repeated high-fidelity CFD evaluations in many-query design settings, but current approaches still face two important limitations: they often scale poorly to the very large fields arising in realistic 3D aerodynamics, and they rarely produce latent representations that are directly useful for analysis and design. We introduce AeroJEPA, a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for aerodynamic field modeling that addresses both issues. Rather than predicting the full flow field directly from geometry, AeroJEPA predicts a target latent representation of the flow from a context latent representation of the geometry and operating conditions, and optionally reconstructs the field through a continuous implicit decoder. This formulation decouples latent prediction from field resolution while encouraging the latent space to organize semantically. We evaluate AeroJEPA on two complementary datasets: HiLiftAeroML, which stresses the method in a high-fidelity regime with extremely large boundary-layer fields, and SuperWing, which tests large-scale generalization and latent-space optimization over a broad family of transonic wings. Across these benchmarks, AeroJEPA is competitive as a continuous surrogate for aerodynamic fields, scales naturally to high-resolution outputs, and learns context and predicted latents that encode geometry and aerodynamic quantities not used directly as supervision. We further show that the resulting latent space supports controlled interpolation, linear probing, concept-vector arithmetic, and a constrained design latent-optimization experiment. These results suggest that predictive latent learning is a promising direction for scalable and design-meaningful aerodynamic surrogate modeling.

</details>


### [175] [Optimal Contextual Pricing under Agnostic Non-Lipschitz Demand](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05609)
*Jianyu Xu,Yu-Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing algorithm achieving $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ optimal regret for contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations and agnostic non-Lipschitz noise, closing the regret gap left by prior $\tilde O(T^{3/4})$ methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing contextual dynamic pricing algorithms rely on smooth-demand assumptions (Lipschitz noise), but real-world noise can be discontinuous with jumps and atoms. Prior work achieved only $\tilde O(T^{3/4})$ regret, and closing this gap to the known $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ lower bound was an open problem.

Method: The algorithm combines three key components: (1) randomized parameter estimation for linear valuations, (2) conservative residual-grid probing to handle discontinuities, and (3) confidence-based one-step redirection for adaptive pricing decisions.

Result: Achieves $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ regret, matching the information-theoretic lower bound of Kleinberg and Leighton (2003) up to logarithmic factors, improving over the previous $\tilde O(T^{3/4})$ upper bound of Xu and Wang (2022).

Conclusion: Under stochastic well-conditioned contexts, this result closes the long-standing open regret gap in linear-valuation contextual pricing with agnostic non-Lipschitz noise distributions.

Abstract: We study contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations and bounded-support agnostic noise, whose induced demand curve may be non-Lipschitz with arbitrary jumps and atoms. Such discontinuities break the cross-context interpolation arguments used by smooth-demand pricing algorithms, while the best previous method achieved only $\tilde O(T^{3/4})$ regret. We propose Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing, a polynomial-time algorithm that combines randomized parameter estimation, conservative residual-grid probing, and confidence-based one-step redirection. Our algorithm achieves $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ optimal regret, matching the known lower bounds of Kleinberg and Leighton (2003) up to logarithmic factors and improving over the previous upper bound of Xu and Wang (2022). Under stochastic well-conditioned contexts, this closes the long-existing open regret gap in linear-valuation contextual pricing under agnostic non-Lipschitz noise distribution.

</details>


### [176] [LLMSpace: Carbon Footprint Modeling for Large Language Model Inference on LEO Satellites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05615)
*Lei Jiang,Adrian Ildefonso,Daniel Loveless,Fan Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMSpace is the first carbon modeling framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites, jointly modeling operational and embodied carbon across lifecycle including launch, manufacturing, and radiation-hardened hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs' growing energy demands create a carbon crisis; solar-powered LEO satellites could reduce terrestrial electricity use, but their lifecycle carbon footprint is poorly understood due to launch emissions, manufacturing, and radiation-hardened hardware costs.

Method: LLMSpace jointly models operational and embodied carbon, peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators/memories, and LLM-specific workload characteristics (prefill-decode behavior, token generation) using realistic satellite and GPU configurations.

Result: Reveals key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime for sustainable space-based LLM inference.

Conclusion: Sustainable space-based LLM inference requires careful balancing of carbon footprint, latency, hardware design, and lifetime; LLMSpace provides the first unified modeling framework for this purpose.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) impose rapidly growing energy demands, creating an emerging energy and carbon crisis driven by large-scale inference. Solar-powered, AI-enabled low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites have been proposed to mitigate terrestrial electricity consumption, but their lifecycle carbon footprint remains poorly understood due to launch emissions, satellite manufacturing, and radiation-hardened hardware requirements. This paper presents \textit{LLMSpace}, the first carbon modeling framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites. LLMSpace jointly models operational and embodied carbon, peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators and memories, and LLM-specific workload characteristics such as prefill-decode behavior and token generation. Using realistic satellite and GPU configurations, LLMSpace reveals key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime for sustainable space-based LLM inference. Source code: https://github.com/UnchartedRLab/LLMSpace.

</details>


### [177] [Region-adaptable retrieval of coastal biogeochemical parameters from near-surface hyperspectral remote sensing reflectance using physics-aware meta-learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05623)
*Yiqing Guo,Nagur R. C. Cherukuru,Eric A. Lehmann,S. L. Kesav Unnithan,Tim J. Malthus,Gemma Kerrisk,Xiubin Qi,Faisal Islam,Tisham Dhar,Mark J. Doubell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework for retrieving coastal biogeochemical parameters from hyperspectral reflectance data. It uses a bio-optical forward model to generate synthetic data for pre-training, then fine-tunes with local samples. Experiments on five Australian coastal sites show the method outperforms benchmark models and captures temporal dynamics well.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalizing retrieval algorithms for aquatic biogeochemical parameters across different water bodies is challenging because the relationship between remote sensing reflectance and BGC parameters varies regionally due to differences in environmental conditions and bio-optical properties.

Method: A two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework: Stage 1 uses a bio-optical forward model to generate a large synthetic dataset from an in situ spectral library, then pre-trains a region-agnostic base model via meta-learning. Stage 2 fine-tunes the base model for specific regions using local samples.

Result: (1) BGC parameters and hyperspectral Rrs signatures showed clear regional distinctions; (2) the synthetic dataset was physically plausible and aligned with real-world data; (3) the proposed approach outperformed five benchmark models; (4) time series of predicted BGC parameters agreed well with in situ measurements in magnitude and temporal dynamics.

Conclusion: The physics-aware meta-learning framework effectively generalizes BGC retrieval across diverse coastal waters by combining physics-based synthetic data pre-training with region-specific fine-tuning, demonstrating superior performance over conventional approaches.

Abstract: Hyperspectral in situ sensing has shown promise in retrieving aquatic biogeochemical (BGC) parameters, such as total suspended solids, dissolved organic carbon, and total chlorophyll-a, for cost-effective monitoring of coastal water quality. However, generalising such retrieval algorithms across water bodies remains challenging, as the relationship between remote sensing reflectance (Rrs) and BGC parameters can vary considerably from one region to another due to regional distinctions in environmental conditions and biogeochemistry that lead to different BGC ranges and bio-optical properties. In this study, we propose a two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework for retrieving coastal BGC parameters from near-surface Rrs observations. In the first stage, a bio-optical forward model is used to generate a large synthetic dataset based on an in situ bio-optical spectral library with broad representativeness of Australian coastal waters. This dataset is then used to pretrain a region-agnostic base model with meta-learning, allowing the model to learn fundamental physical relationships. In the second stage, the pretrained base model is fine-tuned for specific regions with local samples. We collected in situ hyperspectral Rrs and BGC measurements from five geographically distinct sites in Australian coastal waters. Our experimental results suggest: (1) the BGC parameters and their corresponding hyperspectral Rrs signatures exhibited clear regional distinctions among the experimental sites; (2) the synthetic dataset was physically plausible and closely aligned with real-world samples in both parameter distributions and inter-parameter correlations; (3) the proposed approach outperformed five benchmark models in BGC retrieval; and (4) time series of in situ measured and model-predicted BGC parameters showed good agreement in both magnitude and temporal dynamics.

</details>


### [178] [Scaling Pretrained Representations Enables Label-Free Out-of-Distribution Detection Without Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05638)
*Brett Barkley,Preston Culbertson,David Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that frozen pretrained model representations encode enough geometric structure for accurate label-free OOD detection, with both global (Mahalanobis) and local (ReSCOPED diffusion-based) detectors improving as representation quality scales, and performance gaps diminishing at larger scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models often fail to signal out-of-distribution inputs, and prior work assumed OOD detection requires class-conditional or supervised fine-tuning. The authors revisit this assumption for modern pretrained models.

Method: The authors compare two label-free OOD detectors (global Mahalanobis estimator and local ReSCOPED diffusion-based typicality estimator) across 59 backbone-task pairs in vision and language, analyzing how performance changes with representation scaling.

Result: Both detectors' absolute performance improves with better representation quality, and performance gaps between local and global detectors disappear as representations scale, across both vision and language tasks.

Conclusion: Label-free OOD detection depends strongly on the geometry of frozen pretrained representations. Detector choice becomes less important as backbone scale increases, enabling efficient deployment directly on frozen models.

Abstract: Models trained with deep learning often fail to signal when inputs fall outside their training data manifold, leading to unreliable predictions under distribution shift. Prior work suggests that effective out-of-distribution (OOD) detection often requires class-conditional modeling or specialized models obtained through supervised fine-tuning. We revisit this assumption in modern pretrained models and show that their frozen representations already encode sufficient geometric structure for accurate label-free OOD detection. Across 59 backbone-task pairings spanning vision and language, we compare two complementary label-free detectors: a global Mahalanobis estimator fit on unlabeled latent representations, and ReSCOPED, a lightweight, diffusion-based typicality estimator operating on the same features at a local level. Despite their different detection mechanisms, representation scaling reveals a consistent regime-dependent pattern: both local and global detectors' absolute performance improves with better representation quality, and performance gaps between the two detectors disappear across both language and vision tasks as representations scale. These results suggest that label-free OOD detection depends strongly on the geometry exposed by frozen pretrained backbones, reducing the importance of detector choice as backbone scale increases and enabling efficient deployment directly on frozen models.

</details>


### [179] [Information-Preserving Domain Transfer with Unlabeled Data in Misspecified Simulation-Based Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05652)
*Joon Jang,Eunho Jeong,Kyu Sung Choi,Hyeonjin Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPIN is a simulation-based inference framework that handles model misspecification by using domain transfer to preserve parameter-relevant information when translating between simulated and real-world data, improving posterior inference without requiring labeled real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simulation-based inference degrades under model misspecification where real-world observations don't match simulator data. Existing methods align data distributions but fail to preserve parameter-relevant information needed for posterior inference.

Method: SPIN uses domain transfer with cycle-consistent translation between simulated and real-world domains. It translates labeled simulator observations to real-world and back, using original simulator labels to preserve parameter-relevant mutual information during domain transfer. At test time, it transports real-world observations into the simulator domain for inference.

Result: SPIN improves real-world posterior inference on both synthetic and physical real-world benchmarks, with greater improvements under stronger model misspecification.

Conclusion: Domain transfer that preserves parameter-relevant information is effective for handling model misspecification in SBI, and SPIN achieves this using only unlabeled, unpaired real-world data.

Abstract: Simulation-based inference (SBI) provides amortized Bayesian parameter inference from simulator-generated data without requiring explicit likelihood evaluation. Its reliability can degrade under model misspecification, where real-world observations are not well represented by the simulator used for training. Existing methods using unlabeled real-world data often align simulated and real-world data distributions, but marginal alignment alone does not directly preserve parameter-relevant information needed for posterior inference. We propose SPIN, an SBI framework with parameter-relevant information-preserving domain transfer using unlabeled, unpaired real-world observations. During training, SPIN translates labeled simulator observations toward the real-world domain and back to the simulator domain, using the original simulator labels to encourage domain transfer that preserves parameter-relevant mutual information. At test time, the learned real-to-simulator transport maps real-world observations into the simulator domain for posterior inference, without requiring real-world parameter labels or paired real--simulator observations. Across controlled synthetic and physical real-world benchmarks, SPIN improves real-world posterior inference, with the improvement becoming clearer as misspecification increases.

</details>


### [180] [Structural Correspondence and Universal Approximation in Diagonal plus Low-Rank Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05659)
*Ying Chen,Aoxi Li,Jihun Kim,Javad Lavaei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that purely low-rank neural networks (like rank-1 layers) suffer from approximation collapse despite being able to interpolate arbitrary data points. To fix this, they propose a Structural Correspondence framework with Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) components, proving that adding a minimal sparse diagonal to low-rank layers restores universal approximation capabilities. They demonstrate both additive (width) and multiplicative (depth) decomposition strategies, with depth offering better parameter-to-expressivity scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While low-rank structures (LoRA, low-rank factorization) are widely used for parameter efficiency in deep learning, theoretical guarantees for their expressive power remain limited and often rely on restrictive assumptions like pretrained base matrices or specific activation functions. The authors aim to understand the fundamental limits of low-rank constrained neural networks and find a theoretically grounded way to maintain parameter efficiency while restoring universal approximation.

Method: The authors first analyze the limitations of purely low-rank neural networks, identifying a paradox where rank-1 layers can interpolate arbitrary scalar datasets but collapse for function approximation. They then propose the Structural Correspondence framework, augmenting low-rank layers with a minimal sparse diagonal component (DLoR). They prove full-rank reconstruction via additive (width expansion) and multiplicative (depth expansion) decompositions, and analyze asymptotic Taylor remainders to verify universal approximation restoration for general activation functions.

Result: DLoR neural networks fully restore the Universal Approximation Theorem for general activation functions without requiring dense matrices or specific activations. Multiplicative depth decomposition provides superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width decomposition.

Conclusion: Dense matrices and specific activation functions are not topological prerequisites for universal expressivity. The DLoR framework offers a principled, parameter-efficient alternative that maintains theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: The massive computational costs of scaling modern deep learning architectures have driven the widespread use of parameter-efficient low-rank structures, such as LoRA and low-rank factorization. However, theoretical guarantees for their expressive power are less explored, often relying on restrictive priors like a pretrained base matrix, ReLU activations or non-verifiable singularity conditions. We first investigate the limits of neural networks constrained strictly to low-rank manifolds without pretrained dense priors. We demonstrate a theoretical paradox: while purely rank-1 layers can exactly interpolate arbitrary scalar datasets, they collapse for function approximations. To overcome this bottleneck without surrendering parameter efficiency, we introduce a unified \textit{Structural Correspondence} framework. We prove that augmenting low-rank layers with only a minimal sparse diagonal component, say a Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) structure, is sufficient to reach Universal Approximation. We show that any full-rank transformation can be exactly reconstructed using these DLoR components by trading off network width (additive decomposition) or depth (multiplicative decomposition). By tracking asymptotic Taylor remainders, we prove that DLoR neural networks fully restore the Universal Approximation Theorem for general activation functions. Finally, we establish that multiplicative depth provides superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width. Our results show that dense matrices and specific activation functions are not topological prerequisites for universal expressivity.

</details>


### [181] [Distributionally Robust Multi-Objective Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05660)
*Yufeng Yang,Fangning Zhuo,Ziyi Chen,Heng Huang,Yi Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces distributionally robust multi-objective optimization (DR-MOO), which minimizes multiple objectives under worst-case distributions to handle distributional shifts. The authors propose Pareto-type solution concepts, develop MGDA algorithms with provable guarantees, and achieve improved sample complexity from O(ε⁻¹²) (double-loop) to O(ε⁻⁴) (single-loop double-clip), with experiments showing competitiveness against baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MOO formulations do not account for distributional shifts in data, which is critical for applications where test distributions may differ from training distributions. The paper aims to bridge MOO and distributionally robust optimization.

Method: The paper proposes DR-MOO with Pareto-type solution concepts, develops a double-loop MGDA using Lagrangian dual reformulation (inner loop estimates dual variables, O(ε⁻¹²) complexity), and a single-loop double-clip MGDA incorporating gradient clipping that handles generalized-smooth and biased gradient estimates (O(ε⁻⁴) complexity).

Result: The single-loop double-clip MGDA achieves substantially improved sample complexity O(ε⁻⁴) compared to O(ε⁻¹²) for the double-loop variant. The theory applies to nonconvex settings without requiring bounded objectives or gradients. Experiments show competitiveness with state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.

Conclusion: DR-MOO effectively addresses distributional shifts in multi-objective optimization, with provably efficient algorithms that are practical and competitive with existing methods.

Abstract: Multi-objective optimization (MOO) has received growing attention in applications that require learning under multiple criteria. However, the existing MOO formulations do not explicitly account for distributional shifts in the data. We introduce distributionally robust multi-objective optimization (DR-MOO), which minimizes multiple objectives under their respective worst-case distributions. We propose Pareto-type solution concepts for DR-MOO and develop multi-gradient descent algorithms (MGDA) with provable guarantees. Leveraging a Lagrangian dual reformulation, we first design a double-loop MGDA that uses an inner loop to estimate dual variables and achieves a total sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-12})$ for reaching an $ε$-Pareto-stationary point. To further improve efficiency, we incorporate gradient clipping to handle generalized-smooth and biased gradient estimates, removing the need for double sampling. This yields a single-loop double-clip MGDA with substantially improved sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-4})$. Our theory applies to the nonconvex setting and does not require bounded objectives or gradients. Experiments demonstrate that our methods are competitive with state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.

</details>


### [182] [Temporal Functional Circuits: From Spline Plots to Faithful Explanations in KAN Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05685)
*Naveen Mysore*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Temporal Functional Circuits, a framework that transforms KAN edge functions into faithful, temporally grounded explanations for time-series forecasting, using a gated residual KAN that decomposes forecasts into linear base and sparse KAN correction, achieving 59% lower MSE on regime-switching signals while providing interpretable edge functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLPs lack interpretability in time-series forecasting; KANs expose explicit learnable edge functions but lack faithful, temporally grounded explanations.

Method: A gated residual KAN architecture that decomposes forecasts into a linear base and sparsely activated KAN correction, with edge-to-lag mapping via output-aware attribution, ranking by learned activation range, and faithfulness validation through edge-level interventions (zeroing and spline removal).

Result: Gated KAN achieves 59% lower MSE than linear-only models on regime-switching signals; the learned gate opens progressively wider as signal complexity grows; competitive performance across eight benchmarks.

Conclusion: Spline shape itself carries predictive value beyond base activation (SiLU); KAN-based corrections offer interpretable edge functions that MLP-based corrections cannot provide, enabling mechanistic explanation in time-series forecasting.

Abstract: Unlike MLPs, Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) expose explicit learnable edge functions on every connection, enabling mechanistic explanation in time-series forecasting. This paper introduces Temporal Functional Circuits, a framework that transforms KAN edge functions from latent visualizations into faithful, temporally grounded explanations. Built on a gated residual KAN that decomposes forecasts into a linear base and a sparsely activated KAN correction, the framework (i) maps each edge to input lags via output-aware attribution, (ii) ranks edges by learned activation range, and (iii) validates faithfulness through edge-level interventions including zeroing and spline removal. Removing the learned B-spline component while retaining the base SiLU term degrades forecasts, providing evidence that the spline shape itself carries predictive value beyond the base activation. On four synthetic regimes of increasing complexity, the learned gate opens progressively wider as signal complexity grows. On regime-switching signals, gated KAN achieves 59% lower MSE than linear-only models. Across eight benchmarks, the gated architecture is competitive with linear, attention, and MLP alternatives, while providing interpretable edge functions that MLP-based corrections cannot offer.

</details>


### [183] [Budgeted Attention Allocation: Cost-Conditioned Compute Control for Efficient Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05697)
*Amrit Nidhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Budgeted Attention Allocation, a method enabling a single Transformer model to operate at multiple cost-quality trade-off points via a monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on a requested attention budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deployed systems often require multiple cost-quality operating points, but Transformers typically expose only one inference cost per trained model, motivating the need for controllable attention budget mechanisms.

Method: The method uses a monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on a requested attention budget, with dense warm-starting for stability, and supports both soft cost control and hard-gate adaptation for measured speedups.

Result: On synthetic tasks, reaches 99.7% at 0.303 cost and 100% at 0.504. On AG News with custom transformer: 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x speedup at budget 0.50. On BERT-Mini AG News: 87.6% with 1.20x speedup. On DBpedia14: 97.4% at budget 0.50 vs 96.6% dense baseline.

Conclusion: The contribution is a reproducible feasibility study showing one controllable checkpoint can trade attention cost for accuracy and be converted into measured structural speedups on small CPU benchmarks, though not universally dominant over static baselines.

Abstract: Transformers usually expose one inference cost per trained model, while deployed systems often need multiple cost-quality operating points. We study Budgeted Attention Allocation, a monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on a requested attention budget. Dense warm-starting is important for stability: on a robust synthetic sequence task, one budgeted model reaches 99.7% accuracy at 0.303 estimated attention cost and 100.0% accuracy at 0.504 cost. On held-out AG News with a custom word-level transformer, hard-gate adaptation turns soft cost control into measured single-thread CPU speed, reaching 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x speedup at budget 0.50. In pretrained BERT-Mini AG News, budgeted structural pruning reaches 87.6% accuracy with 1.20x speedup at budget 0.50; a validation-ranked zero-shot dense post-hoc structural baseline reaches 86.1%, and one recovery epoch raises that per-budget specialist to 87.9%. On DBpedia14, BERT-Mini budgeted gates reach 97.4% at exact budget 0.50 versus 96.6% for dense full attention. Static fixed-budget gates and recovered dense specialists remain strong. The contribution is therefore not universal dominance, but a reproducible feasibility study of one controllable checkpoint across budgets that can trade attention cost for accuracy and be converted into measured structural speedups on small CPU benchmarks.

</details>


### [184] [On the Blessing of Pre-training in Weak-to-Strong Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05710)
*Wei Yao,Wang Zhaoyang,Gengze Xu,Chen Qian,Dongrui Liu,Ziqiao Wang,Yong Liu,Yunbei Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies pre-training as the essential prerequisite for Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG), proving theoretically that W2SG requires a geometric warm start from pre-training, and empirically showing it emerges via a phase transition during pre-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To theoretically and empirically establish that pre-training is the decisive factor enabling Weak-to-Strong Generalization, which was previously under-explored.

Method: Formalizes W2SG within a high-dimensional single-index model using spiked Gaussian data, models pre-training as spectral initialization, proves generalization bounds under perturbed strong-convexity geometry, and validates through synthetic simulations and massive-scale LLM checkpoint evaluations.

Result: Proves W2SG is achievable when pre-training provides a geometric warm start; shows an initial performance improvement followed by saturation bottleneck; demonstrates W2SG emerges via a phase transition coupled with pre-training progression in LLMs.

Conclusion: Pre-training is the essential prerequisite for W2SG; W2SG is not an innate capability but emerges through a phase transition during pre-training, with a rigorous theoretical framework explaining the underlying dynamics.

Abstract: The paradigm of Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG) suggests that a pre-trained strong model can surpass its weak supervisor, yet the decisive role of pre-training remains theoretically and empirically under-explored. In this work, we identify pre-training as the essential prerequisite for the emergence of W2SG. Theoretically, we formalize the W2SG problem within a high-dimensional single-index model framework using spiked Gaussian data, modeling pre-training as a spectral initialization step. Building upon prior impossibility results regarding the failure of learning under random initialization, we prove that W2SG is achievable when pre-training provides a geometric warm start that places the model within an "effective region" characterized by a perturbed strong-convexity geometry. Within this region, we derive a rigorous generalization bound that naturally captures the optimization dynamics: an initial performance improvement followed by a saturation bottleneck dictated by the weak supervisor's bias. Empirically, we first validate all our assumptions and theoretical insights through controlled synthetic simulations. Finally, through a massive-scale evaluation of hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models, we demonstrate that W2SG is not an innate capability but emerges via a phase transition tightly coupled with the progression of pre-training.

</details>


### [185] [Enabling Federated Inference via Unsupervised Consensus Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05718)
*Yui Hashimoto,Takayuki Nishio,Yuichi Kitagawa,Takahito Tanimura*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CE-FI enables multiple pretrained models to cooperate during inference without sharing parameters, raw data, or a common encoder, using only unlabeled data to train a consensus embedding layer and a cooperative output layer, achieving strong results on image, text, and time-series tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cooperative inference frameworks require sharing input data, model parameters, or a common encoder, making them unsuitable for privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings where such sharing is infeasible.

Method: CE-FI introduces a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer to map heterogeneous intermediate representations into a shared embedding space and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer for prediction; both are trained using only shared unlabeled data.

Result: On CIFAR-10/100 under non-IID conditions, CE-FI outperforms solo inference and matches conventional sharing-based methods. It generalizes to text and time-series tasks, with performance depending on ensemble strategy. Representation alignment is identified as the main bottleneck.

Conclusion: CE-FI is a viable privacy-preserving framework for cooperative inference across independently deployed models, though future work should address representation alignment challenges to improve overall performance.

Abstract: Cooperative inference across independently deployed machine learning models is increasingly desirable in distributed environments, as there is a growing need to leverage multiple models while keeping their data and model parameters private. However, existing cooperative frameworks typically rely on sharing input data, model parameters, or a common encoder, which limits their applicability in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings. To address this challenge, we propose Consensus Embedding-based Federated Inference (CE-FI), a framework that enables pretrained models to cooperate at inference time without sharing model parameters or raw inputs and without assuming a common encoder. CE-FI introduces two components: a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, so the cooperative stage does not require additional labeled data. Experiments on image classification benchmarks -- CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 -- under diverse non-IID conditions show that CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks indicate applicability beyond image classification, although performance depends on the ensemble strategy. Further analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

</details>


### [186] [WARP: A Benchmark for Primal-Dual Warm-Starting of Interior-Point Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05728)
*Dhruv Suri,Helgi Hilmarsson,Shourya Bose*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prior ML-based warm-start methods for AC-OPF overstate iteration reductions by using the inappropriate flat start baseline; against the solver's actual default (variable-bound midpoint), primal-only predictions provide no benefit. The paper shows primal accuracy is anticorrelated with convergence, and that full primal-dual-barrier state prediction achieves 85% iteration reduction. They release benchmark suite and WARP, a graph network predicting the complete interior-point state, achieving 76% reduction while handling N-1 contingencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To correct the flawed evaluation baseline in prior ML warm-start methods for AC-OPF and enable rigorous evaluation, while demonstrating that primal-only warm-start is fundamentally incapable of reducing IPM iterations due to geometric properties of interior-point methods.
