Method: Identifies that prior work benchmarks against flat start (V_m=1, V_a=0) instead of solver default (variable-bound midpoint). Shows primal prediction accuracy anticorrelates with convergence. Proposes WARP: a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts full interior-point state (primal, dual, barrier variables) on heterogeneous constraint graphs.

Result: Oracle full state reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 (85% reduction). WARP achieves 76% iteration reduction. Primal-only methods show zero reduction against corrected baseline. WARP handles N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.

Conclusion: Primal-only warm-start methods are fundamentally insufficient for IPM acceleration. Predicting the complete primal-dual-barrier state is necessary and sufficient for significant iteration reductions. The released benchmark suite enables standardized evaluation.

Abstract: Solving AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) is of central importance in electricity market operations, where interior-point methods (IPMs) such as IPOPT are the standard solvers. A growing body of work uses machine learning to predict primal warm-start iterates, reporting iteration reductions of 30-46\%. We show that these reported gains rest on an inappropriate evaluation baseline: prior methods benchmark against the flat start $V_m = 1, V_a = 0$, whereas the solver's actual default - the variable-bound midpoint $(l+u)/2$ - is near-optimal for log-barrier centrality. Against this corrected baseline, no primal-only warm-start method reduces solver iterations. We trace the failure to a geometric property of interior-point methods: primal prediction accuracy is anticorrelated with convergence speed, and providing the ground-truth optimal solution $x^*$ without dual variables causes the solver to diverge. Oracle experiments establish that the complete primal-dual-barrier state $(x^*, λ^*, z^*, μ^*)$ reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 - an 85\% reduction that is structurally inaccessible to primal-only methods. To enable rigorous evaluation of warm-start methods on this task, we release a benchmark suite comprising dual-labeled AC-OPF datasets with IPOPT-extracted solutions, a corrected evaluation protocol, and WARP - a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts the full interior-point state $(\hat{x}, \hatλ, \hat{z}, \hatμ)$ on the heterogeneous constraint graph. WARP achieves a 76\% reduction in IPOPT iterations while natively accommodating N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.

</details>


### [187] [CRAFT: Forgetting-Aware Intervention-Based Adaptation for Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05732)
*Md Anwar Hossen,Fatema Siddika,Juan Pablo Munoz,Tanya Roosta,Ali Jannesari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRAFT is a continual learning framework for LLMs that avoids weight updates by learning low-rank interventions on hidden representations, using a KL-based objective to unify routing, regularization, and merging across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can acquire new capabilities through fine-tuning, but continual adaptation often leads to catastrophic forgetting. Existing methods like LoRA still suffer from forgetting when adapting sequentially.

Method: CRAFT works in three stages: (1) routes each task to similar tasks using output-distribution divergence; (2) fine-tunes via KL divergence against the group's prior state to control forgetting; (3) merges interventions using the same KL signal. All stages are unified under a single KL-based objective.

Result: CRAFT improves overall performance and reduces forgetting compared to strong LoRA-based approaches across multiple benchmarks and model scales, while remaining robust to task ordering.

Conclusion: Controlling adaptation in representation space, guided by output-space divergence, provides a scalable and principled approach to continual learning in LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can acquire new capabilities through fine-tuning, but continual adaptation often leads to catastrophic forgetting. We propose CRAFT, a continual learning framework that avoids updating model weights by instead learning low-rank interventions on hidden representations. CRAFT proceeds in three stages: it first routes each task to a group of similar tasks based on output-distribution divergence; it then fine-tunes the model using a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence against the group's prior state, which directly controls forgetting and determines convergence; finally, it merges interventions for the updated task into the shared representation using the same KL signal. This design unifies routing, regularization, and merging through a single KL-based objective. CRAFT improves overall performance and reduces forgetting compared to strong LoRA-based approaches across multiple benchmarks and model scales, while remaining robust to task ordering. These results suggest that controlling adaptation in representation space, guided by output-space divergence, provides a scalable and principled approach to continual learning in LLMs.

</details>


### [188] [CoMemNet: Contrastive Sampling with Memory Replay Network for Continual Traffic Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05738)
*Mei Wu,Wenchao Weng,Wenxin Su,Wenjie Tang,Wei Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoMemNet is a dual-branch continual learning framework for streaming traffic prediction, featuring an Online branch for prediction, a Target branch with Dynamic Contrastive Sampler using Wasserstein Distance, and a Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) to prevent catastrophic forgetting and memory explosion, achieving SOTA on three datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing spatio-temporal methods rely on static graph structures and fail to capture continuously expanding and evolving patterns in streaming traffic networks.

Method: Dual-branch continual learning framework: (1) Online branch handles prediction tasks, (2) Target branch with momentum updates uses Wasserstein Distance for Dynamic Contrastive Sampling to select nodes with significant feature changes, (3) Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) replays old knowledge and prevents memory explosion.

Result: CoMemNet achieves state-of-the-art performance across all three large-scale real-world datasets. Two new open-source datasets are also provided.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses catastrophic forgetting and memory explosion in streaming traffic prediction, outperforming existing methods on multiple real-world benchmarks.

Abstract: In recent years, the integration of non-topological space modeling with temporal learning methods has emerged as an effective approach for capturing spatio-temporal information in non-Euclidean graphs. However, most existing methods rely on static underlying graph structures, which are inadequate for capturing the continuously expanding and evolving patterns in streaming traffic networks. To address this challenge, we propose a simple yet efficient dual-branch continual learning framework for traffic prediction, named CoMemNet. The fast-converging Online branch undertakes the primary prediction tasks, while the momentum-updated Target branch extracts historical information using Wasserstein Distance features to create a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler (DC Sampler). This sampler selects a node set with significant dynamic network feature changes for training, effectively mitigating the issue of catastrophic forgetting. Additionally, the backbone incorporates a lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) to consolidate old knowledge through memory replay and address the risk of memory explosion. Finally, we provide two newly curated open-source datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that CoMemNet achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across all three large-scale real-world datasets. The code is available at: https://github.com/meiwu5/CoMemNet.

</details>


### [189] [Multi-Dimensional Behavioral Evaluation of Agentic Stock Prediction Systems Using LLM Judges with Closed-Loop Reinforcement Learning Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05739)
*Mohammad Al Ridhawi,Mahtab Haj Ali,Hussein Al Osman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The authors propose a behavioral evaluation framework for agentic stock prediction systems that scores LLM-judged behavioral traces across six dimensions, uses perturbation-based validation, and improves SAC-based trading via credit-assigned reward penalties, achieving significant gains in MAPE, directional accuracy, and Sharpe ratio on test data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aggregate metrics like MAPE and directional accuracy obscure the quality of individual decisions in multi-step agentic stock prediction systems (regime detection, routing, RL control). A finer-grained behavioral evaluation is needed to diagnose and improve specific deficiencies.

Method: Behavioral traces from autonomous decision points are grouped into five-day episodes and scored by an LLM ensemble (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro) along six dimensions. Perturbation tests validate dimension specificity. Deficient dimensions are converted into credit-assigned penalty terms added to the SAC reward, followed by three fine-tuning cycles on validation data.

Result: Perturbation yields targeted score drops (-1.6 to -2.4) versus minimal cross-dimension impact (-0.32 avg); cross-model agreement up to Krippendorff's α=0.85. Composite behavioral score correlates ρ=0.72 with 20-day Sharpe. Fine-tuning reduces test MAPE from 0.61% to 0.54% (11.5% relative reduction), increases directional accuracy from 71% to 74%, and improves Sharpe ratio by 18% (8.2%-27.4% CI), especially in high-volatility episodes.

Conclusion: Behavioral evaluation via LLM judges can diagnose and guide targeted improvement of agentic trading systems, though results are limited to offline backtesting and do not address live deployment effects.

Abstract: Agentic stock prediction systems make sequences of interdependent decisions (regime detection, pathway routing, reinforcement learning control) whose individual quality is hidden by aggregate metrics such as mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) or directional accuracy. We present a behavioral evaluation framework that addresses this gap. Behavioral traces logged at every autonomous decision point are grouped into five-day episodes and scored along six domain-specific dimensions (regime detection, routing, adaptation, risk calibration, strategy coherence, error recovery) by an ensemble of three large language model (LLM) judges (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro). Perturbation-based validation on 420 episodes yields targeted score drops of $-1.6$ to $-2.4$ on intended dimensions versus an average of $-0.32$ on the remaining five, with cross-model agreement up to Krippendorff's $α= 0.85$. The composite behavioral score, used here only for cross-episode reporting, correlates at $ρ= 0.72$ with realized 20-day Sharpe ratio from offline backtesting. Closing the loop, the framework converts deficient per-dimension scores into a credit-assigned penalty term added to the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reward. Three short fine-tuning cycles, all confined to the validation period, produce on the held-out 2017-2025 test period a one-day MAPE reduction from 0.61% to 0.54% (an 11.5% relative reduction; $p<0.001$, Cohen's $d=0.31$), a directional accuracy increase from 71% to 74%, and an 18% Sharpe ratio improvement (95% bootstrap CI [8.2%, 27.4%]), with gains concentrated in high-volatility episodes where the original system was most behaviorally deficient. Results are from offline backtesting and do not address effects specific to live deployment.

</details>


### [190] [Weak-to-Strong Generalization is Nearly Inevitable (in Linear Models)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05742)
*Scott Geng,Dutch Hansen,Jerry Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows weak-to-strong generalization occurs in simple linear logistic regression under mild assumptions, and argues it is almost inevitable even without model capacity mismatch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and characterize weak-to-strong generalization, a phenomenon observed in frontier language models, by analyzing it in a simpler and more controlled setting.

Method: The authors study weak-to-strong generalization in standard linear logistic regression under mild distributional assumptions, analyzing most student-teacher pairs without requiring capacity mismatch.

Result: Weak-to-strong generalization occurs for most student-teacher pairs in linear logistic regression, and does not require the student to be more expressive or have higher model capacity than the teacher.

Conclusion: Weak-to-strong generalization is almost inevitable even in basic settings, contrary to the prevailing belief that model capacity mismatch is a central mechanism.

Abstract: Weak-to-strong generalization is a phenomenon in post-training whereby a strong student model, when finetuned solely with feedback from a weaker teacher, can not only surpass the teacher, but can improve upon its own capabilities. Recent work of Burns et al. (2023) demonstrated that this can occur in the setting of frontier language models, and subsequently there has been a flurry of both empirical work trying to exploit this phenomenon, as well as theoretical work attempting to understand it. In this work, we demonstrate that weak-to-strong generalization occurs in standard linear logistic regression, under mild distributional assumptions on the data. In fact, we show that this happens for most student-teacher pairs, suggesting that weak-to-strong generalization is in fact \emph{almost inevitable}, even in this basic setting. Notably, our setting does not require the student to be more expressive or have more model capacity in any way compared to the teacher, which runs contrary to the prevailing theoretical belief that a mismatch in model capacity is a central mechanism to weak-to-strong generalization.

</details>


### [191] [RVPO: Risk-Sensitive Alignment via Variance Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05750)
*Ivan Montero,Tomasz Jurczyk,Bhuwan Dhingra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RVPO is a risk-sensitive RLHF method that penalizes inter-reward variance using a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator, preventing constraint neglect in multi-objective alignment and improving performance on medical/scientific reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current critic-less RLHF methods aggregate multi-objective rewards via arithmetic mean, which causes constraint neglect—high success in one objective can numerically offset critical failures in others (e.g., safety or formatting), masking low-performing bottleneck rewards.

Method: RVPO penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation via a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator, shifting the objective from "maximize sum" to "maximize consistency." This acts as a smooth variance penalty shown via Taylor expansion.

Result: RVPO improves HealthBench scores (0.261 vs. 0.215 for GDPO at 14B, p<0.001) and maintains competitive accuracy on GPQA-Diamond without late-stage degradation seen in other multi-reward methods.

Conclusion: Variance regularization mitigates constraint neglect across model scales (1.5B to 14B) without sacrificing general capabilities, making it effective for reliable multi-objective alignment.

Abstract: Current critic-less RLHF methods aggregate multi-objective rewards via an arithmetic mean, leaving them vulnerable to constraint neglect: high-magnitude success in one objective can numerically offset critical failures in others (e.g., safety or formatting), masking low-performing "bottleneck" rewards vital for reliable multi-objective alignment. We propose Reward-Variance Policy Optimization (RVPO), a risk-sensitive framework that penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation, shifting the objective from "maximize sum" to "maximize consistency." We show via Taylor expansion that a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator effectively acts as a smooth variance penalty. We evaluate RVPO on rubric-based medical and scientific reasoning with up to 17 concurrent LLM-judged reward signals (Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B) and on tool-calling with rule-based constraints (Qwen2.5-1.5B/3B). By preventing the model from neglecting difficult constraints to exploit easier objectives, RVPO improves overall scores on HealthBench (0.261 vs. 0.215 for GDPO at 14B, $p < 0.001$) and maintains competitive accuracy on GPQA-Diamond without the late-stage degradation observed in other multi-reward methods, demonstrating that variance regularization mitigates constraint neglect across model scales without sacrificing general capabilities.

</details>


### [192] [Full-Spectrum Graph Neural Network: Expressive and Scalable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05759)
*Xiaohan Wang,Deyu Bo,Longlong Li,Kelin Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FSpecGNN is a second-order spectral GNN that lifts signals to node-pair domain and uses bivariate spectral filters, achieving universal approximation of node-pair signals and strong performance on heterophilic graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical spectral GNNs are limited by the 1-WL test and cannot universally approximate higher-order (e.g., node-pair) signals, which is problematic for tasks like heterophilic graph learning.

Method: FSpecGNN generalizes spectral GNNs to second order by (1) lifting signals from node domain to node-pair domain, and (2) extending univariate spectral filters over eigenvalues to bivariate filters over eigenvalue pairs. It also uses low-rank approximations for scalability.

Result: FSpecGNN can universally approximate node-pair signals, is at most as expressive as Local 2-GNN, and delivers strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks.

Conclusion: FSpecGNN pushes beyond the 1-WL expressivity barrier for spectral GNNs while remaining scalable, and is especially effective for heterophilic graph learning.

Abstract: It is well established that spectral graph neural networks (GNNs) can universally approximate node signals; however, their expressive power remains bounded by the 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Lehman test, which is mirrored in their lack of universality for higher-order signals. To go beyond this bound, we propose the Full-Spectrum GNN (FSpecGNN), a second-order generalization of classical spectral GNNs. FSpecGNN advances spectral filtering in two perspectives: (1) it lifts the signal from the node domain to the node-pair domain; and (2) it extends the univariate spectral filter over eigenvalues to a bivariate filter over eigenvalue pairs. We show that classical spectral GNNs arise as a diagonal special case of FSpecGNN, and prove that FSpecGNN can be at most as expressive as Local 2-GNN while universally approximating node-pair signals, the latter being particularly beneficial for heterophilic graph learning. Moreover, FSpecGNN admits scalable implementations that avoid explicit node-pair-level computations; combined with a low-rank approximation that reduces full-spectrum convolution to a combination of polynomial spectral filters, it enables learning on large graphs. Empirically, FSpecGNN validates the predicted expressivity and delivers strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks.

</details>


### [193] [Adaptive Selection of LoRA Components in Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05769)
*Myoungjun Kim,Sangwoo Park,Yoseob Han,Jin-Hyun Ahn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AS-LoRA is an adaptive framework for differentially private federated fine-tuning of large models that addresses aggregation errors from LoRA's multiplicative structure by enabling layer-wise, round-wise adaptive selection of LoRA components based on a curvature-aware score, achieving significant accuracy improvements with lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing federated LoRA fine-tuning methods suffer from aggregation error amplified by DP noise due to LoRA's multiplicative structure. Current approaches apply a single update mode uniformly across all layers and rounds, ignoring the structural asymmetry between LoRA factors and round-wise training dynamics.

Method: AS-LoRA adaptively selects active LoRA components along three axes: (i) layer-wise freedom (each layer independently selects its active component), (ii) round-wise adaptivity (selection updates over communication rounds), and (iii) a curvature-aware score derived from a second-order approximation of the loss.

Result: AS-LoRA improves over federated LoRA baselines by up to +7.5 pp on GLUE and +12.5 pp on MNLI-mm under strict DP budgets, matches or exceeds SVD-based methods at 33–180× lower aggregation cost, with negligible communication overhead.

Conclusion: AS-LoRA eliminates the reconstruction-error floor of layer-tied schedules, accelerates convergence, implicitly biases toward flatter minima, and incurs no additional privacy cost, making it effective for DP federated fine-tuning.

Abstract: Differentially private federated fine-tuning of large models with LoRA suffers from aggregation error caused by LoRA's multiplicative structure, which is further amplified by DP noise and degrades both stability and accuracy. Existing remedies apply a single update mode uniformly across all layers and all communication rounds (or alternate them on a fixed schedule), ignoring both the structural asymmetry between the two LoRA factors and the round-wise dynamics of training. We propose AS-LoRA, an adaptive framework defined by three axes (i) layer-wise freedom, in which each layer independently selects its active component, (ii) round-wise adaptivity, in which the selection updates over communication rounds, and (iii) a curvature-aware score derived from a second-order approximation of the loss. Theoretically, AS-LoRA eliminates the reconstruction-error floor of layer-tied schedules, accelerates convergence, implicitly biases solutions toward flatter minima, and incurs no additional privacy cost. Across GLUE, SQuAD, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under strict DP budgets and non-IID partitions, AS-LoRA improves over the federated LoRA baselines by up to $+7.5$ pp on GLUE and $+12.5$ pp on MNLI-mm for example, while matching or exceeding SVD-based aggregation methods at $33\text{--}180 \times$ lower aggregation cost and with negligible communication overhead. Code for the proposed method is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/as_lora-F75F/.

</details>


### [194] [A Measure-Theoretic Finite-Sample Theory for Adaptive-Data Fitted Q-Iteration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05791)
*Manuel Haussmann,Mustafa Mert Çelikok,Melih Kandemir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper bridges three disconnected theoretical traditions (measure-theoretic MDPs, error propagation analysis, and PAC bounds) to provide a unified finite-sample, adaptive-data performance bound for fitted Q-iteration (FQI) on general continuous spaces, using sequential Rademacher complexity and Bellman-operator contractions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a profound gap between the empirical success of model-free off-policy deep RL and its theoretical understanding, which is limited to tabular or linear settings. This gap arises from the isolation of three separate theoretical traditions that each address different aspects but ignore others.

Method: The authors unify measure-theoretic MDP foundations, deterministic error propagation via concentrability, and PAC generalization bounds into a single framework. They use sequential Rademacher complexity to control Bellman-regression generalization under policy-dependent data collection, and chain measure-theoretic probability with Bellman-operator contraction in Banach spaces.

Result: They obtain a finite-sample, adaptive-data performance bound for FQI on general measurable Borel spaces, and the first cumulative, pathwise online regret guarantee for FQI in continuous spaces.

Conclusion: The unified framework lays necessary theoretical foundations for the formal analysis of many modern deep RL algorithms.

Abstract: While reinforcement learning (RL) promises to revolutionize the control of complex nonlinear robotic systems, a profound gap persists between the heuristic success of model-free off-policy deep RL and the underlying theory, which remains largely confined to tabular or linearizable settings. We identify the cause of this gap as an emergent isolation of three traditions: (i) measure-theoretic MDP foundations on general spaces limit their analysis to exact dynamic programming and ignore all error sources of a learning process; (ii) deterministic error propagation analysis addresses the approximation error via concentrability coefficients without a finite-sample analysis of the estimation error; and (iii) PAC generalization bounds characterize the estimation errors of simplified topologies. We bridge these traditions with a unified theoretical framework for fitted Q-iteration (FQI) on general measurable Borel spaces. Our main result provides a finite-sample, adaptive-data performance bound by chaining measure-theoretic probability with Bellman-operator contraction in Banach spaces. We prove that sequential Rademacher complexity controls Bellman-regression generalization under policy-dependent data collection. We further extend this analysis to provide the first cumulative, pathwise online regret guarantee for FQI in continuous spaces. These results lay the necessary foundations for the formal analysis of many modern deep RL algorithms.

</details>


### [195] [Revealing Modular Gradient Noise Imbalance in LLMs: Calibrating Adam via Signal-to-Noise Ratio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05794)
*Ziqing Wen,Zhouyang Liu,Jiahuan Wang,Ping Luo,Li Shen,Dongsheng Li,Tao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoLS automatically scales per-module learning rates using signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) estimates, improving LLM training convergence and generalization without manual tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs' heterogeneous module composition leads to gradient heterogeneity that Adam(W) cannot explicitly handle, causing slow convergence and instability. Existing module-specific tuning is costly and not generalizable.

Method: MoLS estimates per-module signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) and uses them to scale Adam updates, enabling automated module-wise learning rate allocation.

Result: MoLS improves convergence speed and generalization across multiple LLM training benchmarks, matching carefully tuned module-specific learning rates, and is compatible with memory-efficient training.

Conclusion: MoLS offers a principled, automated approach to module-wise learning rate scaling that outperforms standard Adam and generalizes across tasks and models.

Abstract: The impressive performance of large language models (LLMs) arises from their massive scale and heterogeneous module composition. However, this structural heterogeneity introduces additional optimization challenges. While adaptive optimizers such as Adam(W) provide per-parameter adaptivity, they do not explicitly account for module-level gradient heterogeneity, resulting in slower convergence, suboptimal performance, or training instability. Existing approaches typically rely on manually tuned module-specific learning rates or specific optimization strategies, which are computationally costly and difficult to generalize across tasks or models. To establish a more principled approach, we first analyze the noise-damping behavior of Adam in high-noise modules and introduce \textbf{Module-wise Learning Rate Scaling via SNR (MoLS)}. MoLS estimates module-level SNRs to scale Adam updates, allowing automated module-wise learning rate allocation without manual tuning. Empirical results through multiple LLM training benchmarks demonstrate that MoLS improves convergence speed and generalization, achieving performance comparable to carefully tuned module-specific learning rates, while remaining compatible with memory-efficient training algorithms.

</details>


### [196] [Reward Shaping and Action Masking for Compositional Tasks using Behavior Trees and LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05795)
*Nicholas Potteiger,Ankita Samaddar,Taylor T. Johnson,Xenofon Koutsoukos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces MRBT (masking reward behavior tree), a symbolic structure that serves as a reactive and modular reward and action mask function for compositional tasks. Using LLMs, SMT-solvers, and neurosymbolic RL, the approach improves training efficiency and task success rates over baselines, with advantages in transferability, modularity, and verifiability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based approaches for reward shaping and action masking in RL do not fully address reactivity to subtask failure and modularity for varying objects in compositional tasks.

Method: The authors design MRBT templates with logical specifications, use an LLM to generate MRBTs robust to varying objects, an SMT-solver to verify correctness, and a neurosymbolic RL loop to train agents.

Result: Successful generation and refinement of five MRBTs, with consistently improved training efficiency and task success rates over baselines and MRBTs without action masking.

Conclusion: MRBTs offer three key advantages: transferability across tasks, modularity for composition, and formal verifiability.

Abstract: Decomposing complex tasks into a sequence of simpler subtasks can improve learning efficiency for an autonomous agent. Reinforcement learning (RL) can be used to optimize agent policies to complete subtasks, but requires well-defined subtask rewards and benefits from action masking. Recent work uses large language models (LLMs) to automate reward shaping and action masking, however none of them fully address reactivity to subtask failure and modularity to varying objects for compositional tasks. To overcome these challenges, we develop masking reward behavior tree (MRBT), a symbolic structure used as a reactive and modular reward and action mask function. We design an MRBT template and derive logical specifications to construct and verify MRBTs for a sequence of object-interaction subtasks. Further, we develop an automated pipeline that uses an LLM to generate MRBTs robust to varying task objects, an SMT-solver to verify correctness of specifications, and a neurosymbolic RL loop to train agents on compositional tasks. Experiments demonstrate successful generation and refinement of five MRBTs, consistently improving training efficiency and task success rates over baselines and MRBTs without action masking. We further highlight three advantages of MRBTs: transferability, modularity, and verifiability.

</details>


### [197] [Selective Rollout: Mid-Trajectory Termination for Multi-Sample Agent RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05802)
*Zhiyuan Zhai,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRPO wastes compute on zero-variance rollout groups (~40% of all groups). This paper proposes early stopping of group rollouts when partial action sequences have converged (low prefix edit distance), reducing wall-clock time by 10.7% and improving success rate by +2.5 pp on ALFWorld.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO in agentic environments samples multiple parallel rollouts per prompt, but ~40% of groups yield identical rewards (zero variance), contributing no gradient and wasting compute. Existing methods filter groups only before or after rollout, not during.

Method: A one-parameter gate that stops a group early when the mean pairwise prefix edit distance between partial action sequences falls below a threshold, indicating the rollouts have converged to the same action prefix and will likely yield identical rewards.

Result: On ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-7B (60-iteration GRPO, 4 seeds): 10.7% faster wall-clock time (bootstrap 95% CI excludes 0), +2.5 pp held-out success rate gain on 50 unseen tasks due to reduced zero-advantage gradient-batch dilution.

Conclusion: Partial trajectory divergence at intermediate steps can predict zero-variance groups, enabling early stopping that saves compute and improves training quality by reducing gradient dilution from uninformative groups.

Abstract: Group-relative RL training (GRPO) samples a small group of parallel rollouts for every training prompt and uses their within-group reward spread to compute per-trajectory advantages. In agentic environments each rollout is a long multi-turn dialogue with one LLM call per step, so this multi-sample multiplier dominates the total training cost. When every rollout of a prompt ends with the same reward, the group has zero reward variance and contributes no gradient, so the extra rollouts add no information; such groups are common in practice (typically around 40% of all groups), so the wasted-compute fraction is substantial rather than marginal. Existing methods filter such groups at the prompt level, either after their rollouts are paid for or before any rollout begins, but both decide without using information that becomes available during the rollout itself. We instead ask whether the in-group divergence between the partial trajectories at an intermediate step can already predict that the group will be zero-variance: when the parallel rollouts have already converged on the same action prefix, the group is on track to produce a single reward, and we can stop early. We propose a one-parameter gate that stops a group when the mean pairwise prefix edit distance between its partial action sequences falls below a threshold. On a 60-iteration on-policy GRPO run on ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-7B, averaged over four random seeds, the gated arm finishes 10.7% faster in wall-clock (bootstrap 95% CI excludes 0) and shifts held-out success rate on 50 unseen tasks by +2.5 pp, with the held-out gain tracing to a measurable reduction in zero-advantage gradient-batch dilution. Code is available at https://github.com/zhiyuanZhai20/selective-rollout.

</details>


### [198] [Retrieval from Within: An Intrinsic Capability of Attention-Based Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05806)
*Elad Hoffer,Yochai Blau,Ron Banner,Daniel Soudry,Boris Ginsburg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INTRA proposes an attention-based encoder-decoder framework that performs retrieval directly from its own internal representations, unifying retrieval and generation without needing external retrieval modules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RAG treats retrieval and generation as separate systems, causing a retriever-generator mismatch. The authors aim to eliminate this by leveraging attention mechanisms already present in encoder-decoder models.

Method: INTRA uses decoder attention queries to score pre-encoded evidence chunks, then reuses these chunks as context for generation. This unifies retrieval and generation and amortizes context encoding by reusing precomputed encoder states across queries.

Result: Outperforms strong engineered retrieval pipelines on both evidence recall and end-to-end answer quality on QA benchmarks.

Conclusion: Attention-based models already possess a retrieval mechanism that can be elicited internally, rather than requiring external retrieval modules.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) typically treats retrieval and generation as separate systems. We ask whether an attention-based encoder-decoder can instead retrieve directly from its own internal representations. We introduce INTRA (INTrinsic Retrieval via Attention), a framework where decoder attention queries score pre-encoded evidence chunks that are then directly reused as context for generation. By construction, INTRA unifies retrieval and generation, eliminating the retriever-generator mismatch typical of RAG pipelines. This design also amortizes context encoding by reusing precomputed encoder states across queries. On question-answering benchmarks, INTRA outperforms strong engineered retrieval pipelines on both evidence recall and end-to-end answer quality. Our results demonstrate that attention-based models already possess a retrieval mechanism that can be elicited, rather than added as an external module.

</details>


### [199] [A Testable Certificate for Constant Collapse in Teacher-Guided VAEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05813)
*Zegu Zhang,Jianhua Peng,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a measurable threshold for detecting input-independent constant collapse in VAEs by showing that the alignment cost between a teacher distribution and a constant student equals the teacher mutual information, providing a certificate that guaranteed non-constant latent usage when alignment loss falls below this bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Posterior collapse in VAEs is often diagnosed by symptoms (small KL, strong decoder, weak latent use) but lacks a concrete collapse boundary definition. The authors aim to formalize a precise, measurable threshold for a specific collapse case.

Method: The authors derive an exact threshold for input-independent constant collapse: for a nonconstant teacher T, the best constant student is the averaged teacher, with alignment cost equal to the teacher mutual information I_T(X;T). They use this certificate in CIFAR-100 and Tiny-ImageNet-200 experiments with per-seed teacher search.

Result: Full training stays on the certified side; removing alignment drives into constant-student regime; restarting from collapsed checkpoints with alignment restores certificate. Standard VAE baselines (reconstruction-preserving, post-hoc predictable) remain negative under this certificate.

Conclusion: The certificate provides a narrow but rigorous guarantee that nonconstant teacher-relative variation passes through the latent pathway, rather than claiming elimination of all posterior collapse forms.

Abstract: Posterior collapse in variational autoencoders is often diagnosed by its symptoms: a small KL term, a strong decoder, or weak use of the latent code. These signals are useful, but they do not define a collapse boundary. We study a concrete failure mode, input-independent constant collapse, and show that this case admits an exact threshold. For any fixed nonconstant teacher distribution \(T(\cdot\mid x)\), the best constant student is the dataset-average teacher distribution, and its alignment cost is the teacher mutual information \(I_T(X;T)\). Therefore, if a strictly latent-only raw witness achieves alignment loss below this value, with a safety margin, the witness cannot be constant in the input.
  This identity turns a qualitative failure mode into a measurable one. In CIFAR-100 experiments with per-seed teacher search, full training stays on the certified side of the boundary, removing alignment drives the raw witness into the constant-student regime, and restarting from a collapsed checkpoint with alignment enabled restores the certificate. Tiny-ImageNet-200 fixed-target runs show the same prevention--collapse--rescue pattern across three independently searched teachers. Standard VAE-style baselines, including methods that preserve reconstruction quality or post-hoc predictability, remain negative under the raw certificate. The guarantee is intentionally narrow: it certifies that the matched nonconstant teacher-relative variation passes through the latent pathway, rather than claiming that all forms of posterior collapse have been ruled out.

</details>


### [200] [HCInfer: An Efficient Inference System via Error Compensation for Resource-Constrained Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05819)
*Shen Xu,Xiangwen Zhuge,Zhe Xu,Yingkun Hu,Zheng Yang,Yunhao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HCInfer is a heterogeneous inference system that offloads residual compensation (LoRA-style branches) to CPU while running compressed LLM backbone on GPU, using asynchronous pipelines and dynamic rank allocation to improve accuracy (up to 5.2%) and speed (up to 10.4x) in memory-constrained deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are too large to deploy on consumer-grade hardware. Existing solutions like compression cause accuracy loss, and offloading causes throughput bottlenecks.

Method: HCInfer uses a heterogeneous system: compressed backbone runs on GPU, residual compensation (LoRA branches) runs on CPU. It includes asynchronous compensation pipeline and sensitivity-aware dynamic rank allocation to hide overhead and maximize recovery.

Result: Up to 5.2% accuracy improvement over compressed models, and up to 10.4x speedup compared to full-precision models.

Conclusion: Offloading compensation to CPU while keeping the compressed model on GPU is an effective strategy for memory-constrained LLM inference, balancing accuracy and throughput.

Abstract: LLMs often struggle with memory-constrained deployment on consumer-grade hardware due to their massive parameter sizes. While existing solutions such as model compression and offloading improve deployment feasibility, they often suffer from substantial accuracy degradation or severe throughput bottlenecks. Recent error compensation methods recover accuracy through auxiliary LoRA-style branches, and we observe that these branches are inherently amenable to offloading: they require substantial parameter storage but access only a small subset of compensation parameters during each inference step. Motivated by this opportunity, we propose HCInfer, a heterogeneous inference system that offloads residual compensation to the CPU while executing the compressed backbone on the GPU, and further introduces an asynchronous compensation pipeline and sensitivity-aware dynamic rank allocation to hide compensation overhead and maximize accuracy recovery. Experimental results show that HCInfer achieves a maximum accuracy improvement of 5.2% on downstream tasks compared to compression model and sustaining a maximum speedup of 10.4x compared to full-precision model.

</details>


### [201] [MDN: Parallelizing Stepwise Momentum for Delta Linear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05838)
*Yulong Huang,Xiang Liu,Hongxiang Huang,Xiaopeng Lin,Zunchang Liu,Xiaowen Chu,Zeke Xie,Bojun Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Momentum DeltaNet (MDN) enhances linear attention by incorporating momentum-based SGD updates into recurrent linear models, using a chunkwise parallel algorithm and stable gating constraints, achieving better performance than Mamba2 and GDN while maintaining training efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear attention models (e.g., Mamba2, GDN) interpret recurrences as online SGD, but naive SGD suffers from rapid information decay and suboptimal convergence. Momentum-based optimizers could help but are hard to implement efficiently.

Method: (1) Develops a chunkwise parallel algorithm for LA with stepwise momentum rule via geometric reordering of update coefficients. (2) Analyzes momentum-based recurrence as a second-order dynamical system with complex conjugate eigenvalues to design stable gating constraints. (3) Implements with Triton kernels for efficient training.

Result: MDN achieves comparable training throughput to Mamba2 and KDA, and shows consistent performance improvements over Transformers, Mamba2, and GDN on 400M and 1.3B parameter models across diverse downstream benchmarks.

Conclusion: Adding momentum to linear attention recurrences with proper gating constraints and parallel chunkwise computation yields a practical, efficient, and more effective linear attention model.

Abstract: Linear Attention (LA) offers a promising paradigm for scaling large language models (LLMs) to long sequences by avoiding the quadratic complexity of self-attention. Recent LA models such as Mamba2 and GDN interpret linear recurrences as closed-form online stochastic gradient descent (SGD), but naive SGD updates suffer from rapid information decay and suboptimal convergence in optimization. While momentum-based optimizers provide a natural remedy, they pose challenges in simultaneously achieving training efficiency and effectiveness. To address this, we develop a chunkwise parallel algorithm for LA with a stepwise momentum rule by geometrically reordering the update coefficients. Further, from a dynamical systems perspective, we analyze the momentum-based recurrence as a second-order system that introduces complex conjugate eigenvalues. This analysis guides the design of stable gating constraints. The resulting model, Momentum DeltaNet (MDN), leverages Triton kernels to achieve comparable training throughput with competitive linear models such as Mamba2 and KDA. Extensive experiments on the 400M and 1.3B parameter models demonstrate consistent performance improvements over strong baselines, including Transformers, Mamba2 and GDN, across diverse downstream evaluation benchmarks. Code: https://github.com/HuuYuLong/MomentumDeltaNet .

</details>


### [202] [Hypothesis generation and updating in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05851)
*Hua-Dong Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs show Bayesian-like reasoning in the number game but with systematic biases: a strong-sampling assumption (implicit Occam's razor favoring narrower hypotheses), a shift toward prior reliance in thinking mode, and an evaluation-generation gap. These patterns do not extrapolate, limiting LLMs as general problem solvers for scientific inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how LLMs perform hypothesis inference from partial data and how close their reasoning is to optimal Bayesian inference.

Method: Using the number game as a controlled setting, the authors measure LLMs' posterior over hypotheses via three probes (posterior prediction, hypothesis evaluation, hypothesis generation) and compare against optimal Bayesian models and human behavior.

Result: LLMs fit a two-parameter Bayesian model but with systematic biases: strong-sampling assumption (implicit Occam's razor), thinking mode shifts toward prior reliance, and a robust evaluation-generation gap. These patterns fail to extrapolate to unseen hypothesis domain regions.

Conclusion: LLMs exhibit a "Bayesian-with-bias" pattern that does not generalize, revealing fundamental limitations as general problem solvers, particularly for scientific inference requiring hypotheses beyond observed data.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly help people solve problems, from debugging code to repairing machinery. This process requires generating plausible hypotheses from partial descriptions, then updating them as more information arrives. Yet how LLMs perform this form of inference, and how close it is to optimal, remains unclear. We study this question in the number game, a controlled setting in which a learner infers the hypothesis supported by a few positive integers, such as $\{16, 8, 2, 64\}$: a rule like powers of 2 or an interval like numbers near 20. We measure the posterior over hypotheses using three complementary probes: posterior prediction, hypothesis evaluation, and hypothesis generation. We then compare LLM behavior with an optimal Bayesian model and human behavior, and test whether the same posterior is expressed across probes. LLMs are often well described by a two-parameter Bayesian fit, but with systematic offsets: by default they show a strong-sampling assumption that creates an implicit Occam's razor, favoring narrower hypotheses, while thinking mode shifts them toward greater prior reliance. We also find a robust evaluation--generation gap: LLMs select more correct hypotheses during hypothesis evaluation but generate simpler, more rule-like hypotheses. Finally, this Bayesian-with-bias pattern does not extrapolate. Models can behave as if they hold rule-like hypotheses over observed examples, yet generalize poorly to parts of the hypothesis domain not covered by those examples. Our results highlight a limitation of LLMs as general problem solvers, especially for scientific inference, where hypotheses must go beyond the data.

</details>


### [203] [Measuring Learning Progress via Gradient-Momentum Coupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05856)
*Samuel Blad,Martin Längkvist,Amy Loutfi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GMC replaces prediction error with a gradient-momentum coupling signal for more robust curiosity-driven exploration in RL, filtering noise and enabling curriculum learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prediction error signals fail to distinguish meaningful learnable patterns from random noise, limiting curiosity-driven exploration.

Method: Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC) measures per-parameter normalized absolute product of each sample's gradient with momentum from previous gradients, leveraging momentum's natural noise filtering.

Result: GMC shows noise robustness, emergent curriculum learning (prioritizing tasks by learning speed), and improves robustness to observation noise on MiniGrid.

Conclusion: GMC is a promising alternative to prediction error for curiosity-driven exploration, effectively filtering noise while identifying meaningful learning signals.

Abstract: Measuring learning progress is essential for curiosity-driven exploration in reinforcement learning, but widely used signals such as prediction error often fail to distinguish meaningful, learnable patterns from random noise. This paper proposes Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC), a signal derived from optimization dynamics that quantifies how useful each sample's gradient is for ongoing learning by measuring its per-parameter normalized absolute product with the momentum from previous gradients. By leveraging momentum's natural filtering of noise and oscillations, GMC identifies samples that contribute to ongoing parameter updates. Controlled experiments demonstrate noise robustness and emergent curriculum learning, with the signal prioritizing tasks by learning speed rather than difficulty. Experiments on MiniGrid suggest that replacing prediction error with GMC within existing curiosity-driven architectures can improve robustness to observation noise.

</details>


### [204] [Offline Reinforcement Learning for Rotation Profile Control in Tokamaks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05857)
*Rohit Sonker,Hiro Josep Farre Kaga,Jiayu Chen,Andrew Rothstein,Ian Char,Ricardo Shousha,Egemen Kolemen,Jeff Schneider*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper applies offline reinforcement learning methods to control the plasma rotation profile in tokamaks using only historical data from DIII-D, demonstrating promising real-world results despite challenges of limited data and complex dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Controlling the plasma rotation profile in tokamaks is crucial for stability and confinement, but it's a challenging high-dimensional, multi-input multi-output control problem that lacks accurate simulators.

Method: The authors investigate offline RL and offline model-based RL algorithms trained solely on historical DIII-D tokamak data, using probabilistic models of plasma dynamics to generate rollouts for RL training.

Result: The RL policy was deployed on the DIII-D Tokamak and showed promising real-world results.

Conclusion: Offline RL offers a viable approach for plasma rotation profile control without requiring accurate simulators, though key challenges remain in training and deploying policies on complex physical devices with limited past data.

Abstract: Tokamaks remain leading candidates for achieving practical fusion energy, yet many important control problems inside these devices are still difficult or unsolved. One such challenge is controlling the plasma rotation profile, which strongly influences stability, confinement, and transport. While the average rotation can be controlled, controlling the full profile is challenging due to high dimensionality, response to multiple actuators and dependence on plasma condition. Learning-based control methods, such as reinforcement learning (RL), provide a potential solution to this challenging problem with ability to model complex interactions leading to effective multi-input multi-output control. However, learning such policies is challenging due to the lack of accurate simulators that can model the rotation profile dynamics. In this work, we investigate the use of offline RL and offline model-based RL algorithms for rotation profile control, training them solely on historical data from the DIII-D tokamak. Our final method uses probabilistic models of plasma dynamics to generate rollouts for RL training. We deploy this policy on the DIII-D Tokamak and observe promising real-world results. We conclude by highlighting key challenges and insights from training and deploying an RL policy on a complex physical device while using only limited past data.

</details>


### [205] [Do Neural Operators Forget Geometry? The Forgetting Hypothesis in Deep Operator Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05862)
*Yanming Xia,Angelica I. Aviles-Rivero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural operators lose geometric information as depth increases ("Geometric Forgetting Hypothesis"), degrading performance. The paper proposes a lightweight geometry memory injection mechanism to restore geometric constraints and mitigate this issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operators perform well on structured domains but poorly on irregular geometries. The authors aim to understand and address this limitation, showing it's not just an encoding issue but a depth-wise failure mode in deep operator architectures.

Method: The authors formalize the Geometric Forgetting Hypothesis, use layer-wise geometric probing to demonstrate geometric fidelity loss in spectral and attention-based operators, and introduce a lightweight geometry memory injection mechanism to restore geometric constraints at intermediate depths.

Result: Geometric forgetting degrades accuracy, stability, and generalization. The proposed geometry memory injection consistently mitigates forgetting and reveals a geometric shortcut instability in transformer-based operators.

Conclusion: Geometric retention is a structural requirement rather than a design choice. The lightweight intervention effectively restores geometric constraints with minimal architectural overhead.

Abstract: Neural operators perform well on structured domains, yet their behaviour on irregular geometries remains poorly understood. We show that this limitation is not merely an encoding issue, but a depth-wise failure mode inherent to deep operator architectures.
  We formalise the Geometric Forgetting Hypothesis: due to the Markovian structure of operator layers and their reliance on global mixing mechanisms, neural operators progressively lose access to domain geometry as depth increases. Using layer-wise geometric probing, we demonstrate that both spectral and attention-based operators systematically lose geometric fidelity.
  We show that this geometric forgetting degrades accuracy, stability, and generalisation. To counteract it, we introduce a lightweight geometry memory injection mechanism that restores geometric constraints at intermediate depths with minimal architectural overhead. This simple intervention consistently mitigates forgetting and exposes a geometric shortcut instability in transformer-based operators, revealing that geometric retention is a structural requirement rather than a design choice.

</details>


### [206] [SOPE: Stabilizing Off-Policy Evaluation for Online RL with Prior Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05863)
*Carlo Romeo,Girolamo Macaluso,Alessandro Sestini,Andrew D. Bagdanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOPE automatically determines when to stop offline pretraining in online RL by using Off-Policy Policy Evaluation, improving performance up to 45.6% while reducing computation up to 22x on 25 continuous control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for incorporating prior data into online RL face a trade-off between computational cost and manual tuning of offline training phases, risking either wasted prior knowledge or overfitting.

Method: SOPE uses an actor-aligned Off-Policy Policy Evaluation (OPE) signal as an automated early-stopping mechanism, evaluating the critic on a held-out validation split under the current policy's action distribution to halt gradient updates when OOD benefits saturate.

Result: On 25 Minari benchmark tasks, SOPE improves performance by up to 45.6% and reduces TFLOPs by up to 22x compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Adaptive, evaluation-driven update schedules are more effective than static, exhaustive update schedules for balancing sample and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Incorporating prior data into online reinforcement learning accelerates training but typically forces a difficult trade-off between high computational costs and long, multi-stage training pipelines. While fixed-length stabilization phases are significantly more computationally efficient than static update schedules, they require task-dependent manual tuning, risking either the waste of prior knowledge or severe overfitting. To address this, we propose SOPE, an algorithm that uses an actor-aligned Off-Policy Policy Evaluation (OPE) signal as an automated early-stopping mechanism to dynamically control the length of offline training phases. By evaluating the critic on a held-out validation split under the current policy's action distribution, SOPE halts gradient updates exactly when out-of-distribution benefits saturate, eliminating the need for manual schedule tuning. Evaluated on 25 continuous control tasks from the Minari benchmark suite, SOPE improves baseline performance by up to 45.6% while reducing the required TFLOPs by up to 22x, thus balancing the tradeoff between sample and computational efficiency. These findings demonstrate that adaptive, evaluation-driven update schedules are more effective than relying on static, exhaustive update schedules.

</details>


### [207] [QuadraSHAP: Stable and Scalable Shapley Values for Product Games via Gauss-Legendre Quadrature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05870)
*Majid Mohammadi,Grigory Reznikov,Pavel Sinitcyn,Krikamol Muandet,Siu Lun Chau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces QuadraSHAP, a method for efficiently computing Shapley values in product games (where coalition value is a product of per-player terms). The key insight is that the Shapley value can be expressed as a one-dimensional integral of a polynomial, enabling exact Gauss-Legendre quadrature with O(d m_q) work and O(log d) parallel time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Computing Shapley values for feature attribution in machine learning is computationally expensive (exponential in the number of features). The authors identify that for product games — which arise naturally in kernel methods with product kernels and tree-based models — the Shapley value computation has special structure that can be exploited for efficiency.

Method: The authors prove that the Shapley value in a product game reduces to a one-dimensional integral of a degree-(d-1) polynomial over [0,1]. They apply Gauss-Legendre quadrature, which is provably exact when the number of quadrature nodes m_q ≥ ⌈d/2⌉, and otherwise provides geometric error decay. They implement this via log-space evaluation for numerical stability and use associative scan primitives for O(d m_q) work and O(log d) parallel time.

Result: QuadraSHAP provides provably exact or near-exact Shapley value computation. With a few hundred quadrature nodes, it achieves highly precise estimates even for thousands of features. Empirically, it is the fastest numerically stable method across all tested configurations.

Conclusion: By exploiting the multiplicative factorization property of product games, the authors transform an exponentially complex problem into an efficient, parallelizable, and numerically stable algorithm, making Shapley value computation practical for large-scale models with product structure.

Abstract: We study the efficient computation of Shapley values for \emph{product games} -- cooperative games in which the coalition value factorizes as a product of per-player terms. Such games arise in machine learning explainability whenever the value function inherits a multiplicative structure from the underlying model, as in kernel methods with product kernels and tree-based models. Our key result is that the Shapley value of each player in a product game admits an exact one-dimensional integral representation: the weighted sum over exponentially many feature coalitions collapses to the integral of a degree-$(d-1)$ polynomial over $[0,1]$, where $d$ is the total number of features. This yields a Gauss--Legendre quadrature scheme that is \emph{provably exact} whenever the number of nodes satisfies $m_q \geq \lceil d/2 \rceil$, and otherwise provides a \emph{near-exact} approximation with error provably decaying geometrically in $m_q$. In practice, a few hundred nodes can achieve highly precise estimates even with thousands of features. Building on this formulation, we derive a numerically stable implementation via log-space evaluation, together with an efficient parallel implementation based on associative scan primitives that achieves $O(d\,m_q)$ total work and $O(\log d)$ parallel time. Experiments show that \textsc{QuadraSHAP} is the fastest numerically stable method across all tested configurations.

</details>


### [208] [Retain-Neutral Surrogates for Min-Max Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05871)
*Junhao Cai,Dohun Kim,Dowon Kim,Sung Il Choi,Chengjun Jin,Juhyun Park,Changhee Joo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ROSU improves machine unlearning by constraining surrogate perturbations to be retain-orthogonal, minimizing retain loss damage while maximizing forget gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing min-max unlearning approaches can inadvertently increase retain loss when forget/retain gradients are strongly aligned, due to unconstrained forget-maximizing perturbations moving surrogate points to positions that harm retain performance.

Method: ROSU constrains the inner surrogate construction by maximizing first-order forget gain subject to zero first-order retain change under a fixed perturbation budget, yielding a closed-form retain-orthogonal perturbation with lightweight transported outer update and amplification along retain-neutral direction.

Result: Establishes curvature-controlled second-order bound on retain damage, positive-alignment regime where ROSU reduces surrogate retain loss vs standard min-max, and near-equivalence when gradients are nearly orthogonal. Empirically validated on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, TOFU, WMDP.

Conclusion: ROSU achieves clearest gains in high-coupling (strong gradient alignment) regimes while remaining competitive elsewhere, offering a principled solution to the retain-damage problem in approximate unlearning.

Abstract: Machine unlearning seeks to remove the influence of designated training data while preserving performance on the remaining data. Approximate unlearning can be viewed as a local editing problem; in min-max unlearning, the key local object is the surrogate point at which the retain objective is evaluated. When forget and retain gradients are strongly aligned, an unconstrained forget-maximizing perturbation can move to a surrogate point that increases retain loss. We propose Retain-Orthogonal Surrogate Unlearning (ROSU), which constrains the inner surrogate construction by maximizing first-order forget gain subject to zero first-order retain change under a fixed perturbation budget. This yields a closed-form retain-orthogonal perturbation, a lightweight transported outer update, and amplification along the retain-neutral direction. Our analysis establishes (i) a curvature-controlled second-order bound on retain damage, (ii) a positive-alignment regime in which ROSU strictly reduces surrogate retain loss relative to standard min-max perturbations, and (iii) near-equivalence when the two gradients are nearly orthogonal. Across vision and language benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, TOFU, WMDP), the empirical pattern follows this geometry: ROSU gives its clearest gains in high-coupling regimes while remaining competitive elsewhere.

</details>


### [209] [RepFlow: Representation Enhanced Flow Matching for Causal Effect Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05890)
*Yifei Xie,Jian Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RepFlow is a framework that combines representation learning with Conditional Flow Matching to estimate causal effects from observational data, outperforming existing methods in both point and distributional estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing causal inference methods are largely limited to point estimates and lack capacity for distribution modeling, missing the need to capture the full distribution of potential outcomes.

Method: RepFlow formulates causal effect estimation as a joint optimization problem integrating representation learning with Conditional Flow Matching (CFM), minimizes entropically regularized Wasserstein distance between treated and control representations to mitigate selection bias, and introduces L2 normalization constraint on latent representations for numerical stability.

Result: Consistently outperforms existing methods across wide range of benchmarks in both point and distributional causal effect estimation.

Conclusion: The balanced representation from RepFlow enables flow models to accurately capture the distribution of potential outcomes, advancing beyond point estimates in causal inference.

Abstract: Estimating causal effects from observational data has become increasingly critical in diverse fields including healthcare, economics, and social policy. The fundamental challenge in causal inference arises from the missing counterfactuals and the selection bias. Existing methods are largely limited to point estimates and lack the capacity for distribution modeling. In this work, we propose RepFlow, a novel framework that formulates causal effect estimation as a joint optimization problem integrating representation learning with Conditional Flow Matching (CFM).
  RepFlow mitigates selection bias by minimizing the entropically regularized Wasserstein distance between treated and control representations.
  To enhance numerical stability, we further introduce an $L_2$ normalization constraint on latent representations.
  This balanced representation enables the flow model to accurately capture the distribution of potential outcomes. Extensive experiments across a wide range of benchmarks demonstrate that RepFlow consistently outperforms existing methods in both point and distributional causal effect estimation.

</details>


### [210] [VARS-FL: Validation-Aligned Client Selection for Non-IID Federated Learning in IoT Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05896)
*Mohamed Lakas,Mohamed Amine Ferrag*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VARS-FL proposes a reputation-based client selection framework for federated learning that uses server-side validation loss reduction to score clients, combined with sliding-window averaging and logarithmic participation scaling, outperforming standard methods on a non-IID IoT intrusion detection task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stateless client selection in FL ignores historical contribution quality, causing slow convergence and unstable training under non-IID data, especially problematic in IoT/IIoT environments with highly heterogeneous data distributions.

Method: VARS-FL quantifies each client's contribution by measuring the reduction in server-side validation loss induced by its update, then aggregates per-round signals into a Reputation score using a sliding-window average of recent contributions plus a logarithmically scaled participation term for exploration-exploitation balance.

Result: Evaluated on 15-class non-IID IoT intrusion detection (Edge-IIoTset, 100 clients), VARS-FL consistently improves accuracy, F1-Macro, and loss over FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice, achieving up to 36% fewer rounds to reach 80% accuracy.

Conclusion: Validation-aligned, history-aware client selection (VARS-FL) provides more reliable and efficient FL training in heterogeneous IoT environments, with no modifications needed to local training or aggregation, and full compatibility with FedAvg.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) systems typically employ stateless client selection, treating each communication round independently and ignoring accumulated evidence of client contribution quality. Under non-IID data, this leads to slow convergence and unstable training, particularly when selection relies on local proxies (e.g., training loss) that are misaligned with the global optimization objective. These challenges are especially pronounced in Internet of Things (IoT) and Industrial IoT (IIoT) environments, where data is highly heterogeneous and distributed across devices observing different traffic patterns. In this paper, we propose VARS-FL (Validation-Aligned Reputation Scoring for Federated Learning), a client selection framework that quantifies each client's contribution using the reduction in server-side validation loss induced by its update. These per-round signals are aggregated into a Reputation score that combines a sliding-window average of recent contributions with a logarithmically scaled participation term, enabling robust exploration-exploitation selection. VARS-FL requires no changes to local training or aggregation and remains fully compatible with standard FedAvg. We evaluate VARS-FL on a 15-class non-IID IoT intrusion detection task using the Edge-IIoTset dataset, with 100 clients across multiple seeds, and compare it against FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice. VARS-FL consistently improves accuracy, F1-Macro, and loss, while accelerating convergence (up to 36% fewer rounds to reach 80% accuracy). These results demonstrate that validation-aligned, history-aware client selection provides a more reliable and efficient training process for federated learning in heterogeneous IoT environments.

</details>


### [211] [VisMMOE: Exploiting Visual-Expert Affinity for Efficient Visual-Language MoE Offloading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05899)
*Cheng Xu,Xiaofeng Hou,Jiacheng Liu,Chao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VisMMoE is a system for efficiently deploying VL-MoE models on memory-constrained platforms. It leverages the insight that pruning redundant visual tokens concentrates expert accesses (visual-expert affinity), enabling better expert prediction, caching, and pipeline orchestration. It achieves up to 2.68x speedup over baselines while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MoE offloading systems are designed for text-centric workloads and perform poorly on visual-heavy inputs, where large numbers of visual tokens cause broad and unpredictable expert accesses. Efficient deployment of VL-MoE models on memory-constrained platforms remains difficult.

Method: The key insight is visual-expert affinity: pruning redundant visual tokens makes expert accesses more concentrated within layers and more stable across layers. VisMMoE combines affinity-aware token compression, lookahead expert prediction, and cache/pipeline orchestration to improve expert locality and prefetch effectiveness under tight memory budgets.

Result: VisMMoE improves end-to-end inference performance by up to 2.68x and 1.61x over strong baselines for today's VL-MoE deployments while maintaining competitive accuracy.

Conclusion: Pruning redundant visual tokens reshapes expert demand patterns (visual-expert affinity), enabling more efficient offloading for VL-MoE models on memory-constrained platforms.

Abstract: Large-scale vision-language mixture-of-experts (VL-MoE) models provide strong multimodal capability, but efficient deployment on memory-constrained platforms remains difficult. Existing MoE offloading systems are largely designed for text-centric workloads and become much less effective for visual-heavy inputs, where large numbers of visual tokens induce broader and less predictable expert accesses. We present VisMMoE, a VL-MoE offloading system built on a single systems insight: pruning redundant visual tokens can improve offloading not only by reducing computation, but also by reshaping expert demand. We refer to this effect as \textit{visual-expert affinity}: token pruning makes expert accesses more concentrated within layers and more stable across layers, producing a smaller and more predictable expert working set. Guided by this insight, VisMMoE combines affinity-aware token compression, lookahead expert prediction, and cache/pipeline orchestration to improve expert locality and prefetch effectiveness under tight memory budgets. We implement VisMMoE on multiple frameworks and evaluate it on representative VL-MoE models and benchmarks. VisMMoE improves end-to-end inference performance by up to 2.68x and 1.61x, respectively, over strong baselines for today's VL-MoE deployments while maintaining competitive accuracy.

</details>


### [212] [Quadratic Objective Perturbation: Curvature-Based Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05905)
*Daniel Cortild,Coralia Cartis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP) for differentially private empirical risk minimization, replacing the linear perturbation in standard LOP with a quadratic random perturbation to achieve privacy under weaker assumptions by leveraging curvature rather than bounded gradient assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear Objective Perturbation (LOP) requires the strong assumption of bounded gradients of the loss function, which excludes many modern machine learning models. The authors aim to relax this assumption while maintaining differential privacy guarantees.

Method: The paper introduces Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP), which perturbs the objective function with a random quadratic form. This induces strong convexity and stability through curvature, allowing sensitivity control via spectral properties instead of gradient bounds. The analysis also covers approximate solutions and utility guarantees in terms of empirical excess risk.

Result: QOP achieves (ε,δ)-differential privacy under weaker assumptions (interpolation regime), preserves privacy guarantees under inexact solves, and provides utility guarantees. Theoretical and numerical comparisons show advantages over LOP.

Conclusion: Curvature-based perturbations (QOP) offer a promising alternative to gradient-based perturbations (LOP) for differential privacy, enabling broader applicability to modern ML models. The resulting problems can be solved efficiently using splitting schemes.

Abstract: Objective perturbation is a standard mechanism in differentially private empirical risk minimization. In particular, Linear Objective Perturbation (LOP) enforces privacy by adding a random linear term, while strong convexity and stability are ensured by an additional deterministic quadratic term. However, this approach requires the strong assumption of bounded gradients of the loss function, which excludes many modern machine learning models. In this work, we introduce Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP), which perturbs the objective with a random quadratic form. This perturbation induces strong convexity and enforces stability of the problem through curvature, thereby enabling privacy and allowing sensitivity to be controlled through spectral properties of the perturbation rather than assumptions on the gradients. As a result, we obtain $(\varepsilon, δ)$-differential privacy under weaker assumptions, in the interpolation regime. Furthermore, we extend the analysis to account for approximate solutions, showing that privacy guarantees are preserved under inexact solves. Additionally, we derive utility guarantees in terms of empirical excess risk, and provide a theoretical and numerical comparison to LOP, highlighting the advantages of curvature-based perturbations. Finally, we discuss algorithmic aspects and show that the resulting problems can be solved efficiently using modern splitting schemes.

</details>


### [213] [From Drops to Grid: Noise-Aware Spatio-Temporal Neural Process for Rainfall Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05912)
*Rafael Pablos Sarabia,Joachim Nyborg,Morten Birk,Ira Assent*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DropsToGrid uses Neural Processes to fuse noisy private weather station data with radar to generate high-resolution, accurate rainfall maps with quantified uncertainty, outperforming both operational and deep learning baselines even with sparse data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional rainfall observations are biased and low-resolution, and existing deep learning methods struggle with rainfall's skewed distribution, noise, and limited spatio-temporal fusion.

Method: Neural Process-based approach combining multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention, and multi-modal fusion to integrate noisy irregular station data with radar spatial context.

Result: Outperforms operational and deep learning baselines, generates accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, effective even with few stations and in cross-regional scenarios.

Conclusion: DropsToGrid effectively addresses key limitations in rainfall densification by producing stochastic, continuous estimates with explicit uncertainty quantification.

Abstract: High-resolution rainfall observations are crucial for weather forecasting, water management, and hazard mitigation. Traditional operational measurements are often biased and low-resolution, limiting their ability to capture local rainfall. Accurate high-resolution rainfall maps require integrating sparse surface observations, yet existing deep learning densification methods are hindered by rainfall's skewed, localized nature, noise, and limited spatio-temporal fusion. We present DropsToGrid, a Neural Process-based method that generates dense rainfall fields by fusing temporal sequences from noisy, irregularly distributed private weather stations with spatial context from radar. Leveraging multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention, and multi-modal fusion, the model produces stochastic, continuous rainfall estimates and explicitly quantifies uncertainty. Evaluations on real-world datasets demonstrate that DropsToGrid outperforms both operational and deep learning baselines, generating accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, even when only few stations are available and in cross-regional scenarios.

</details>


### [214] [Near-Policy: Accelerating On-Policy Distillation via Asynchronous Generation and Selective Packing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05940)
*Miao Rang,Zhenni Bi,Hang Zhou,Kai Han,Xuechun Wang,An Xiao,Xinghao Chen,Yunhe Wang,Hanting Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Near-Policy Distillation (NPD) improves knowledge distillation efficiency for autoregressive models by decoupling student generation from training via asynchronous updates, achieving 8.1x speedup over on-policy methods and 8.09% improvement over SFT, enabling a 1B model to outperform a 1.7B model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
