Motivation: Standard knowledge distillation for autoregressive models suffers from distribution mismatch. On-policy methods mitigate this but rely on computationally expensive RL frameworks. The goal is to achieve efficient, stable distillation without heavy RL overhead.

Method: NPD decouples student generation from training asynchronously, enabling SFT with sequence packing. To handle policy lag and sample noise from asynchronous updates, it integrates sparse student updates and a Δ-IFD filtering mechanism that heuristically filters extreme out-of-distribution samples to keep updates in a safe proximal learning zone.

Result: NPD achieves 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines, outperforms SFT by 8.09%, and enables openPangu-Embedded-1B to reach 68.73% SOTA score, beating Qwen3-1.7B.

Conclusion: NPD effectively narrows the exploration space for subsequent RL, enabling smaller models to outperform larger ones through efficient near-policy distillation.

Abstract: Standard knowledge distillation for autoregressive models often suffers from distribution mismatch. While on-policy methods mitigate this by leveraging student-generated outputs, they rely on computationally expensive Reinforcement Learning (RL) frameworks. To improve efficiency, we propose Near-Policy Distillation (NPD), an asynchronous approach that decouples student generation from training. This reformulation enables Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with sequence packing. However, asynchronous updates inevitably introduce policy lag and sample noise, which can cause the behavior to drift from near-policy toward off-policy. To counteract this without sacrificing efficiency, NPD integrates sparse student updates and the $Δ$-IFD filtering mechanism, a heuristic sample selection mechanism that empirically stabilizes the optimization trajectory. By filtering extreme out-of-distribution samples, $Δ$-IFD prevents noise from dominating the gradients, ensuring updates remain within a safe proximal learning zone. Empirically, the NPD framework achieves a 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines and outperforms SFT by 8.09%. Crucially, by effectively narrowing the exploration space for subsequent RL, our method enables openPangu-Embedded-1B to reach a state-of-the-art score of 68.73%, outperforming the substantially larger Qwen3-1.7B. Codes will be released soon.

</details>


### [215] [Knowing but Not Correcting: Routine Task Requests Suppress Factual Correction in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05957)
*Zixuan Chen,Hao Lin,Zizhe Chen,Yizhou Tian,Garry Yang,Depeng Wang,Ya Guo,Huijia Zhu,James Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs often fail to correct false claims when they appear in task-oriented contexts, a phenomenon termed "correction suppression" (19%-90% suppression across models). The root cause is not lack of knowledge but response selection — the model knows the error but diverts attention due to task context. Two training-free interventions (CDS and DPA) improve factual strictness without harming reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLMs reliably correct false claims in isolation, but when the same false claims are embedded in task-oriented requests, they comply instead of correcting. This reveals a critical reliability gap that existing evaluations overlook, motivating the need to study this "correction suppression" failure mode systematically.

Method: (1) Construct a benchmark of 300 false premises to evaluate correction suppression across eight models. (2) Conduct mechanistic analysis via probing and attention analysis to trace suppression to early-layer attention diversion and middle-layer output intent crystallization. (3) Propose two training-free interventions: Correction Direction Steering (CDS) injects a correction-compliance direction at middle layers; Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA) amplifies payload token representations at the final layer using attention divergence.

Result: Suppression rates range from 19% to 90% across eight models, with four models exceeding 80%. Mechanistic analysis confirms it is a response-selection failure ("knowing but not correcting"), not a knowledge failure. CDS achieves 0% → 58.2% correction rate on Qwen3.5-9B. DPA preserves or improves reasoning capability on both Qwen3.5-9B and LLaMA3.1-8B.

Conclusion: Correction suppression is a prevalent failure mode distinct from knowledge gaps. The work introduces "factual strictness" as a new reliability dimension, and demonstrates that lightweight, training-free interventions can substantially improve it without degrading reasoning.

Abstract: LLMs reliably correct false claims when presented in isolation, yet when the same claims are embedded in task-oriented requests, they often comply rather than correct. We term this failure mode \emph{correction suppression} and construct a benchmark of 300 false premises to systematically evaluate it across eight models. Suppression rates range from 19\% to 90\%, with four models exceeding 80\%, establishing correction suppression as a prevalent and severe phenomenon. Mechanistic analysis reveals that suppression is not a knowledge failure: the model registers the error internally but task context diverts early-layer attention from the false claim as output intent crystallizes toward compliance at middle layers. We characterize this as \emph{knowing but not correcting} -- suppression occurs at response selection rather than knowledge encoding. Guided by this mechanism, we propose two training-free interventions. Correction Direction Steering (CDS) estimates a correction-compliance direction from matched pairs and injects it at middle layers before output intent crystallizes. Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA) localizes payload tokens via attention divergence between early and late layers and amplifies their representation at the final layer, requiring no calibration data. Experiments on Qwen3.5-9B and LLaMA3.1-8B show both methods substantially improve factual strictness. CDS achieves the highest correction rate on Qwen3.5-9B (0\%$\to$58.2\%). DPA is the only method that preserves or improves reasoning capability on both models. These findings introduce \emph{factual strictness} -- the willingness to uphold accuracy against contextual pressures -- as a new dimension of model reliability.

</details>


### [216] [Uncertainty Estimation via Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05964)
*Eunseo Choi,Ho-Yeon Kim,Jaewon Lee,Taeyong jo,Myungjun lee,Heejin Ahn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HCM is a sampling-free, distribution-free uncertainty estimation framework that uses hyperspherical geometry (magnitude + unit direction) to quantify uncertainty as violation of a geometric constraint, matching ensemble methods with lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantifying uncertainty in neural networks is critical for high-stakes domains, but existing methods rely on costly sampling or restrictive distributional assumptions, motivating a simpler and more efficient approach.

Method: HCM decomposes network outputs into magnitude and a normalized direction vector on the unit hypersphere, interpreting uncertainty as the degree to which the geometric constraint (unit norm) is violated.

Result: Across diverse benchmarks and real-world industrial tasks, HCM matches or surpasses ensemble and evidential methods with far lower inference cost and stronger confidence-error alignment.

Conclusion: Geometric structure is powerful for uncertainty estimation; HCM is a versatile, deterministic alternative to conventional techniques.

Abstract: Quantifying uncertainty in neural network predictions is essential for high-stakes domains such as autonomous driving, healthcare, and manufacturing. While existing approaches often depend on costly sampling or restrictive distributional assumptions, we propose Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping (HCM), a simple yet principled framework for sampling-free and distribution-free uncertainty estimation. HCM decomposes outputs into a magnitude and a normalized direction vector constrained to lie on the unit hypersphere, enabling a novel interpretation of uncertainty as the degree of violation of this geometric constraint. This yields deterministic and interpretable estimates applicable to both regression and classification. Experiments across diverse benchmarks and real-world industrial tasks demonstrate that HCM matches or surpasses ensemble and evidential approaches, with far lower inference cost and stronger confidence-error alignment. Our results highlight the power of geometric structure in uncertainty estimation and position HCM as a versatile alternative to conventional techniques.

</details>


### [217] [Beyond Uniform Credit Assignment: Selective Eligibility Traces for RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05965)
*Chaoli Mou,Zhan Zhuang,Xinning Chen,Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: S-trace is a critic-free reinforcement learning algorithm that improves reasoning in LLMs by using sparse eligibility traces and selective masking of low-entropy tokens, outperforming GRPO with higher sample and token efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing critic-free RL methods like GRPO use uniform credit assignment, failing to distinguish critical reasoning steps, which hinders learning efficiency.

Method: Propose P-trace (critic-free eligibility traces with partial trust region preservation), then build S-trace on top, which implements sparse eligibility traces by selectively masking low-entropy tokens for fine-grained credit assignment.

Result: S-trace outperforms GRPO: +0.49% on Qwen3-1.7B, +3.16% on Qwen3-4B, +2.98% on Qwen3-8B in average pass@16, with higher sample and token efficiency.

Conclusion: S-trace effectively addresses uniform credit assignment limitations in critic-free RLVR, achieving better reasoning performance with greater efficiency.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a key approach for improving the reasoning abilities of large language models. However, widely used critic-free algorithms such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) necessitate a ``uniform credit assignment'' assumption that indiscriminately broadcast trajectory-level advantages, hindering learning efficiency by failing to distinguish critical reasoning steps. To address this limitation, we propose Selective Eligibility Traces (S-trace). Grounded in the intuition of partial trust region preservation, we initially introduce P-trace as a sample-efficient, critic-free eligibility traces method, upon which we build S-trace, implementing a sparse eligibility traces mechanism to further mitigate variance and achieve fine-grained credit assignment by selectively masking low-entropy tokens. Theoretically, we contextualize the recent Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) method within the critic-free eligibility traces framework, identifying it as a special instance of the eligibility traces method operating under uniform credit assignment. Experiments demonstrate that S-trace not only outperforms GRPO, showing gains of 0.49\% on Qwen3-1.7B and 3.16\% on Qwen3-4B, and maintaining a robust 2.98\% improvement when scaled further to Qwen3-8B in average pass@16, but notably achieves this with simultaneously higher sample and token efficiency.

</details>


### [218] [Sharper Guarantees for Misspecified Kernelized Bandit Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05967)
*Davide Maran,Csaba Szepesvári*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that misspecification amplification in kernelized bandit optimization can be reduced to logarithmic/polylogarithmic growth via localization techniques, both in offline (spectral Lebesgue constant) and online (domain-splitting algorithm) settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing misspecified kernelized bandit bounds suffer from multiplicative penalties involving kernel complexity (e.g., $\sqrt{d_\mathrm{eff}}$ or $\sqrt{γ_n}$), which can be large. The authors aim to significantly reduce this amplification.

Method: In offline setting, the authors use spectral localization to bound misspecification via a spectral Lebesgue constant, achieving logarithmic/polylogarithmic amplification. In online setting, they modify a domain-splitting algorithm under localized eigendecay assumptions to remove the extra $\sqrt{γ_n}$ factor from the misspecification term.

Result: Offline: Logarithmic amplification for 1D monotone spectra; polylogarithmic for multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. Online: $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(\sqrt{γ_n n}+n\varepsilon)$ regret bound, removing the $\sqrt{γ_n}$ factor from the misspecification penalty.

Conclusion: Localization (spectral in offline, spatial in online) prevents local misspecification errors from being amplified globally, leading to significantly improved theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: Existing guarantees for misspecified kernelized bandit optimization pay for misspecification through kernel complexity: in generic offline bounds, the misspecification level $\varepsilon$ is multiplied by $\sqrt{d_\mathrm{eff}}$, where $d_\mathrm{eff}$ is the kernel effective dimension, while in online regret bounds, the corresponding penalty is $\sqrt{γ_n}\,n\varepsilon$, where $γ_n$ is the maximum information gain after $n$ rounds of interaction.
  In this work, we show that, for a large class of kernels, the misspecification amplification can be reduced to logarithmic or polylogarithmic growth. In the offline setting, we first prove high-probability simple-regret bounds whose misspecification term is governed by a spectral Lebesgue constant. This yields logarithmic amplification for one-dimensional monotone spectra and polylogarithmic amplification for multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. In the online setting, we modify a domain-splitting algorithm and prove a cumulative regret bound of $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(\sqrt{γ_n n}+n\varepsilon)$ under mild localized eigendecay assumptions, removing the extra $\sqrt{γ_n}$ factor from the misspecification term. The common principle is localization: spectral localization controls the Lebesgue constant of the offline approximation operator, while domain splitting implements the spatial analogue of this mechanism in the online setting, preventing local misspecification errors from being amplified globally.

</details>


### [219] [Training Transformers for KV Cache Compressibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05971)
*Yoav Gelberg,Yam Eitan,Michael Bronstein,Yarin Gal,Haggai Maron*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KV-CAT is a continued pretraining method that forces models to learn more compressible KV cache representations via train-time KV sparsification, improving downstream compression quality across multiple tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The KV cache bottleneck in long-context modeling grows linearly with prefix length. Existing post-hoc compression methods are limited by how well the model's internal representations can be compressed, motivating a need to guide transformers toward more compressible representations during training.

Method: The paper formalizes KV compressibility as a property of learned representations. It proposes KV-Compression Aware Training (KV-CAT), a continued pretraining procedure with a train-time KV sparsification policy that masks KV slots during training, encouraging the model to use fewer slots and learn more compressible representations.

Result: KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across retrieval, long-context QA, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.

Conclusion: KV compressibility depends on learned representations, not just context alone. Guiding transformers toward compressible representations during training (via KV-CAT) significantly enhances post-hoc compression effectiveness.

Abstract: Long-context language modeling is increasingly constrained by the Key-Value (KV) cache, whose memory and decode-time access costs scale linearly with the prefix length. This bottleneck has motivated a range of context-compression methods, from token-level summarization to recent optimization-based KV compression methods. These post-hoc methods operate on the KV cache of a fixed pretrained model, so their effectiveness is fundamentally limited by how well the model's internal representations can be compressed. In this work, we formalize the notion of KV compressibility and show that it is a property of the learned representations, rather than of the context alone. We prove that almost any sequence-to-vector function admits both highly compressible and inherently non-compressible transformer implementations, highlighting the need to guide transformers toward compressible representations during training. Motivated by this, we propose KV-Compression Aware Training (KV-CAT), a continued pretraining procedure that incentivizes the emergence of compressible representations. We introduce a train-time KV sparsification policy that masks KV slots during training. This forces the model to use fewer KV slots and encourages it to learn representations amenable to post-hoc compression. Empirically, we show that KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across retrieval, long-context question answering, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.

</details>


### [220] [Physical Fidelity Reconstruction via Improved Consistency-Distilled Flow Matching for Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05975)
*Sicheng Ma,Tianyue Yang,Xiuzhe Wu,Xiao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper distills an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher into a one-step consistency model for fast scientific flow reconstruction, achieving 12x speedup with half the parameters while retaining similar accuracy on three fluid benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion and flow-matching models for flow field reconstruction rely on iterative sampling, making them too slow for latency-sensitive workflows like ensemble forecasting and real-time visualization.

Method: Distill an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher into a one-step consistency model; low-fidelity observations are incorporated by initializing the generative trajectory from a noised observation along the transport path, enabling conditional reconstruction without retraining the teacher.

Result: On Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, and Kolmogorov Flow (up to 256x256), the distilled student retains similar spectrum metrics, uses half the parameters, achieves 12x inference speedup, and outperforms a scratch-trained consistency model by 23.1% in SSIM.

Conclusion: Teacher distillation offers a promising route to turn high-capacity scientific generative models into compact, fast, and deployable reconstruction models.

Abstract: Reconstructing high-fidelity flow fields from low-fidelity observations is a central problem in scientific machine learning, yet recent diffusion and flow-matching models typically rely on iterative sampling, making them costly for latency-sensitive workflows such as ensemble forecasting, real-time visualization, and simulation-in-the-loop inference. We study whether a high-fidelity flow-matching generative model can be compressed into a compact one-step model for fast scientific flow reconstruction. Our approach distills an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher into a one-step consistency model. Low-fidelity observations are incorporated at inference by initializing the generative trajectory from a noised observation along the transport path, allowing an unconditional high-fidelity flow model to perform conditional reconstruction without retraining the teacher. We evaluate this distillation strategy on three fluid benchmarks, Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, and Kolmogorov Flow, using coarse-to-fine reconstruction as a controlled testbed at field sizes up to $256 \times 256$. Across these settings, the distilled student retains similar performance of the teacher's model on spectrum metrics, while using roughly half as many parameters and achieving a $12\times$ inference speedup over the flow-matching teacher. Under the same training budget, the distilled student also outperforms a one-step consistency model trained directly from scratch by $23.1\%$ in SSIM, showing that teacher distillation improves training efficiency rather than merely accelerating sampling. These results suggest a promising route for turning future high-capacity scientific generative models into compact reconstruction models that are faster to train, cheaper to run, and easier to deploy.

</details>


### [221] [Towards Steering without Sacrifice: Principled Training of Steering Vectors for Prompt-only Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05983)
*Yuntai Bao,Qinfeng Li,Xinyan Yu,Xuhong Zhang,Ge Su,Wenqi Zhang,Liu Yan,Haiqin Weng,Jianwei Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes joint training of steering factors and directions to eliminate per-SV factor selection, and introduces Prompt-only SV (PrOSV) that intervenes only on prompt tokens to improve generation quality over full-sequence SVs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fine-tuned steering vectors (SVs) require careful per-SV steering factor selection and full-sequence intervention sacrifices generation quality. Authors aim to address both limitations.

Method: 1) Joint training of steering factors and directions to remove post-hoc factor selection. 2) Using neural network scaling theory for proper initialization sizes and learning rates. 3) Introducing Prompt-only SV (PrOSV) that only intervenes on prompt tokens, inspired by representation fine-tuning.

Result: PrOSV outperforms traditional full-sequence SVs (FSSVs) on AxBench and achieves a better tradeoff between general model utility and adversarial robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed joint training scheme and PrOSV effectively address key limitations of current fine-tuned SVs, offering better steering effectiveness and generation quality without per-SV tuning.

Abstract: Recently, steering vectors (SVs) have emerged as an effective and lightweight approach to steer behaviors of large language models (LLMs), among which fine-tuned SVs are more effective than optimization-free ones. However, current approaches to fine-tuned SVs suffer from two limitations. First, they require careful selection of steering factors on a per-SV basis to balance steering effectiveness and generation quality at inference time. Second, they operate as full-sequence SVs (FSSVs), which can sacrifice generation quality regardless of factor selection due to excessive intervention on the model generation process. To address the first limitation, we propose joint training of steering factors and directions, such that post-hoc factor selection is no longer required. Using neural network scaling theory, we find that moderately large initialization sizes and learning rates for steering factors are essential for stability and efficiency of joint training. To tackle the second limitation, we draw inspiration from representation fine-tuning and introduce Prompt-only SV (PrOSV), an SV that intervenes only on a few prompt tokens. Our empirical results show that PrOSV outperforms traditional FSSVs on AxBench when using our joint training scheme. We also find that PrOSV achieves a better tradeoff between general model utility and adversarial robustness than FSSV.

</details>


### [222] [DiBA: Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation for Neural Network Weight Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05994)
*Nobutaka Ono*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiBA introduces a compact matrix factorization using diagonal and binary matrices for neural network weight compression, achieving significant accuracy improvements while reducing computational complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To compress dense matrix weights (linear layers, convolutions, attention projections, embeddings) in neural networks while maintaining accuracy and enabling efficient computation.

Method: DiBA approximates weight matrices as product of diagonal and binary matrices, reducing FLOPs from mn to m+k+n. Uses DiBA-Greedy solver with closed-form least-squares for diagonal factors and one-bit improvement tests for binary factors. DiBARD retunes only diagonal factors on downstream data.

Result: On 40 pretrained matrices, DiBA-Greedy shows consistent SNR improvements. DiBARD improves DistilBERT accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Audio Spectrogram Transformer from 0.7684 to 0.9781.

Conclusion: DiBA provides effective neural network weight compression via diagonal-binary factorization, with strong empirical results across NLP and audio tasks, enabling efficient deployment without full model retraining.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose DiBA (Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation), a compact matrix factorization for neural network weight compression. Many components of modern networks, including linear layers, $1\times1$ convolutions, attention projections, and embedding layers, have dense matrix weights. DiBA approximates $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ by $\widehat A=D_1B_1D_2B_2D_3$, where $D_1,D_2,D_3$ are diagonal matrices and $B_1,B_2$ are $0/1$ binary matrices. The intermediate dimension $k$ controls the trade-off between theoretical storage and approximation accuracy. For matrix-vector products, DiBA decomposes dense multiplication into three element-wise scaling operations and two binary mixing operations, reducing the floating-point multiplication count from $mn$ to $m+k+n$. For optimization, we introduce DiBA-Greedy, an alternating solver that combines closed-form least-squares updates for the diagonal factors with exact one-bit improvement tests for the binary factors. We also introduce DiBARD (DiBA with Retuning only Diagonal factors), which replaces dense-matrix layers by DiBA factors, freezes the binary matrices, and retunes only the diagonal entries on downstream data. This preserves compact binary mixing without discrete search during adaptation. On 40 dense weight matrices extracted from public pretrained models, DiBA-Greedy yields consistent SNR improvements as the theoretical storage ratio increases. After DiBA replacement in two component-replacement studies, DiBARD improves DistilBERT/WikiText masked-token accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Speech Commands test accuracy for an Audio Spectrogram Transformer from 0.7684 to 0.9781 without reoptimizing the binary factors.

</details>


### [223] [A Fine-Grained Understanding of Uniform Convergence for Halfspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06004)
*Aryeh Kontorovich,Kasper Green Larsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes uniform convergence of halfspaces, showing that inhomogeneous halfspaces in ℝ^d (d≥2) have tight worst-case VC bounds, while homogeneous halfspaces in ℝ^2 achieve significantly better rates (O(1/n) in realizable case, log-free bandwise bounds with only ln ln n overhead in agnostic case), revealing sharp dimensional and structural thresholds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize the fine-grained uniform convergence behavior of halfspaces beyond worst-case VC bounds, identifying which structural conditions (homogeneity, dimension) lead to improved generalization guarantees.

Method: The authors use critical-wedge localization arguments to analyze homogeneous halfspaces in ℝ^2, deriving bandwise, log-free deviation bounds. For inhomogeneous halfspaces, they prove matching lower bounds showing standard VC bounds are essentially tight.

Result: Inhomogeneous halfspaces in ℝ^d (d≥2) have tight Θ(d ln(n/d)/n) bounds in realizable case and √(τ ln(1/τ)) deviation in agnostic case. Homogeneous halfspaces in ℝ^2 achieve O(1/n) error in realizable case and log-free bandwise bounds with ln ln n overhead in agnostic case, with matching lower bounds.

Conclusion: The paper provides a nearly complete fine-grained picture of uniform convergence for halfspaces, revealing that homogeneity and low dimensionality are key structural factors enabling significantly better generalization than worst-case VC bounds suggest.

Abstract: We study the fine-grained uniform convergence behavior of halfspaces beyond worst-case VC bounds. For inhomogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with $d\ge 2$, we show that standard first-order VC bounds are essentially tight: even consistent hypotheses can incur population error $Θ(d\ln(n/d)/n)$, and in the agnostic setting the deviation scales as $\sqrt{τ\ln(1/τ)}$ at true error $τ$. In contrast, homogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^2$ exhibit a markedly different behavior. In the realizable case, every hypothesis consistent with the sample has error $O(1/n)$. In the agnostic case, we prove a bandwise, log-free deviation bound on each dyadic risk band via a critical-wedge localization argument. Unioning over bands incurs only a $\ln\ln n$ overhead, and we establish a matching lower bound showing this overhead is unavoidable. Together, these results give a fine-grained and nearly complete picture of uniform convergence for halfspaces, revealing sharp dimensional and structural thresholds.

</details>


### [224] [Quantizing With Randomized Hadamard Transforms: Efficient Heuristic Now Proven](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06014)
*Ran Ben-Basat,William Kuszmaul,Michael Mitzenmacher,Amit Portnoy,Shay Vargaftik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that composing multiple randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs) can asymptotically match the performance of uniform random rotations (URRs) for various compression and quantization tasks, with two RHTs sufficient for marginal Gaussianity and three RHTs for vector quantization, while also proposing a linear-time adaptive check for practical inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform random rotations (URRs) are widely used in quantization and compression but are computationally expensive. Randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs) offer fast alternatives but fail for worst-case inputs. The paper aims to bridge this gap by determining how many RHTs are needed to match URR performance.

Method: The authors theoretically analyze the marginal distribution of coordinates after composing two RHTs, proving convergence to Gaussian in Kolmogorov and 1-Wasserstein distance. They extend analysis to three RHTs for vector quantization, showing decaying coordinate covariance. They also propose an O(d) runtime check on input moments to dynamically adapt the number of RHTs.

Result: Two RHTs achieve marginal distributions within O(d^{-1/2}) of Gaussian, asymptotically matching URRs for DRIVE and QUIC-FL schemes. Three RHTs ensure decaying covariance, making VQ codebooks optimized for URRs have nearly the same expected error. The adaptive check improves practical performance.

Conclusion: Composing multiple RHTs provides a computationally efficient alternative to URRs that asymptotically matches their performance, with the number of required RHTs depending on the application (2 for marginal tasks, 3 for vector quantization).

Abstract: Uniform random rotations (URRs) are a common preprocessing step in modern quantization approaches used for gradient compression, inference acceleration, KV-cache compression, model weight quantization, and approximate nearest-neighbor search in vector databases. In practice, URRs are often replaced by randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs), which preserve orthogonality while admitting fast implementations. The remaining issue is the performance for worst-case inputs. With a URR, each coordinate is individually distributed as a shifted beta distribution, which converges to a Gaussian distribution in high dimensions. Generally, one RHT is not suitable in the worst case, as individual coordinates can be far from these distributions. We show that after composing two RHTs on any $d$-sized input vector, the marginal distribution of every fixed coordinate of the normalized rotated vector is within $O(d^{-1/2})$ of a standard Gaussian both in Kolmogorov distance and in $1$-Wasserstein distance. We then plug these bounds into the analyses of modern compression schemes, namely DRIVE and QUIC-FL, and show that two RHTs achieve performance that asymptotically matches URRs.
  However, we show that two RHTs may not be sufficient for Vector Quantization (VQ), which often requires weak correlation across fixed-size blocks of coordinates (as opposed to only marginal distribution convergence for single coordinates). We prove that a composition of three RHTs leads to decaying coordinate covariance. This ensures that any fixed, bounded, multi-dimensional VQ codebook optimized for URRs has the same expected error when using three RHTs, up to an additive term that vanishes with the dimension.
  Finally, because practical inputs are rarely adversarial, we propose a linear-time ${O}(d)$ check on the input's moments to dynamically adapt the number of RHTs used at runtime to improve performance.

</details>


### [225] [Matrix-Decoupled Concentration for Autoregressive Sequences: Dimension-Free Guarantees for Sparse Long-Context Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06017)
*Pei-Sen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) framework that establishes a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences, resolving both scalar collapse and causal filtration bottlenecks in autoregressive LLMs, and achieving a dimension-free O(1) variance proxy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing concentration bounds for autoregressive LLMs suffer from two bottlenecks: (i) classical inequalities cause scalar collapse with suboptimal O(N) variance proxy for sparse rewards; (ii) spatial methods lack the causal filtration needed for sequential generation.

Method: The paper proposes a Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) framework based on a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences, governed by matrix-vector multiplication of the causal dependency resolvent and the target sensitivity vector, preserving coordinate-wise sparsity within a strictly causal framework.

Result: MDC recovers optimal constants for Markov chains, yields order-optimal bounds for causal trees via d-separation, and guarantees a dimension-free O(1) variance proxy, preventing scalar collapse.

Conclusion: The framework provides rigorous mathematical justification for the stability of long-context reasoning in autoregressive LLMs.

Abstract: Sequence-level evaluations in autoregressive Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on highly dependent token generation. Establishing tight concentration bounds for these processes remains a challenge due to two fundamental bottlenecks in existing frameworks: (i) classical inequalities typically separate dependency structures from target sensitivities, leading to a scalar collapse that inflates the variance proxy to a suboptimal $\mathcal{O}(N)$ for sparse terminal rewards; (ii) conversely, while certain spatial methods achieve tighter bounds, they lack the strictly causal filtration required by sequential generation, rendering them inapplicable to the autoregressive setting. To resolve both bottlenecks, we establish a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences, governed strictly by the exact matrix-vector multiplication of the causal dependency resolvent and the target sensitivity vector. This Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) framework natively recovers optimal constants for Markov chains and exploits directed $d$-separation to yield order-optimal bounds for causal trees. Crucially, by exactly preserving the coordinate-wise sparsity of rewards within a strictly causal framework, MDC mathematically prevents scalar collapse, guaranteeing a dimension-free $\mathcal{O}(1)$ variance proxy and providing a rigorous mathematical justification for the stability of long-context reasoning.

</details>


### [226] [Multi-agent decision making: A Blackwell's informativeness approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06028)
*Zheng Zhang,Cuong C. Nguyen,Kevin Wells,Gustavo Carneiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper applies Blackwell's informativeness framework to analyze multi-LLM decision-making, showing voting and debate are no more informative than pooled private information, and proposes a product-of-posteriors method that outperforms existing approaches on QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-LLM aggregation methods (voting, debate) are ad-hoc and lack formal guarantees on decision informativeness, motivating a principled theoretical analysis.

Method: Uses Blackwell's informativeness framework to analyze voting/debate as information structures; proposes a practical product-of-posteriors estimator to approximate Bayesian pooled posterior maximization.

Result: The proposed product-of-posteriors method outperforms state-of-the-art multi-LLM debate and voting methods across six QA benchmarks.

Conclusion: Bayesian pooled posterior maximization is an information-theoretic upper bound under Blackwell ordering, and the proposed method offers a practical approximation with superior empirical performance.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has motivated research on decision-making in multi-agent systems, where multiple agents collaborate to achieve shared objectives. Existing aggregation approaches, such as voting and debate, are largely ad-hoc and lack formal guarantees regarding the informativeness of the resulting decisions. In this paper, we provide a principled approach to analyse decisions made in the multi-LLM setting using Blackwell's informativeness framework. Within the Blackwell information-structure abstraction, we show that voting and debate induce information structures that are no more informative than the pooled private information of all agents. This result identifies Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation as an information-theoretic upper-bound decision rule under the Blackwell ordering. Motivated by this theoretical analysis, we introduce a practical method for LLM-based question-answering (QA) tasks that estimates each agent's posterior and approximates the pooled posterior using a product-of-posteriors estimator. Extensive experiments on six QA benchmarks demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art multi-LLM debate and voting methods.

</details>


### [227] [Does Synthetic Data Help? Empirical Evidence from Deep Learning Time Series Forecasters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06032)
*Hugo Cazaux,Eyjólfur Ingi Ásgeirsson,Hlynur Stefánsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A large-scale empirical study (4,218 runs) showing synthetic data augmentation for time series is sharply architecture-conditional: helps channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) but hurts channel-independent ones (DLinear, PatchTST). Only Seasonal-Trend generators are reliably beneficial, and hard curriculum switching is harmful. Provides actionable guidelines for practitioners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic data has transformed language model training, but its role in time series forecasting is poorly understood. The authors aim to systematically investigate when and how synthetic augmentation helps or hurts time series forecasting.

Method: Large-scale empirical study with nine experiment groups, 4,218 runs across five architectures (TimesNet, iTransformer, DLinear, PatchTST, etc.), four synthetic signals, and seven datasets. Evaluates architecture-conditional effects, synthetic signal types, and curriculum learning strategies.

Result: (1) Synthetic augmentation helps channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) but degrades channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST). (2) Averaged across all architectures, augmentation hurts in 67% of trials. (3) TimesNet with synthetic augmentation on just 10% of Weather data surpasses the full-data baseline. (4) Only Seasonal-Trend generator is reliably beneficial. (5) Hard curriculum switching causes +24% MSE degradation.

Conclusion: Provides actionable guidelines: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures, use gradual annealing schedules, and treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent.

Abstract: Synthetic data has transformed language model training, yet its role in time series forecasting remains poorly understood. We present a large-scale empirical study: nine experiment groups, 4,218 runs systematically evaluating synthetic time series augmentation across five architectures, four synthetic signals and seven datasets. The effect is sharply architecture-conditional: channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) benefit in the majority of trials, while channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are consistently degraded. In selected low-resource settings the gains are striking: TimesNet trained on only 10\% of Weather data with synthetic augmentation surpasses the full-data baseline (4 of 16 sparsity-dataset combinations). Averaged across all architectures, augmentation hurts in 67\% of trials. We further find that only the Seasonal-Trend generator reliably helps across the tested benchmarks, and that hard curriculum switching is actively harmful (+24\% MSE degradation). These results provide concrete, actionable guidelines on how to use synthetic data: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures, use gradual annealing schedules, and treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/hugoiscracked/synthetic-ts/tree/main}

</details>


### [228] [Optimal Transport for LLM Reward Modeling from Noisy Preference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06036)
*Licheng Pan,Haochen Yang,Haoxuan Li,Yunsheng Lu,Yongqi Tong,Yinuo Wang,Shijian Wang,Zhixuan Chu,Lei Shen,Yuan Lu,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SelectiveRM uses optimal transport with mass relaxation to denoise preference data in RLHF, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world preference datasets are corrupted by noisy labels. Existing denoising methods rely on homogeneous noise assumptions that fail to capture complex linguistic preference errors.

Method: Proposes SelectiveRM with (1) Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align model predictions with preference distribution via optimal transport, and (2) Mass Relaxation mechanism using partial transport to exclude noisy samples that contradict semantic consistency.

Result: Theoretically proves SelectiveRM optimizes a tighter upper bound on unobserved clean risk. Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

Conclusion: Optimal transport with mass relaxation provides an effective framework for handling heterogeneous noise in preference data for RLHF.

Abstract: Reward models are fundamental to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), yet real-world datasets are inevitably corrupted by noisy preference. Conventional training objectives tend to overfit these errors, while existing denoising approaches often rely on homogeneous noise assumptions that fail to capture the complexity of linguistic preferences. To handle these challenges, we propose SelectiveRM, a framework grounded in optimal transport. We first devise a Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align the distribution of model predictions with preference data. Furthermore, to address the limitation of strict mass conservation which compels the model to fit outliers, we incorporate a Mass Relaxation mechanism via partial transport. This enables the autonomous exclusion of samples with noisy preference that contradict semantic consistency. Theoretically, we demonstrate that SelectiveRM optimizes a tighter upper bound on the unobserved clean risk. Extensive experiments validate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

</details>


### [229] [Requests of a Feather Must Flock Together: Batch Size vs. Prefix Homogeneity in LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06046)
*Saksham Rathi,Preeti,Mythili Vutukuru*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feather is a prefix-aware scheduler using reinforcement learning to optimally balance batch size and prefix homogeneity, achieving 2-10x higher throughput by reducing KV cache accesses in LLM decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auto-regressive LLM decoding is memory-bound due to KV cache attention. Prior work focuses on maximizing batch size, but overlooks that smaller, prefix-homogeneous batches can outperform larger heterogeneous ones due to better cache locality. Existing schedulers also incur high CPU overhead from radix-tree prefix detection.

Method: (1) Reinforcement learning-based scheduler (Feather) that learns optimal tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity. (2) Chunked Hash Tree (CHT), a lightweight data structure for fast prefix detection and efficient request selection, replacing expensive radix-tree traversals.

Result: 2-10x higher end-to-end throughput compared to existing schedulers in vLLM and SGLang; no worse than status quo when prefix sharing is limited; reduces total KV cache accesses.

Conclusion: Prioritizing prefix homogeneity over pure batch size maximization with learned scheduling decisions significantly improves decode throughput, and CHT enables efficient prefix detection without CPU overhead.

Abstract: Auto-regressive token generation in large language models is memory-bound because it requires "attending to" key and value tensors (KV cache) of all previous tokens. Prior work aims to improve the efficiency of this decode process by batching multiple requests together, and maximizing batch size subject to GPU memory constraints. The key observation of our work is that with prefix-sharing workloads, smaller, prefix-homogeneous batches -- where all requests share a common prefix -- can achieve higher decode throughput than larger, heterogeneous batches, due to better spatial and temporal locality during KV cache accesses. However, prefix-aware schedulers in state-of-the-art inference engines maximize prefix reuse within a batch only to reduce KV cache memory footprint, but do not stop batch formation at smaller homogeneous batches that could have performed better. Further, we show that shared prefix detection in existing schedulers relies on radix-tree traversals, incurring substantial CPU overhead that is often comparable to GPU execution time. This paper presents Feather, a prefix-aware scheduler that uses reinforcement learning (RL) to learn the optimal tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity. We also introduce Chunked Hash Tree (CHT), a lightweight data structure that enables fast prefix detection and efficient request selection for the RL scheduler, avoiding expensive tree traversals. We integrate Feather into vLLM and SGLang, and our evaluation shows that Feather achieves 2--10$\times$ higher end-to-end throughput as compared to existing schedulers, while doing no worse than the status quo when the workload does not have enough prefix sharing. Feather achieves these gains by reducing the total number of KV cache accesses, surpassing the performance of prefix-aware attention kernels that have the same goal.

</details>


### [230] [TFM-Retouche: A Lightweight Input-Space Adapter for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06047)
*Duong Nguyen,Mohammed Jawhar,Nicolas Chesneau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TFM-Retouche is a lightweight, architecture-agnostic input-space residual adapter that improves frozen Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs) by learning small input corrections, with a post-training identity guard for fallback. TabICLv2-Retouche achieves top leaderboard performance on TabArena-Lite with +56 Elo over the frozen base.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TFMs have fixed inductive biases at inference time; adapting them via full fine-tuning is expensive, and PEFT methods like LoRA must be tailored to each TFM's architecture. Evidence on whether weight-space fine-tuning improves accuracy or calibration is mixed.

Method: TFM-Retouche learns a small residual correction in input space, architecture-agnostic w.r.t. the frozen TFM backbone. It is trained end-to-end through the frozen TFM with a post-training identity guard that falls back to the unmodified TFM when adaptation does not help on held-out validation.

Result: On TabArena-Lite (51 datasets), TabICLv2-Retouche is the top-ranked method, lifting aggregate Elo by +56 over frozen TabICLv2, and sits on the Pareto front of predictive quality vs. training and inference time.

Conclusion: The proposed input-space residual adapter effectively and efficiently adapts frozen TFMs without architecture modifications, achieving superior predictive quality with light per-task tuning and ensembling.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models (TFMs), such as TabPFN-2.6, TabICLv2, ConTextTab, Mitra, LimiX, and TabDPT, achieve strong zero-shot performance through in-context learning, but their inductive biases remain fixed at inference time. Adapting a pretrained TFM to a specific dataset or task typically requires either full fine-tuning, which is computationally expensive, or parameter-efficient tuning methods (PEFT) such as LoRA, which must be tailored to the internal architecture of each TFM. Furthermore, the evidence on whether weight-space fine-tuning improves accuracy or calibration is mixed \citep{tanna_exploring_2026,rubachev_finetuning_2025}. We introduce TFM-Retouche, a lightweight input-space residual adapter that is architecture-agnostic by design with respect to the frozen TFM backbone. TFM-Retouche learns a small residual correction in the input space to align the input data with the inductive biases of the pretrained model. The adapter is trained end-to-end through the frozen TFM, with a post-training identity guard that falls back to the unmodified TFM whenever adaptation does not help on held-out validation. On TabArena-Lite (51 datasets spanning binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression), TabICLv2-Retouche -- the framework instantiated on TabICLv2 -- is the top-ranked method on the leaderboard with light per-task tuning and ensembling, lifting aggregate Elo by +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base and sitting on the Pareto front of predictive quality versus both training and inference time.

</details>


### [231] [When Brain Networks Travel: Learning Beyond Site](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06050)
*Yingxu Wang,Kunyu Zhang,Yanwu Yang,Thomas Wolfers,Yujie Wu,Siyang Gao,Nan Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CORE is a unified framework for cross-site brain network learning that handles out-of-distribution fMRI data by decoupling site confounders, modeling transient dynamics, and using prior-guided subject-adaptive gating, outperforming baselines by up to 6.7% across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based fMRI methods fail under cross-site out-of-distribution settings due to site-conditioned confounders creating non-pathological shortcuts, and temporal averaging of functional connectivity obscuring transient neurodynamics, limiting generalization to unseen sites.

Method: CORE first performs site-aware confounder decoupling to remove site bias and extract cross-site population scaffold of diagnostic edges. Then profiles transient pathway dynamics using lightweight temporal descriptors and organizes scaffold edges into a line graph. Finally, uses prior-guided subject-adaptive gating combining population priors with subject-specific variability.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines with up to 6.7% relative gain under leave-one-site-out evaluation on ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, and ABCD datasets. Remains robust across different brain parcellation schemes.

Conclusion: CORE effectively addresses cross-site generalization in brain network analysis by modeling both population-level structure and subject-specific dynamics, demonstrating strong robustness to site variations and atlas differences.

Abstract: Graph-based learning on functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has shown strong potential for brain network analysis. However, existing methods degrade under cross-site out-of-distribution (OOD) settings because site-conditioned confounders induce non-pathological shortcuts, while functional connectivity constructed by temporal averaging obscures transient neurodynamics, limiting generalization to unseen sites. In this paper, we propose Cross-site OOD Robust brain nEtwork (CORE), a unified framework for brain network learning across unseen sites. CORE first performs site-aware confounder decoupling to mitigate site-conditioned bias and extract a cross-site population scaffold of reproducible diagnostic connectivity edges. It then profiles transient pathway dynamics over this scaffold using lightweight temporal descriptors and organizes scaffold edges into a line graph for transferable pathway-level modeling. Finally, CORE introduces a prior-guided subject-adaptive gating mechanism that leverages scaffold-derived population priors while preserving subject-specific connectivity variability. Extensive experiments under leave-one-site-out evaluation on real-world datasets (ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, and ABCD) show that CORE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with up to 6.7% relative gain. Furthermore, CORE remains robust to atlas variations, maintaining performance gains across different brain parcellation schemes.

</details>


### [232] [Towards Generation-Efficient Uncertainty Estimation in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06053)
*Mingcheng Zhu,Yu Liu,Tingting Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates low-cost uncertainty estimation for LLMs, showing that partial generations or even input-only information can effectively predict output uncertainty, challenging the need for full autoregressive generations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty estimation is critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains (healthcare, finance) where hallucinations can be fluent but factually incorrect. Existing methods require full autoregressive generations, incurring high inference costs and delayed assessment.

Method: The authors develop a unified framework formulating uncertainty estimation as an early estimation problem over autoregressive generation. They propose two low-cost methods: (1) Logit Magnitude, which uses top-M logit evidence from early-stopped generation prefixes, and (2) MetaUE, which distills generation-based uncertainty into a lightweight input-only estimator.

Result: Logit Magnitude achieves strong performance using partial generations. MetaUE provides competitive input-only approximation in several settings. Both methods demonstrate that effective uncertainty estimation requires less generation than commonly assumed.

Conclusion: Uncertainty estimation can be effectively performed with partial generation or even without generation, enabling earlier identification of unreliable responses and reducing inference costs.

Abstract: Uncertainty estimation is important for deploying LLMs in high-stakes applications such as healthcare and finance, where hallucinations can appear fluent and plausible while being factually incorrect, making it difficult for users to judge whether an output should be trusted. Existing methods require one or more full autoregressive generations to estimate uncertainty, which introduces substantial inference cost and often delays uncertainty assessment. In this paper, we investigate whether effective uncertainty estimation can be achieved with partial generation or even input-only information. Specifically, we first develop a unified framework that formulates uncertainty estimation as an early estimation problem over the autoregressive generation process of LLMs. This framework organises existing and proposed estimators by the information they observe, ranging from multi-generation to input-only prediction, and clarifies the performance-cost trade-off underlying different uncertainty estimation methods. Building on this view, we study two largely underexplored low-cost settings: estimating uncertainty with part of the generation, and predicting uncertainty from the input prompt. We propose Logit Magnitude, which uses top-M logit evidence to estimate uncertainty from an early-stopped generation prefix, and MetaUE, which distils generation-based uncertainty into a lightweight input-only estimator trained with uncertainty scores. Extensive experiments on general and domain-specific benchmarks show that Logit Magnitude achieves strong performance, and partial generations of LLMs are often sufficient for effective uncertainty estimation. MetaUE further provides a competitive input-only approximation in several settings. These findings suggest that effective uncertainty estimation requires less generation than commonly assumed, enabling unreliable responses to be identified earlier.

</details>


### [233] [Towards Self-Explainable Document Visual Question Answering with Chain-of-Explanation Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06058)
*Kjetil Indrehus,Adrian Duric,Changkyu Choi,Ali Ramezani-Kebrya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoExVQA is a self-explainable DocVQA framework that uses a chain-of-explanation design to first identify relevant evidence, localize answer regions, then decode answers from grounded regions, achieving state-of-the-art explainable performance with 12% ANLS improvement on PFL-DocVQA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DocVQA models entangle question-relevant evidence with answer localization and operate as black boxes, offering limited means to verify how predictions depend on visual evidence.

Method: A chain-of-explanation design that (1) identifies question-relevant evidence, (2) explicitly localizes the answer region, and (3) decodes the answer exclusively from the grounded region.

Result: Achieves SotA explainable DocVQA performance on PFL-DocVQA, improving ANLS by 12% over current explainable baselines.

Conclusion: Restricting decoding to grounded evidence enables transparent and verifiable predictions while maintaining strong performance.

Abstract: Document Visual Question Answering (DocVQA) requires vision-language models to reason not only about what information in a document is relevant to a question, but also where the answer is grounded on the page. Existing DocVQA models entangle question-relevant evidence and answer localization and operate largely as black boxes, offering limited means to verify how predictions depend on visual evidence. We propose CoExVQA, a self-explainable DocVQA framework with a grounded reasoning process through a chain-of-explanation design. CoExVQA first identifies question-relevant evidence, then explicitly localizes the answer region, and finally decodes the answer exclusively from the grounded region. Prediction via CoExVQA's chain-of-explanation enables direct inspection and verification of the reasoning process across modalities. Empirical results show that restricting decoding to grounded evidence achieves SotA explainable DocVQA performance on PFL-DocVQA, improving ANLS by 12% over the current explainable baselines while providing transparent and verifiable predictions.

</details>


### [234] [Geometry-Aware Simplicial Message Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06061)
*Elena Xinyi Wang,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Geometric Simplicial Weisfeiler-Lehman (GSWL) test, which extends the WL test by incorporating vertex coordinates to capture geometric information, and shows it bounds the expressivity of geometry-aware simplicial message passing networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing WL and SWL tests are blind to geometry—they cannot distinguish meshes with identical connectivity but different embeddings. The authors aim to develop a geometric version of the WL test to characterize geometry-aware message passing networks.

Method: The authors propose GSWL, a color refinement scheme that incorporates vertex coordinates into simplicial complexes. They theoretically prove that (i) GSWL upper-bounds geometry-aware simplicial message passing expressivity, and (ii) there exist parameters where GSWL's discriminating power is matched by such schemes on finite families. They combine GSWL with the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT) for a complete invariant and approximation framework.

Result: The theory establishes a hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models, validated through experiments on synthetic and mesh datasets.

Conclusion: GSWL provides a geometric expressivity characterization for geometry-aware simplicial message passing networks, with the ECT combination offering a complete invariant and practical approximation framework.

Abstract: The Weisfeiler--Lehman (WL) test and its simplicial extension (SWL) characterize the combinatorial expressivity of message passing networks, but they are blind to geometry, i.e., meshes with identical connectivity but different embeddings are indistinguishable. We introduce the Geometric Simplicial Weisfeiler--Lehman (GSWL) test, which incorporates vertex coordinates into color refinement for geometric simplicial complexes. In addition, we show that (i) the expressivity of geometry-aware simplicial message passing schemes is bounded above by GSWL, and (ii) that there exist parameters such that the discriminating power of GSWL is matched by these schemes on any fixed finite family of geometric simplicial complexes. Combined with the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT), a complete invariant for geometric simplicial complexes, this yields a geometric expressivity characterization together with an approximation framework. Experiments on synthetic and mesh datasets serve to validate our theory, showing a clear hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models.

</details>


### [235] [Causal Reinforcement Learning for Complex Card Games: A Magic The Gathering Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06066)
*Cristiano da Costa Cunha,Ajmal Mian,Tim French,Wei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MTG-Causal-RL is a new benchmark for causal reinforcement learning built on Magic: The Gathering, featuring high-dimensional partial observations, masked actions, an explicit Structural Causal Model, and evaluation protocols for causal credit assignment, cross-archetype transfer, and policy auditability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal RL lacks benchmarks that simultaneously combine sequential decision-making, hidden information, large masked action spaces, and explicit causal structure. Existing benchmarks cannot pose questions about causal credit assignment under action masking, structural transfer, or SCM-grounded policy auditability together.

Method: The paper builds MTG-Causal-RL on Magic: The Gathering (3,077-dim observation, 478-action masked space, 5 archetypes, 3 reward schemes, hand-specified SCM). They propose CGFA-PPO, a causal agent using SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets with an intervention-calibration loss. Evaluation uses paired seeds, bootstrap confidence intervals, and Holm-Bonferroni correction.

Result: Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO reach competitive in-distribution win rates exceeding random baseline. Per-factor calibration trajectories and leave-one-out transfer gaps reveal diagnostic structure beyond scalar win rate. The benchmark enables measuring causal credit assignment, structural transfer, and SCM-grounded policy auditability.

Conclusion: MTG-Causal-RL provides a strategically rich, partially observed domain with an explicit causal interface and rigorous statistical protocol, serving as a shared testbed for causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research.

Abstract: Causal reinforcement learning (RL) lacks benchmarks for complex systems that combine sequential decision making, hidden information, large masked action spaces, and explicit causal structure. We introduce MTG-Causal-RL, a Gymnasium benchmark built on Magic: The Gathering with a 3,077-dimensional partial observation, a 478-action masked discrete action space, five competitive Standard archetypes, three reward schemes, and a hand-specified Structural Causal Model (SCM) over strategic variables. Every episode exposes causal variables, SCM-predicted intervention effects, and per-factor credit traces, making causal credit assignment, leave-one-out cross-archetype transfer, and policy auditability first-class metrics. We adapt a panel of reference baselines: random, heuristic, masked PPO, a causal-world-model PPO variant, and an architecture-matched scalar control. We propose Causal Graph-Factored Advantage PPO (CGFA-PPO) as a reference causal agent that uses SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets with an intervention-calibration loss. All comparisons use paired seeds, paired-bootstrap confidence intervals, and Holm-Bonferroni correction within pre-registered families. Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO reach competitive in-distribution win rates and exceed the random baseline; per-factor calibration trajectories and leave-one-out transfer gaps expose diagnostic structure that scalar win rate alone cannot. We release the benchmark, reference-baseline results, and full evaluation protocol openly. By coupling a strategically rich, partially observed domain with an explicit causal interface and statistical protocol, MTG-Causal-RL gives causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research a shared testbed for questions current benchmarks cannot pose together: causal credit assignment under masked action spaces, structural transfer across archetypes, and SCM-grounded policy auditability.

</details>


### [236] [Normalized Architectures are Natively 4-Bit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06067)
*Maxim Fishman,Brian Chmiel,Ron Banner,Daniel Soudry,Boris Ginsburg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: nGPT's hypersphere constraint makes it inherently robust to 4-bit (NVFP4) training, achieving stable low-precision training without extra interventions, validated on models up to 30B parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training large language models at 4-bit precision is critical for efficiency, but standard architectures suffer from quality degradation requiring complex interventions like random Hadamard transforms and per-tensor scaling.

Method: nGPT constrains weights and hidden representations to the unit hypersphere, which enhances weak positive correlations among element-wise dot products, leading to constructive signal accumulation across the hidden dimension while noise averages out, yielding higher effective SNR.

Result: Validated on a 1.2B dense model and hybrid MoE models up to 3B/30B parameters; shows stable end-to-end NVFP4 training without quality-preserving interventions; robustness strengthens as hidden dimension grows.

Conclusion: nGPT's normalized architecture enables efficient 4-bit LLM training with increasing advantages at scale, removing the need for complex low-precision training techniques.

Abstract: Training large language models at 4-bit precision is critical for efficiency. We show that nGPT, an architecture that constrains weights and hidden representations to the unit hypersphere, is inherently more robust to low-precision arithmetic. This removes the need for interventions-such as applying random Hadamard transforms and performing per-tensor scaling calculations-to preserve model quality, and it enables stable end-to-end NVFP4 training. We validate this approach on both a 1.2B dense model and hybrid (Mamba-Transformer) MoE models of up to 3B/30B parameters. We trace this robustness to the dot product: while quantization noise remains largely uncorrelated in both standard and normalized architectures, the signal behaves differently. In nGPT, the hypersphere constraint enhances weak positive correlations among the element-wise products, leading to a constructive accumulation of the signal across the hidden dimension while the noise continues to average out. This yields a higher effective signal-to-noise ratio and a flatter loss landscape, with the effect strengthening as the hidden dimension grows, suggesting increasing advantages at scale. A reference implementation is available at https://github.com/anonymous452026/ngpt-nvfp4

</details>


### [237] [PRISM: Iterative Cross-Modal Posterior Refinement for Dynamic Text-Attributed Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06073)
*Trimble Chang,Yihang Liu,Mingjing Han,Han Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRISM is an iterative cross-modal posterior refinement framework for Dynamic Text-Attributed Graphs that progressively transforms semantic priors into behavior-conditioned posteriors, outperforming existing methods on temporal link prediction and node retrieval tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DyTAG methods rely on rigid modality partitions and one-shot fusion strategies, failing to capture intrinsic and evolving dependencies between node semantics and interaction behaviors.

Method: PRISM organizes DyTAG information into semantic and behavioral modalities as an intrinsic alternative to carrier-level partitions, and learns a refinement trajectory that progressively transforms semantic priors into behavior-conditioned posterior states via iterative cross-modal interaction.

Result: PRISM achieves strong performance on temporal link prediction and destination node retrieval tasks on DTGB benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: Semantic-behavioral modeling and iterative posterior refinement are effective for DyTAG representation learning.

Abstract: Dynamic text-attributed graphs (DyTAGs) provide a powerful framework for modeling evolving systems in which node semantics and time-dependent interactions are tightly coupled. Recently, multimodal learning has emerged as a promising yet underexplored direction for enhancing DyTAG representation learning. However, existing methods typically rely on rigid modality partitions and one-shot fusion strategies, which limit their ability to capture the intrinsic and evolving dependencies between node semantics and interaction behaviors. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{PRISM}, an iterative cross-modal posterior refinement framework for DyTAG representation learning. PRISM organizes DyTAG information into semantic and behavioral modalities, providing a more intrinsic alternative to carrier-level modality partitions. Instead of fusing the two modalities in a single step, PRISM learns a refinement trajectory that progressively transforms semantic priors into behavior-conditioned posterior states through cross-modal interaction with behavioral evidence. Extensive experiments on DTGB benchmark datasets show that PRISM achieves strong performance on temporal link prediction and destination node retrieval tasks. Further ablation studies validate the effectiveness of semantic--behavioral modeling and iterative posterior refinement.

</details>


### [238] [Understanding diffusion models requires rethinking (again) generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06077)
*Pierre Marion,Yu-Han Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This position paper argues that understanding generalization in diffusion models requires new theoretical frameworks, as memorization and generalization are fundamentally incompatible in these models (unlike supervised learning). The authors call for shifting research focus from "why diffusion models don't memorize" to "what they actually learn before memorization occurs," supported by empirical studies on CIFAR-10.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing theoretical frameworks (classical statistical learning theory and benign overfitting) are insufficient to explain generalization in diffusion models. The authors highlight a unique tension: in diffusion models, full memorization prevents generalization (producing copies instead of novel samples), unlike supervised learning where memorization can coexist with generalization.

Method: The paper adopts a position/opinion approach, synthesizing existing theoretical explanations (capacity limitations, implicit regularization, architectural inductive biases) and conducting empirical studies on diffusion models trained on CIFAR-10 to distill findings into concrete open questions.

Result: Key findings include: (1) memorization and generalization are incompatible in diffusion models, (2) existing proposed explanations for generalization are disconnected and their interactions unclear, (3) empirical observations from CIFAR-10 experiments yield specific open questions about what diffusion models learn during the pre-memorization phase.

Conclusion: The field should pivot from explaining non-memorization toward understanding what diffusion models actually learn in the pre-memorization phase. The paper provides concrete open questions to guide future theoretical research on generalization in diffusion models.

Abstract: This position paper argues that understanding generalization in diffusion models requires fundamentally new theoretical frameworks that go beyond both classical statistical learning theory and the benign overfitting paradigm developed for supervised learning. In diffusion models, unlike in supervised learning, memorization of training data and generalization to novel samples are incompatible: a model that has fully memorized its training set generates copies rather than novel data. Several theoretical explanations for why practical diffusion models nevertheless generalize have been proposed, based on capacity limitations, implicit regularization from optimization, or architectural inductive biases, but their interactions remain unclear. We argue that the field should pivot from explaining why the diffusion models do not memorize to investigating what the model actually learns during pre-memorization phase. To highlight our stance, we conduct empirical study of diffusion models trained on CIFAR-10, and we distill the findings into concrete open questions that we believe are key to improve understanding of generalization in diffusion models.

</details>


### [239] [Fast Gauss-Newton for Multiclass Cross-Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06081)
*Mikalai Korbit,Mario Zanon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Fast Gauss-Newton (FGN), a decomposition of the multiclass Gauss-Newton matrix that drops the within-competitor covariance term, yielding a scalable approximation that is exact for binary classification and behaves well when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full multiclass GGN curvature couples all output logits through softmax covariance, making curvature-vector products hard to scale as the number of classes grows.

Method: Decompose multiclass GGN into true-vs-rest term and within-competitor covariance term; retain only the first (FGN). Use whitened row-space system solved by matrix-free conjugate gradient with Jacobian-vector products of a scalar margin map.

Result: FGN stays close to full GGN when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large; deviates as the dropped within-competitor covariance grows.

Conclusion: FGN is a practical, scalable under-approximation of multiclass GGN with exactness guarantees for binary case, enabling efficient second-order optimization for large-class problems.

Abstract: In multiclass softmax cross-entropy, the full generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) curvature couples all output logits through the softmax covariance, making curvature-vector products harder to scale as the number of classes grows. We show that the standard multiclass GGN can be decomposed exactly into a true-vs-rest term and a positive semidefinite within-competitor covariance term. Fast Gauss-Newton (FGN) retains the first term and drops the second, yielding a positive semidefinite under-approximation of the multiclass GGN that is exact for binary classification. The derivation uses an exact true-vs-rest scalar-margin representation of softmax cross-entropy: the loss and gradient are unchanged, and the approximation enters only at the curvature level. Exploiting the FGN curvature structure, the damped update can be written as an equivalent whitened row-space system with one row per mini-batch example. We solve this system matrix-free by conjugate gradient using Jacobian-vector and vector-Jacobian products of the scalar margin map. Targeted mechanism experiments and an evaluation on a fixed-feature multiclass head support the predictions from the decomposition: FGN stays closest to the full softmax GGN when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large, and deviates as the dropped within-competitor covariance grows.

</details>


### [240] [Beyond Autoregressive RTG: Conditioning via Injection Outside Sequential Modeling in Decision Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06104)
*Yongyi Wang,Hanyu Liu,Lingfeng Li,Bozhou Chen,Ang Li,Qirui Zheng,Xionghui Yang,Chucai Wang,Wenxin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SlimDT improves Decision Transformer by removing Return-to-Go tokens from the sequence and injecting RTG information into state representations, reducing sequence length by one-third and improving both efficiency and performance on D4RL benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RTG is a scalar containing far less information than state/action vectors but consumes the same computational budget per token, and its inclusion as a separate token increases quadratic self-attention cost unnecessarily.

Method: SlimDT removes RTG from the autoregressive sequence and instead injects RTG information into state representations before sequential modeling, allowing the Transformer to process only (state, action) sequences.

Result: Surpasses standard DT across various D4RL tasks and achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods, while reducing sequence length by one-third for improved inference efficiency.

Conclusion: Decoupling a sparse conditioning signal from an information-rich sequence yields both computational gains and higher task performance.

Abstract: Decision Transformer (DT) formulates offline reinforcement learning as autoregressive sequence modeling, achieving promising results by predicting actions from a sequence of Return-to-Go (RTG), state, and action tokens. However, RTG is a scalar that summarizes future rewards, containing far less information than typical state or action vectors, yet it consumes the same computational budget per token. Worse, the self-attention cost of Transformers grows quadratically with sequence length, so including RTG as a separate token adds unnecessary overhead. We propose SlimDT, which removes RTG from the autoregressive sequence. Instead, we inject RTG information into the state representations before the sequential modeling step, allowing the Transformer to process only a compact (state, action) sequence. This reduces the sequence length by one-third, directly improving inference efficiency. On the D4RL benchmark, SlimDT surpasses standard DT across various tasks and achieves performance comparable to existing state-of-the-art methods. Decoupling a sparse conditioning signal from an information-rich sequence thus yields both computational gains and higher task performance.

</details>
