Motivation: Standard TopK SAEs use a fixed sparsity level (K) across all inputs, ignoring that natural data has varying local intrinsic dimensionality. Simple inputs may need fewer features, complex ones more — fixed K introduces noise or misses structure.

Method: SoftSAE uses a differentiable Soft Top-K operator to learn an input-dependent sparsity level, dynamically adjusting the number of active features based on each input's complexity.

Result: SoftSAE finds meaningful features and selects the right number of features for each concept, better matching data structure than fixed-K alternatives.

Conclusion: Dynamic, input-dependent sparsity is more effective than fixed sparsity for representing real-world data with varying complexity.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have become an important tool in mechanistic interpretability, helping to analyze internal representations in both Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs). By decomposing polysemantic activations into sparse sets of monosemantic features, SAEs aim to translate neural network computations into human-understandable concepts. However, common architectures such as TopK SAEs rely on a fixed sparsity level. They enforce the same number of active features (K) across all inputs, ignoring the varying complexity of real-world data. Natural data often lies on manifolds with varying local intrinsic dimensionality, meaning the number of relevant factors can change significantly across samples. This suggests that a fixed sparsity level is not optimal. Simple inputs may require only a few features, while more complex ones need more expressive representations. Using a constant K can therefore introduce noise in simple cases or miss important structure in more complex ones. To address this issue, we propose SoftSAE, a sparse autoencoder with a Dynamic Top-K selection mechanism. Our method uses a differentiable Soft Top-K operator to learn an input-dependent sparsity level k. This allows the model to adjust the number of active features based on the complexity of each input. As a result, the representation better matches the structure of the data, and the explanation length reflects the amount of information in the input. Experimental results confirm that SoftSAE not only finds meaningful features, but also selects the right number of features for each concept. The source code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SoftSAE-8F71/.

</details>


### [268] [Cumulative-Goodness Free-Riding in Forward-Forward Networks: Real, Repairable, but Not Accuracy-Dominant](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06240)
*Amirhossein Yousefiramandi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies "layer free-riding" in Forward-Forward training, where later layers fail to learn due to exponential gradient decay, proposes three local remedies, and shows that while these fixes improve layer separation dramatically (4x-45x in deeper layers), they have minimal impact on final accuracy—indicating that free-riding is a real but not dominant limitation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize and characterize the layer free-riding problem in cumulative-goodness Forward-Forward training, where later layers inherit partially separated tasks from earlier layers, leading to insufficient learning in deeper blocks.

Method: Formal analysis of exponential gradient decay with softplus FF criterion; three local remedies: per-block, hardness-gated, and depth-scaled training modifications; experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny ImageNet.

Result: Layer-separation statistics improved by 4x-45x in deeper layers, but final accuracy changes by less than 1 percentage point for non-degenerate cases. Architecture and augmentation choices affect accuracy more than training rule modifications.

Conclusion: Cumulative free-riding is a real and repairable pathology in FF training, but it is not the dominant factor limiting accuracy—other factors like architecture and augmentation matter more.

Abstract: Forward-Forward (FF) training allows each layer to learn from a local goodness criterion. In cumulative-goodness variants, however, later layers can inherit a task that earlier layers have already partially separated. We formalize this phenomenon as layer free-riding: under the softplus FF criterion, the class-discrimination gradient reaching block $d$ decays exponentially with the positive margin accumulated by preceding blocks. We then study three local remedies -- per-block, hardness-gated, and depth-scaled -- that recover current-layer separation measures without relying on backpropagated gradients. On CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, these remedies dramatically improve layer-separation statistics, with $4\times$--$45\times$ gains in deeper layers, while changing accuracy by less than one percentage point for non-degenerate training procedures. Tiny ImageNet provides a tougher cross-dataset check for our selected block-wise configuration and reveals the same qualitative gap between layer-health diagnostics and final accuracy. Calibration experiments further show that architecture and augmentation choices have a larger effect on final accuracy than the training-rule modifications studied here. Cumulative free-riding is therefore a real and repairable optimization pathology. Nonetheless, for the FF training rules, architectures, and datasets we study, it is not the dominant factor limiting achievable accuracy.

</details>


### [269] [A Note on TurboQuant and the Earlier DRIVE/EDEN Line of Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18555)
*Ran Ben-Basat,Yaniv Ben-Itzhak,Gal Mendelson,Michael Mitzenmacher,Amit Portnoy,Shay Vargaftik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This note shows that TurboQuant is a suboptimal special case of the earlier EDEN quantization scheme, with biased EDEN outperforming TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ and unbiased EDEN significantly outperforming TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$ across all tested setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify and correct the relationship between TurboQuant and prior work (DRIVE/EDEN), showing that TurboQuant's contributions are not novel and its performance is suboptimal compared to existing methods.

Method: The authors theoretically analyze TurboQuant as a special case of EDEN with fixed scale S=1, identify three suboptimal design choices in TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$, and empirically compare all methods on the same benchmarks from the TurboQuant paper.

Result: Biased EDEN (optimized S) beats TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$; unbiased EDEN beats TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$ by >1 bit (e.g., 2-bit EDEN > 3-bit TurboQuant). EDEN outperforms TurboQuant in every experimental setup.

Conclusion: TurboQuant offers no advantages over EDEN and is consistently worse; researchers should use EDEN instead.

Abstract: This note clarifies the relationship between the recent TurboQuant work and the earlier DRIVE (NeurIPS 2021) and EDEN (ICML 2022) schemes. DRIVE is a 1-bit quantizer that EDEN extended to any $b>0$ bits per coordinate; we refer to them collectively as EDEN.
  First, TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ is a special case of EDEN obtained by fixing EDEN's scalar scale parameter to $S=1$. EDEN supports both biased and unbiased quantization, each optimized by a different $S$ (chosen via methods described in the EDEN works). The fixed choice $S=1$ used by TurboQuant is generally suboptimal, although the optimal $S$ for biased EDEN converges to $1$ as the dimension grows; accordingly TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ approaches EDEN's behavior for large $d$.
  Second, TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$ combines a biased $(b-1)$-bit EDEN step with an unbiased 1-bit QJL quantization of the residual. It is suboptimal in three ways: (1) its $(b-1)$-bit step uses the suboptimal $S=1$; (2) its 1-bit unbiased residual quantization has worse MSE than (unbiased) 1-bit EDEN; (3) chaining a biased $(b-1)$-bit step with a 1-bit unbiased residual step is inferior to unbiasedly quantizing the input directly with $b$-bit EDEN.
  Third, some of the analysis in the TurboQuant work mirrors that of the EDEN works: both exploit the connection between random rotations and the shifted Beta distribution, use the Lloyd-Max algorithm, and note that Randomized Hadamard Transforms can replace uniform random rotations.
  Experiments support these claims: biased EDEN (with optimized $S$) is more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$, and unbiased EDEN is markedly more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$, often by more than a bit (e.g., 2-bit EDEN beats 3-bit TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$). We also repeat all accuracy experiments from the TurboQuant paper, showing that EDEN outperforms it in every setup we have tried.

</details>


### [270] [The Role of Node Features in Graph Pooling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06250)
*Jan von Pichowski,Alžbeta Hrabošová,Ingo Scholtes,Christopher Blöcker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes why graph pooling often shows marginal gains over WL-1 expressive GNNs, finding that pooling requires node features well-aligned with graph topology. The authors formalize requirements for effective pooling and introduce a quantitative measure of feature quality, showing that when these conditions are met, pooling can improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the gap between the theoretical promise of graph pooling and its inconsistent empirical performance over standard WL-1 expressive GNNs, particularly examining how node features and graph topology interact to affect pooling objectives.

Method: The paper analyzes the interaction between node features and graph topology, formalizes fundamental requirements for node features to enable effective pooling, and introduces a quantitative measure of feature quality. Empirical evaluation validates these conditions on appropriate datasets.

Result: Pooling operators require node features well-aligned with graph topology — a condition often overlooked and not guaranteed in empirical networks. When the formalized requirements are satisfied, pooling becomes beneficial and improves performance.

Conclusion: The marginal/ inconsistent gains of pooling are due to misalignment between node features and graph topology. By ensuring this alignment through the proposed requirements and quality measure, pooling can be effectively leveraged for graph classification.

Abstract: Graph pooling is commonly applied in graph classification, yet its empirical gains over standard WL-1 expressive GNNs are often marginal or inconsistent. We study this gap by analysing the interaction between node features and graph topology and their effect on pooling objectives. Our analysis reveals that pooling operators require node features that are well-aligned with the graph's topology -- a condition often overlooked and not guaranteed in empirical networks. We formalise fundamental requirements for node features to enable effective pooling, and introduce a quantitative measure of feature quality. Our empirical evaluation shows that, when these requirements are satisfied, pooling can be beneficial and improve performance on appropriate datasets.

</details>


### [271] [The Weight Gram Matrix Captures Sequential Feature Linearization in Deep Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06258)
*Taehun Cha,Daniel Beaglehole,Adityanarayanan Radhakrishnan,Donghun Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a feature-centric framework for analyzing neural network training, introducing the Feature Learning Equation and Virtual Covariance to understand how features evolve during gradient descent. It shows deep networks learn to transform representations toward "target-linear" structure, unifying phenomena like Neural Collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how deep neural networks learn representations remains a central challenge in machine learning theory.

Method: The authors propose a feature-centric framework using the Feature Learning Equation (relating weight updates to feature evolution), the weight Gram matrix, Virtual Covariance (capturing representation dynamics), and Target Linearity (measuring alignment between features and targets).

Result: Deep networks learn to sequentially transform representations toward target-linear structure during training.

Conclusion: The linearization perspective provides a unified interpretation of empirical phenomena including Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models.

Abstract: Understanding how deep neural networks learn representations remains a central challenge in machine learning theory. In this work, we propose a feature-centric framework for analyzing neural network training by relating weight updates to feature evolution. We introduce a simple identity, the Feature Learning Equation, which identifies the weight Gram matrix as the key object capturing feature dynamics. This enables us to interpret gradient descent as implicitly inducing a hypothetical evolution of features, whose covariance structure - termed the Virtual Covariance - characterizes how representations evolve during training. Building on this perspective, we introduce Target Linearity, a measure quantifying the linear alignment between features and targets. By analyzing the training and layer-wise dynamics, we show that deep networks learn to sequentially transform representations toward target-linear structure. This linearization perspective provides a unified interpretation of several empirical phenomena, including Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models.

</details>


### [272] [Trade-off Functions for DP-SGD with Subsampling based on Random Shuffling: Tight Upper and Lower Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06259)
*Marten van Dijk,Murat Bilgehan Ertan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides tight theoretical bounds on the privacy-utility trade-off for DP-SGD with random shuffling under the $f$-DP framework, showing that with sufficient noise ($\sigma \geq \sqrt{3/\ln M}$), meaningful differential privacy can be achieved with practical parameter settings. It introduces closed-form bounds via Berry-Esseen theorem and a novel asymptotic result using a generalization of the law of large numbers, showing that the composed trade-off function approaches the ideal random guessing diagonal with only $O(\sqrt{E})$ dependency on epochs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide transparent, interpretable closed-form bounds for DP-SGD with random shuffling (as opposed to non-closed implicit formulas for Poisson subsampling), and to resolve contrasting theoretical results about the feasibility of privacy guarantees in different noise regimes.

Method: The authors apply the Berry-Esseen theorem to derive tight closed-form bounds for the trade-off function in a single epoch setting. They also introduce a new proof technique based on a generalization of the law of large numbers to analyze asymptotic behavior under composition over multiple epochs.

Result: For a single epoch with $\delta=1/100$ and $\sigma=1$, $M \approx 1.14\times 10^6$ rounds and $N \approx 1.14\times 10^7$ samples suffice. The composed trade-off function over $E$ epochs achieves $f^{\otimes E}(a) \to 1-a$ with only $O(\sqrt{E})$ $\delta$-dependency, compared to linear $E$ dependency in the Berry-Esseen regime. The regime $\sigma \leq 1/\sqrt{2\ln M}$ yields negative results.

Conclusion: Random shuffling admits tighter, more interpretable privacy analysis than Poisson subsampling in DP-SGD. The asymptotic regime shows promise for scalability, though explicit convergence rates remain an open question.

Abstract: We derive a tight analysis of the trade-off function for Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD) with subsampling based on random shuffling within the $f$-DP framework. Our analysis covers the regime $σ\geq \sqrt{3/\ln M}$, where $σ$ is the noise multiplier and $M$ is the number of rounds within a single epoch. Unlike $f$-DP analyses for Poisson subsampling, which yield non-closed implicit formulas that can be machine computed but are non-transparent, random shuffling admits a tight analysis yielding transparent and interpretable closed-form bounds. Our concrete bounds, derived via the Berry-Esseen theorem, are tight up to constant factors within the proof framework. We demonstrate worked parameter settings for a single epoch ($E=1$) with a corresponding trade-off function $\geq 1-a-δ$, that is, only $δ$ below the ideal random guessing diagonal $1-a$: For $δ= 1/100$ and $σ= 1$, roughly $M \approx 1.14\times 10^6$ rounds and $N \approx 1.14\times 10^7$ training samples suffice to achieve meaningful differential privacy. This is in contrast to recent negative results for the regime $σ\leq 1/\sqrt{2 \ln M}$. Our concrete bounds can be composed over multiple epochs leading to $δ$ having a linear in $E$ dependency, which restricts $E=O(\sqrt{M})$. To go beyond Berry--Esseen, we introduce a new proof technique based on a generalization of the law of large numbers that yields an asymptotic random guessing diagonal-limit result: if $E=c_M^2M$ with $c_M\to 0$, then the $E$-fold composed trade-off function satisfies $f^{\otimes E}(a)\to 1-a$ uniformly in $a\in[0,1]$ with $δ$ having only an $O(\sqrt{E})$ dependency. We compare this asymptotic regime with the corresponding Poisson subsampling asymptotic, and highlight the characterization of explicit convergence rates as an open question.

</details>


### [273] [Beyond Rigid Alignment: Graph Federated Learning via Dual Manifold Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06260)
*Wentao Yu,Bo Han,Jie Yang,Chen Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedGMC proposes a dual manifold calibration mechanism to handle semantic and structural heterogeneity in Graph Federated Learning, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on homophilic and heterophilic graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GFL methods enforce rigid alignment of model parameters/prototypes under a restrictive global linearity assumption, which compresses clients' personalized representation spaces and fails to preserve diverse local graph distributions.

Method: FedGMC introduces a dual manifold calibration mechanism: (1) For semantic heterogeneity, the server constructs a geometrically optimal semantic manifold via equidistant semantic anchors to guide local calibration. (2) For structural heterogeneity, the server builds global structural templates to construct a global structural manifold for local calibration. The server dynamically refines both manifolds by aggregating local manifolds.

Result: Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs show FedGMC significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baseline methods, effectively balancing global commonality and local personalization.

Conclusion: FedGMC offers a unified manifold perspective to address both semantic and structural heterogeneity in GFL, maximizing personalized representation space while preserving global commonalities, leading to superior performance.

Abstract: Graph Federated Learning (GFL) enables collaborative representation learning across distributed subgraphs while preserving privacy. However, heterogeneity remains a critical challenge, as subgraphs across clients typically differ significantly in both semantics and structures. Existing methods address heterogeneity by enforcing the rigid alignment of model parameters or prototypes between clients and the server. However, these alignments implicitly rely on a restrictive global linearity assumption that summarizes local data distributions using a single and globally consistent representation space. This severely compresses the personalized representation space of clients and fails to preserve diverse local graph distributions. To overcome these limitations, we propose Federated Graph Manifold Calibration (FedGMC), a novel paradigm that tackles semantic heterogeneity and structural heterogeneity from a unified manifold perspective. Instead of enforcing rigid alignment, FedGMC introduces a dual manifold calibration mechanism that preserves global commonalities while maximizing the personalized representation space of local clients. Specifically, for semantic heterogeneity, the server constructs a geometrically optimal semantic manifold via equidistant semantic anchors, so as to guide the calibration of local semantic manifolds. For structural heterogeneity, the server constructs a global structural manifold by building global structural templates, so as to guide the calibration of local structural manifolds. Finally, the server dynamically refines both global semantic manifolds and structural manifolds by aggregating local manifolds. Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs demonstrate that FedGMC effectively balances global commonality and local personalization, thereby significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baseline methods.

</details>


### [274] [Inference-Time Refinement Closes the Synthetic-Real Gap in Tabular Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06261)
*Eugenio Lomurno,Filippo Balzarini,Francesco Benelle,Francesca Pia Panaccione,Matteo Matteucci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TARDIS is an inference-time refinement framework that improves pre-trained tabular diffusion models without retraining, achieving a median +8.6% downstream improvement over real data across 15 benchmarks through a novel Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR) pattern combining continuous score-level gradients and discrete batch-ranking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Closing the synthetic-real data utility gap for tabular data generation has so far been pursued only at training time (architectural advances, scaling, retraining). Inference-time refinement of frozen pre-trained backbones remains unexplored, yet could potentially exceed real-data utility without expensive retraining.

Method: TARDIS uses a frozen pre-trained diffusion backbone with: (1) Tree-structured Parzen Estimator search over score-level guidance during reverse diffusion, (2) inner grid search over post-hoc sample selectors, (3) optional soft-label distillation, all encoding a Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR) pattern that minimizes the symmetric Chamfer functional between synthetic and real samples both continuously (score-level gradient) and discretely (batch-ranking).

Result: Across 15 benchmarks, TARDIS achieves median +8.6% downstream improvement over real data (95% CI [+3.3, +16.4], p=0.016, 11/15 strict wins), improves over TabDiff backbone on all 15 datasets (mean +12.9%, p<10^-4), while matching backbone on fidelity, diversity, and privacy. Runs in 1-80 minutes on a single consumer GPU.

Conclusion: Inference-time refinement of a pre-trained tabular diffusion backbone can reach and exceed real-data utility, with BCR emerging as the dominant refinement pattern across datasets. This opens a new direction for improving synthetic tabular data without costly retraining.

Abstract: Diffusion-based generators set the current state of the art for synthetic tabular data. These methods approach but rarely exceed real-data utility, and closing this synthetic-real gap has so far been pursued exclusively at training time, via architectural advances, scaling, and retraining of monolithic generators. The inference-time alternative, i.e., refining the outputs of a pre-trained backbone with parameters left untouched, has remained largely unexplored for tabular synthesis. We introduce TARDIS (Tabular generation through Refinement, Distillation, and Inference-time Sampling), an inference-time refinement framework that operates on a frozen pre-trained backbone, configured per dataset by a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator search over score-level guidance during reverse diffusion, with each trial's objective set by an inner grid search over post-hoc sample selectors and an optional soft-label distillation step. The search space encodes a single mathematical pattern we name Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR): the symmetric Chamfer functional between synthetic and real samples is minimized both continuously, via a score-level gradient, and discretely, via batch-ranking post-generation. The per-dataset search recovers BCR-aligned configurations on most datasets, evidence for BCR as the dominant refinement pattern. Across 15 binary, multiclass, and regression benchmarks TARDIS achieves a median +8.6% downstream-task improvement over models trained on real data (95% CI [+3.3, +16.4], Wilcoxon p=0.016, 11/15 strict wins) and improves over the TabDiff backbone on all 15 datasets (mean +12.9%, p<10^-4), matching the backbone on manifold fidelity, diversity, and sample-level privacy. Inference-time refinement of a pre-trained tabular diffusion backbone reaches and exceeds real-data utility in 1 to 80 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU.

</details>


### [275] [Can Attribution Predict Risk? From Multi-View Attribution to Planning Risk Signals in End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06264)
*Le Yang,Ruoyu Chen,Haijun Liu,Jiawei Liang,ShangQuan Sun,Xiaochun Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a hierarchical attribution framework for end-to-end autonomous driving that reveals visual evidence behind trajectory generation, using attribution statistics (entropy, spatial variance, Gini coefficient) as predictive signals for planning risk, achieving Spearman correlations of 0.30 with trajectory error and AUROC of 0.77 for collision detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end autonomous driving models are opaque and hard-to-localize risks. Existing explanation methods are decoupled from planning and fail to reveal visual evidence underlying trajectory generation, while attribution methods are underexplored for risk identification in planning tasks.

Method: A coarse-to-fine hierarchical region attribution strategy using L2 consistency with original trajectory as objective, extracting three attribution statistics: attribution entropy, within-camera spatial variance, and cross-camera Gini coefficient.

Result: Achieves Spearman correlations of 0.30±0.07 with trajectory error and AUROC of 0.77±0.04 for collision detection on BridgeAD, UniAD, and GenAD models.

Conclusion: Attribution statistics can serve as effective predictive signals for planning risk, generalizing to held-out scenes and remaining stable under alternative attribution baselines.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving models generate future trajectories from multi-view inputs, improving system integration but introducing opaque decisions and hard-to-localize risks. Existing methods either rely on auxiliary monitoring models or generate textual explanations, but are decoupled from the planning process and fail to reveal the visual evidence underlying trajectory generation. While attribution offers a direct alternative, planning differs from image classification by taking six-view camera images as input and predicting continuous multi-step trajectories, requiring attribution to capture both critical views and regions and their influence on outputs. Moreover, whether attribution maps can support risk identification remains underexplored. To address this, we propose a hierarchical attribution framework for end-to-end planning. Specifically, using L2 consistency with the original trajectory as the objective, we design a coarse-to-fine region attribution strategy that searches candidate regions across the full six-view input and refines attribution within them. We further extract three attribution statistics as predictive signals for planning risk, including attribution entropy to measure how concentrated the planner's reliance is over the joint visual space, within-camera spatial variance to characterize how spread out the attribution is within each view, and cross-camera Gini coefficient to quantify how unevenly attribution is distributed across the six cameras. Experiments on BridgeAD, UniAD, and GenAD show that these statistics correlate with planning risk, achieving Spearman correlations of $0.30 \pm 0.07$ with trajectory error and AUROC of $0.77 \pm 0.04$ for collision detection. The signal generalizes to held-out scenes with negligible degradation and remains stable under an alternative attribution baseline.

</details>


### [276] [A Flow Matching Algorithm for Many-Shot Adaptation to Unseen Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06272)
*Tyler Ingebrand,Ruihan Zhao,Kushagra Gupta,David Fridovich-Keil,Sandeep P. Chinchali,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FP-FM enables generative model adaptation to new distributions by learning basis functions for velocity fields and using least-squares projection, achieving efficient few-shot generation without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative models struggle to adapt from example data points without retraining. The authors aim to enable efficient model adaptation to new target distributions at inference time.

Method: FP-FM learns basis functions spanning velocity fields from training distributions, then adapts to new distributions via least-squares projection. Multiple variants trade off expressivity and compute by enriching coefficient calculation (e.g., time-dependent coefficients).

Result: FP-FM achieves significantly improved precision and recall over baselines on both synthetic and image datasets, with particularly strong gains on unseen (out-of-distribution) targets.

Conclusion: Function Projection provides an effective, training-free adaptation mechanism for flow matching models, enabling few-shot generation from diverse distributions without additional training at inference.

Abstract: While generative modeling has achieved remarkable success on tasks like natural language-conditioned image generation, enabling model adaptation from example data points remains a relatively underexplored and challenging problem. To this end, we propose Function Projection for Flow Matching (FP-FM), an algorithm that directly conditions generation on samples from the target distribution. FP-FM learns basis functions to span the velocity fields corresponding to a set of training distributions, and adapts to new distributions by computing a simple least-squares projection onto this basis. This enables efficient generation of samples from diverse target distributions without additional training at inference time. We further introduce multiple variants of FP-FM that provide a trade-off in expressivity and compute by enriching the coefficient calculation, e.g., by making the coefficients dependent on time. FP-FM achieves greatly improved precision and recall relative to baselines across synthetic and image-based datasets, with especially strong gains on unseen distributions.

</details>


### [277] [PACE: Prune-And-Compress Ensemble Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06278)
*Fabian Akkerman,Julien Ferry,Théo Guyard,Thibaut Vidal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACE is a two-phase framework that first enriches ensemble diversity by generating new learners, then prunes the enriched ensemble, offering better performance and faithfulness control than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensemble models are accurate but suffer from high complexity due to aggregating many weak learners, hindering deployment, interpretability, and robustness verification.

Method: PACE interleaves generation and pruning in two phases: (1) actively generates diverse learners via theoretically grounded procedure, (2) prunes the enriched ensemble, with fine-grained faithfulness control throughout.

Result: Outperforms prior pruning and compression methods while offering principled faithfulness guarantees.

Conclusion: PACE effectively combines generation and pruning to produce compact yet faithful ensembles, outperforming previous approaches.

Abstract: Ensemble models achieve state-of-the-art performance on prediction tasks, but usually require aggregating a large number of weak learners. This can hinder deployment, interpretability, and downstream tasks such as robustness verification. Remedies to this issue fall into two main camps: pruning, which discards redundant learners, and compression, which generates new ones from scratch. We introduce PACE, a framework that interleaves these paradigms in a two-phase strategy. First, new learners are actively generated via a theoretically grounded procedure to enhance the diversity of the initial ensemble. When no more relevant learners can be found, a second phase of pruning is performed on this enriched ensemble. During both operations, PACE allows fine control on the faithfulness to the original ensemble. Experiments show that our method outperforms prior pruning and compression methods while offering principled control of faithfulness guarantees.

</details>


### [278] [INEUS: Iterative Neural Solver for High-Dimensional PIDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06281)
*Jean-Loup Dupret,Davide Gallon,Patrick Cheridito*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INEUS is a meshfree iterative neural solver for partial integro-differential equations that uses single-jump sampling and recursive regression to efficiently handle nonlocal terms, delivering accurate and scalable solutions for high-dimensional linear and nonlinear problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an efficient neural solver for high-dimensional PIDEs that avoids the expensive computation of nonlocal jump integrals and full PIDE residuals, which are bottlenecks in traditional PINNs-based approaches.

Method: INEUS replaces explicit nonlocal jump integral evaluation with single-jump sampling and reformulates PIDE solving as a sequence of recursive regression problems, learning global solutions over the entire space-time domain in a meshfree manner.

Result: Contraction-based convergence proof for linear PIDEs, and numerical experiments demonstrating accurate and scalable solutions for various high-dimensional linear and nonlinear PIDEs.

Conclusion: INEUS offers a more efficient treatment of nonlocal terms than PINNs, making it particularly well suited for high-dimensional PDEs and PIDEs with strong convergence guarantees for linear cases.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce INEUS, a meshfree iterative neural solver for partial integro-differential equations (PIDEs). The method replaces the explicit evaluation of nonlocal jump integrals with single-jump sampling and reformulates PIDE solving as a sequence of recursive regression problems. Like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), INEUS learns global solutions over the entire space-time domain, yet it offers a more efficient treatment of nonlocal terms and avoids the computationally expensive differentiation of full PIDE residuals. These features make INEUS particularly well suited for high-dimensional PDEs and PIDEs. Supported by a contraction-based convergence proof for linear PIDEs, our numerical experiments show that INEUS delivers accurate and scalable solutions for various high-dimensional linear and nonlinear examples.

</details>


### [279] [Attributions All the Way Down? The Metagame of Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06295)
*Hubert Baniecki,Przemyslaw Biecek,Fabian Fumagalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A metagame framework quantifies second-order interactions of model explanations by measuring how one feature's influence affects another feature's attribution, using Shapley values to decompose attributions hierarchically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attribution methods capture first-order feature importance but fail to account for second-order interaction effects where one feature's attribution depends on another feature's presence or value.

Method: Treats the attribution method as a cooperative game and computes meta-attributions via Shapley values, measuring directional influence of feature j on the attribution of feature i.

Result: Attributions hierarchically decompose into meta-attributions; meta-attributions are established as directional extensions of existing interaction indices.

Conclusion: The metagame provides practical insights across diverse domains: token interactions in instruction-tuned LLMs, cross-modal similarity in vision-language models, and text-to-image concepts in multimodal diffusion transformers.

Abstract: We introduce the metagame, a conceptual framework for quantifying second-order interaction effects of model explanations. For any first-order attribution $φ(f)$ explaining a model $f$, we measure the directional influence of feature $j$ on the attribution of feature $i$, denoted as meta-attribution $\varphi_{j \to i}(f)$, by treating the attribution method itself as a cooperative game and computing its Shapley value. Theoretically, we prove that attributions hierarchically decompose into meta-attributions, and establish these as directional extensions of existing interaction indices. Empirically, we demonstrate that the metagame delivers insights across diverse interpretability applications: (i) quantifying token interactions in instruction-tuned language models, (ii) explaining cross-modal similarity in vision-language encoders, and (iii) interpreting text-to-image concepts in multimodal diffusion transformers.

</details>


### [280] [Region Seeding via Pre-Activation Regularization: A Geometric View from Piecewise Affine Nerual Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06300)
*Yi Wei,Xuan Qi,Furao Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies how deep neural networks with piecewise affine activations partition input space into affine regions, proposes a theory linking neuron switching surface proximity to increased local region counts, and introduces a region-seeding regularizer that boosts early partitioning and improves performance on toy datasets and ImageNet-1k.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard training realizes far fewer affine region refinements near data than architecture could support; existing region-count theory is architectural and doesn't address how optimization shapes partitions near data.

Method: Provides a sufficient condition under which bringing neuron switching surfaces close to data ensures local region intersection and strict increase in affine-region count; proposes a plug-and-play region-seeding regularizer that encourages early partitioning while allowing task-driven refinement later.

Result: Regularizer increases realized affine regions via exact enumeration, improves performance on toy datasets, boosts early-stage accuracy, and achieves comparable/slightly improved final accuracy on ImageNet-1k.

Conclusion: The proposed theory and regularizer offer a principled training-time handle for seeding data-relevant partitions early in optimization, benefiting both expressivity and performance.

Abstract: Deep networks with continuous piecewise affine activations induce polyhedral partitions of the input space, making the number of realized affine regions a natural measure of expressive capacity and a key determinant of how well the model can approximate nonlinear target functions. In practice, standard training realizes far fewer region refinements in data-visited neighborhoods than the architecture could in principle support, while existing region-count theory is primarily architectural and offers little guidance on how optimization shapes the realized partition near the data. Our theory provides a sufficient condition under which bringing neuron switching surfaces sufficiently close to data points ensures their intersection with local neighborhoods, which in turn implies a strict increase in the local affine-region count, yielding a principled training-time handle for seeding data-relevant partitions early in optimization. Guided by these results, we propose a plug-and-play region-seeding regularizer that encourages early partitioning while allowing task-driven refinement to dominate later in training. Experiments show that the regularizer increases the number of realized affine regions via exact enumeration and improves overall performance on toy datasets, while also improving early-stage accuracy and achieving comparable (or slightly improved) final accuracy on ImageNet-1k for classical models.

</details>


### [281] [Molecules Meet Language: Confound-Aware Representation Learning and Chemical Property Steering in Transformer-VAE Latent Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06303)
*Zakaria Elabid,Jan Andrzejewski,Bartosz Brzoza,Attila Cangi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether latent spaces of molecular generative models (Transformer-VAE on SELFIES) encode chemically meaningful directions or just SELFIES artifacts. They introduce confound-aware evaluation—residualization, alignment analysis, and decoded traversal—and find robust steering for some properties (cLogP, TPSA, etc.) while others require local gradients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Molecular generative models may exhibit apparent property predictability due to sequence-level shortcuts (e.g., SELFIES tokens) rather than genuine chemical organization. The authors aim to disentangle true chemical signal from these confounds.

Method: They train an unsupervised autoregressive Transformer-VAE on SELFIES, freeze it, and fit linear probes to RDKit descriptors. They introduce three confound-aware evaluation techniques: residualization, confound-direction alignment analysis, and decoded-molecule traversal. They also apply nonlinear probes.

Result: SELFIES length, branch tokens, ring tokens, and token entropy are strongly encoded in latent space. After controlling for confounds, robust monotonic steering is found for cLogP, FractionCSP3, HeavyAtomCount, TPSA, BertzCT, and HBA. Nonlinear probes reveal some properties have stable global directions while others need local gradients.

Conclusion: Chemically meaningful steering can emerge in entangled molecular latent spaces, but only when validated through decoded molecules and controlled for representation-level confounds.

Abstract: Molecular generative models often assume meaningful latent geometry, but apparent property predictability can reflect sequence-level shortcuts rather than chemical organization. We study this issue in an unsupervised autoregressive Transformer-VAE trained on SELFIES. After training, we freeze the model, fit linear probes to RDKit descriptors, and use the probe weights as candidate global steering directions. To separate chemical signal from SELFIES artifacts, we introduce a confound-aware evaluation based on residualization, confound-direction alignment analysis, and decoded-molecule traversal. This is necessary because SELFIES length, branch tokens, ring tokens, and token entropy are strongly encoded in the latent space. Under this confound-aware evaluation, we find robust monotonic steering for cLogP, FractionCSP3, HeavyAtomCount, TPSA, BertzCT, and HBA. Nonlinear probes further show that some properties admit stable global directions, while others are better described by local latent gradients. Overall, our results show that chemically meaningful steering can emerge in entangled molecular latent spaces, but only when validated through decoded molecules and controlled for representation-level confounds.

</details>


### [282] [Perceive, Route and Modulate: Dynamic Pattern Recalibration for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06310)
*Siru Zhong,Zhao Meng,Haohuan Fu,Haoyang Li,Qingsong Wen,Yuxuan Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Dynamic Pattern Recalibration (DPR), a backbone-agnostic mechanism that addresses the limitation of fixed weight matrices in deep forecasting models by computing token-level adaptive modulation vectors, achieving competitive performance across diverse architectures and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep forecasting models use fixed weight matrices uniformly across all temporal tokens, creating a static pattern response that fails to adapt to shifting local temporal dynamics in real-world time series.

Method: DPR introduces a "Perceive-Route-Modulate" pipeline that computes a soft-routing distribution over a learned basis of adaptive response patterns, generating a time-aware modulation vector that recalibrates hidden states via a residual Hadamard product.

Result: As a backbone-agnostic adapter, DPR enhances forecasting across diverse architectures with minimal overhead. As a standalone model, DPRNet achieves competitive performance across 12 benchmarks.

Conclusion: Dynamic recalibration effectively addresses a general bottleneck in forecasting models and validates against macroscopic parameter scaling.

Abstract: Local temporal patterns in real-world time series continuously shift, rendering globally shared transformations suboptimal. Current deep forecasting models, despite their scale and complexity, rely on fixed weight matrices applied uniformly to all temporal tokens. This creates a static pattern response: models settle into a compromised average, unable to adapt to changing local dynamics. We introduce Dynamic Pattern Recalibration (DPR), a backbone-agnostic mechanism that resolves this via token-level recalibration. Through a lightweight "Perceive-Route-Modulate" pipeline, DPR computes a soft-routing distribution over a learned basis of adaptive response patterns, generating a time-aware modulation vector that recalibrates hidden states via a residual Hadamard product. As a backbone-agnostic adapter, DPR enhances forecasting across diverse architectures with minimal overhead, confirming it addresses a general bottleneck. As a minimalist standalone model, DPRNet achieves competitive performance across 12 benchmarks, validating dynamic recalibration against macroscopic parameter scaling.

</details>


### [283] [When Does $\ell_2$-Boosting Overfit Benignly? High-Dimensional Risk Asymptotics and the $\ell_1$ Implicit Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06314)
*Ye Su,Jian Li,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper characterizes benign overfitting under ℓ₁ implicit bias of greedy ensembles (ℓ₂-Boosting). It shows that benign overfitting fails at linear rate—excess variance decays only logarithmically (Θ(σ²/log(p/n))) under pure noise, and risk converges slowly under spiked-isotropic designs (Θ(σ²/log(r₂/n))). They derive an early stopping rule that recovers the Lasso basic inequality and minimax-optimal empirical prediction rate under ℓ₁-bounded signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benign overfitting is well-understood in ℓ₂ geometries but poorly understood under ℓ₁ implicit bias of greedy ensembles. The non-linear coupling of coordinate selection thresholds makes standard spectral resolvent tools inapplicable. The authors aim to analytically resolve the non-smooth ℓ₁ interpolant and characterize the high-dimensional risk of ℓ₂-Boosting.

Method: Uses continuous-time ℓ₂-Boosting over p features and n samples. Couples the Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem with asymptotic expansions of double-sided truncated Gaussian moments to resolve the ℓ₁ interpolant. Analyzes non-smooth subdifferential dynamics for early stopping.

Result: Benign overfitting fails at linear rate under ℓ₁ bias: excess variance decays as Θ(σ²/log(p/n)) under pure noise, and risk converges as Θ(σ²/log(r₂/n)) under spiked-isotropic designs (slower than ℓ₂'s linear decay). A tuning-free early stopping rule attains minimax-optimal empirical prediction rate for ℓ₁-bounded signals.

Conclusion: ℓ₁ implicit bias of greedy ensembles yields logarithmic rather than linear decay, indicating benign overfitting is less effective in ℓ₁ geometries. Early stopping can recover Lasso-level performance. Exact signal-noise decomposition under signals remains open.

Abstract: Benign overfitting is well-characterized in $\ell_2$ geometries, but its behavior under the $\ell_1$ implicit bias of greedy ensembles remains challenging. The analytical barrier stems from the non-linear coupling of coordinate selection thresholds, which invalidates standard spectral resolvent tools. To isolate this algorithmic bias, we characterize the high-dimensional risk of continuous-time $\ell_2$-Boosting over $p$ features and $n$ samples. By coupling the Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem with delicate asymptotic expansions of double-sided truncated Gaussian moments, we analytically resolve the non-smooth $\ell_1$ interpolant. Under an isotropic pure-noise model, we prove that benign overfitting fails at the linear rate: greedy selection localizes noise into sparse active sets, and the excess variance decays at a logarithmic rate $Θ(σ^2/\log(p/n))$ for noise variance $σ^2$. We remark that while this localization mechanism should persist in the presence of signals, the exact signal-noise decomposition remains an open problem. For spiked-isotropic designs with $k^*$ head eigenvalues and $r_2 = p - k^*$ tail dimensions, the risk converges to zero when $r_{2} \gg n$, but only at a logarithmic rate $Θ(σ^2/\log(r_2/n))$, which is slower than the linear decay observed in $\ell_2$ geometries. To avoid this slow convergence, we analyze the non-smooth subdifferential dynamics of the boosting flow. This yields a tuning-free early stopping rule that, under a bounded $\ell_1$-path condition, recovers the Lasso basic inequality and attains the minimax-optimal empirical prediction rate for $\ell_1$-bounded signals.

</details>


### [284] [Pro-KLShampoo: Projected KL-Shampoo with Whitening Recovered by Orthogonalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06316)
*Ruotong Sun,Ermin Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pro-KLShampoo exploits the spike-and-flat eigenvalue structure in KL-Shampoo's Kronecker preconditioners by restricting one factor to a parametric family with tracked subspace and orthogonalization, consistently outperforming KL-Shampoo across four LLM pre-training scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Kronecker-factored preconditioning (KL-Shampoo) and gradient momentum orthogonalization (Muon) are developed in isolation; the authors aim to bridge them by observing structural properties of KL-Shampoo's preconditioners and leveraging that structure for efficiency.

Method: Observing a spike-and-flat eigenvalue pattern in KL-Shampoo's Kronecker factors (rank-ρ signal-plus-noise model), the authors restrict one factor to a parametric family: full spectral tracking on an r-dimensional subspace and a single shared eigenvalue for the remaining n-r directions, where orthogonalization is applied. An identity shows this matches full KL-Shampoo's preconditioner form.

Result: On GPT-2 124M/350M and LLaMA 134M/450M, Pro-KLShampoo consistently outperforms KL-Shampoo in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory, and wallclock time to reach each loss level across all tested subspace ranks.

Conclusion: Exploiting the structured eigenvalue spectra of Kronecker preconditioners (spike-and-flat) yields a more efficient variant (Pro-KLShampoo) that bridges explicit preconditioning and orthogonalization approaches.

Abstract: Optimizers that exploit the matrix structure of gradients are central to modern LLM pre-training, with two distinct frontiers: explicit Kronecker-factored preconditioning -- most recently KL-Shampoo, which estimates the preconditioner via KL divergence minimization -- and orthogonalization of the gradient momentum, exemplified by Muon and analyzed as steepest descent under the spectral norm. The two routes are typically developed in isolation. We make a structural observation about KL-Shampoo's Kronecker preconditioners: their eigenvalue spectra exhibit a \emph{spike-and-flat} shape -- a few dominant eigenvalues followed by an approximately uniform tail -- across layers and training stages, holding exactly under a rank-$ρ$ signal-plus-noise gradient model. We exploit this structure by restricting one of KL-Shampoo's Kronecker factors to a parametric family aligned with the spike-and-flat shape: full spectral structure on a tracked $r$-dimensional subspace, single shared eigenvalue across the remaining $n-r$ directions. On these directions, we apply orthogonalization. An identity shows that this orthogonalization recovers the algebraic form of full KL-Shampoo's preconditioner. On four pre-training scales (GPT-2 124M / 350M, LLaMA 134M / 450M), Pro-KLShampoo consistently outperforms KL-Shampoo at every subspace rank we test in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory, and wallclock time to reach each loss level.

</details>


### [285] [SMolLM: Small Language Models Learn Small Molecular Grammar](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06322)
*Akhil Jindal,Harang Ju*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMolLM is a 53K-parameter transformer that generates valid SMILES strings better than a GPT with 10x more parameters, and its internal computation resolves molecular grammar constraints in a fixed order (brackets → rings → valence), with one attention head specifically handling bracket matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how language models learn chemical grammar (SMILES syntax) at a mechanistic level, and to develop a compact, interpretable molecular generator.

Method: Trains a 53K-parameter weight-shared transformer (SMolLM) on SMILES generation; uses error classification, linear probing, and sparse autoencoders to analyze internal representations; performs systematic ablation across attention heads to localize specific functions.

Result: SMolLM achieves 95% valid SMILES on ZINC-250K, outperforming a 10x larger GPT. The model resolves constraints in a fixed order: brackets first, rings second, valence last. One single attention head is responsible for bracket matching.

Conclusion: Compact molecular generators can be both performant and mechanistically interpretable, providing a testbed for studying iterative computation in formal-language domains.

Abstract: Language models for molecular design have scaled to hundreds of millions of parameters, yet how they learn chemical grammar is poorly understood. We train SMolLM, a 53K-parameter weight-shared transformer, to generate novel SMILES with 95% validity on the ZINC-250K drug-like-molecule benchmark, outperforming a standard GPT with 10 times more parameters. Mechanistically, the same block resolves SMILES constraints across passes in a fixed order: brackets first, rings second, and valence last, as shown by error classification, linear probing, and sparse autoencoders. A systematic ablation across attention heads and passes further localizes the first bracket-matching step to a single attention head. Together, these results yield a compact, mechanistically interpretable molecular generator and a testbed for studying iterative computation in formal-language domains.

</details>


### [286] [LINC: Decoupling Local Consequence Scoring from Hidden Matching in Constructive Neural Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06332)
*Shaofeng Qin,Li Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LINC is a decoder-side architecture that explicitly computes deterministic consequences (travel, waiting, slack, capacity) for neural routing solvers, using a shared linear local scorer for relative consequences and feasible-set summaries to modulate context, achieving significant gap reductions on CVRPTW, CVRP, and TSP benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing constructive neural routing solvers score actions by matching decoder context to embeddings, but hide deterministic one-step consequences (e.g., travel, waiting, slack, capacity changes), forcing the hidden state to rediscover transition arithmetic.

Method: LINC computes explicit deterministic consequences of candidate actions, uses centered relative consequences compared by a shared linear local scorer, and modulates decoder context with feasible-set summaries, preserving standard global matching.

Result: For CVRPTW, LINC reduces PolyNet's Solomon/Homberger gaps from 13.83%/38.15% to 7.26%/14.71%; also improves external-benchmark gaps for TSP and CVRP.

Conclusion: Explicitly incorporating deterministic consequences at the decoder side (via LINC) significantly improves neural routing solver performance across constrained routing problems without altering the global matching framework.

Abstract: Constructive neural routing solvers usually score the next action by matching a decoder context to candidate embeddings, hiding deterministic one-step consequences such as travel, waiting, slack, and capacity changes. We propose LINC (Local Inference via Normed Comparison), a decoder-side candidate decision architecture that computes these consequences explicitly. LINC uses them according to their decision role: centered relative consequences are compared by a shared linear local scorer, while feasible-set summaries modulate the decoder context. This preserves standard global matching and relieves the hidden state from rediscovering transition arithmetic. The Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (CVRPTW) serves as the main constrained-routing stress test; the same interface extends to the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) and Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP). In particular, for CVRPTW, LINC reduces PolyNet's Solomon/Homberger gaps from 13.83\%/38.15\% to 7.26\%/14.71\%; for TSP and CVRP, it also improves external-benchmark gaps.

</details>


### [287] [Eliciting associations between clinical variables from LLMs via comparison questions across populations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06335)
*Fabian Kabus,Kian Kordtomeikel,Thomas Brox,Heinz Wiendl,Daiana Stolz,Harald Binder*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes an indirect method using patient comparison triplets and invariant causal prediction (ICP) to recover correlation and causal links from LLMs trained on biomedical literature, demonstrated on COPD and MS datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLMs contain biomedical knowledge spanning diverse patient populations, but direct elicitation of correlations or causal links is prone to biases and pitfalls. The authors aim to safely recover meaningful association and causal structures from LLMs without accessing internal model activations.

Method: The approach uses structured triplet comparison questions (e.g., "Which two patients are more similar?") where a second variable is provided for one patient, inducing prompt-level environment shifts. Correlation estimates are derived from LLM similarity decisions across environments, enabling invariant causal prediction (ICP) to identify conservative candidate causal parent links.

Result: In COPD and MS domains, the elicited correlations are smooth, stable, and clinically interpretable across environments. Correlations vary significantly across subpopulations, supporting downstream invariance testing. ICP yields a small, plausible set of candidate invariant parent links.

Conclusion: Indirect elicitation via triplet comparisons can recover meaningful association structure from LLMs, providing a cautious pathway from implicit correlations to causal statements consistent with LLM answering patterns.

Abstract: The training data of large language models (LLMs) comprises a wide range of biomedical literature, reflecting data from many different patient populations. We investigate how it might be possible to recover information on correlation and causal links between patient characteristics, as a key building block for medical decision making. To avoid the pitfalls of direct elicitation, we propose an approach based on structured comparison questions, specifically patient comparison triplet questions. This is combined with a statistical model for the LLM representation that provides estimates of correlations without access to activations or model internals. Intuitively, we consider how similarity decisions of LLMs based on a first variable are affected by providing information on a second variable for one of the patients being assessed. We then induce prompt-level environment shifts to obtain correlation estimates for different subpopulations, which enables an invariant causal prediction (ICP) approach to obtain conservative candidate parent links. We demonstrate the method in two clinical domains, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and multiple sclerosis (MS). Across prompted environments, the elicited correlations are smooth, stable, and clinically interpretable, yet vary in a statistically significant way that supports downstream invariance testing, such that ICP provides a small set of candidate invariant parent links. These results show that indirect elicitation via triplet comparisons can recover meaningful association structure from LLMs and offer a cautious route from implicit correlations to causal statements that are congruent with LLM answering patterns.

</details>


### [288] [Is Escalation Worth It? A Decision-Theoretic Characterization of LLM Cascades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06350)
*Dylan Bouchard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a decision-theoretic framework for LLM model cascades, characterizing the cost-quality tradeoff frontier. It finds that deterministic two-model cascades (pairwise envelope) perform comparably to more complex k-model cascades, and that a lightweight pre-generation router outperforms cascades mainly by avoiding cheap model costs on deferred queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cascade approaches treat the deferral threshold as an empirical hyperparameter with limited guidance on the cost-quality frontier geometry over a model pool.

Method: A decision-theoretic framework using constrained optimization and duality; piecewise concavity analysis of cost-quality frontiers; first-order conditions for k-model cascades; empirical validation on 5 benchmarks with 8 models from 5 providers.

Result: Full fixed chains underperform the pairwise envelope; optimized subsequence cascades offer no meaningful held-out gains; a lightweight pre-generation router exceeds best cascade on 4/5 datasets by avoiding cheap model generation costs.

Conclusion: Cascade performance is limited primarily by structural cost (paying cheap model before escalation) rather than by a shortage of intermediate stages.

Abstract: Model cascades, in which a cheap LLM defers to an expensive one on low-confidence queries, are widely used to navigate the cost-quality tradeoff at deployment. Existing approaches largely treat the deferral threshold as an empirical hyperparameter, with limited guidance on the geometry of the resulting cost-quality frontier over a model pool. We develop a decision-theoretic framework grounded in constrained optimization and duality. For a two-model cascade, we establish piecewise concavity of the cost-quality frontier on decreasing-benefit regions of the confidence support, with reciprocal shadow prices linking the budget- and quality-constrained formulations. Given a pool of $k$ models, we characterize the frontier achievable by deterministic two-model threshold cascades as the pointwise envelope over $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise cascades, with switching points where the optimal pair changes. For $k$-model cascades, we derive first-order conditions in which a single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost across stage boundaries. We validate the framework on five benchmarks (MATH, MMLU, TriviaQA, SimpleQA, LiveCodeBench) across eight models from five providers. Within the deterministic threshold-cascade class, full fixed chains underperform the pairwise envelope, and optimized subsequence cascades do not deliver practically meaningful held-out gains over it. A lightweight pre-generation router exceeds the best cascade policy on four of five datasets, mainly because it avoids the cheap model's generation cost on queries sent directly to a larger model rather than because of a stronger routing signal. These results suggest that cascade performance is limited primarily by structural cost, since cascades pay the cheap model before any escalation decision, rather than by a shortage of intermediate stages.

</details>


### [289] [Topological Signatures of Grokking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06352)
*Yifan Tang,Qiquan Wang,Inés García-Redondo,Anthea Monod*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper uses persistent homology (topological data analysis) to study grokking in neural networks, finding that a sharp increase in first homology ($H_1$) persistence serves as a clear topological signature of generalization, distinguishing it from memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the grokking phenomenon—where models suddenly generalize after prolonged memorization—by providing a principled topological explanation beyond existing spectral and geometric methods.

Method: Applied persistent homology to point clouds from embedding matrices of models trained on modular arithmetic tasks; computed maximum/total persistence of $H_1$ features and compared with Fourier analysis and local intrinsic dimension.

Result: Discovered a sharp increase in maximum and total persistence of $H_1$ during grokking; emergence of dominant long-lived topological features reflecting the cyclic structure; topological transitions tied to generalization not memorization.

Conclusion: Persistent homology provides a unified, interpretable geometric and topological framework for analyzing how neural networks internalize latent structure, offering advantages over existing spectral/geometric diagnostics.

Abstract: We study the grokking phenomenon through the lens of topology. Using persistent homology on point clouds derived from the embedding matrices of a range of models trained on modular arithmetic with varying primes, we identify a clear and consistent topological signature of grokking: a sharp increase in both the maximum and total persistence of first homology ($H_1$). Persistence diagrams reveal the emergence of a dominant long-lived topological feature together with increasingly structured secondary features, reflecting the underlying cyclic structure of the task. Compared to existing spectral and geometric diagnostics -- specifically, Fourier analysis and local intrinsic dimension -- persistent homology provides a unified geometric and topological characterization of representation learning, capturing both local and global multi-scale structure. Ablations across data regimes and control settings show that these topological transitions are tied to generalization rather than memorization. Our results suggest that persistent homology offers a principled and interpretable framework for analyzing how neural networks internalize latent structure during training.

</details>


### [290] [Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Modelling with Missing Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06355)
*Ignacio Peis,Pablo M. Olmos,Jes Frellsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends order-agnostic autoregressive models to handle missing data by showing their standard training implicitly handles random missingness, introducing a principled framework for training on incomplete data, and using them for active information acquisition. The resulting MO-ARM model outperforms established imputation baselines on real-world benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Order-agnostic autoregressive models have shown strong performance in generative modeling, but their application to settings with incomplete data has been largely unexplored.

Method: (1) Reinterpreting standard training as implicit imputation under MCAR, (2) introducing a principled training framework for incomplete data under general missingness mechanisms, and (3) leveraging amortized conditional density estimation for active information acquisition.

Result: MO-ARM consistently outperforms established imputation baselines across real-world benchmarks.

Conclusion: Order-agnostic autoregressive models can be effectively adapted for missing data settings, with strong imputation and active information acquisition capabilities.

Abstract: Order-Agnostic autoregressive models have demonstrated strong performance in deep generative modeling, yet their use in settings with incomplete data remains largely unexplored. In this work, we reinterpret them through the lens of missing data. First, we show that their standard training procedure on fully observed data implicitly performs imputation under a missing completely at random mechanism, resulting in robust out-of-sample imputation performance in settings with high missingness. Second, we introduce the first principled framework for training them directly on incomplete datasets under general missingness mechanisms. Third, we leverage their amortized conditional density estimation to perform active information acquisition, i.e., sequentially selecting the most informative missing variables for downstream prediction or inference. Across a suite of real-world benchmarks, our Missingness-Aware Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Model (MO-ARM) consistently outperforms established imputation baselines.

</details>


### [291] [Preliminary Insights in Chronos Frequency Data Understanding and Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06361)
*Alessandro Pagani,Marco Cominelli,Liying Han,Gaofeng Dong,Sergio Benini,Francesco Gringoli,Mattia Savardi,Mani B. Srivastava,Trevor Bihl,Erik P. Blasch,Daniel O. Brigham,Kara Combs,Lance M. Kaplan,Federico Cerutti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes whether the Chronos foundation model can internally represent frequency information from time-series data, using controlled sinusoid signals and lightweight probes on the decoder to test frequency separability, revealing both strengths and limitations in frequency encoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models for time-series data are increasingly used but it's unclear whether they encode fundamental signal properties like frequency; this gap limits interpretability and trust in their use for signal processing tasks.

Method: The authors use discrete sinusoids at fixed frequencies as input, then apply lightweight online minimum description length probes to the decoder architecture of Chronos to test for presence and separability of frequency information in internal representations.

Result: Frequency information is captured in Chronos's internal representations, but the quality of representation varies across the frequency spectrum, with some regimes showing degradation.

Conclusion: The findings provide practical guidance for using Chronos in signal processing and information fusion, and contribute to improving interpretability and evaluation of foundation models for temporal data.

Abstract: This paper presents a preliminary analysis of the ability of Chronos foundation model to process and internally represent frequency domain information. Foundation models that process time-series data offer practitioners a unified architecture capable of learning generic temporal representations across diverse tasks and domains, reducing the need for task-specific feature engineering and enabling transfer across signal modalities. Despite their growing adoption, the extent to which such models encode fundamental signal properties remains insufficiently characterised. We address this gap by analysing Chronos under controlled conditions, starting from the simplest class of signals: discrete sinusoids generated at fixed frequencies. Using lightweight online minimum description length probes applied to the decoder architecture, we test for the presence and separability of frequency information in the model's internal representations. The results provide insight into how frequential content is captured across the frequency spectrum and highlight regimes in which representation quality may degrade or require particular care. These findings offer practical guidance for users of Chronos in signal processing and information fusion contexts, and contribute to ongoing efforts to improve the interpretability and evaluation of foundation models for temporal data.

</details>


### [292] [Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06364)
*Xin Peng,Ang Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AuxPath-FM generalizes conditional flow matching by allowing arbitrary auxiliary distributions (not just Gaussian) for probability path design, enabling diverse geometric properties and specialized tasks like label-guided generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing flow matching methods restrict auxiliary components to Gaussian noise, limiting the flexibility of probability path design. The authors aim to generalize the framework to arbitrary auxiliary distributions.

Method: Introduces an auxiliary variable η drawn from any distribution into the probability path, producing trajectories X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η. The framework preserves the continuity equation and marginal training objective.

Result: Demonstrates valid flow matching with diverse priors (Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, Rademacher), each offering different geometric properties. Also enables label-guided generation by encoding semantic information into the auxiliary distribution.

Conclusion: AuxPath-FM provides a principled, general foundation for probability path design, offering both theoretical generality and practical flexibility for diverse generative modeling tasks.

Abstract: We introduce a new generative modeling framework, \textbf{Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths (AuxPath-FM)}, which generalizes conditional flow matching by incorporating an auxiliary variable drawn from an arbitrary distribution into the probability path. Unlike prior methods that restrict auxiliary components to Gaussian noise, AuxPath-FM allows the variable $η$ to follow any distribution, producing trajectories of the form $X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η$. We theoretically demonstrate that this construction preserves the continuity equation and maintains a training objective consistent with the marginal formulation. This flexibility enables the design of diverse probability paths using various priors, including Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, and discrete Rademacher distributions, each offering unique geometric properties for generative flows. Furthermore, our framework allows for specialized tasks such as label-guided generation by encoding structured semantic information into the auxiliary distribution. Overall, AuxPath-FM provides a principled and general foundation for probability path design, offering both theoretical generality and practical flexibility for diverse generative modeling tasks.

</details>


### [293] [Layer Collapse in Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06366)
*Alexander Conzelmann,Albert Catalan-Tatjer,Shiwei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion language models (DLMs) exhibit a layer-collapse property where early layers have collapsed activation patterns dominated by a single super-outlier that is paradoxically critical for generation. Unlike AR models where deeper layers are redundant due to undertraining, DLM layer collapse stems from overtraining. DLMs are far more robust to quantization (3-bit GPTQ: -1.8% vs -64.7% on GSM8K) and have reversed optimal sparsity allocation compared to AR models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the poorly understood activation dynamics of diffusion language models (DLMs) compared to autoregressive (AR) language models, particularly why they differ in behavior and compression properties.

Method: Analyze activation dynamics in LLaDA-8B (a DLM), identify layer-collapse via outlier analysis, perform ablation experiments (pruning the outlier), apply quantization (GPTQ), test sparsity allocation strategies, and validate via controlled pre-training experiments.

Result: (1) Early DLM layers exhibit collapsed activation patterns dominated by a single persistent super-outlier; pruning it causes repetitive random token loops. (2) DLM redundancy is most pronounced in early layers (reverse of AR models). (3) Layer collapse is caused by overtraining, not undertraining. (4) DLMs are extremely robust to quantization (LLaDA -1.8% vs Llama -64.7% under 3-bit GPTQ on GSM8K). (5) Optimal sparsity allocation reverses: early-layer-heavy sparsity helps DLMs (+8.4%) but hurts AR models (-8.4%).

Conclusion: The DLM training objective fundamentally reshapes layer dynamics compared to AR models, producing distinct redundancy patterns and compression properties, with direct practical implications for model deployment and optimization.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently emerged as competitive alternatives to autoregressive (AR) language models, yet differences in their activation dynamics remain poorly understood. We characterize these dynamics in LLaDA-8B and identify a striking layer-collapse property: a few early layers exhibit highly similar, collapsed activation patterns dominated by a single large super-outlier persisting over a long token range. Despite its apparent redundancy, this outlier is critical: pruning it causes outputs to degrade into repetitive random token loops. Paradoxically, layers in LLaDA contain more redundant representations overall, with redundancy most pronounced in earlier layers -- the reverse of AR models, where deeper layers grow redundant due to undertraining. Our analysis indicates that layer collapse in DLMs is not driven by undertraining but by overtraining: a dominant outlier becomes an indispensable information carrier while remaining representations collapse into redundant structure. These findings have strong practical implications, verified through controlled pre-training experiments. DLMs are surprisingly robust to compression: LLaDA under 3-bit GPTQ quantization drops only -1.8% on GSM8K, whereas Llama-3.1-8B drops -64.7%. Optimal sparsity allocation also reverses between families: at 50% average sparsity, allocating more to early layers in LLaDA yields +8.4% over the reverse strategy, while the same allocation costs Llama -8.4%. Our findings reveal that the DLM training objective fundamentally reshapes layer dynamics relative to AR models, with direct consequences for compression and deployment. Code: github.com/Conzel/super-outlier-dlm.

</details>


### [294] [A Unified Pair-GRPO Family: From Implicit to Explicit Preference Constraints for Stable and General RL Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06375)
*Hao Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes the Pair-GRPO family (Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO) that reformulates RLHF preference learning with theoretical guarantees for stability, gradient direction, and convergence, outperforming baselines on LLM alignment and continuous control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLHF methods suffer from unstable policy updates, ambiguous gradient directions, poor interpretability, and high gradient variance in pairwise preference learning paradigms, motivating a unified theoretical framework to address these limitations.

Method: The Pair-GRPO family includes Soft-Pair-GRPO (replacing GRPO's group-normalized rewards with binary pairwise preferences while retaining clipped surrogate and KL regularization) and Hard-Pair-GRPO (adding local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting). A gradient equivalence theorem shows Soft-Pair-GRPO's gradient is a positive scalar multiple of GRPO's under first-order Taylor expansion.
