Result: Both variants consistently outperform state-of-the-art baselines on HH-RLHF, UltraFeedback, and HalfCheetah-v4 in alignment quality, win rate, training stability, and generalization, with ablation studies validating each component.

Conclusion: The Pair-GRPO family provides a principled, theoretically grounded solution to RLHF instability, with monotonic policy improvement, deterministic gradients, variance reduction, and dynamic convergence guarantees.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) alignment via reinforcement learning from human preferences (RLHF) suffers from unstable policy updates, ambiguous gradient directions, poor interpretability, and high gradient variance in mainstream pairwise preference learning paradigms. To systematically address these limitations, we establish a unified theoretical framework for preference-based RL optimization centered on the Pair-GRPO family, comprising two tightly coupled variants: Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO. Soft-Pair-GRPO is a minimal modification of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) that replaces group-normalized scalar rewards with binary pairwise preference rewards, retaining GRPO's clipped surrogate and KL-regularized structure. We prove a critical gradient equivalence theorem: under first-order Taylor expansion around the current policy, Soft-Pair-GRPO's gradient is a positive scalar multiple of standard GRPO's gradient, explaining its empirical stability despite discarding continuous reward magnitudes. Building on this foundation, we propose Hard-Pair-GRPO, an advanced variant introducing explicit local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting optimization to further suppress gradient noise and global policy drift. We provide comprehensive theoretical guarantees for both variants--including monotonic policy improvement, deterministic gradient direction, gradient-variance reduction, and dynamic step-size convergence. Extensive experiments on standard LLM alignment benchmarks (HH-RLHF,UltraFeedback) and the MuJoCo continuous control task HalfCheetah-v4 demonstrate that our Pair-GRPO family consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in alignment quality, human preference win rate, training stability, and generalization to general reinforcement learning. Ablation studies validate the critical contributions of each core component.

</details>


### [295] [MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06384)
*Alessandro Ronca*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) use MinMax algebra to create recurrence that avoids vanishing/exploding gradients, provably captures all regular languages, supports parallel log-time evaluation, maintains bounded states, preserves gradient flow, and shows strong empirical performance on synthetic and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recurrent neural networks suffer from vanishing and exploding gradients, which limits their ability to learn long-range dependencies. The authors aim to design a recurrence mechanism that is both expressive and free from these gradient pathologies.

Method: The paper proposes MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs), which use MinMax algebra (min and max operations) to define recurrence. Multiple layers of neurons are cascaded. The model leverages the algebraic properties of MinMax to ensure bounded activations and gradient properties.

Result: The model achieves: (1) expressivity of all regular languages, (2) parallel evaluation with logarithmic runtime, (3) uniformly bounded states, (4) bounded and almost everywhere defined gradients, (5) no vanishing state gradient (gradient can be exactly 1 across long distances). Empirically, it solves synthetic tasks perfectly and a 127M parameter model shows competitive next-token prediction performance.

Conclusion: MinMax RNCs combine strong theoretical guarantees (expressivity, gradient stability, parallelizability) with practical effectiveness, suggesting they are a promising alternative to standard recurrent architectures.

Abstract: We show that the MinMax algebra provides a form of recurrence that is expressively powerful, efficiently implementable, and most importantly it is not affected by vanishing or exploding gradient. We call MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) the models obtained by cascading several layers of neurons that employ such recurrence. We show that MinMax RNCs enjoy many favourable theoretical properties. First, their formal expressivity includes all regular languages, arguably the maximal expressivity for a finite-memory system. Second, they can be evaluated in parallel with a runtime that is logarithmic in the input length given enough processors; and they can also be evaluated sequentially. Third, their state and activations are bounded uniformly for all input lengths. Fourth, at almost all points, their loss gradient exists and it is bounded. Fifth, they do not exhibit a vanishing state gradient: the gradient of a state w.r.t. a past state can have constant value one regardless of the time distance between the two states. Finally, we find empirical evidence that the favourable theoretical properties of MinMax RNCs are matched by their practical capabilities: they are able to perfectly solve a number of synthetic tasks, showing superior performance compared to the considered state-of-the-art recurrent neural networks; also, we train a MinMax RNC of 127M parameters on next-token prediction, and the obtained model shows competitive performance for its size, providing evidence of the potential of MinMax RNCs on real-world tasks.

</details>


### [296] [Data-Driven Covariate Selection for Nonparametric and Cycle-Agnostic Causal Effect Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06385)
*Ana Leticia Garcez Vicente,Gijs van Seeventer,Saber Salehkaleybar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that a local, data-driven covariate selection method based on conditional independence, previously known to work in acyclic causal models, also remains sound and complete in cyclic causal models, leveraging the invariance of conditional independence under σ-acyclification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating causal effects from observational data is challenging when latent confounding and feedback loops exist. Existing methods assume acyclicity or rely on global causal structure learning, which limits scalability and real-world applicability.

Method: The authors analyze a local covariate selection method based on conditional independence tests and prove its validity extends to cyclic settings via the invariance of conditional independence assertions under σ-acyclification.

Result: The method remains sound and complete in cyclic causal models, validated through extensive synthetic data experiments showing reliable performance.

Conclusion: The findings establish a unified, cycle-agnostic framework for covariate selection and causal effect estimation, demonstrating that the same local method works across both cyclic and acyclic settings without modification.

Abstract: Estimating causal effects from observational data requires identifying valid adjustment sets. This task is especially challenging in realistic settings where latent confounding and feedback loops are present. Existing approaches typically assume acyclicity or rely on global causal structure learning, limiting applicability and computational efficiency. In this work, we study a local, data-driven method for covariate selection based on conditional independence information. While this method is known to be sound and complete in acyclic causal models, its validity in the presence of cycles has remained unclear. Our main contribution is to show that these guarantees extend to cyclic causal models. In particular, our result relies on the invariance of conditional independence assertions under $σ$-acyclification. These findings establish a unified, cycle-agnostic perspective on covariate selection and causal effect estimation, showing that the method applies across cyclic and acyclic settings without modification. Empirically, we validate this on extensive synthetic data, showing reliable performance in cyclic causal models.

</details>


### [297] [Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation: Bridging Exploitation and Imitation at the Token Level](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06387)
*Nan Jia,Haojin Yang,Xing Ma,Jiesong Lian,Shuailiang Zhang,Weipeng Zhang,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai,Zequn Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies three weaknesses in standard On-Policy Distillation (OPD) — high variance updates, vanishing gradients, and exploration bottlenecks — and proposes Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD), which replaces negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions. AOPD achieves consistent improvements on math reasoning benchmarks (4.09/8.34 average gains) with better entropy retention and capability preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard on-policy distillation (OPD) suffers from three structural weaknesses: high variance updates, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks when corrective signals are insufficient.

Method: Proposes Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD), which replaces ineffective negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions while preserving positive reinforcement learning.

Result: AOPD consistently outperforms standard OPD, with average gains of 4.09 / 8.34 under strong / weak initialization on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. It also maintains higher policy entropy during training and better capability retention during sequential tool-use adaptation.

Conclusion: AOPD is an effective improvement over standard OPD, addressing its key weaknesses and leading to better performance, exploration, and capability retention.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on its own trajectories with token-level teacher feedback and often outperforms off-policy distillation and standard reinforcement learning. However, we find that its standard advantage weighted policy gradient suffers from three structural weaknesses, including high variance updates, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks when corrective signals are insufficient.We therefore propose Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD), which replaces ineffective negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions while preserving positive reinforcement learning. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that AOPD consistently outperforms standard OPD, with average gains of 4.09 / 8.34 under strong / weak initialization, respectively. AOPD also maintains higher policy entropy during training and better capability retention during sequential tool-use adaptation.

</details>


### [298] [SparseForge: Efficient Semi-Structured LLM Sparsification via Annealing of Hessian-Guided Soft-Mask](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06402)
*Liu Hanzuo,Chaofan Lin,Weixuan Sun,Yulong Wang,Key,Rayying,Mingyu Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SparseForge is a post-training framework for semi-structured sparsity in LLMs that optimizes sparsity masks directly using Hessian-aware importance estimation and progressive annealing, achieving better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs (e.g., 57.27% accuracy with 5B tokens vs. state-of-the-art 57.52% with 40B tokens on LLaMA-2-7B 2:4 sparsity).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training semi-structured pruning degrades quality significantly, and existing methods require large-scale retraining (e.g., 40B tokens) to recover accuracy, which is computationally expensive.

Method: SparseForge combines Hessian-aware importance estimation with progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity, directly optimizing the sparsity mask instead of scaling up retraining tokens.

Result: On LLaMA-2-7B with 2:4 sparsity, SparseForge achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy using only 5B retraining tokens, surpassing the dense model (56.43%) and approaching the state-of-the-art (57.52%) which requires 40B tokens.

Conclusion: SparseForge significantly improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off in sparse LLM recovery, with consistent gains across model families.

Abstract: Semi-structured sparsity provides a practical path to accelerate large language models (LLMs) with native hardware support, but post-training semi-structured pruning often suffers from substantial quality degradation due to strong structural coupling. Existing methods rely on large-scale sparse retraining to recover accuracy, resulting in high computational cost.
  We propose SparseForge, a post-training framework that improves recovery efficiency by directly optimizing the sparsity mask rather than scaling up retraining tokens. SparseForge combines Hessian-aware importance estimation with progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity, enabling stable and efficient sparse recovery. On LLaMA-2-7B under 2:4 sparsity, SparseForge achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy with only $\textbf{5B}$ retraining tokens, surpassing the dense model's 56.43% accuracy and approaching the 57.52% result of a state-of-the-art method using $\textbf{40B}$ tokens. Such improvements on the accuracy-efficiency trade-off from SparseForge are shown to be consistent across model families.

</details>


### [299] [FRInGe: Distribution-Space Integrated Gradients with Fisher--Rao Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06404)
*Gabriele Martino,Sebastian Tschiatschek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FRInGe improves Integrated Gradients by using a maximum-entropy predictive reference and Fisher-Rao geodesic paths in probability space, leading to better calibration-oriented attribution metrics across ImageNet models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrated Gradients (IG) explanations are brittle due to heuristic baselines, straight-line paths, discretization, and saturation issues.

Method: FRInGe replaces input baselines with a maximum-entropy predictive reference and follows a Fisher-Rao geodesic on the probability simplex, with input-space trajectory realized through pullback Fisher metric and stabilized by KL and Euclidean trust regions.

Result: Across six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe improves calibration-oriented attribution metrics (especially MAS scores) while remaining competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity.

Conclusion: Moving attribution paths from input space to predictive distribution space yields more reliable and calibration-friendly explanations.

Abstract: Gradient-based attribution methods are model-faithful and scalable, but Integrated Gradients (IG) can be brittle because explanations depend on heuristic baselines, straight-line paths, discretization, and saturation. We propose Fisher--Rao Integrated Gradients (FRInGe), which defines both the reference and interpolation schedule in predictive distribution space. FRInGe replaces input baselines with a maximum-entropy predictive reference and follows a Fisher-Rao geodesic on the probability simplex. The corresponding input-space trajectory is realized through the pullback Fisher metric and stabilized by KL and Euclidean trust regions; attributions are obtained by integrating input gradients along this trajectory. Across six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe most clearly improves calibration-oriented attribution metrics, especially MAS scores, while remaining competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity.

</details>


### [300] [Federated Cross-Client Subgraph Pattern Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06433)
*Selin Ceydeli,Rui Wang,Kubilay Atasu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses the representation-equivalence gap in distributed subgraph pattern detection via GNNs, proposing a per-step layer-wise embedding exchange framework that recovers centralized-equivalent node representations without exposing raw data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized GNN-based subgraph detection fails when graphs are distributed across parties, as client-local computations diverge from centralized ones due to a structural observability problem—partition-boundary-crossing patterns become locally unidentifiable.

Method: A per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange framework where clients synchronize intermediate node representations at each forward layer (fresh per-step, not stale per-epoch), under an extended-subgraph assumption and shared model parameters, without exposing raw features or labels.

Result: On synthetic directed multigraphs (cycles, bicliques, scatter-gather patterns), embedding exchange combined with federated parameter aggregation recovers most of the representation gap, and fresh per-step exchange outperforms stale per-epoch exchange.

Conclusion: Embedding exchange and federated parameter aggregation are complementary, not interchangeable; their combination effectively bridges the representation-equivalence gap for distributed subgraph detection.

Abstract: Subgraph pattern detection aims to uncover complex interaction structures in graphs. However, state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN)-based solutions assume centralized access to the entire graph. When graphs are instead distributed across multiple parties, client-local GNN computations diverge from those of a centralized model, resulting in a representation-equivalence gap. We formalize this as a structural observability problem, where subgraph patterns crossing partition boundaries become locally unidentifiable. To bridge this gap, we propose a per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange framework in which clients synchronize intermediate node representations at each layer of the forward pass, without exposing raw features or labels. Under an extended-subgraph assumption and shared model parameters across clients, this framework recovers the same node representations as a centralized GNN over the full graph. Experiments on synthetic directed multigraphs with cycles, bicliques, and scatter-gather patterns show that embedding exchange and federated parameter aggregation are complementary rather than interchangeable: their combination recovers most of the representation gap, provided exchanged embeddings are fresh per-step rather than stale per-epoch.

</details>


### [301] [FedFrozen: Two-Stage Federated Optimization via Attention Kernel Freezing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06446)
*Junye Du,Zhenghao Li,Yushi Feng,Long Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedFrozen is a two-stage federated learning framework for Transformers that first warm-ups the full model then freezes query/key blocks to optimize only value blocks, improving stability under heterogeneous clients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning with heterogeneous clients suffers from client drift. Transformers show inherent robustness, motivating investigation into which attention components contribute to this robustness.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) full-model warm-up training, (2) freeze query/key blocks while continuing value block optimization. Linear-attention formulation analysis reveals interpretation as regularized optimization.

Result: Simulations validate predicted bias-drift behavior. Real-data experiments show improved stability and effectiveness of Transformers in heterogeneous federated learning.

Conclusion: FedFrozen improves Transformer-based federated learning under heterogeneity. The warm-up length choice involves an explicit trade-off.

Abstract: Federated learning with heterogeneous clients remains a significant challenge for deep learning, primarily due to client drift arising from inconsistent local updates. Existing federated optimization methods typically address this issue through objective-level regularization or update-correction mechanisms. Recent studies, however, suggest that Transformer-based architectures may be inherently more robust than conventional models under heterogeneous federated training. Motivated by this observation, we investigate how different parameter components within the attention mechanism influence federated optimization. Specifically, we decompose the attention module into a query/key block, which determines the attention kernel, and a value block, which performs semantic transformation under the induced kernel. Based on this perspective, we propose FedFrozen, a two-stage federated optimization framework that first performs full-model warm-up training and then freezes the query/key block while continuing to optimize the value block. Under a linear-attention formulation, we show that the warm-up stage can be interpreted as an inexact descent procedure on a regularized kernel-profile objective, while the frozen stage reduces to a restricted value-block optimization problem under a fixed attention kernel. Our analysis further reveals an explicit trade-off that governs the choice of warm-up length. Simulations validate the predicted bias-drift behavior, and real-data experiments demonstrate that FedFrozen improves both the stability and effectiveness of Transformer models in heterogeneous federated learning.

</details>


### [302] [Scene-Adaptive Continual Learning for CSI-based Human Activity Recognition with Mixture of Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06447)
*Wenhan Zheng,Yuyi Mao,Ivan Wang-Hei Ho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAMoE-C proposes a mixture-of-experts approach with a semantic router for scalable cross-domain CSI-based human activity recognition, achieving high accuracy with low inference cost and minimal replay buffer requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CSI-based HAR suffers from performance degradation under domain shifts across different physical environments, and existing continual learning solutions scale poorly, rely on large replay buffers, or have linearly growing inference costs.

Method: Proposes Scene-Adaptive Mixture of Experts with Clustered Specialists (SAMoE-C), which uses an attention-based semantic router to selectively activate only relevant experts per input, combined with a training protocol requiring only a tiny replay buffer.

Result: On a four-scene CSI dataset, SAMoE-C achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art methods while maintaining significantly lower inference cost.

Conclusion: SAMoE-C enables scalable, efficient cross-domain CSI-based HAR deployment with low training overhead and high computational efficiency in real-world settings.

Abstract: Channel state information (CSI)-based human activity recognition (HAR) is vulnerable to performance degradation under domain shifts across varying physical environments. Continual learning (CL) offers a principled way to learn new domains sequentially while preserving past knowledge, but existing CL solutions for CSI-based HAR scale poorly with accumulating domains, rely on a large replay buffer, or incur linearly growing inference cost. In this letter, we propose Scene-Adaptive Mixture of Experts with Clustered Specialists (SAMoE-C), which formulates cross-domain CSI-based HAR as a mixture-of-experts system that enables scene-specific adaptation, via an attention-based semantic router that activates only selected experts for each input. Moreover, we develop a novel training protocol, which requires only a tiny replay buffer for stabilizing domain discrimination of the router. Experimental results on a four-scene CSI dataset demonstrate that SAMoE-C approaches the state-of-the-art accuracy, while maintaining a significantly lower inference cost. By jointly combining modular experts, selective activation with router and a lightweight training protocol, SAMoE-C enables scalable cross-domain CSI-based HAR deployment with low training overhead and high computational efficiency in real-world settings.

</details>


### [303] [ORTHOBO: Orthogonal Bayesian Hyperparameter Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06454)
*Maresa Schröder,Pascal Janetzky,Michael Klar,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies acquisition estimation noise (Monte Carlo error) as an overlooked failure mode in Bayesian optimization, and proposes OrthoBO—a framework using an orthogonal acquisition estimator with control variates to reduce variance, stabilize rankings, and improve hyperparameter optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Acquisition estimation noise from finite-sample Monte Carlo error is an overlooked failure mode that perturbs acquisition values, flips candidate rankings, and leads to suboptimal BO decisions.

Method: Orthogonal acquisition estimator using optimally weighted score-function control variates to produce acquisition residuals orthogonal to posterior score directions, combined with ensemble surrogates and outer log transformation.

Result: Theoretical guarantees show variance reduction and improved pairwise ranking stability; numerical experiments confirm reduced estimation variance, stabilized rankings, and strong performance; downstream utility shown in neural network hyperparameter optimization.

Conclusion: OrthoBO effectively mitigates acquisition estimation noise, leading to more stable and reliable Bayesian optimization decisions.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization is widely used for hyperparameter optimization when model evaluations are expensive; however, noisy acquisition estimates can lead to unstable decisions. We identify acquisition estimation noise as a failure mode that was previously overlooked: even when the surrogate model and acquisition target are correctly specified, finite-sample Monte Carlo error can perturb acquisition values. This can, in turn, flip candidate rankings and lead to suboptimal BO decisions. As a remedy, we aim at variance reduction and propose an orthogonal acquisition estimator that subtracts an optimally weighted score-function control variate, which yields an acquisition residual orthogonal to posterior score directions and which thus reduces Monte Carlo variance. We further introduce OrthoBO: a Bayesian optimization framework that combines our orthogonal acquisition estimator with ensemble surrogates and an outer log transformation. We show theoretically that our estimator preserves the target, leads to variance reduction, and improves pairwise ranking stability. We further verify the theoretical properties of OrthoBO through numerical experiments where our framework reduces acquisition estimation variance, stabilizes candidate rankings, and achieves strong performance. We also demonstrate the downstream utility of OrthoBO in hyperparameter optimization for neural network training and fine-tuning.

</details>


### [304] [Invariant Features in Language Models: Geometric Characterization and Model Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06458)
*Agnibh Dasgupta,Abdullah Tanvir,Xin Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Language models encode semantic invariance as structured geometric subspaces in latent space, where paraphrastic variation is separated from semantic content; this enables contrastive subspace discovery, causal analysis, and zero-shot model attribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models exhibit robustness to paraphrasing, suggesting stable internal semantic representations, yet the structure and origin of such invariance remain poorly understood.

Method: The authors propose a local geometric framework treating semantically equivalent inputs as occupying structured regions in latent space, with paraphrastic variation along nuisance directions and semantic identity preserved in invariant subspaces. They develop a contrastive subspace discovery method to separate semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation.

Result: Invariant structure emerges in specific depth regions; semantic displacement lies largely outside the nuisance subspace; representation-level interventions show causal roles of invariant components; invariant representations capture model-specific patterns enabling accurate zero-shot attribution.

Conclusion: Semantic invariance can be viewed as a local geometric property of latent representations, offering a principled perspective on how language models organize meaning.

Abstract: Language models exhibit strong robustness to paraphrasing, suggesting that semantic information may be encoded through stable internal representations, yet the structure and origin of such invariance remain unclear. We propose a local geometric framework in which semantically equivalent inputs occupy structured regions in latent space, with paraphrastic variation along nuisance directions and semantic identity preserved in invariant subspaces. Building on this view, we make three contributions: (1) a geometric characterization of invariant latent features, (2) a contrastive subspace discovery method that separates semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation, and (3) an application of invariant representations to zero-shot model attribution. Across models and layers, empirical results support these contributions. Invariant structure emerges in specific depth regions, semantic displacement lies largely outside the nuisance subspace, and representation-level interventions indicate a causal role of invariant components in model outputs. Invariant representations also capture model-specific geometric patterns, enabling accurate attribution. These findings suggest that semantic invariance can be viewed as a local geometric property of latent representations, offering a principled perspective on how language models organize meaning.

</details>


### [305] [MINER: Mining Multimodal Internal Representation for Efficient Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06460)
*Weien Li,Rui Song,Zeyu Li,Haochen Liu,Gonghao Zhang,Difan Jiao,Zhenwei Tang,Bowei He,Haolun Wu,Xue Liu,Ye Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MINER is a lightweight plug-in module that probes and fuses internal signals across transformer layers into a single compact embedding, achieving strong retrieval quality while preserving the storage and latency advantages of dense single-vector retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing late-interaction retrievers achieve strong quality but store hundreds of vectors per page, incurring large index footprints and high costs. Dense single-vector retrievers are efficient but lag in quality because they compress all information into a single final-layer embedding. The authors aim to bridge this quality gap without sacrificing efficiency.

Method: MINER has two stages: (1) Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing - attaches lightweight probes at each layer to identify which dimensions carry retrieval-relevant signal, and (2) Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion - applies performance-adaptive neuron-level masking to selected layers and fuses surviving signals into a single compact dense vector.

Result: On ViDoRe V1/V2/V3, MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on most benchmarks with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement. In some settings, it narrows the gap to strong late-interaction baselines to only 0.2 nDCG@5 while preserving dense retrieval's efficiency advantages.

Conclusion: Internal representations across transformer layers contain retrieval-relevant signal that can be effectively mined and fused into a single embedding, allowing dense single-vector retrievers to approach late-interaction quality without sacrificing storage or latency benefits.

Abstract: Visual document retrieval has become essential for accessing information in visually rich documents. Existing approaches fall into two camps. Late-interaction retrievers achieve strong quality through fine-grained token-level matching but store hundreds of vectors per page, incurring large index footprints and high serving costs. By contrast, dense single-vector retrievers retain storage and latency advantages but consistently lag in quality because they compress all information into a single final-layer embedding. In this work, we first conduct a layerwise diagnostic on single-vector retrievers, revealing that retrieval-relevant signal resides in internal representations. Motivated by these findings, we propose MINER (Mining Multimodal Internal RepreseNtation for Efficient Retrieval), a lightweight plug-in module that probes and fuses internal signals across transformer layers into a single compact embedding without modifying the backbone or sacrificing single-vector efficiency. The first Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing stage attaches a lightweight probe at each layer, surfacing which dimensions carry retrieval-relevant information. The subsequent Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion stage applies performance-adaptive neuron-level masking to the selected layers and fuses the surviving signals into the final dense vector. Across ViDoRe V1/V2/V3, MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on the majority of benchmarks, with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement over its corresponding backbone. Compared to strong late-interaction baselines, in some settings MINER substantially narrows the nDCG@$5$ gap to $0.2$ while preserving the storage and serving advantages of dense retrieval.

</details>


### [306] [Invariant-Based Diagnostics for Graph Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06462)
*Richard von Moos,Mathieu Alain,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using graph invariants (permutation-invariant, task-agnostic structural descriptors) as a diagnostic framework for graph benchmarks, showing they are more expressive than standard GNNs, predict multi-task performance, and often match or exceed trained message-passing models across 26 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmark practices conflate contributions of node features and graph structure, making it unclear whether models actually learn from connectivity or even need to. This hinders progress on graph foundation models.

Method: The authors propose using graph invariants—permutation-invariant, task-agnostic structural descriptors—as a diagnostic framework. They analyze expressivity, structural heterogeneity, multi-task performance prediction, and compare invariant-based models against transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets.

Result: Graph invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs; they characterize structural heterogeneity within/across datasets; they predict multi-task performance; and simple invariant-based models are competitive with, and sometimes exceed, transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets.

Conclusion: Expressivity is not the main driver of predictive performance. Non-trainable structural proxies often match trained message-passing models. Invariant baselines should become standard for evaluating whether structure is needed for a task and whether a model captures it.

Abstract: Progress on graph foundation models is hindered by benchmark practices that conflate the contributions of node features and graph structure, making it hard to tell whether a model actually learns from connectivity, or whether it even needs to. We propose addressing this using graph invariants, i.e., permutation-invariant, task-agnostic structural descriptors that serve as a diagnostic framework for graph benchmarks. We show that (i) invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs, (ii) invariants characterize structural heterogeneity within and across benchmark datasets, (iii) invariants predict multi-task performance, and (iv) simple invariant-based models are competitive with, and sometimes exceed, transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets. Our results suggest that expressivity is not the main driver of predictive performance, and that on tasks where structure matters, a non-trainable structural proxy often matches trained message-passing models. We thus posit that invariant baselines should become a standard for evaluating whether structure is required for a task and whether a model picks up on it, serving as a stepping stone towards graph foundation models.

</details>


### [307] [Diversity Curves for Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06466)
*Katharina Limbeck,Nadja Häusermann,Martin Carrasco,Guy Wolf,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "diversity curves" — graph-level embeddings that track structural diversity across coarsening levels using a novel isometry invariant called "spread." These embeddings are interpretable, scalable, and directly comparable across graphs of different sizes, with proven expressivity improvements over structural descriptors. Applications include clustering simulated graphs, analyzing single-cell data, comparing molecular datasets, and characterizing geometric shapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Comparing graphs with different cardinalities (sizes) sampled from the same distribution is challenging. Existing unsupervised graph-level representations often lack interpretability, scalability, and size-awareness. The authors aim to develop a method that addresses these gaps.

Method: The method tracks the structural diversity of a graph across coarsening levels by measuring the "spread" (a novel isometry invariant) at each level, producing diversity curves. Edge contraction coarsening is used, and the authors prove this improves expressivity compared to structural descriptors alone.

Result: Diversity curves outperform baseline methods in four tasks: (i) clustering and visualizing simulated graphs of varying sizes, (ii) distinguishing geometries of single-cell graphs, (iii) comparing molecular graph datasets, and (iv) characterizing geometric shapes.

Conclusion: The proposed diversity curves provide interpretable, efficient, and directly comparable graph-level representations that effectively handle graphs of different sizes, with proven theoretical advantages and strong empirical performance across diverse domains.

Abstract: Graph-level representations are crucial tools for characterising structural differences between graphs. However, comparing graphs with different cardinalities, even when sampled from the same underlying distribution, remains challenging. Unsupervised tasks in particular require interpretable, scalable, and reliable size-aware graph representations. Our work addresses these issues by tracking the structural diversity of a graph across coarsening levels. The resulting graph embeddings, which we denote diversity curves, are interpretable by construction, efficient, and directly comparable across coarsening hierarchies. Specifically, we track the spread of graphs, a novel isometry invariant that is inherently well-suited for encoding the metric diversity and geometry of graphs. We utilise edge contraction coarsening and prove that this improves expressivity, thus leading to more powerful graph-level representations than structural descriptors alone. Demonstrating their utility over a range of baseline methods in practice, we use diversity curves to (i) cluster and visualise simulated graphs across varying sizes, (ii) distinguish the geometry of single-cell graphs, (iii) compare the structure of molecular graph datasets, and (iv) characterise geometric shapes.

</details>


### [308] [No Triangulation Without Representation: Generalization in Topological Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06467)
*Johannes S. Schmidt,Martin Carrasco,Ernst Röell,Guy Wolf,Nello Blaser,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends the MANTRA benchmark for topological deep learning, shows that both GNNs and higher-order methods can saturate it with proper feature engineering, introduces a new evaluation protocol using representational diversity and triangulation refinement, and finds that existing models fail to generalize beyond combinatorial structure, highlighting a gap in topology-aware learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no consensus on how to evaluate topological deep learning models, especially since topological objects support operations like structural refinements that are not suitable for graph data.

Method: Extended MANTRA to more diverse manifolds, evaluated GNNs and HOMP methods, and proposed a new evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement.

Result: Both GNNs and HOMP methods can saturate the benchmark with appropriate representation and feature assignment; however, no existing model generalizes beyond the combinatorial structure of the data.

Conclusion: There is a research gap in developing models that understand topological structure independent of scale, and the proposed scaffolding can guide future topology-aware models.

Abstract: Despite an ever-increasing interest in topological deep learning models that target higher-order datasets, there is no consensus on how to evaluate such models. This is exacerbated by the fact that topological objects permit operations, such as structural refinements, that are not appropriate for graph data. In this work, we extend MANTRA, a benchmark dataset containing manifold triangulations, to a larger class of manifolds with more diverse homeomorphism types. We show that, unlike prior claims, both graph neural networks (GNNs) and higher-order message passing (HOMP) methods can saturate the benchmark. However, we find that this is contingent on the right representation and feature assignment, emphasizing their importance in baseline models. We thus provide a novel evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement. Surprisingly, we find no indication that existing models are capable of generalizing beyond the combinatorial structure of the data. This points towards a research gap in developing models that understand topological structure independent of scale. Our work thus provides the necessary scaffolding to evaluate future models and enable the development of topology-aware inductive biases.

</details>


### [309] [Hitting Time Isomorphism for Multi-Stage Planning with Foundation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06470)
*Magnus Victor Boock,Abdullah Akgül,Mustafa Mert Çelikok,Melih Kandemir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new operator-theoretic representation learning framework (IEL) for offline RL that learns asymmetric Hilbert-space displacement geometry from hitting times, with theoretical guarantees and improved maze navigation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior methods produce symmetric distances or violate triangle inequality, failing to capture the directed temporal geometry of controlled Markov processes from hitting time observations.

Method: Proposes Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL) — a goal-agnostic foundation policy learning algorithm using Hilbert-space displacements, HILP-style consistency objectives, and explicit hitting-time regression to learn asymmetric, compositional temporal geometry.

Result: IEL improves state-of-the-art offline foundation policy learning on maze locomotion tasks; finite-sample guarantees show bounded hitting-time error related to one-step transition error and transient spectral radius.

Conclusion: Learning asymmetric, compositional temporal geometry via operator-theoretic embedding enables robust multi-stage planning for long-horizon navigation in offline RL.

Abstract: We present a new operator-theoretic representation learning framework for offline reinforcement learning that recovers the directed temporal geometry of a controlled Markov process from hitting time observations. While prior art often produces symmetric distances or fails to satisfy the triangle inequality, our framework learns a Hilbert-space displacement geometry where expected hitting times are realized as linear functionals of latent displacements. We prove that this representation exists under latent linear closure and is uniquely identifiable up to a bounded linear isomorphism. For finite-dimensional implementations, we show that global hitting-time error is bounded by one-step transition error amplified by the environment's transient spectral radius. Furthermore, we provide finite-sample guarantees accounting for approximation, statistical complexity, and trajectory-label mismatch. Derived from this theory, we curate Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL) as a new goal-agnostic foundation policy learning algorithm that anchors a HILP-style consistency objective with explicit hitting-time regression to ensure that the learned geometry reflects actual decision-time progress. This asymmetric and compositional structure enables robust graph-based multi-stage planning for long-horizon navigation. Our experiments demonstrate that IEL improves the state of the art of learning foundation policy policies from offline maze locomotion data. Our code can be found on https://github.com/MagnusBoock/IEL

</details>


### [310] [Efficient Serving for Dynamic Agent Workflows with Prediction-based KV-Cache Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06472)
*Haoyu Zheng,Fangcheng Fu,Jia Wu,Binhang Yuan,Yongqiang Zhang,Hao Wang,Yuanyuan Zhu,Xiao Yan,Jiawei Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PBKV is a prediction-based KV-Cache management system that speeds up dynamic LLM agent workflows by predicting future agent invocations to optimize cache reuse, achieving up to 1.85× speedup over LRU and 1.26× over KVFlow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KV-Cache management methods either fail to exploit reuse opportunities in dynamic agent workflows (agent-level) or assume static agent sequences (workflow-level), but real-world workflows are dynamic—agent invocation order varies per task context.

Method: PBKV predicts future agent invocations by fusing historical workflow patterns with target workflow context, estimates cache entry reuse potential, keeps high-potential entries in GPU memory, and uses conservative strategies for cache eviction and prefetching to handle prediction errors.

Result: PBKV achieves up to 1.85× speedup over LRU on dynamic workflows and up to 1.26× speedup over the SOTA baseline KVFlow on static workflows.

Conclusion: Prediction-based KV-Cache management is effective for dynamic LLM workflows, outperforming both agent-level and static workflow-level caching approaches.

Abstract: LLM-based workflows compose specialized agents to execute complex tasks, and these agents usually share substantial context, allowing KV-Cache reuse to save computation. Existing approaches either manage KV-Cache at agent level and fail to exploit the reuse opportunities within workflows, or manage cache at the workflow level but assume that each workflow calls a static sequence of agents. However, practical workflows are typically dynamic, where the sequence of invoked agents and thus induced cache reuse opportunities depend on the context of each task. To serve such dynamic workflows efficiently, we build a system dubbed PBKV (\textbf{P}rediction-\textbf{B}ased \textbf{KV}-Cache Management). For each workflow, PBKV predicts the agent invocations in several future steps by fusing the guidance from historical workflows and context of the target workflow. Based on the predictions, PBKV estimates the reuse potential of cache entries and keeps the high-potential entries in GPU memory. To be robust to prediction errors, PBKV utilizes the predictions conservatively during both cache eviction and prefetching. Experiments on three workflow benchmarks show that PBKV achieves up to $1.85\times$ speedup over LRU on dynamic workflows, and up to $1.26\times$ speedup over the SOTA baseline KVFlow on the static workflow.

</details>


### [311] [Q-MMR: Off-Policy Evaluation via Recursive Reweighting and Moment Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06474)
*Xiang Li,Nan Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-MMR is a novel off-policy evaluation framework that learns scalar weights for each data point via moment matching, achieving dimension-free finite-sample guarantees under only realizability of Q^π, with connections to importance sampling and linear FQE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an off-policy evaluation method with strong theoretical guarantees that does not depend on the statistical complexity of the function class, addressing limitations of existing approaches.

Method: Q-MMR learns scalar weights for each data point via a top-down inductive process using moment matching against a value-function discriminator class, reweighting rewards to approximate target policy expected returns.

Result: Achieves data-dependent finite-sample guarantee under only realizability of Q^π with a dimension-free error bound; establishes connections to importance sampling and linear FQE.

Conclusion: Q-MMR provides a theoretically grounded OPE method with favorable guarantees, and offers new insights into the nature of coverage in offline RL.

Abstract: We present a novel theoretical framework, Q-MMR, for off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs. Q-MMR learns a set of scalar weights, one for each data point, such that the reweighted rewards approximate the expected return under the target policy. The weights are learned inductively in a top-down manner via a moment matching objective against a value-function discriminator class. Notably, and perhaps surprisingly, a data-dependent finite-sample guarantee for general function approximation can be established under only the realizability of $Q^π$, with a dimension-free bound -- that is, the error does not depend on the statistical complexity of the function class. We also establish connections to several existing methods, such as importance sampling and linear FQE. Further theoretical analyses shed new light on the nature of coverage, a concept of fundamental importance to offline RL.

</details>


### [312] [Operator-Guided Invariance Learning for Continuous Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06500)
*Zuyuan Zhang,Fei Xu Yu,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VPSD-RL discovers exact and approximate value-preserving structures in continuous RL via Lie-group actions, improving data efficiency and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous RL is data-intensive and brittle under nuisance variability/shift; existing methods focus on special cases (prescribed symmetries, exact equivariance) without discovering more general value-preserving structures requiring nonlinear operators.

Method: Models continuous RL as controlled diffusion; uses Lie-group actions and pullback operators for value-preserving mappings; searches for Lie group operators via determining-equation residual minimization; exponentiates them via ODE flows; integrates via transition augmentation and transformation-consistency regularization.

Result: Shows bounded generator/reward mismatch implies quantitative stability of optimal value; observes improved data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.

Conclusion: VPSD-RL provides a principled framework for discovering both exact and approximate value-preserving structures, with theoretical guarantees and practical gains in continuous RL.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) with continuous time and state/action spaces is often data-intensive and brittle under nuisance variability and shift, motivating methods that exploit value-preserving structures to stabilize and improve learning. Most existing approaches focus on special cases, such as prescribed symmetries and exact equivariance, without addressing how to discover more general structures that require nonlinear operators to transform and map between continuous state/action systems with isomorphic value functions. We propose \textbf{VPSD-RL} (Value-Preserving Structure Discovery for Reinforcement Learning). It models continuous RL as a controlled diffusion with value-preserving mappings defined through Lie-group actions and associated pullback operators. We show that a value-preserving structure exists exactly when pulling back the value function and pushing forward actions commute with the controlled generator and reward functional. Further, approximate value-preserving structures with rigorous guarantees can be found when the Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman mismatch is small. This framework discovers exact and approximate value-preserving structures by searching for the associated Lie group operators. VPSD-RL fits differentiable drift, diffusion, and reward models; learns infinitesimal generators via determining-equation residual minimization; exponentiates them with ODE flows to obtain finite transformations; and integrates them into continuous RL through transition augmentation and transformation-consistency regularization. We show that bounded generator/reward mismatch implies quantitative stability of the optimal value function along approximate orbits, with sensitivity governed by the effective horizon, and observe improved data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.

</details>


### [313] [Cubit: Token Mixer with Kernel Ridge Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06501)
*Chuanyang Zheng,Jiankai Sun,Yihang Gao,Yuehao Wang,Liangchen Tan,Mac Schwager,Anderson Schneider,Yuriy Nevmyvaka,Xiaodong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Cubit, a Transformer variant that replaces the standard attention mechanism (Nadaraya-Watson regression) with Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR), achieving stronger long-sequence modeling performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a stronger mathematical foundation for Transformers by replacing the attention mechanism (interpreted as Nadaraya-Watson regression) with Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR), which offers more principled kernel-based learning.

Method: Cubit modifies attention by incorporating the closed-form solution of KRR, combining kernel similarity-based value aggregation with normalization via the inverse kernel matrix. It also introduces Limited-Range Rescale (LRR) to stabilize training by rescaling the value layer within a controlled range.

Result: Cubit shows stronger long-sequence modeling capability, and its performance advantage over vanilla Transformer grows as the training sequence length increases.

Conclusion: Cubit, as a KRR-based architecture, provides a stronger mathematical foundation than vanilla Transformer's attention (Nadaraya-Watson regression) and demonstrates promising long-sequence performance.

Abstract: Since its introduction in 2017, the Transformer has become one of the most widely adopted architectures in modern deep learning. Despite extensive efforts to improve positional encoding, attention mechanisms, and feed-forward networks, the core token-mixing mechanism in Transformers remains attention. In this work, we show that the attention module in Transformers can be interpreted as performing Nadaraya-Watson regression, where it computes similarities between tokens and aggregates the corresponding values accordingly. Motivated by this perspective, we propose Cubit, a potential next-generation architecture that leverages Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR), while the vanilla Transformer relies on Nadaraya-Watson regression. Specifically, Cubit modifies the classical attention computation by incorporating the closed-form solution of KRR, combining value aggregation through kernel similarities with normalization via the inverse of the kernel matrix. To improve the training stability, we further propose the Limited-Range Rescale (LRR), which rescales the value layer within a controlled range. We argue that Cubit, as a KRR-based architecture, provides a stronger mathematical foundation than the vanilla Transformer, whose attention mechanism corresponds to Nadaraya-Watson regression. We validate this claim through comprehensive experiments. The experimental results suggest that Cubit may exhibit stronger long-sequence modeling capability. In particular, its performance gain over the Transformer appears to increase as the training sequence length grows.

</details>


### [314] [PACZero: PAC-Private Fine-Tuning of Language Models via Sign Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06505)
*Murat Bilgehan Ertan,Xiaochen Zhu,Phuong Ha Nguyen,Marten van Dijk,Srinivas Devadas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACZero introduces PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms for fine-tuning LLMs that achieve usable utility even at zero mutual information (I=0), using sign-quantized subset-aggregated gradients and a unanimity-based approach to eliminate conditional mutual information during steps where all candidate subsets agree.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DP-based fine-tuning for LLMs requires a trade-off between privacy (epsilon) and utility, and cannot achieve useful utility at epsilon<1. The authors aim to achieve strong MIA resistance (posterior equal to prior) while maintaining usable utility, which DP can only match at epsilon=0 with infinite noise.

Method: PACZero uses PAC Privacy to charge mutual information only when the release depends on which candidate subset is the secret. It sign-quantizes subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients, exploiting frequent unanimity steps where all subsets agree on update direction—these steps cost zero conditional mutual information. Two variants: PACZero-MI (budgeted MI via exact calibration) and PACZero-ZPL (I=0 via uniform coin flip on disagreement steps).

Result: On SST-2 with OPT-1.3B full fine-tuning at I=0, PACZero-ZPL achieves 88.99±0.91 accuracy, within 2.1pp of non-private MeZO baseline (91.1). No prior method produces usable utility at epsilon<1. PACZero-ZPL also obtains competitive SST-2 accuracy and nontrivial SQuAD F1 across OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B at I=0.

Conclusion: PACZero achieves a previously unattainable point in the privacy-utility trade-off: usable utility at zero mutual information (I=0), which corresponds to perfect MIA resistance (posterior success rate equal to prior). This demonstrates that PAC Privacy provides a more favorable framework than DP for private LLM fine-tuning.

Abstract: We introduce PACZero, a family of PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms for fine-tuning large language models that delivers usable utility at $I(S^*; Y_{1:T})=0$. This privacy regime bounds the membership-inference attack (MIA) posterior success rate at the prior, an MIA-resistance level the DP framework matches only at $\varepsilon=0$ and infinite noise. All DP-ZO comparisons below are matched at the MIA posterior level. The key insight is that PAC Privacy charges mutual information only when the release depends on which candidate subset is the secret. Sign-quantizing subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients creates frequent unanimity, steps at which every candidate subset agrees on the update direction; at these steps the released sign costs zero conditional mutual information. We propose two variants that span the privacy-utility trade-off: PACZero-MI (budgeted MI via exact calibration on the binary release) and PACZero-ZPL ($I=0$ via a uniform coin flip on disagreement steps). We evaluate on SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B in both LoRA and full-parameter tracks. On SST-2 OPT-1.3B full fine-tuning at $I=0$, PACZero-ZPL reaches ${88.99\pm0.91}$, within $2.1$pp of the non-private MeZO baseline ($91.1$ FT). No prior method produces usable utility in the high-privacy regime $\varepsilon<1$, and PACZero-ZPL obtains competitive SST-2 accuracy and nontrivial SQuAD F1 across OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B at $I=0$.

</details>


### [315] [Is One Layer Enough? Understanding Inference Dynamics in Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06510)
*Amir Rezaei Balef,Mykhailo Koshil,Katharina Eggensperger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents the first large-scale mechanistic analysis of layerwise dynamics in tabular foundation models, revealing depthwise redundancy and inference stages distinct from LLMs, then uses these insights to build a looped single-layer model with 20% of original parameters that achieves comparable performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer-based tabular foundation models (TFMs) dominate tabular predictive benchmarks, but their inference mechanisms remain largely unexplored. The authors aim to open the "black box" of how these models reason internally.

Method: Mechanistic study of layerwise dynamics across 6 state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models; analysis of prediction emergence across depth, identification of inference stages, and latent-space dynamics comparison with LLMs. A proof-of-concept looped single-layer model is designed based on discovered redundancy.

Result: Substantial depthwise redundancy exists across multiple TFMs, indicating iterative refinement with overlapping computations. The proposed looped single-layer model uses only 20% of original parameters yet achieves comparable performance.

Conclusion: Tabular foundation models exhibit fundamentally different internal dynamics from language models, with high layer redundancy suggesting that much fewer layers are needed. This opens new directions for efficient TFM design.

Abstract: Transformer-based tabular foundation models (TFMs) dominate small to medium tabular predictive benchmark tasks, yet their inference mechanisms remain largely unexplored. We present the first large-scale mechanistic study of layerwise dynamics in 6 state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models. We explore how predictions emerge across depth, identify distinct stages of inference and reveal latent-space dynamics that differ from those of language models. Our findings indicate substantial depthwise redundancy across multiple models, suggesting iterative refinement with overlapping computations during inference stages. Guided by these insights, we design a proof-of-concept, looped single-layer model that uses only 20% of the original model's parameters while achieving comparable performance. The code is available at https://github.com/amirbalef/is_one_layer_enough.

</details>


### [316] [Efficient Techniques for Data Reconstruction, with Finite-Width Recovery Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06519)
*Edward Tansley,Roy Makhlouf,Estelle Massart,Coralia Cartis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified optimization framework for data reconstruction attacks on neural networks, proves its effectiveness in random feature models with PAC-style bounds, and introduces a subspace-aware reconstruction algorithm that leverages low-dimensional data structure for improved efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data reconstruction attacks on trained neural networks can recover training data, posing serious privacy risks. The authors aim to develop a theoretically grounded and practically efficient reconstruction method.

Method: The authors propose a unified optimization formulation based on initial and trained parameter values. They analyze it theoretically using random feature models with PAC-style finite-width bounds. For general networks, they propose a subspace-aware algorithm that estimates the low-dimensional data subspace from first-layer weight changes and reconstructs data using only last-layer weights.

Result: Theoretical results show high-probability reconstruction in random feature models when width is large, with relaxed requirements if data lies in a low-dimensional subspace. Experiments on synthetic data and CIFAR-10 demonstrate that subspace-aware reconstruction outperforms standard full-space techniques.

Conclusion: The unified formulation and subspace-aware algorithm provide both theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency for data reconstruction attacks, highlighting privacy vulnerabilities in overparameterized neural networks.

Abstract: Data reconstruction attacks on trained neural networks aim to recover the data on which the network has been trained and pose a significant threat to privacy, especially if the training dataset contains sensitive information. Here, we propose a unified optimization formulation of the data reconstruction problem based on initial and trained parameter values, incorporating state-of-the-art proposals. We show that in the random feature model, this formulation provably leads to training data reconstruction with high probability, provided the network width is sufficiently large; this unprecedented finite-width result uses PAC-style bounds. Furthermore, when the data lies in a low-dimensional subspace, we show that the network width requirement for successful reconstruction can be relaxed, with bounds depending on the subspace dimension rather than the ambient dimension. For general neural network models and unknown data orientations, we propose an efficient reconstruction algorithm that approximates the low-dimensional data subspace through the change in the first-layer weights during training and uses only the last-layer weights for reconstruction, thus reducing the search space dimension and the required network width for high-quality reconstructions. Our numerical experiments on synthetic datasets and CIFAR-10 confirm that our subspace-aware reconstruction approach outperforms standard full-space techniques.

</details>


### [317] [On the Implicit Reward Overfitting and the Low-rank Dynamics in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06523)
*Hao Ye,Jisheng Dang,Junfeng Fang,Bimei Wang,Yizhou Zhang,Ning Lv,Wencan Zhang,Hong Peng,Bin Hu,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that RLVR's enhanced reasoning comes from rank-1 components, discovers implicit reward overfitting (good test performance despite low training rewards), and characterizes three properties: rank-1 components isolate math reasoning, RLVR optimizes singular spectrum with heavy-tailed distribution, and left singular vectors align during training to improve sampling efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how RLVR shapes model parameters and why models acquire reasoning capabilities through rank-1 components, revealing potential overfitting phenomena and structural changes during RL training.

Method: Periodic Rank-1 Substitution to analyze model behavior; singular value decomposition and spectral analysis of linear layers; alignment analysis of left singular vectors during training.

Result: (1) Implicit reward overfitting exists - models perform well on test sets despite low training rewards. (2) Rank-1 components in RLVR exclusively encode math reasoning, not other knowledge. (3) RLVR optimizes singular spectrum leading to heavy-tailed singular value distributions. (4) Left singular vectors show stronger alignment during training, indicating optimized sampling efficiency.

Conclusion: RLVR primarily shapes model parameters through rank-1 singular spectrum optimization, and these insights can help improve existing RL paradigms and enable continual learning approaches.

Abstract: Recent extensive research has demonstrated that the enhanced reasoning capabilities acquired by models through Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) are primarily concentrated within the rank-1 components. Predicated on this observation, we employed Periodic Rank-1 Substitution and identified a counterintuitive phenomenon: RLVR may exhibit implicit reward overfitting to the training dataset. Specifically, the model can achieve satisfactory performance on the test set even when its rewards remain relatively low during the training process. Furthermore, we characterize three distinct properties of RL training: (1) The effective rank-1 component in RLVR don't maintain other model knowledge except mathematical reasoning capability. (2) RLVR fundamentally functions by optimizing a specific singular spectrum. The distribution of singular values of almost all linear layers in RLVR-trained model behaves like heavy-tailed distribution. (3) the left singular vectors associated with rank-1 components demonstrate a stronger alignment tendency during training, which echoes the discovery that RLVR is optimizing sampling efficiency in essence. Taken together, our findings and analysis further reveal how RLVR shapes model parameters and offer potential insights for improving existing RL paradigms or other training paradigms to implement continual learning.

</details>


### [318] [Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling: A Feynman-Kac Analysis of Bias and Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06538)
*Matias G. Delgadino,Sebastien Motsch,Advait Parulekar,William Porteous,Sanjay Shakkottai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper characterizes the bias in diffusion-based posterior samplers (like DPS and STSL) by introducing a PDE-based surrogate path framework, deriving explicit Radon-Nikodym corrections, and explaining instabilities and mitigation strategies such as early guidance-stopping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based posterior samplers are widely used but their theoretical behavior, especially bias and low-temperature instability, remains poorly understood even with exact prior scores.

Method: The authors introduce a tractable surrogate path connecting the true posterior to a standard Gaussian, compare it to the sampler's path via a parabolic PDE for the density ratio, and use Feynman-Kac representation to derive an explicit path expectation for the Radon-Nikodym correction.

Result: For DPS, the correction is an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck path expectation coupling data conditional covariance with reward curvature. STSL is reinterpreted as an auxiliary drift that flattens the DPS reaction term by steering toward low-uncertainty regions. Early guidance-stopping is characterized as a mitigation for forward-Euler instability.

Conclusion: The framework clarifies sampler bias, explains existing correctives, and guides the design of stable variants for diffusion-based posterior sampling.

Abstract: Diffusion-based posterior samplers use pretrained diffusion priors to sample from measurement- or reward-conditioned posteriors, and are widely used for inverse problems. Yet their theoretical behavior remains poorly understood: even with exact prior scores, their outputs are biased, and in low-temperature regimes their discretizations can become unstable. We characterize this bias by introducing a tractable surrogate path connecting the true posterior to a standard Gaussian and comparing it to the sampler's path. Their density ratio satisfies a parabolic PDE whose reaction term measures the accumulated bias. A Feynman-Kac representation then expresses the Radon-Nikodym correction as an explicit path expectation, identifying which posterior regions are over- or under-sampled.
  We apply this framework to DPS and STSL, a related sampler. For DPS, the correction is an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck path expectation coupling the data conditional covariance with the reward curvature, revealing where DPS over- or under-samples. Next, we reinterpret STSL as an auxiliary drift that steers trajectories toward low-uncertainty regions, flattening the spatially varying part of the DPS reaction term. Finally, we characterize early guidance-stopping, a common mitigation for low-temperature instabilities caused by forward-Euler integration of the vector field. Together, these results clarify sampler bias, explain existing correctives, and guide stable variant designs.

</details>


### [319] [Hedging Memory Horizons for Non-Stationary Prediction via Online Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06541)
*Yutong Wang,Yannig Goude,Qiwei Yao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MELO is a model-agnostic online learning method that hedges across multiple adaptation scales to handle distribution shifts, achieving 34.7% RMSE reduction in French electricity-load forecasting during COVID-19 without using any regime indicators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online prediction under distribution shift requires predictors that stay stable in quiet periods but adapt quickly during regime changes, with the optimal adaptation memory unknown in advance.

Method: MELO wraps any non-anticipating base-predictor pool with EWLS adaptation experts at multiple forgetting factors, then aggregates raw and EWLS-adapted forecasts using MLpol, a parameter-free online aggregation rule.

Result: MELO reduces overall RMSE by 34.7% relative to base-only MLpol on French national electricity-load forecasting through COVID-19 lockdown, and outperforms a TabICL reference that uses external COVID policy covariates.

Conclusion: MELO provides deterministic oracle inequalities with path-length-dependent tracking cost and sublinear aggregation overhead, requiring only lightweight per-step recursive updates without model retraining.

Abstract: We study online prediction under distribution shift, where inputs arrive chronologically and outcomes are revealed only after prediction. In this setting, predictors must remain stable in quiet regimes yet adapt when regimes shift, and the right adaptation memory is unknown in advance. We propose MELO (Memory-hedged Exponentially Weighted Least-Squares Online aggregation), a model-agnostic method that hedges across adaptation scales: it wraps any non-anticipating base-predictor pool with exponentially weighted least-squares (EWLS) adaptation experts at multiple forgetting factors, and aggregates raw and EWLS-adapted forecasts with MLpol, a parameter-free online aggregation rule. Under boundedness conditions, we establish deterministic oracle inequalities showing that it competes with both the best raw predictor and the best bounded, time-varying affine combinations of the base predictions, up to a path-length-dependent tracking cost and a sublinear aggregation overhead. We evaluate MELO on French national electricity-load forecasting through the COVID-19 lockdown using no regime indicators, lockdown dates, or policy covariates. MELO reduces overall RMSE by 34.7\% relative to base-only MLpol and achieves lower overall RMSE than a TabICL reference supplied with an external COVID policy-response covariate. Moreover, MELO requires only lightweight per-step recursive updates without model retraining.

</details>


### [320] [Sequential Design of Genetic Circuits Under Uncertainty With Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06552)
*Michal Kobiela,Diego A. Oyarzún,Michael U. Gutmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reinforcement learning framework that optimizes genetic circuits under both intrinsic stochasticity and cross-laboratory variability using an amortized approach that avoids expensive per-cycle inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biological system design suffers from uncertainty due to molecular stochasticity and variable lab conditions. Previous Bayesian methods require computationally expensive inference and optimization after each experimental round, causing delays.

Method: A sequential RL policy-based framework using simulator models (differential equations or Markov jump processes). An amortized approach is trained up-front across a distribution of uncertain parameters, eliminating the need for explicit parameter inference during the design cycle.

Result: Demonstrated on heterologous gene expression and repressilator circuit models, showing efficient handling of both molecular noise and cross-laboratory variability.

Conclusion: The amortized RL framework enables immediate, observation-based adaptation for genetic circuit optimization without costly per-experiment inference steps.

Abstract: The design of biological systems is hindered by uncertainty arising from both intrinsic stochasticity of biomolecular reactions and variability across laboratory or experimental conditions. In this work, we present a sequential framework to optimize genetic circuits under both forms of uncertainty. By employing simulator models based on differential equations or Markov jump processes alongside a reinforcement learning (RL) policy-based approach, our method suggests experiments that adapt to unknown laboratory conditions while accounting for inherent stochasticity. While previous Bayesian methods address uncertainty through iterative experiment-inference-optimization cycles, they typically require computationally expensive inference and optimization steps after each experimental round, leading to delays. To overcome this bottleneck, we propose an amortized approach trained up-front across a distribution of possible uncertain parameters. This strategy sidesteps the need for explicit parameter inference during the design cycle, enabling immediate, observation-based adaptation. We demonstrate our framework on models for heterologous gene expression and a repressilator circuit, showing that it efficiently handles both molecular noise and cross-laboratory variability.

</details>


### [321] [Diverse Sampling in Diffusion Models with Marginal Preserving Particle Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06553)
*Gal Vinograd,Idan Achituve,Ethan Fetaya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EDDY is a training-free guidance mechanism for diffusion/flow models that enhances sample diversity by adding divergence-free drift perturbations that preserve marginal distributions, with practical approximations for efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion and flow matching models often suffer from limited diversity when generating samples, producing repetitive or mode-collapsed outputs. Existing diversity-promoting methods typically sacrifice sample quality or require additional training.

Method: EDDY exploits Fokker-Planck symmetries via divergence-free drift perturbations (anti-symmetric kernel-based pairwise matrix fields from repulsive directions) that alter particle trajectories without changing marginal distributions. Practical approximations are proposed for efficiency in high-dimensional tasks like text-to-image generation.

Result: On synthetic distributions and text-to-image generation, EDDY improves sample diversity while maintaining strong distributional fidelity compared to common baselines, without requiring additional training.

Conclusion: EDDY offers an effective, training-free approach to diversifying diffusion and flow matching model outputs by leveraging exact marginal-preserving drift perturbations, with practical approximations making it viable for complex generative tasks.

Abstract: We present EDDY (Exact-marginal Diversification via Divergence-free dYnamics), a guidance mechanism for diffusion and flow matching models that promotes diversity among samples generated while maintaining quality. EDDY exploits symmetries of the Fokker-Planck equation, using drift perturbations that change particle trajectories while preserving the evolving marginal distribution. We instantiate this principle through kernel-based anti-symmetric pairwise matrix fields, constructed from the repulsive directions. The resulting divergence-free dynamics promote diversity at the joint particle level while preserving each particle's marginal distribution without any additional training. As computing the guidance can be computationally expensive in cases such as text-to-image generation with perceptual embeddings, we propose practical approximations as an effective and efficient solution. Experiments on synthetic distributions and text-to-image generation show that EDDY improves diversity while maintaining strong distributional fidelity compared to common baselines.

</details>
