Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GA3T is a real-world multi-robot collaborative perception dataset featuring a ground robot (LiDAR, stereo, IMU, GPS) and an aerial robot (RGB, thermal, GPS) operating in unstructured outdoor terrains (forests, rocky/muddy/snowy/grassy areas). It contains ~13,000 synchronized frames, 8,000+ manual labels, and SAM-based segmentation, captured in early-spring conditions where sparse canopies enable partial cross-robot visibility for occlusion-aware perception research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The lack of real-world datasets with overlapping multi-modal observations from heterogeneous air-ground robot teams operating in unstructured terrain has constrained progress in multi-robot collaborative perception.

Method: The dataset is collected using a Clearpath Husky UGV (3D LiDAR, stereo camera, IMU, GPS) and an Autel EVO II UAV (RGB, thermal/infrared, GPS) across 4 unstructured environments (forest trails, rocky paths, muddy terrain, snow piles, grass fields). Includes SAM 3-based zero-shot segmentation and over 8,000 manually labeled images. Early-spring collection allows partial visibility through sparse tree canopies.

Result: Over 13,000 synchronized frames across ~29 minutes of operation in 4 diverse unstructured environments, with both SAM-based zero-shot and manual segmentation labels.

Conclusion: GA3T is designed to support cross-view perception, air-ground viewpoint fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding in real off-road environments, filling a gap left by prior multi-robot datasets focused on SLAM or simulated cooperative driving.

Abstract: Heterogeneous air-ground robot teams combine complementary sensing modalities, mobility characteristics, and spatial viewpoints that can significantly enhance perception in complex outdoor environments. However, progress in multi-robot collaborative perception has been constrained by the lack of real-world datasets featuring overlapping multi-modal observations from platforms operating in unstructured terrain. We present GA3T (Ground-Aerial Team for Terrain Traversal), a real-world multi-robot collaborative perception dataset collected using a Clearpath Husky UGV and an Autel EVO~II UAV across diverse unstructured environments, including forest trails, rocky paths, muddy terrain, snow piles, and grass-covered fields. The ground platform provides 3D LiDAR, stereo camera, IMU, and GPS data, while the aerial platform contributes RGB imagery, thermal/infrared observations, and GPS from a complementary overhead viewpoint, allowing for rich cross-modal and cross-view perception. The dataset is collected in 4 unique environments, with over 13,000 synchronized frames across approximately 29 minutes of operation, and includes both SAM~3-based zero-shot segmentation and over 8,000 manually labeled images. A unique aspect of the dataset is its early-spring collection period, during which sparse tree canopies allow the aerial robot to partially observe the ground robot and terrain through the trees, allowing for occlusion-aware collaborative perception. Unlike prior multi-robot datasets that focus on SLAM or simulated cooperative driving, GA3T is specifically designed to support research on cross-view perception, air-ground viewpoint fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding in real off-road environments.

</details>


### [376] [OA-WAM: Object-Addressable World Action Model for Robust Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06481)
*Yushan Liu,Peibo Sun,Shoujie Li,Yifan Xie,Lingfeng Zhang,Xintao Chao,Shiyuan Dong,Fang Chen,Xiao-Ping Zhang,Wenbo Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OA-WAM introduces object-addressable slot representations into world action models, achieving strong manipulation performance and robust object binding under scene shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing World Action Models represent predicted scenes holistically (images, video tokens, global latents), making it difficult for action decoders to handle instruction-referenced objects under scene shifts where object identity is entangled with context.

Method: Decomposes frames into N+1 slot states (1 robot + N objects), each with a persistent address vector and time-varying content vector. Uses address-only keys for cross-slot attention routing, resets address slices at every transformer layer, and jointly predicts next-frame slots and 16-step action chunks via flow matching.

Result: Matches strong baselines on LIBERO (97.8%) and SimplerEnv (79.3%), achieves SOTA on LIBERO-Plus geometric axes. Causal slot-intervention test yields 0.87 swap-binding cosine vs. ≤0.09 for holistic baselines.

Conclusion: Addressable object states provide an effective interface for robust world-action modeling under scene perturbations.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) enhance Vision-Language-Action policies by jointly predicting scene evolution and robot actions, but existing methods usually represent the predicted world as holistic images, video tokens, or global latents. These representations are difficult for an action decoder to address when an instruction refers to a particular object, especially under scene shifts where object identity is entangled with context. We propose OA-WAM, an Object-Addressable World Action Model for robust robot manipulation. OA-WAM decomposes each frame into N+1 slot states, with one robot slot and N object slots. Each slot contains a persistent address vector and a time-varying content vector, and is fused with text, image, proprioception, and past-action tokens in a block-causal sequence. A world head predicts next-frame slot states, while a flow-matching action head decodes a 16-step continuous action chunk in the same forward pass. Addressability is enforced by routing cross-slot attention through address-only keys and resetting the address slice at every transformer layer, separating which object to act on from what that object currently is without adding extra tokens. OA-WAM matches strong VLA and WAM baselines on LIBERO (97.8%) and SimplerEnv (79.3%), reaches state-of-the-art performance on the most relevant LIBERO-Plus geometric axes, and remains competitive on the seven-axis aggregate. A causal slot-intervention test yields a swap-binding cosine of 0.87, versus at most 0.09 for holistic baselines. These results suggest that addressable object states provide an effective interface for robust world-action modeling under scene perturbations.

</details>


### [377] [Lie Group Formulation of Recursive Dynamics Algorithms of Higher Order for Floating-Base Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06498)
*Ahmed Ali,Chiara Gabellieri,Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents algorithms for computing higher-order time derivatives of key dynamics algorithms (Newton-Euler, Articulated-Body Inertia, hybrid dynamics) for floating-base robotic systems on SE(3) and T^n × R^m manifolds, derives closed-form equations with passivity-preserving Coriolis matrix, and demonstrates quadratic (vs exponential for autodiff) computational scaling up to 5th order on a 12-DoF aerial manipulator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable efficient computation of higher-order dynamics derivatives for floating-base robotic systems (e.g., aerial manipulators), which are needed for advanced control, trajectory optimization, and sensitivity analysis but are currently computationally prohibitive using automatic differentiation.

Method: The authors extend Lie-group Newton-Euler, Articulated-Body Inertia, and hybrid dynamics algorithms to compute arbitrary-order time derivatives for floating-base trees where the base moves on SE(3) and the kinematic chain lives on T^n × R^m. They use spatial twist representations, derive closed-form equations of motion, identify an admissible Coriolis matrix satisfying passivity, and show articulated inertia tensor invariance across derivative orders.

Result: The methods successfully compute dynamics up to 5th order for a 12-DoF aerial manipulator. The computational cost scales quadratically with derivative order, while automatic differentiation scales exponentially in the benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed algorithms enable substantially more efficient higher-order dynamics computations than autodiff baselines, making them practical for applications requiring high-order derivatives (e.g., model-predictive control, trajectory optimization) in floating-base robotic systems.

Abstract: In this paper, we describe procedures for computing higher-order time derivatives of the Lie-group Newton-Euler, Articulated-Body Inertia, and hybrid dynamics algorithms for floating-base trees, where the base configuration evolves on SE(3) and the attached mechanism is an open kinematic tree with configuration on the (n1+n2)-dimensional manifold T^{n1} \times R^{n2}, using spatial representation of twists. After presenting the algorithms, we collect the resulting recursions into closed-form equations of motion, identifying an admissible Coriolis matrix satisfying the passivity property, and showing that the articulated inertia tensor remains unchanged across all time derivatives. We then apply the developed methods to a 12-DoF aerial manipulator to derive analytical expressions for its geometric forward and inverse dynamics along with their first time derivatives whereas the numerical simulations successfully evaluate these dynamics up to fifth order. Finally, to demonstrate their practical utility, we benchmark the proposed extensions and show that, in the considered tests, their computational cost scales quadratically with the derivative order, whereas the automatic-differentiation baseline exhibits exponential scaling.

</details>


### [378] [ReActor: Reinforcement Learning for Physics-Aware Motion Retargeting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06593)
*David Müller,Agon Serifi,Sammy Christen,Ruben Grandia,Espen Knoop,Moritz Bächer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A bilevel optimization framework that jointly adapts reference motions to robot morphology while training a tracking policy via RL, producing physically plausible motions for imitation learning without manual tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing motion retargeting methods produce physical inconsistencies (foot sliding, self-collisions, dynamically infeasible motions) that hinder downstream imitation learning.

Method: Bilevel optimization framework that jointly adapts reference motions to robot morphology while training a tracking policy using RL, with an approximate gradient for the upper-level loss and sparse semantic rigid-body correspondences.

Result: Validated in simulation and on hardware, demonstrating challenging motions for morphologies significantly different from a human, including retargeting onto a quadruped.

Conclusion: The framework produces physically plausible motions that facilitate robust imitation learning, eliminates manual tuning, and works across diverse embodiments including quadrupeds.

Abstract: Retargeting human kinematic reference motion onto a robot's morphology remains a formidable challenge. Existing methods often produce physical inconsistencies, such as foot sliding, self-collisions, or dynamically infeasible motions, which hinder downstream imitation learning. We propose a bilevel optimization framework that jointly adapts reference motions to a robot's morphology while training a tracking policy using reinforcement learning. To make the optimization tractable, we derive an approximate gradient for the upper-level loss. Our framework requires only a sparse set of semantic rigid-body correspondences and eliminates the need for manual tuning by identifying optimal values for a parameterization expressive enough to preserve characteristic motion across different embodiments. Moreover, by integrating retargeting directly with physics simulation, we produce physically plausible motions that facilitate robust imitation learning. We validate our method in simulation and on hardware, demonstrating challenging motions for morphologies that differ significantly from a human, including retargeting onto a quadruped.

</details>


### [379] [Cross-Modal Navigation with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06595)
*Shuo Liu,Xinzichen Li,Christopher Amato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CRONA is a MARL framework for cross-modal navigation that enables lightweight modality-specialized agents to collaborate via control-relevant auxiliary beliefs and a centralized multi-modal critic, outperforming single-agent baselines in visual-acoustic navigation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality multi-modal data is difficult to obtain; monolithic models struggle with complex representations and enlarged policy space. Cross-modal collaboration among lightweight agents offers a scalable alternative.

Method: CRONA uses Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) with control-relevant auxiliary beliefs for inter-agent communication and a centralized multi-modal critic that leverages global state to coordinate modality-specialized agents.

Result: Multi-agent methods significantly outperform single-agent baselines. Homogeneous collaboration works for short-range navigation; heterogeneous collaboration is generally efficient; complex environments need richer perception and larger models.

Conclusion: CRONA demonstrates that cross-modal collaboration among specialized agents is a scalable and effective paradigm for robust embodied navigation, with different collaboration strategies suited to different task complexities.

Abstract: Robust embodied navigation relies on complementary sensory cues. However, high-quality and well-aligned multi-modal data is often difficult to obtain in practice. Training a monolithic model is also challenging as rich multi-modal inputs induce complex representations and substantially enlarge the policy space. Cross-modal collaboration among lightweight modality-specialized agents offers a scalable paradigm. It enables flexible deployment and parallel execution, while preserving the strength of each modality. In this paper, we propose \textbf{CRONA}, a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework for \textbf{Cro}ss-Modal \textbf{Na}vigation. CRONA improves collaboration by leveraging control-relevant auxiliary beliefs and a centralized multi-modal critic with global state. Experiments on visual-acoustic navigation tasks show that multi-agent methods significantly improve performance and efficiency over single-agent baselines. We find that homogeneous collaboration with limited modalities is sufficient for short-range navigation under salient cues; heterogeneous collaboration among agents with complementary modalities is generally efficient and effective; and navigation in large, complex environments requires both richer multi-modal perception and increased model capacity.

</details>


### [380] [Multi-Robot Coordination in V2X Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06662)
*John Pravin Arockiasamy,Alexey Vinel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a V2X communication framework for decentralized multi-robot cooperation in urban traffic, introducing Robot Awareness Service (RAS) and Robot Maneuver Coordination Service (RMCS) built on ETSI standards. It demonstrates real-world coordination between a humanoid and quadrupedal robot assisting a pedestrian crossing, and simulations show effective VRU clustering with reduced channel load.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable decentralized, infrastructure-free cooperation among social robots in complex urban traffic environments by extending existing V2X communication standards to support robot-specific awareness and maneuver coordination.

Method: Introduces two ETSI-aligned facility-layer services: RAS (via RAM messages) for role-aware, task-oriented robot awareness including non-V2X VRUs, and RMCS (via RMCM messages) for event-driven, low-latency maneuver coordination. Validated via real-world proof of concept (humanoid + quadrupedal robot assisting pedestrian crossing) and simulations of VRU clustering in mixed V2X environments.

Result: Real-world demonstration shows deterministic multi-robot coordination governed by a formally specified finite-state model. Simulations show RAS-based clustering integrates non-V2X VRUs in safety-critical areas while reducing redundant transmissions from V2X-enabled VRUs, lowering channel load.

Conclusion: The proposed RAS and RMCS services provide a scalable, standards-aligned foundation for integrating cooperative robots into future Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility (CCAM) ecosystems.

Abstract: This paper presents a Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication framework that enables decentralized cooperation among social robots operating in complex urban traffic environments. Building on ETSI Cooperative Awareness and Maneuver Coordination services, the framework introduces two robot-centric facility-layer services: the Robot Awareness Service (RAS) and the Robot Maneuver Coordination Service (RMCS), realized through the Robot Awareness Message (RAM) and the Robot Maneuver Coordination Message (RMCM), respectively. RAS enables role-aware, task-oriented robot awareness while integrating externally detected Vulnerable Road Users (VRUs), including non-V2X pedestrians, into cooperative awareness. RMCS supports event-driven, low-latency coordination of robot maneuvers under explicitly established roles, without centralized infrastructure or prior pairing. A real-world proof of concept demonstrates deterministic multi-robot coordination between a humanoid robot and a quadrupedal robot assisting a pedestrian during a road-crossing scenario, governed by a formally specified finite-state coordination model. Complementary simulations evaluate robot-mediated VRU clustering in mixed V2X environments, showing that RAS-based clustering integrates non-V2X VRUs in safety-critical areas while reducing redundant transmissions from V2X-enabled VRUs, thereby lowering channel load. Together, the proposed services provide a scalable and standards-aligned foundation for integrating cooperative robots into future Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility ecosystems.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [381] [MPNet: A Robust and Efficient Manifold Pooling Network for Multi-Rhythm EEG Signal Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05212)
*Guoqing Cai,Kai Zeng,Shoulin Huang,Ting Ma*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: MPNet introduces a rhythm-adaptive convolutional frontend and a manifold node pooling layer to reduce computational costs of deep Riemannian networks for EEG decoding, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with up to 10x speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep Riemannian networks for EEG decoding suffer from high-dimensional inputs and significant computational costs due to the need to model complex temporal dynamics across multiple rhythms.

Method: MPNet uses a rhythm-adaptive convolutional frontend to extract time-frequency representations and generate multi-view Riemannian nodes, then a novel manifold node pooling layer aggregates these nodes into a single fixed-size fusion node for efficient deep Riemannian processing.

Result: Experiments on two public EEG datasets show MPNet achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, runs up to 10x faster than comparable Riemannian models, and maintains robust performance under limited-data conditions.

Conclusion: MPNet demonstrates practicality and efficiency for real-world EEG applications by balancing high accuracy with significantly reduced computational costs.

Abstract: Deep Riemannian networks provide a powerful framework for Electroencephalography (EEG) decoding, but their practical applications are severely constrained. Accurately decoding EEG signals requires modeling complex temporal dynamics across multiple rhythms, which results in high-dimensional Riemannian inputs and significant computational costs. To address this, we propose the Manifold Pooling Network (MPNet). MPNet uses a rhythm-adaptive convolutional frontend to extract comprehensive time-frequency representations and generate multi-view Riemannian nodes. A novel manifold node pooling layer is then proposed to aggregate these nodes into a single fusion node with a fixed size, enabling the following deep Riemannian network to process it with greatly reduced costs. Experiments on two public EEG datasets show that MPNet achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, runs up to 10 times faster than the comparable Riemannian model, and maintains robust performance under limited-data conditions. These findings highlight MPNet's practicality and efficiency for real-world EEG applications.

</details>


### [382] [MedMamba: Recasting Mamba for Medical Time Series Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05214)
*ZhengXiao He,Huayu Li,Xiwen Chen,Janet M Roveda,Jinghao Wen,Siyuan Tian,Ao Li*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: MedMamba is a multi-scale bidirectional state space architecture for medical time series classification that outperforms Transformers with 4.6x faster inference and linear complexity, achieving state-of-the-art results on EEG, ECG, and human activity benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing models (CNNs, RNNs, Transformers) struggle with medical time series due to either limited long-range dependency capture or quadratic computational complexity, and they fail to align with the intrinsic structural properties of physiological signals.

Method: MedMamba incorporates three inductive biases (spatial centralization, multi-timescale composition, non-causal dependency) via a channel-mixing module, multi-scale convolutional tokenization, and bidirectional Mamba blocks for linear-complexity global context modeling.

Result: Outperforms SOTA on six datasets; achieves 85.97% on PTB, 54.72% accuracy on ADFTD (new SOTA), strong on SleepEDF, and 4.6x inference speedup.

Conclusion: Principle-guided state space modeling is an effective, scalable alternative to Transformers for medical time series analysis.

Abstract: Medical time series, such as electrocardiograms (ECG) and electroencephalograms (EEG), exhibit complex temporal dynamics and structured cross-channel dependencies, posing fundamental challenges for automated analysis. Conventional convolutional and recurrent models struggle to capture long-range dependencies, while Transformer-based approaches incur quadratic complexity and often introduce redundant interactions that are misaligned with the intrinsic structure of physiological signals. To address these limitations, we propose MedMamba, a principle-driven multi-scale bidirectional state space architecture tailored for medical time series classification. Our design is guided by three key inductive biases of physiological signals: spatial centralization, multi-timescale temporal composition, and non-causal contextual dependency. These principles are instantiated through a lightweight channel-mixing module for cross-channel reparameterization, multi-scale convolutional tokenization for temporal decomposition, and bidirectional Mamba blocks for efficient global context modeling with linear complexity. Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets spanning EEG, ECG, and human activity signals demonstrate that MedMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse modalities. Notably, it achieves 85.97% accuracy on PTB and establishes new state-of-the-art performance on the challenging ADFTD dataset (54.72% accuracy and 52.01% F1-score). Strong results on long-sequence benchmarks, such as SleepEDF, further validate its capability in modeling long-range dependencies. Moreover, MedMamba achieves a speedup of 4.6x in inference, highlighting its practicality for real-time clinical deployment. These results suggest that principle-guided state space modeling offers an effective and scalable alternative to Transformer-based approaches for medical time series analysis.

</details>


### [383] [PPO-Based Dynamic Positioning of HAPS-BS in Wind-Disturbed Stratospheric Maritime Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05240)
*Azim Akhtarshenas,German Svistunov,Matteo Bernabè,Kuangyu Zheng,David López-Pérez*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a DRL-based (PPO) framework for dynamic positioning of wind-disturbed HAPS-mounted base stations in maritime networks, showing improved coverage stability and throughput under wind disturbances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HAPS offer wide-area maritime wireless coverage, but dynamic ship mobility and stratospheric wind disturbances challenge reliable performance.

Method: A centralized DRL agent (using PPO) deployed on a coordinator HAPS controls multiple serving HAPS based on radio measurements and network feedback.

Result: The proposed approach effectively mitigates wind-induced positioning deviations while ensuring reliable wide-area connectivity for maritime users.

Conclusion: DRL-based dynamic positioning enhances coverage stability and system throughput under wind disturbances for maritime HAPS networks.

Abstract: High-Altitude Platform Stations (HAPS) offer a promising solution for wide-area wireless coverage in maritime regions lacking terrestrial infrastructure. However, maintaining reliable performance is challenging due to dynamic ship mobility and atmospheric disturbances, particularly stratospheric wind effects on HAPS positioning. This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based framework for dynamic positioning of wind-disturbed HAPS-mounted base stations in maritime networks. A centralized DRL agent deployed on a coordinator HAPS controls multiple serving HAPS using radio measurements and network feedback, capturing realistic channel conditions and user mobility. A Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm is employed to learn robust positioning policies that enhance coverage stability and system throughput under wind disturbances. Simulation results show that the proposed approach effectively mitigates wind-induced positioning deviations while ensuring reliable wide-area connectivity for maritime users.

</details>


### [384] [Memory-Efficient EDA Denoising via Knowledge Distillation for Wearable IoT Under Severe Motion Artifacts and Underwater Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05246)
*Yongbin Lee,Andrew Peitzsch,Youngsun Kong,Jarod Zizza,Dong-hee Kang,Farnoush Baghestani,Ki H. Chon*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A robust EDA denoising framework using CNN-Transformer knowledge distillation, achieving significant model compression (7.87MB→0.51MB) and strong denoising performance across harsh environments including underwater, with downstream CNS-OT prediction improved to AUROC 0.806 and early prediction up to 6.9 minutes before symptom onset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EDA signals in wearable IoMT systems are highly vulnerable to motion artifacts and environmental noise, limiting reliable deployment in harsh conditions like underwater environments.

Method: A hybrid CNN-Transformer teacher model distilled into a lightweight depth-wise separable CNN student via knowledge distillation, plus a realistic data augmentation scheme simulating motion artifacts and environmental distortions.

Result: Model size reduced from 7.87MB to 0.51MB (93.5% reduction), FLOPs from 105.1M to 11.61M; MAE 0.144 and SNR improvement 12.08dB on public dataset; underwater MAE reduced from 2.809 to 0.215; CNS-OT AUROC 0.806 with early prediction 6.9 minutes before symptom onset.

Conclusion: The proposed framework improves EDA signal quality and clinically relevant prediction performance while remaining deployable in resource-constrained wearable IoT systems in harsh environments.

Abstract: Electrodermal activity (EDA) is widely used in wearable Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) systems for continuous health monitoring, including autonomic assessment. However, EDA signals are highly vulnerable to motion artifacts and environmental noise, limiting reliable deployment in harsh operating conditions such as underwater. This study proposes a robust, deployable EDA denoising framework that generalizes across multiple measurement locations and harsh environments. The framework integrates a hybrid CNN-Transformer teacher model with a lightweight depth-wise separable CNN student model via a knowledge distillation (KD) strategy. To further improve robustness, a realistic data augmentation scheme is introduced to simulate diverse motion artifacts and environmental distortions. The KD-based student model significantly reduces model size (7.87 MB to 0.51 MB) and computational cost (105.1M to 11.61M FLOPs) while maintaining denoising performance (MAE: 0.144, SNR improvement: 12.08 dB) using the public dataset validation. In real-world underwater conditions (UMAC dataset) testing, the proposed method substantially improves skin conductance response reconstruction, reducing mean absolute error from 2.809 to 0.215. Furthermore, on independent testing using the CNS-OT dataset, the denoised signals enhanced downstream CNS-OT prediction performance, achieving the highest AUROC (0.806) compared to prior denoising methods. The proposed method also improved the early prediction rate (sensitivity) from 0.550 to 0.767, enabling CNS-OT prediction up to a median of 6.9 minutes before symptom onset. These results demonstrate that the proposed framework not only improves EDA signal quality but also enhances clinically relevant prediction performance while remaining suitable for deployment in resource-constrained wearable Internet of Things systems operating in harsh environments.

</details>


### [385] [Covariance Square Root Second-Order Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05560)
*Keith A. LeGrand,Braden Hastings,Jackson Kulik*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents the first square root computation for second-order covariance mapping in recursive state estimation, which improves numerical accuracy and efficiency compared to standard full covariance computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Numerical errors from finite precision arithmetic can degrade recursive state estimation algorithms, causing covariance matrices to lose symmetry and positive semidefiniteness. While square root factorizations exist for first-order mappings (e.g., EKF), higher-order moment mappings lack such techniques despite their value in nonlinear settings.

Method: The authors developed a square root computation method for second-order covariance mapping, extending prior square root factorization techniques from first-order to higher-order moment mappings.

Result: The square root computation is more accurate than full covariance computation, demonstrated in two numerical experiments, and generally requires fewer floating point operations.

Conclusion: The proposed square root method for second-order covariance mapping offers both improved numerical accuracy and computational efficiency, filling a gap in higher-order moment mapping techniques.

Abstract: In recursive state estimation, numerical error can play a major role in an algorithm's overall performance and reliability. Roundoff errors due to finite precision arithmetic can violate theoretical guarantees, leading to asymmetric and non-positive-semidefinite covariance matrices. In algorithms employing first-order covariance mappings, such as the extended Kalman filter, these issues have been mitigated by employing square root factorizations of the covariance matrix. However, existing techniques do not directly extend to higher-order moment mappings, which show great value in highly nonlinear settings. This paper presents the first known square root computation of the second-order covariance mapping. The square root computation is not only more accurate, as is shown in two distinct numerical experiments, but generally requires fewer floating point operations compared to the full covariance matrix computation.

</details>


### [386] [Optimizing In Vivo Oral Lesion Classification from Electrical Impedance Spectroscopy Using Data-driven Approaches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05729)
*Sophie A. Lloyd,Jacob P. Thönes,Safina S. Suratwala,Noor Zaghlula,Liang Lu,Joseph Paydarfar,Ethan K. Murphy,Sascha Spors,Ryan J. Halter*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper develops a machine-learning pipeline for classifying oral pathology using electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data from a handheld device. After collecting data from 104 patients, they reduced dimensionality by up to 99% using PCA and achieved strong classification results (binary accuracy 80%, AUC 0.90; multi-class AUC >0.82), with logistic regression performing best.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Oral cancer is a major global health burden, and early detection is critically needed. While EIS offers a promising non-invasive approach for tissue characterization, classification frameworks that jointly leverage multiple impedance features for in vivo oral lesion discrimination remain underdeveloped.

Method: EIS data were collected from 104 patients using a handheld bedside device. Signal frequencies were ranked and reduced via PCA, and different IIVV pattern geometries were tested. Three classification tasks were evaluated using leave-one-patient-group-out cross-validation, with dimensionality reduced by up to 99%.

Result: Logistic regression achieved the best binary accuracy (80%) and AUC of 0.90. Multi-class tasks maintained AUCs above 0.82. All top models used the reduced IIVV set, and dimensionality reduction improved accuracy over full-dataset baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed ML pipeline provides a robust, computationally efficient, and clinically practical framework for early oral cancer diagnosis via EIS, with methodology generalizable to other EIS devices and applications.

Abstract: Oral cancer is a significant global health burden, and early detection remains a critical clinical need. Electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) offers a promising non-invasive approach for real-time tissue characterization, but classification frameworks that jointly leverage multiple impedance features for in vivo oral lesion discrimination remain underdeveloped. This paper presents a machine-learning (ML) pipeline to optimize classification of in vivo oral pathology from EIS data collected using a handheld, bedside device. Impedance measurements were acquired from 104 patients undergoing oral cancer resection or biopsy. Three classification tasks were evaluated: (1) healthy vs. cancer, (2) multi-class lesion-type discrimination (cancer, high-grade dysplasia, non-malignant), and (3) multi-class discrimination between the three lesion pathologies and healthy tissue. For each task, signal frequencies were independently ranked and reduced using PCA, and different current injection/voltage measurement (IIVV) pattern geometries were tested. Classification performance was assessed through leave-one-patient-group-out cross-validation to ensure robustness on unseen patients. Input data dimensionality was reduced by up to 99% across all tasks while improving diagnostic accuracy over baseline models trained on the full dataset. A logistic regression model achieved the highest binary classification accuracy of 80% with an AUC of 0.90, while multi-class scenarios maintained AUCs above 0.82. All top-performing models utilized the significantly reduced IIVV set as input. The proposed pipeline advances EIS-based cancer detection by providing a robust, computationally efficient, and clinically practical framework for early diagnosis of oral cancer lesions, with a methodology readily generalizable to other EIS devices and applications.

</details>


### [387] [Indoor 60 GHz Radio Channel Dataset Enabling Digital Twin Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05824)
*Davide Scazzoli,Daniele de Santis,Francesco Linsalata,Fortunato Santucci,Umberto Spagnolini,Maurizio Magarini*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a high-density mmWave beamspace dataset (350 points, 63x63 directions) collected using a 60 GHz RFSoC testbed with quasi-orthogonal chirp waveforms for rapid SLAM/ISAC research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern 6G/IIoT systems need advanced mmWave hardware for beamforming and SLAM, but lack high-density benchmark datasets for spatial channel modeling and ISAC.

Method: Uses Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ RFSoC + Sivers 60 GHz front-end; segment-scrambled quasi-orthogonal chirp waveforms for fast exhaustive beamspace scanning (63 TX directions, 63x63 intensity map in 200µs); integrated with Pynq Linux for real-time control.

Result: Collected 350 measurement points over a 1.95m x 3.60m indoor grid, achieving full 63x63 beamspace intensity mapping in 200µs per point.

Conclusion: The dataset provides a benchmark for spatial channel modeling, Digital Twins, and ISAC research in mmWave/6G/IIoT scenarios.

Abstract: The ambitious performance targets of modern wireless networks, including 6G and Industrial IoT (IIoT) systems, necessitate advanced hardware platforms utilizing millimeter-wave (mmWave) technology. High-frequency signals provide the bandwidth and low latency required for these systems, but rely on beamforming to overcome path loss and exploit channel sparsity. This kind of architecture provides all the specifications needed to build a SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) system. This paper presents a dataset based on a validated, high-performance testbed integrating a Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ RFSoC with a Sivers 60 GHz beamforming front-end. We demonstrate a novel methodology using segment-scrambled, quasi-orthogonal chirp waveforms to perform rapid exhaustive beamspace sampling. The system is integrated with Pynq Linux for real-time control and high-speed waveform upload. We present a high-density spatial dataset consisting of 350 measurement points across a 1.95 m x 3.60 m indoor grid. We exploited the system's ability to scan 63 transmit directions and construct a complete 63x63 beamspace intensity map in 200us. This dataset serves as a benchmark for spatial channel modeling, Digital Twins and Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) research.

</details>


### [388] [TGPP: Trajectory-Guided Plug-and-Play Priors for Sparse Radio Map Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05844)
*Jiawen Zhang,Zhiyuan Jiang,Sheng Zhou,Zhisheng Niu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes Trajectory-Guided Plug-and-Play Priors (TGPP) to improve radio map reconstruction from trajectory-sampled measurements, achieving up to 43.1% NMSE reduction across various backbone models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radio map reconstruction is crucial for environment-aware networks, but real-world measurements are collected along mobility trajectories rather than randomly scattered. This creates spatially heterogeneous uncertainty where near-trajectory regions are well-constrained while distant or occluded areas remain weakly observed, causing degraded accuracy in under-constrained regions.

Method: The authors propose TGPP, a general guidance module with two components: (1) an explicit guidance map serving as interpretable input-space risk prior, and (2) an implicit guide feature projected and fused with backbone hidden representations. They also introduce RadioFlow-LDM, a latent flow-based generative backbone. TGPP is plug-and-play, attachable to deterministic, adversarial, graph-based, and latent generative reconstruction models.

Result: Experiments on RadioMapSeer with five trajectory sampling rates show trajectory-sampled reconstruction differs substantially from random sparse interpolation. TGPP improves most reconstruction metrics across backbones, achieving up to 43.1% NMSE reduction compared to base backbones without trajectory-guided priors.

Conclusion: Trajectory-guided priors significantly enhance radio map reconstruction under practical trajectory-sampled measurement conditions, and the proposed TGPP module is an effective, backbone-agnostic solution.

Abstract: Radio map (RM) reconstruction is essential for environment-aware wireless networks, but practical measurements are often collected along mobility trajectories rather than randomly scattered over the target region. Such trajectory-sampled observations induce spatially heterogeneous uncertainty: near-trajectory regions are directly constrained, whereas distant or occluded regions remain weakly observed, leading to degraded reconstruction accuracy in under-constrained areas. To address this problem, we propose Trajectory-Guided Plug-and-Play Priors (TGPP), a general guidance module for sparse RM reconstruction. TGPP learns an explicit guidance map as an interpretable input-space risk prior, and an implicit guide feature that is projected and fused with backbone hidden representations. TGPP can be attached to different reconstruction backbones without changing their original task formulation. We further introduce RadioFlow-LDM, a latent flow-based generative backbone, and apply TGPP to deterministic, adversarial, graph-based, and latent generative reconstruction models. Experiments on RadioMapSeer with five trajectory sampling rates show that trajectory-sampled reconstruction differs substantially from random sparse interpolation. TGPP improves most reconstruction metrics across backbones, achieving up to 43.1% NMSE reduction relative to the corresponding base backbone without trajectory-guided priors.

</details>


### [389] [From Multi-Port Models to Cascade Structures: Optimization of Active Unilateral Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05884)
*Andrea Abrardo,Giulio Bartoli,Alberto Toccafondi,Marco Di Renzo*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces a multi-port S-parameter framework for analyzing and optimizing stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIMs) with unilateral active interconnections, using a non-reciprocal two-port network model per unit cell. It derives a gradient-based optimization algorithm with lower complexity than reciprocal SIMs, validated through full-wave simulations showing trade-offs in spacing, gain, and size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable efficient analysis and optimization of SIMs with active, non-reciprocal interconnections, moving beyond conventional reciprocal SIM architectures that have higher computational complexity.

Method: Models each unit cell as a non-reciprocal two-port network, constructs a feed-forward cascade representation for end-to-end transfer function, and derives a gradient-based optimization algorithm with reduced computational complexity.

Result: Full-wave simulations reveal trade-offs among inter-layer spacing, active gain, and SIM size in terms of channel diagonalization and achievable spectral efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables accurate and efficient optimization of active SIMs, with clear performance trade-offs that inform design choices.

Abstract: This paper develops a multi-port S-parameter framework for the analysis and optimization of stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIMs) with unilateral active interconnections. By modeling each unit cell as a non-reciprocal two-port network, the resulting SIM exhibits a feed-forward structure that enables a recursive, cascade-like representation of the end-to-end transfer function while preserving electromagnetic accuracy. Based on this model, we derive an efficient gradient-based optimization algorithm with reduced computational complexity compared to conventional reciprocal SIM architectures. Numerical results, obtained from full-wave simulations, illustrate the trade-offs among inter-layer spacing, active gain, and SIM size in terms of channel diagonalization and achievable spectral efficiency.

</details>


### [390] [CredibleDFGO: Differentiable Factor Graph Optimization with Credibility Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06100)
*Liang Qian,Penggao Yan,Penghui Xu,Li-Ta Hsu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: CredibleDFGO (CDFGO) is a differentiable GNSS factor graph framework that explicitly trains for covariance credibility by using proper scoring rules (NLL, ES) on East-North predictive distributions, improving both uncertainty credibility and positioning accuracy across urban scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing differentiable factor graph optimization (DFGO) methods improve mean estimates but produce unreliable covariance (too small, too large, or wrong shape) because they only optimize position accuracy, not covariance credibility.

Method: Proposes CDFGO: a Weighting Generation Network (WGN) predicts per-satellite reliability weights; a differentiable Gauss-Newton solver maps weights to position estimate and posterior covariance; proper scoring rules (NLL, Energy Score, and their combination) supervise the East-North predictive distribution end-to-end.

Result: On UrbanNav test scenes: consistent gains in uncertainty credibility; positioning accuracy improved on medium-urban and harsh-urban scenes; on harsh-urban Mong Kok scene, CDFGO-Combined reduced mean horizontal error from 13.77m to 11.68m, NLL from 40.63 to 6.59, and ES from 12.31 to 9.05.

Conclusion: Making covariance credibility an explicit training target through proper scoring rules yields more reliable uncertainty outputs and improved positioning accuracy in urban GNSS navigation.

Abstract: Global navigation satellite system (GNSS) positioning is widely used for urban navigation, but the covariance reported by the GNSS solver is often unreliable in urban canyons. Existing differentiable factor graph optimization (DFGO) methods already learn measurement weighting through the solver, but they still use position-only objectives. As a result, the mean estimate may improve while the reported covariance remains too small, too large, or wrong in shape. In this work, we propose CredibleDFGO (CDFGO), a differentiable GNSS factor graph framework that makes covariance credibility an explicit training target. The Weighting Generation Network (WGN) predicts per-satellite reliability weights. The differentiable Gauss--Newton solver maps these weights to a position estimate and posterior covariance, and proper scoring rules supervise the East--North predictive distribution end-to-end. We study negative log-likelihood (NLL), Energy Score (ES), and their combination. Results on three UrbanNav test scenes show consistent gains in uncertainty credibility. Positioning accuracy also improves on the medium-urban and harsh-urban scenes, and the mean horizontal error and 95th-percentile error improve on the deep-urban scene. On the harsh-urban Mong Kok (MK) scene, CDFGO-Combined reduces the mean horizontal error from 13.77\,m to 11.68\,m, reduces NLL from 40.63 to 6.59, and reduces ES from 12.31 to 9.05. The case studies link the MK improvement to better axis-wise consistency, more credible local covariance ellipses, and satellite-level reweighting.

</details>


### [391] [A Family of Hybrid Beyond-Diagonal RIS Architectures: Design and Performance Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06107)
*Konstantinos Ntougias,Ioannis Krikidis*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces hybrid BD-RIS architectures combining inter-element coupling (BD-RIS) with selective active amplification, achieving higher SNR than diagonal counterparts while using fewer amplifiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: BD-RIS improves SNR via inter-element coupling but suffers from multiplicative path loss, while hybrid active/passive RIS alleviates path loss but uses diagonal designs. The paper aims to combine both ideas for improved efficiency.

Method: Partition the surface into passive and active group-connected BD-RIS subsurfaces; derive closed-form SNR-maximizing solution using Takagi's factorization and per-group optimal amplification under power budget.

Result: Three hybrid BD-RIS architectures achieve same or higher receive SNR than diagonal counterparts while using significantly fewer reflect-type amplifiers.

Conclusion: Hybrid BD-RIS architectures effectively balance performance and hardware complexity, offering a promising path for practical RIS deployments.

Abstract: Beyond-diagonal reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (BD-RISs) extend conventional diagonal RISs by allowing inter-element coupling, thereby enlarging the set of attainable scattering matrices and improving the achievable signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). On the other hand, hybrid active/passive RISs use reflect-type power amplifiers in a fraction of the elements to alleviate the multiplicative path loss. In this paper, we bring these two ideas together and introduce a \emph{family of hybrid BD-RIS architectures}, in which the surface is partitioned into two reflecting subsurfaces (RSs), each adopting either a passive or an active group-connected BD-RIS design. We derive a closed-form SNR-maximizing solution that combines, for every BD-RIS group, Takagi's factorization of a certain complex symmetric matrix with an optimal per-group amplification factor that satisfies the reflect-power budget. Three architectures within the proposed family (active/passive, fully-connected-active/sub-connected-active, and sub-connected-active/sub-connected-active hybrid BD-RIS) are studied. Numerical results in a single-input single-output (SISO) link with blocked direct path show that the proposed hybrid BD-RIS architectures attain the same or higher receive SNR than their diagonal counterparts while using significantly fewer reflect-type amplifiers.

</details>


### [392] [Orthogonal Least Squares with Integrated Information Theoretic Criteria for Joint Number of Targets and DoA Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06198)
*Martin Willame,Gilles Monnoyer,François Horlin,Jérôme Louveaux*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes three methods (disjoint rank-based, joint selection-based, and hybrid rank-and-selection-based) to integrate Information Theoretic Criteria with Orthogonal Least Squares for joint estimation of target number and their direction-of-arrivals using antenna arrays. The hybrid ITC-OLS algorithm outperforms other variants and baseline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Joint estimation of target number and DoAs is computationally prohibitive when using standard ITC because it requires maximum-likelihood DoA estimates for each candidate model order, involving a multidimensional search over all angle combinations.

Method: Three ITC-OLS algorithms are proposed: (1) disjoint rank-based, (2) joint selection-based, and (3) hybrid rank-and-selection-based. Each is derived under both AIC and BIC frameworks, integrating model-order selection into the greedy OLS estimation procedure.

Result: Numerical simulations show the hybrid ITC-OLS algorithm consistently outperforms both the other proposed variants and a baseline method from the literature.

Conclusion: The hybrid rank-and-selection-based ITC-OLS approach effectively balances complexity and accuracy for joint target-number and DoA estimation.

Abstract: We address the joint estimation of the number of targets and their direction-of-arrivals (DoAs) using antenna arrays. Target-number estimation can be formulated as a model-order selection problem and solved with the information theoretic criteria (ITC). The ITC minimize an objective function that balances a likelihood term and a complexity penalty. However, direct application of the ITC requires maximum-likelihood DoA estimates for each candidate model order, which is computationally prohibitive because it entails a multidimensional search over all angle combinations. To reduce complexity, many radar processing exploit greedy methods such as orthogonal least squares (OLS). In this paper, we explore three distinct methods to integrate the ITC model-order selection into the OLS estimation procedure for joint target-number and DoA estimation. Specifically, we propose the disjoint rank-based, the joint selection-based, and the hybrid rank-and-selection-based ITC-OLS algorithms. Each algorithm is derived under both the Akaike information criterion (AIC) and the Bayesian information criterion (BIC) frameworks. Numerical simulations show that the proposed hybrid ITC-OLS algorithm consistently outperforms both the other proposed variants and a baseline method from the literature.

</details>


### [393] [Cooperative Multi-Static Target Localization for ISAC in Cluttered Industrial IoT Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06256)
*Mostafa Nozari,Israel Leyva-Mayorga,Gilberto Berardinelli*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes an ISAC framework for collaborative multi-static target localization in dense IIoT environments, featuring clutter suppression, adaptive sensor selection (SFI & PEB), and reliability-aware WLS fusion, achieving ~45 cm RMSE in six iterations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of accurate target localization in dense IIoT environments with heavy environmental clutter, where conventional methods suffer from persistent reflections and inefficient sensor utilization.

Method: 1) Lightweight temporal clutter-suppression learning; 2) Sampling-based field-of-view-aware initialization (SFI); 3) Empirical position error bound (PEB) scheme for adaptive subset selection; 4) Reliability-aware weighted least-squares (WLS) estimator fusing range and AoA measurements.

Result: Reduces localization RMSE by nearly two orders of magnitude within six sensing iterations to about 45 cm, significantly outperforming all benchmarks under the same sensing-resource budget.

Conclusion: The proposed ISAC framework with adaptive node selection and clutter suppression enables fast and accurate collaborative localization in dense IIoT environments.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) framework for collaborative multi-static target localization in dense Industrial Internet-of-Things (IIoT) environments in the presence of environmental clutter. We first develop a lightweight temporal clutter-suppression learning method to mitigate persistent reflections. Building on this, we propose an iterative localization algorithm that integrates two key components introduced in this work: a sampling-based field-of-view-aware initialization (SFI) scheme and an empirical position error bound (PEB) scheme, which together adaptively identify the most informative subset of sensing nodes. A reliability-aware weighted least-squares estimator is then employed to fuse range and angle-of-arrival measurements from the selected sensing receivers for target localization. Numerical results demonstrate rapid convergence of the proposed method, reducing the localization RMSE by nearly two orders of magnitude within six sensing iterations to about 45 cm, while significantly outperforming all considered benchmarks under the same sensing-resource budget.

</details>


### [394] [The frame-level leakage trap: rethinking evaluation protocols for intrinsic image decomposition, with source-separable uncertainty as a case study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06359)
*Jihwan Woo*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper identifies and quantifies a data leakage problem in MPI Sintel evaluation for intrinsic image decomposition, showing frame-level splits inflate performance by 1.6-2.0 dB (up to 10+ dB). It advocates scene-level splits as the standard, provides reference numbers, and proposes a physics-informed decomposition with source-separable uncertainty that shows channel specialization and downstream utility, while also reporting negative results for a more complex variant.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluation protocols for learned intrinsic image decomposition on MPI Sintel have been inconsistent, with prior works using frame-level splits that allow data leakage (spatially similar frames of the same scene appearing in both train and test). No prior work had quantified this leakage effect across different architectures.

Method: Quantified leakage by comparing frame-level vs scene-level splits across three architectures using paired t-tests. Proposed a physics-informed decomposition I = R∘S + N with a source-separable three-way heteroscedastic uncertainty head. Also tested a more elaborate variant with frequency decomposition, cross-task supervision, evidential learning, contrastive loss, and test-time adaptation.

Result: Frame-level split inflates R_PSNR by 1.6-2.0 dB (p<0.01). Under extended training, inflation exceeds 10 dB. The proposed method achieves 15.98±0.41 dB R_PSNR, within 0.8 dB of a 5-member Deep Ensemble at 1/5 the cost. Non-Lambertian uncertainty channel shows r=0.67 correlation with non-Lambertian error. Filtering 75% highest-uncertainty pixels reduces MSE by 77%.

Conclusion: Advocates scene-level splits as the community standard for MPI Sintel evaluation. The proposed physics-informed method with source-separable uncertainty demonstrates channel specialization and downstream utility, while the more elaborate variant yielded negative results, suggesting simpler designs may be more effective.

Abstract: Evaluation protocols for learned intrinsic image decomposition on MPI Sintel have been inconsistent. Several prior works split the dataset by frames, which allows spatially similar frames of the same scene to appear in both train and test partitions. We quantify this leakage effect for the first time, across three architectures: a frame-level split inflates test R_PSNR by 1.6 to 2.0 dB (p less than 0.01 for all three, paired t-test across 3 seeds) relative to a scene-level split, confirming an architecture-independent protocol effect. A three-point gradient (random/temporal/scene) shows the gap is continuous, and under extended training the frame-level inflation exceeds 10 dB. We advocate scene-level splits as the community standard and provide reference numbers for six representative models under this protocol. As a case study within the corrected protocol, we present a physics-informed decomposition I = R composed with S + N with a source-separable three-way heteroscedastic uncertainty head. We empirically verify channel specialization: the non-Lambertian uncertainty channel shows r = 0.67 cross-correlation with non-Lambertian residual error, more than 4 times the texture channel's correlation. We further demonstrate downstream utility: filtering out the 75% highest-uncertainty pixels reduces reconstruction MSE by 77% on retained pixels, whereas random filtering produces no improvement. The specialization also holds on out-of-distribution real photographs. We report negative results for a more elaborate variant combining frequency decomposition, cross-task supervision, evidential learning, contrastive loss, and test-time adaptation. Our method reaches 15.98 plus or minus 0.41 dB R_PSNR, within 0.8 dB of a 5-member Deep Ensemble at one-fifth the cost, with the unique capability of source-separated uncertainty.

</details>


### [395] [Hybrid Pinching-Fluid Antenna Assisted Wireless Communications: Modeling and Performance Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06370)
*Xiao Lin,Yizhe Zhao,Xiangyang Wang,Halvin Yang,Bingxin Zhang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hybrid pinching-fluid antenna system (HPFAS) that combines a pinching antenna at the transmitter and a fluid antenna at the receiver to overcome limitations of each individual antenna type, achieving consistent performance improvements across both LoS and NLoS conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reconfigurable-antenna architectures have scenario-dependent limitations: fluid antennas struggle in LoS-dominant conditions, while pinching antennas perform poorly in severe NLoS scenarios. This motivates the need for a hybrid solution.

Method: The authors propose HPFAS with PA at the transmitter (for LoS enhancement) and FA at the receiver (for spatial diversity). A tractable channel model is developed, and outage probability expressions are derived for both single-user and multi-user scenarios.

Result: Simulation results validate the analytical model and demonstrate that HPFAS consistently outperforms standalone PA-only or FA-only systems under various propagation conditions.

Conclusion: HPFAS effectively combines the strengths of both antenna types, providing robust performance across diverse wireless environments where individual technologies fall short.

Abstract: Reconfigurable-antenna systems have received increasing attention for their ability to adapt wireless channels. However, existing architectures exhibit scenario-dependent limitations: fluid antennas provide strong diversity gains in rich-scattering environments but offer limited benefits under line-of-sight (LoS)-dominant conditions, while pinching antennas can effectively reduce path loss by adjusting the radiation point along a waveguide, yet perform poorly in severe non-LoS (NLoS) scenarios. This letter proposes a hybrid pinching-fluid antenna system (HPFAS), where pinching antenna (PA) is employed at the transmitter and a fluid antenna (FA) is used at the receiver to jointly exploit LoS enhancement and spatial diversity. A tractable channel model is developed, and outage probability expressions are derived for both single-user and multi-user scenarios. Simulation results validate the analysis and show that the proposed HPFAS consistently outperforms systems using only pinching antennas or only fluid antennas under various propagation conditions.

</details>


### [396] [Resource-Efficient CSI Prediction: A Gated Fusion and Factorized Projection Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06578)
*Mohammad Hussain,Maedeh Adibag,Dilara Gurer,Gokhan Kalem,Kerim Serin,Sinem Coleri*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a resource-efficient CSI prediction model combining GRU encoder with Luong attention, bottleneck gated fusion module, and DSLH, achieving -13.84 dB NMSE with 26% fewer parameters and 2.3x higher throughput than LinFormer baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CSI prediction is essential for dynamic MIMO systems but remains computationally demanding, motivating the need for a more resource-efficient predictor.

Method: The model combines a GRU encoder with Luong attention, a bottleneck gated fusion module (integrating local recurrent features with global attention context), and a Dimension-wise Separable Linear Head (DSLH) to reduce output mapping cost.

Result: On 3GPP TR 38.901 channels: average NMSE of -13.84 dB, 26% fewer parameters, ~2.3x higher inference throughput vs. LinFormer baseline.

Conclusion: The model is best suited for LOS and mixed-condition scenarios, offering a practical accuracy-efficiency trade-off for short-horizon CSI prediction at moderate sequence lengths.

Abstract: Accurate Channel State Information (CSI) prediction is essential for dynamic multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems but remains computationally demanding. This letter proposes a resource-efficient predictor that combines a gated recurrent unit (GRU) encoder with Luong attention, a bottleneck gated fusion module, and a Dimension-wise Separable Linear Head (DSLH). The gated fusion module integrates local recurrent features with global attention context, while the DSLH reduces the cost of the output mapping. Evaluated on 3GPP TR 38.901-compliant channels, the proposed model achieves an average NMSE of -13.84 dB with 26% fewer parameters and approximately 2.3x higher inference throughput than a dimension-matched LinFormer baseline. The proposed model is best suited to LOS and mixed-condition scenarios, offering a practical accuracy-efficiency trade-off for short-horizon CSI prediction at moderate sequence lengths.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [397] [On Unbiased Parameter Estimation and Signal Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05276)
*Joonas Lahtinen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper extends depth-unbiased source localization theory to unbiased parameter estimation and signal reconstruction for multiple non-zero parameters, providing theoretical bounds on recoverable parameters and revealing a sensor-number vs. SNR trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To expand depth-unbiased source localization to general unbiased parameter estimation and signal reconstruction, and to mathematically explain noise robustness of standardized/unbiased methods.

Method: Theoretical derivation of upper bounds on recoverable parameters in the noiseless case, definition of a probability measure for assessing correct magnitude-order recovery, and mathematical analysis of noise robustness.

Result: Upper bounds on the number of recoverable parameters, a probability measure for correct magnitude-order recovery, and the discovery of a trade-off between sensor count and signal-to-noise ratio.

Conclusion: The work provides mathematical explanations for noise robustness in standardized/unbiased methods and reveals a fundamental sensor-SNR trade-off, validated by numerical experiments.

Abstract: In this paper, we expand the theory of depth-unbiased source localization to unbiased parameter estimation and signal reconstruction of an arbitrary number of non-zero parameters to be recovered. The topic touches on the concept of exact reconstructibility, most commonly known in compressed sensing and multisource estimation in various imaging problems. The theoretical results derive upper bounds on the number of recoverable parameters in the noiseless case, and a probability measure is defined to assess the probability of obtaining all non-zero parameters with correct magnitude order. The work provides a mathematical explanation of the open question regarding the noise robustness of standardized and unbiased methods. Also, the paper reveals a trade-off between the number of sensors and the signal-to-noise ratio. Numerical experiments demonstrate the theoretical findings.

</details>


### [398] [When Semantic Communication Meets Queueing: Cross-Layer Latency and Task Fidelity Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05514)
*Yalin E. Sagduyu,Tugba Erpek*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes online semantic-rate controllers that adapt the latent dimension of a multi-task semantic autoencoder over block Rayleigh fading channels to optimize the latency-task fidelity tradeoff, achieving lower delay and AoI than fixed-rate baselines under long-term semantic error constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve spectrum efficiency and enable timely semantic updates in wireless communication by dynamically adapting the amount of transmitted semantic information (latent dimension) based on channel conditions, queue state, and task fidelity requirements.

Method: A multi-task semantic autoencoder jointly reconstructs images and predicts labels from received waveforms over block Rayleigh fading with AWGN. Two online controllers are developed: a queue-aware drift-plus-penalty policy minimizing delay under an average semantic error cap, and an age-aware policy minimizing time-average Age of Information (AoI).

Result: Adaptive latent dimension control significantly reduces delay and AoI compared to fixed-rate baselines while satisfying semantic error constraints, improving spectrum utilization in dynamic channel conditions.

Conclusion: Adapting semantic rate to congestion and fidelity requirements enables timely semantic updates with substantially lower delay and AoI than static approaches.

Abstract: Semantic communication (SemCom) with learned encoder-decoder architectures enables end-to-end learning of compact task-oriented representations optimized for the wireless channel, reducing channel resources needed to convey task-relevant information and improving spectrum efficiency. This paper studies semantic image transmission over block Rayleigh fading with AWGN using a multi-task semantic autoencoder that jointly reconstructs images and predicts labels from the received waveform. The latent dimension (complex channel uses per source sample) serves as a cross-layer control variable governing semantic fidelity and channel resource usage. We characterize the resulting latency-task fidelity tradeoff: larger latent representations improve inference accuracy but increase service time, channel uses, and queueing delay. Building on this insight, we develop online semantic-rate controllers that adapt the latent dimension per update under a long-term semantic error constraint. A queue-aware drift-plus-penalty policy minimizes delay subject to an average semantic error cap, while a complementary age-aware policy minimizes time-average Age of Information (AoI). By adapting the semantic rate to congestion and fidelity requirements, the proposed framework improves spectrum utilization and enables timely semantic updates with significantly lower delay and AoI than fixed-rate baselines.

</details>


### [399] [Infinite families of constacyclic codes supporting 3-designs and their applications in coding theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05613)
*Hongsheng Hu,Nian Li,Yanan Wu,Xiangyong Zeng*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper constructs two infinite families of λ-constacyclic codes over 𝔽_{q²} that yield infinite families of 3-designs, generalizing prior work, and applies them to construct maximal entanglement EAQECCs and optimal LRCs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constacyclic codes are theoretically important but underexplored compared to cyclic codes; the paper aims to advance the theory of constacyclic codes and their connection to designs, and to derive practical quantum and classical error-correcting codes.

Method: The paper constructs two infinite families of λ-constacyclic codes over 𝔽_{q²}, fully determines their parameters and weight distributions, and analyzes their subfield subcodes for applications to EAQECCs and LRCs.

Result: Two classes of maximal entanglement EAQECCs (with negative or high positive net rate) and two classes of distance-optimal and dimension-optimal LRCs are obtained.

Conclusion: The work generalizes known results, demonstrates the rich structure of constacyclic codes, and shows their practical value for constructing optimal QECCs and LRCs.

Abstract: Constacyclic codes over finite fields are of theoretical importance as they are closely related to a number of areas of mathematics such as algebra, algebraic geometry, graph theory,
  combinatorial designs and number theory. However, the study of constacyclic codes in this context remains limited compared to classical cyclic codes. This paper provides two infinite families of $λ$-constacyclic codes over $\mathbb{F}_{q^2}$ that support infinite families of 3-designs, which generalize the results in [IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory 69(4): 2341-2354, 2023]. The parameters and weight distributions are determined completely. Besides, we study their subfield subcodes and applications on constructing entanglement-assisted quantum error-correcting codes (EAQECCs) and locally recoverable codes (LRCs). It is worthy to mention that two classes of maximal entanglement EAQECCs with a negative or a high positive net rate are derived. Moreover, two classes of distance-optimal and dimension-optimal LRCs are also obtained.

</details>


### [400] [An Additive Approximation Scheme for Generating Dyadic Codings for the Outputs of an LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05837)
*Daniella Bar-Lev,Farzad Farnoud,Ryan Gabrys*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper develops a polynomial-time additive approximation scheme for partitioning a discrete probability distribution (e.g., LLM next-token distribution) into a dyadic distribution under encoding rate constraints, with applications to LLM-based steganography.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To approximate discrete probability distributions (like LLM next-token distributions) using dyadic distributions induced by binary trees under rate constraints, minimizing total variation distance.

Method: Formulates the task as a tree-based partitioning problem and develops a polynomial-time additive approximation scheme for the rate-constrained setting in the constant-rate regime.

Result: Provides provable guarantees for near-optimal dyadic approximations under rate constraints.

Conclusion: Yields a principled framework for LLM-based steganography, where the rate maps to bits of hidden information per token and the total variation bound controls statistical detectability.

Abstract: We study the problem of approximating a discrete probability distribution, such as the next-token distribution of a large language model, by a dyadic distribution induced by a binary tree under encoding rate constraints. The objective is to partition the support of the distribution and assign dyadic probabilities to minimize total variation distance while achieving a prescribed rate. We formulate this task as a tree-based partitioning problem and develop a polynomial-time additive approximation scheme for the rate-constrained setting in the constant-rate regime. Our results provide provable guarantees for near-optimal dyadic approximations and, as an application, yield a principled framework for LLM-based steganography, where the rate maps to bits of hidden information embedded per token and the total variation bound controls statistical detectability.

</details>


### [401] [Identification for Inverse Gaussian Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06103)
*Mohammad Javad Salariseddigh*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper derives bounds on identification capacity for inverse Gaussian channels (molecular communication), showing super-exponential growth (~2^{(n log n)R}) in codebook size under peak time constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental limits of molecular communications in fluid environments, specifically the identification capacity of inverse Gaussian channels modeling molecule propagation via diffusion and drift.

Method: Derivation of lower and upper bounds on identification capacity for inverse Gaussian channels using deterministic encoding schemes under a peak time constraint, with asymptotic analysis of codebook size growth.

Result: Under a mild regularity condition on the noise (stochastic first arrival time), the identification capacity exhibits super-exponential growth in codeword length n, approximately 2^{(n log n)R} where R is the coding rate.

Conclusion: The identification capacity of inverse Gaussian channels can achieve super-exponential growth, revealing extremely high potential for information identification in molecular communication systems.

Abstract: We derive lower and upper bounds on the identification capacity of inverse Gaussian channels, a fundamental model for molecular communications in fluid environments. The analysis considers deterministic encoding schemes under a peak time constraint and characterizes the asymptotic growth of codebook sizes. A central result reveals that, under a mild regularity condition on the noise, i.e., the stochastic first arrival time of an information-carrying molecule propagating via diffusion and drift to the receiver, the identification capacity exhibits super-exponential growth in the codeword length, $n,$ i.e., $\sim 2^{(n \log n)R},$ where $R$ is the coding rate.

</details>


### [402] [Locally Repairable Codes with Availability via Elliptic Function Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06182)
*Junjie Huang,Chang-An Zhao*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper constructs optimal locally repairable codes (LRCs) with one or two recovering sets using ordinary elliptic curves (not just supersingular ones), achieving longer code lengths \(O(q+2\sqrt{q})\) with flexible locality, and also provides a general framework for LRCs with two recovering sets via elliptic function field automorphisms, yielding codes with length \(O(q^2+2q)\) and small Singleton-defect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of prior work that relied only on maximal supersingular elliptic curves, and to broaden the curve selection for constructing optimal LRCs with improved length and flexibility.

Method: Uses ordinary elliptic curves with many rational points instead of supersingular ones; for two recovering sets, leverages automorphism groups of elliptic function fields and a novel construction for determining functions \(e_i\).

Result: Obtains new families of \(q\)-ary optimal LRCs with length \(O(q+2\sqrt{q})\) and flexible locality; for two recovering sets, achieves \(q^2\)-ary LRCs with length \(O(q^2+2q)\) and Singleton-defect \(O\!\left(\frac{2\ell}{q^2+2q-8\ell}\right)\).

Conclusion: Broadens the available curves for optimal LRC construction and provides a general framework for codes with two recovering sets, advancing the theory of LRCs for distributed storage.

Abstract: Locally repairable codes with availability have become essential components in modern large-scale distributed cloud storage systems and numerous other applications. In this paper, we focus on the construction of locally repairable codes with one or two recovering sets via elliptic function fields. Prior pioneering work by Li et al. (IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory, vol. 65, no. 1, 2019) and Ma and Xing (J. Comb. Theory Ser. A., vol. 193, 2023) employed maximal supersingular elliptic curves to obtain several optimal (classical) locally repairable codes. In contrast, we consider ordinary elliptic curves with many rational points. This approach yields several new families of \(q\)-ary optimal locally repairable codes with length \(O(q+2\sqrt{q})\) and flexible locality. Consequently, our work broadens the selection of curves available for the construction of optimal locally repairable codes.
  Furthermore, we present a general framework for constructing locally repairable codes with two recovering sets via automorphism groups of elliptic function fields. To realize this framework, we devise a novel construction for determining the functions \(e_i\) in the construction of locally repairable codes. By employing both supersingular and ordinary elliptic curves, we obtain several families of locally repairable codes with two recovering sets. In particular, we construct a family of \(q^2\)-ary locally repairable codes with two recovering sets, achieving length \(O(q^2+2q)\) and Singleton-defect \(O\!\left(\frac{2\ell}{q^2+2q-8\ell}\right)\), where \(\ell \mid\mid q + 2\) with \(4\ell < q\).

</details>


### [403] [Fluid Antenna Systems Enabling 6G HRLLC With Port Switching Delay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06275)
*Xusheng Zhu,Kai-Kit Wong,Hao Xu,Chenguang Rao,Hyundong Shin*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper investigates Fluid Antenna Systems (FAS) for hyper-reliable low-latency communications (HRLLC), deriving closed-form expressions for average BLER and achievable rate, proving that reliability, rate, and energy efficiency are strictly unimodal in port dimension, and establishing a switching-delay threshold for net gains over fixed-position antennas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
