We introduce a likelihood-ratio--based confidence sequence that unifies heterogeneous generalized linear observations and yields an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set under a self-concordance assumption. Building on this confidence set, we propose a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that adaptively allocates queries by tracking a minimax-optimal design over a joint action space of arms and pairs. We establish $δ$-correctness and provide high-probability upper bounds on the stopping time. We further extend the framework to a cost-aware setting that accounts for heterogeneous acquisition costs across feedback modalities. Empirical experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithms significantly improve sample efficiency over baseline methods.

</details>


### [456] [Evaluating Explainability in Safety-Critical ATR Systems: Limitations of Post-Hoc Methods and Paths Toward Robust XAI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05748)
*Vanessa Buhrmester,David Muench,Dimitri Bulatov,Michael Arens*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates XAI methods for safety-critical Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) systems, identifying limitations like spurious explanations and instability, and argues current techniques are insufficient for safety-critical deployment, advocating for causally grounded approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High predictive performance in ATR systems is insufficient for safety-critical environments; model decisions must also be interpretable, reliable, and suitable for validation, motivating a structured evaluation of explainability methods.

Method: The authors identify major XAI paradigms (saliency-based, attention-based, surrogate, detection-aware), formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment problem, introduce a taxonomy, and evaluate methods across four dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipulation, and suitability for validation and verification.

Result: Critical failure modes identified include spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust induced by visually convincing outputs. Current post-hoc XAI methods show systematic limitations for safety-critical deployment.

Conclusion: Widely used XAI techniques may be insufficient for safety-critical ATR; the field needs to move beyond visually plausible explanations toward robust, causally grounded, and physically informed methods that support reliable decision-making and system-level assurance.

Abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is increasingly rec ognized as essential for deploying machine learning systems in safety critical environments. In Automatic Target Recognition (ATR), where models operate on image, video, radar, and multisensor data, high pre dictive performance alone is insufficient. Model decisions must also be interpretable, reliable, and suitable for validation. This paper presents a structured evaluation of explainability methods in the context of safety-critical ATR systems: We identify major XAI paradigms, including saliency-based, attention-based, and surrogate ap proaches, as well as recent detection-aware extensions. Based on this, we formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment problem, introduce a taxonomy, and assess these methods with respect to four key dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipula tion, and suitability for validation and verification. The analysis identifies systematic limitations of current post-hoc explanation methods. In par ticular, we derive critical failure modes such as spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust induced by visually con vincing outputs. These findings indicate that widely used XAI techniques may be insufficient for safety-critical deployment. Finally, we discuss implications for ATR systems and outline directions toward more robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explain ability methods. Our results emphasize the need to move beyond visually plausible explanations toward approaches that support reliable decision making and system-level assurance.

</details>


### [457] [Confidence is the key: how conformal prediction enhances the generative design of permeable peptides](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05770)
*Laura van Weesep,Sunay Chankeshwara,Leonardo De Maria,Florian David,Ola Engkvist,Gökçe Geylan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents an RL-guided generative framework that designs permeable cyclic peptides using conformal prediction to handle predictive uncertainty, improving the reliability and efficiency of molecular optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive models used in generative RL for molecular design often suggest molecules outside their domain of applicability, leading to unreliable predictions. This is especially problematic for cyclic peptides, which are understudied yet promising for therapeutic applications.

Method: The authors integrate conformal prediction (CP) into an RL-guided generative framework to quantify uncertainty of a permeability predictor. Generated peptides are rewarded using CP-informed predictions under a user-defined confidence level.

Result: Rewarding generated peptides with CP-informed predictions improves both reliability and efficiency of the peptide optimization process, and discourages exploration outside the predictor's applicability domain.

Conclusion: This is the first work combining generative modeling and conformal prediction for molecular design, effectively bridging predictive uncertainty and RL-guided exploration.

Abstract: Generative models coupled with reinforcement learning (RL), such as REINVENT and PepINVENT, have emerged as a powerful framework for de novo molecular design. During the ideation process these generative frameworks utilize various predictive models as part of the optimization objectives. However, the utility of the predictive models can be limited by their domain of applicability. When RL is used to explore the chemical space with predictive models, it can suggest molecules that lie outside the predictor's domain of applicability. As a result, the predictions may become less reliable, potentially steering designs into high reward but also high uncertainty chemical spaces. This is particularly pronounced for cyclic peptides which show therapeutic promise due to their modifiability and large interaction surfaces but are understudied compared to small molecules. While passive membrane permeation in cyclic peptides has attracted interest, identifying optimal permeable designs remains challenging yet crucial for targeting intracellular sites. We present an RL-guided generative framework that designs permeable cyclic peptides using an uncertainty-aware permeability predictor as the scoring component. To address predictive uncertainty, especially impacted by novel chemistry, we integrate conformal prediction (CP) as our uncertainty quantification method. CP assesses designs based on the calibrated model under a user-defined confidence level. We demonstrate that rewarding generated peptides with CP-informed predictions improves both reliability and efficiency of peptide optimization process. This also discourages exploration outside the predictor's applicability domain. This approach bridges the gap between predictive uncertainty and RL-guided exploration, showing how generative modelling and conformal prediction can be combined for the first time.

</details>


### [458] [CircuitFormer: A Circuit Language Model for Analog Topology Design from Natural Language Prompt](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05773)
*Md Touhidul Islam,Sujan Kumar Saha,Farimah Farahmandi,Mark Tehranipoor*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces CircuitFormer, a 511M-parameter encoder-decoder transformer for analog circuit design, powered by CKT (Circuit Tokenizer) that encodes circuit graph structure by mining frequent subcircuits. It curates the largest annotated analog circuit dataset (31,341 pairs), achieves 100% syntactic correctness and 83% functional success, outperforming open-source LLMs by 10-14% with 240x fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automating analog circuit design is a long-standing EDA challenge. While LLMs excel at software code generation, their application to analog hardware is limited by (i) scarcity of annotated netlist-natural language datasets, and (ii) inefficiency of general-purpose tokenizers like BPE in capturing circuit graph structure.

Method: (1) Curated the largest annotated analog circuit dataset with 31,341 netlist-description pairs. (2) Proposed Circuit Tokenizer (CKT), a graph tokenizer that mines frequent subcircuits, achieving O(1) vocabulary complexity (vs. prior O(n_max)). (3) Trained CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter encoder-decoder transformer using CKT tokenization.

Result: CKT reduces sequence length by 57% and achieves 2.3x better compression ratio than BPE with a fixed vocabulary of 512. CircuitFormer achieves 100% syntactic correctness and 83% functional success across all major analog circuit categories, outperforming SOTA open-source LLMs by 10% and 14% respectively with 240x fewer parameters.

Conclusion: Circuit tokenization tailored to circuit graph structure significantly improves analog design automation. CKT's constant vocabulary complexity enables scalable circuit representation, and CircuitFormer demonstrates that domain-specific, smaller models can dramatically outperform much larger general-purpose LLMs on analog circuit design tasks.

Abstract: Automating analog circuit design remains a longstanding challenge in Electronic Design Automation (EDA). While Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized software code generation, their application to analog hardware design is hindered by two critical limitations: (i) the scarcity of analog design datasets containing natural language description of a design and its corresponding netlist, and (ii) the inefficiency of general-purpose tokenizers (e.g., Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)) in capturing the inherent graph structure of circuits. To bridge this gap, first, we curate the largest annotated dataset of analog circuit netlists to date, comprising 31,341 netlist-natural language description pairs across all major circuit classes. Furthermore, we propose Circuit Tokenizer (CKT), a novel circuit graph tokenizer designed to encode netlist connectivity by explicitly mining frequent subcircuits. In terms of scalability, CKT overcomes the bottleneck of prior circuit graph serialization methods where vocabulary size scales linearly with maximum number of components in the dataset, n_max, (O(n_max)); instead, CKT decouples vocabulary growth from circuit complexity, achieving a constant O(1) complexity. Empirically, CKT outperforms standard BPE on circuit topology representation, reducing sequence length by 57% and achieving a 2.3x superior compression ratio using a compact, fixed vocabulary of size 512. Leveraging this optimized tokenization, we train a circuit-specific language model, CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter encoder-decoder transformer. Our model achieves 100% syntactic correctness and an 83% functional success rate across all major analog circuit categories, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% and 14%, respectively, while requiring 240x fewer parameters. The dataset is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/touhid314/cktformer-dataset.

</details>


### [459] [HEDP: A Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt-based Framework for Domain Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05776)
*Yu Feng,Zhen Tian,Haoran Luo,Xie Yu,Diancheng Cheng,Haoyue Zheng,Shuai Lyu,Ping Zong,Lianyuan Li,Xin Ge,Yifan Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HEDP proposes a Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt framework for Domain Incremental Learning, using Helmholtz free energy-inspired regularization and hybrid energy-distance weighting to improve domain separability, selection, and generalization, achieving 2.57% accuracy gain on benchmarks like CORe50.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shifts in incremental learning cause severe performance degradation and catastrophic forgetting. Existing methods lack effective mechanisms to enhance domain representation separability and generalize to unseen domains.

Method: HEDP introduces (1) an energy regularization loss based on Helmholtz free energy to improve domain representation separability, and (2) a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that combines energy-based and distance-based cues for better domain selection and generalization.

Result: Achieves superior performance on unseen domains with a 2.57% accuracy gain on multiple benchmarks including CORe50, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enhancing open-world adaptability.

Conclusion: The proposed HEDP framework effectively addresses domain incremental learning challenges by leveraging energy-based principles, demonstrating improved generalization and reduced forgetting across domain shifts.

Abstract: Domain Incremental Learning is a critical scenario that requires models to continuously adapt to new data domains without retraining. However, domain shifts often cause severe performance degradation. To address this, we propose Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt, a domain-incremental framework inspired by Helmholtz free energy. HEDP introduces an energy regularization loss to enhance the separability of domain representations and a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues to improve domain selection and generalization. Experiments on multiple benchmarks, including CORe50, show that HEDP achieves superior performance on unseen domains with a 2.57\% accuracy gain, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enhancing open-world adaptability. Our code is \href{https://github.com/dannis97500/HEDP/}{available here}.

</details>


### [460] [Von Neumann Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05780)
*Shekhar S. Chandra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Von Neumann Neuron, a deep learning neuron inspired by John von Neumann's cellular automaton model of the brain. These neurons form Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) that self-engineer their architecture based on input/output locations in a cellular array. The framework extends neural operators and learns Green's functions with convolutions on a diffusion-signature cellular topology. VNNs are proven computationally universal and belong to broader "Cellular Machines." Experiments show VNN-based MLPs outperform equivalent deep learning variants in parameter efficiency and task capability, including extending the Von Neumann hardware architecture to cellular systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To revive and modernize John von Neumann's mid-20th century cellular automaton model of the brain within a deep learning framework, enabling self-engineering neural architectures with specialized, learnable roles based on cellular topology.

Method: The Von Neumann Neuron is constructed by developing von Neumann's cellular system in a modern deep learning setting. The mathematical framework builds on neural operators and learning Green's functions with convolutions applied to a cellular topology characterized by a diffusion signature. Theoretical proofs establish computational universality within a broader class called Cellular Machines.

Result: VNN-based multi-layered perceptrons outperform equivalent standard deep learning variants on basic tasks, achieve better parameter efficiency, and can learn new types of tasks, including solving for and constructing a cellular extension of the Von Neumann hardware architecture.

Conclusion: Von Neumann Networks represent a novel, self-engineering neural network paradigm grounded in cellular automata theory, offering improved performance and parameter efficiency while opening new research opportunities at the intersection of cellular computing and deep learning.

Abstract: In the mid-twentieth century, mathematician and polymath John von Neumann created a computational system on an array of cells as a simple model of the human brain, where each cell had one of a finite set of roles or states that he predicted would be modelled by a diffusion process. In this work, we show that such a system, when developed in a modern deep learning setting, enables the construction of an artificial neuron having specialized roles that can be learnt. We refer to this neuron as the Von Neumann neuron, and the resulting neural network from such neurons result in a self-engineered design whose architecture is only dependent on the structure and locations of its inputs and outputs on this cellular array. The mathematical framework for these Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) is also constructed and shows that they are based on the extension of neural operators and the learning of Green's functions with convolutions on a cellular topology having a diffusion signature. We also prove that these VNNs are part of a more general computational system called Cellular Machines that are computationally universal. Initial experiments show that VNN based multi-layered perceptrons outperform their equivalent deep learning variant on basic tasks, while being more parameter efficient and are capable of learning new types of tasks. This includes the ability to solve for and construct an extension of the Von Neumann (hardware) architecture common to all modern computers to cells and suggests new opportunities that could be explored.

</details>


### [461] [Sheet as Token: A Graph-Enhanced Representation for Multi-Sheet Spreadsheet Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05811)
*Yiming Lei,Yiqi Wang,Yujia Zhang,Bo Guan,Depei Zhu,Chunhui Wang,Zhuonan Hao,Tianyu Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Sheet as Token is a graph-enhanced framework that treats each worksheet as a unified semantic unit (compact dense token) for multi-sheet spreadsheet retrieval, using a Graph Retriever with multi-stage graph transformer to compose semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations for improved listwise retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Workbook-scale spreadsheet understanding is challenging because relevant information is distributed across multiple sheets with heterogeneous schemas, layouts, and implicit relationships, and existing chunk-centric retrieval approaches fragment worksheets into isolated text spans, weakening global sheet-level semantics.

Method: Proposes Sheet as Token framework: (1) extracts schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features; (2) encodes each worksheet into a compact dense token; (3) uses a Graph Retriever to construct a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens with semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations; (4) composes these relations through a multi-stage graph transformer to retrieve supporting sheet sets.

Result: Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus show that sheet-level tokenization learns stable representations, and graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval over a shallow graph baseline with limited additional graph-side computation.

Conclusion: Sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding.

Abstract: Workbook-scale spreadsheet understanding is increasingly important for language-model-based data analysis agents, but remains challenging because relevant information is often distributed across multiple sheets with heterogeneous schemas, layouts, and implicit relationships. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches typically decompose spreadsheets into rows, columns, or blocks to improve scalability; however, such chunk-centric representations can fragment worksheets into isolated text spans and weaken global sheet-level semantics. We propose Sheet as Token, a graph-enhanced framework that treats each worksheet as a unified semantic unit for multi-sheet spreadsheet retrieval. Our method extracts schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features, and encodes each worksheet into a compact dense token. Given a natural-language query, a Graph Retriever constructs a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens using semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations, and composes these channels through a multi-stage graph transformer to retrieve supporting sheet sets. Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus show that sheet-level tokenization learns stable representations, and that graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval over a shallow graph baseline with limited additional graph-side computation. These results suggest that sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding.

</details>


### [462] [Long-Horizon Q-Learning: Accurate Value Learning via n-Step Inequalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05812)
*Armaan A. Abraham,Lucy Xiaoyang Shi,Chelsea Finn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LQL introduces a hinge-loss penalty based on optimality bounds to stabilize long-horizon Q-learning, outperforming 1-step and n-step TD methods without extra computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bootstrapping in long-horizon value-based RL causes estimation errors to propagate backward via TD updates and compound over time, making learning brittle.

Method: LQL uses a hinge loss to penalize violations of optimality bounds (any realized action sequence lower-bounds optimal policy expected return), computed from existing network outputs without extra forward passes.

Result: LQL consistently outperforms both 1-step TD and n-step TD learning across online and offline-to-online benchmarks when combined with multiple SOTA methods.

Conclusion: LQL provides a principled, computationally lightweight stabilization mechanism for Q-learning that effectively mitigates compounding error in long-horizon settings.

Abstract: Off-policy, value-based reinforcement learning methods such as Q-learning are appealing because they can learn from arbitrary experience, including data collected by older policies or other agents. In practice, however, bootstrapping makes long-horizon learning brittle: estimation errors at later states propagate backward through temporal-difference (TD) updates and can compound over time. We propose long-horizon Q-learning (LQL), which introduces a principled backstop against compounding error when learning the optimal action-value function. LQL builds on a prior optimality tightening observation: any realized action sequence lower-bounds what the optimal policy can achieve in expectation, so acting optimally earlier should not be worse than following the observed actions for several steps before switching to optimal behavior. Our contribution is to turn this inequality into a practical stabilization mechanism for Q-learning by using a hinge loss to penalize violations of these bounds. Importantly, LQL computes these penalties using network outputs already produced for the TD error, requiring no auxiliary networks and no additional forward passes relative to Q-learning. When combined with multiple state-of-the-art methods on a range of online and offline-to-online benchmarks, LQL consistently outperforms both 1-step TD and n-step TD learning at similar runtime.

</details>


### [463] [AGPO: Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization for Verifiable Reasoning and Search Ads Relevance at JD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05826)
*Yang Xu,Kun Yao,Yiming Deng,Zheng Fang,Kai Ming Ting,Ming Pang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AGPO is a new RLVR method that uses negative-dominant reinforcement and group advantage mechanism to prevent reasoning boundary shrinkage, achieving SOTA accuracy on math benchmarks and improving search ads relevance optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods improve sampling efficiency but cause reasoning boundary shrinkage—base models achieve higher coverage at large sample sizes while trained models narrow their reasoning capability. The goal is to counteract this boundary shrinkage.

Method: AGPO (Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization) uses two key strategies: (1) negative-dominant reinforcement to suppress incorrect reasoning paths while preserving exploration capacity, and (2) a group advantage mechanism that scales positive updates based on intra-group variance to focus on rare correct paths.

Result: AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on five mathematical benchmarks, consistently improves pass@k performance at scale, and effectively enhances data annotation quality for search ads relevance optimization in a large-scale industrial application.

Conclusion: AGPO successfully counteracts reasoning boundary shrinkage in RLVR, maintaining base model exploration capacity while achieving superior accuracy, with demonstrated effectiveness in both mathematical reasoning and real-world industrial applications.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has demonstrated notable success in enhancing the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs). However, recent studies reveal that while current RLVR methods improve sampling efficiency towards correct paths, they do not elicit fundamentally new reasoning patterns. Instead, the reasoning capability boundary of trained models often narrows compared to their base models, with base models achieving higher coverage at large sample sizes. In this work, we propose Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO) to counteract this boundary shrinkage. AGPO adopts a negative-dominant reinforcement strategy to suppress incorrect reasoning paths, maintaining the base model's exploration capacity. For positive reinforcement, AGPO adopts a group advantage mechanism, which scales positive updates based on intra-group variance, allowing the model to focus on rare correct paths while suppressing updates from trivial paths. Our experiments on five mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while consistently improving pass@$k$ performance at scale. In a large-scale industrial application for search ads relevance optimization, AGPO effectively enhances the quality of the data annotation, leading to substantial performance gains in downstream student models.

</details>


### [464] [MolRecBench-Wild: A Real-World Benchmark for Optical Chemical Structure Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05832)
*Haote Yang,Hui Wang,Chen Zhu,Jingchao Wang,Linye Li,Hongbin Lai,Huijie Ao,Yongxuan Lyu,Jiang Wu,Jiaxing Sun,Lua Chen,Yuanyuan Cao,Ruijie Zhang,Shengxin Lu,Lijun Wu,Bin Wang,Conghui He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces MOSAIC (difficulty framework), MolRecBench-Wild (benchmark), and CARBON (representation language) to address OCSR limitations on real-world molecular diagrams, showing current models struggle significantly on academic literature images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current OCSR systems are unreliable on real-world molecular diagrams due to visual and chemical complexity; existing benchmarks (e.g., patent-based) don't reflect real academic scenarios, creating a performance gap.

Method: Proposes MOSAIC (dual-dimensional difficulty framework with 37 fine-grained labels), constructs MolRecBench-Wild (5,029 structures from 820 recent papers), develops CARBON (representation language for non-standard semantics), and adopts dual-track evaluation (CARBON + SMILES).

Result: Evaluated 18 OCSR-capable models on MolRecBench-Wild; results show severe performance degradation compared to previous patent benchmarks, exposing a large gap between benchmarks and real-world academic scenarios.

Conclusion: Real-world molecular diagram recognition remains a significant challenge; the proposed framework, benchmark, and representation language highlight and help address the gap between current OCSR capabilities and real academic use.

Abstract: Optical Chemical Structure Recognition (OCSR) aims to translate molecular diagrams in scientific literature into machine-readable formats, but current systems remain unreliable on real-world images due to substantial visual and chemical complexity. We introduce MOSAIC, a dual-dimensional difficulty framework with 37 fine-grained labels that jointly characterize visual interference and chemical semantic challenges in molecular diagrams. Based on this framework, we construct MolRecBench-Wild, a benchmark of 5,029 structures from 820 recent chemistry papers, covering the full difficulty spectrum observed in real publications. To enable faithful semantic evaluation beyond SMILES and MolFile, we propose CARBON, a representation language capable of expressing valence variations, icon-based groups, and other non-standard chemical semantics. We further adopt a dual-track evaluation protocol supporting both CARBON and SMILES outputs for broad model compatibility. Comprehensive experiments over 18 OCSR-capable models reveal severe performance degradation on MolRecBench-Wild, exposing a large gap between previous patent benchmarks and real-world academic scenarios.

</details>


### [465] [On the Role of Language Representations in Auto-Bidding: Findings and Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05833)
*Guanyu Zhu,Jining Luan,Hanwen Du,Xinyu Fang,Sibo Xu,Ersheng Ni,Hongji Li,Jincheng Fang,Ronghao Chen,Huacan Wang,Xuanqi Lan,Yongxin Ni,Yiqi Sun,Youhua Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SemBid is a new auto-bidding framework that integrates LLM-encoded semantics (Task, History, Strategy) as tokens into offline bidding trajectories via self-attention, significantly improving controllability, generalization, and constraint satisfaction over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing auto-bidding methods use compact numerical state representations that cannot explicitly capture high-level intent, evolving feedback, or operator-style strategic guidance. While LLMs encode semantic information well, it's unclear when they help and how to integrate them without losing numerical precision.

Method: SemBid injects three types of LLM-encoded semantic inputs (Task, History, Strategy) as tokens alongside numerical trajectory tokens, using self-attention for integration. Preliminary studies confirmed that LLM embeddings contain bidding-relevant cues but cannot replace numerical features, and semantic-numeric integration must be careful rather than naive concatenation.

Result: Across diverse scenarios and budget regimes, SemBid outperforms competitive baselines from offline RL and generative sequence modeling, with consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness.

Conclusion: Careful semantic-numeric integration via token-level injection with self-attention effectively enhances auto-bidding, and the approach is validated across multiple settings.

Abstract: Auto-bidding is a crucial task in real-time advertising markets, where policies must optimize long-horizon value under delivery constraints (e.g., budget and CPA). Existing methods for auto-bidding rely on compact numerical state representations: while they can implicitly capture delivery dynamics, they offer limited support for explicitly representing and controlling high-level intent, evolving feedback, and operator-style strategic guidance in real campaigns. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a powerful method for encoding semantic information, it remains unclear when LLMs help and how to integrate them without sacrificing numerical precision. Through systematic preliminary studies, we find that (1) LLM embeddings contain bidding-relevant cues yet cannot replace numerical features, and (2) gains emerge only with careful semantic--numeric integration rather than naive concatenation. Motivated by these findings, we propose \textit{SemBid}, a novel auto-bidding framework that injects LLM-encoded semantics into offline bidding trajectories at the token level. SemBid introduces three semantic inputs: \textit{Task}, \textit{History}, and \textit{Strategy}. It injects these semantics as tokens alongside numerical trajectory tokens and uses self-attention to integrate them, improving controllability and generalization across objectives. Across diverse scenarios and budget regimes, SemBid outperforms competitive baselines from offline RL and generative sequence modeling, with more consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness. Our code is available at: \href{https://github.com/AlanYu04/SemBid-KDD2026}{\textcolor{blue}{here}}.

</details>


### [466] [Taklif.AI: LLM-Powered Platform for Interest-Based Personalized College Assignments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05842)
*Zaki Kurdya,Mohammed Zuqlam,Salem Amassi,Shady Telbany,Motaz Saad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Taklif.AI is an LLM-powered platform that generates personalized assignments by incorporating students' extracurricular interests and cultural contexts, using a serverless AWS architecture with Llama 3.3 70B. Preliminary testing with 68 participants showed 84% found personalization beneficial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Educators struggle to create engaging, personalized assignments for diverse students; one-size-fits-all approaches reduce engagement and increase plagiarism.

Method: Taklif.AI uses LLMs (Llama 3.3 70B) with structured prompt engineering, input/output guardrails, serverless AWS architecture (Next.js), LiteLLM for load balancing, and LangChain for prompt orchestration to generate assignments tailored to students' interests and cultural contexts.

Result: Preliminary user acceptance testing (68 participants: 65 students, 3 educators) showed 84% rated the personalization feature as beneficial.

Conclusion: The platform shows positive initial reception, with plans for rigorous empirical evaluation of learning outcomes, while current capabilities and limitations are discussed.

Abstract: Educators face significant challenges in creating engaging, personalized assignments that accommodate students' diverse interests and cognitive abilities. Traditional one-size-fits-all assignments frequently lead to decreased student engagement and increased reliance on unethical practices such as plagiarism. To address these challenges, we present Taklif.AI, a platform that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate personalized assignments tailored to individual student interests. Unlike existing AI-powered educational platforms that personalize based on academic performance metrics alone, Taklif.AI incorporates students' extracurricular interests and cultural contexts into the assignment generation process through a structured prompt engineering pipeline with input and output guardrails. The platform employs a serverless architecture on AWS with Next.js, using Llama 3.3 70B as the primary LLM via LiteLLM for multi-provider load balancing and LangChain for prompt orchestration. We describe the system architecture, the prompt design methodology, and the guardrails framework that ensures output quality. Preliminary user acceptance testing with 68 participants (65 students and 3 educators) indicates positive reception, with 84% of participants rating the personalization feature as beneficial. We discuss the platform's current capabilities and limitations, and outline directions for rigorous empirical evaluation of learning outcomes.

</details>


### [467] [AirQualityBench: A Realistic Evaluation Benchmark for Global Air Quality Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05854)
*Xing Xu,Xu Wang,Yudong Zhang,Huilin Zhao,Zhengyang Zhou,Yang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AirQualityBench is a realistic global benchmark for air-quality forecasting that preserves real-world missingness patterns, heterogeneous pollutant scales, and station distribution, revealing that models excelling on sanitized datasets often fail under practical monitoring conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing air-quality forecasting evaluations rely on preprocessed, normalized, and imputed datasets that do not reflect real-world challenges such as uneven coverage, structured missingness, heterogeneous scales, and deployment costs, leading to misleading performance assessments.

Method: The benchmark uses 3,720 stations globally (2021–2025), six pollutants, native observation masks (no imputation), inverse-transformed error reporting on physical concentration scales, and a unified evaluation protocol for spatio-temporal models.

Result: Models that perform well on sanitized/regional datasets do not reliably transfer to the fragmented, global monitoring streams that AirQualityBench provides.

Conclusion: AirQualityBench serves as a realistic testbed for developing scalable, mask-aware, and physically interpretable air-quality forecasting models, filling the gap between lab-style evaluations and real-world deployment.

Abstract: Air-quality forecasting models are commonly evaluated on regional, preprocessed, and normalized datasets, where missing observations are removed or artificially completed. Such protocols simplify comparison but hide the conditions that dominate real monitoring networks: uneven global coverage, structured missingness, heterogeneous pollutant scales, and deployment cost. We introduce \textbf{AirQualityBench}, a global multi-pollutant benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models under these realistic conditions. The benchmark contains hourly observations from 3,720 monitoring stations over 2021--2025, covers six major pollutants, and preserves provider-native observation masks. Rather than imputing a dense data tensor, AirQualityBench exposes missingness as part of the forecasting problem and reports errors on valid future observations after inverse transformation to physical concentration scales. Evaluating representative spatio-temporal models under this unified protocol shows that strong performance on sanitized datasets does not reliably transfer to global, fragmented monitoring streams. AirQualityBench therefore serves as a realistic testbed for scalable, mask-aware, and physically interpretable air-quality forecasting. All benchmark data, code, evaluation scripts, and baseline implementations are available at \href{https://github.com/Star-Learning/AirQualityBench}{GitHub}.

</details>


### [468] [SANEmerg: An Emergent Communication Framework for Semantic-aware Agentic AI Networking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05861)
*Yong Xiao,Haoran Zhou,Yujie Zhou,Marwan Krunz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SANEmerg is a multi-agent emergent communication framework for agentic AI networks that enables bandwidth-constrained, computationally bounded agents to develop task-specific signaling protocols, achieving superior task accuracy with reduced bandwidth and computational overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional networking rigidly decouples communication and computation, causing inefficiencies in large-scale agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems where heterogeneous AI agents must cooperate in real time.

Method: SANEmerg introduces (1) a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter that dynamically prioritizes higher-contribution message dimensions, and (2) a complexity-regularizer based on the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle for computationally bounded signaling emergence.

Result: SANEmerg demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art solutions, achieving superior task accuracy while substantially reducing bandwidth and computational overhead.

Conclusion: Emergent communication with bandwidth-adaptive filtering and MDL-based complexity regularization is a promising approach for efficient, scalable agentic AI networking systems.

Abstract: Future networking systems are envisioned to become part of an agentic AI-native ecosystem in which a vast number of heterogeneous and specialized AI agents cooperate seamlessly to fulfill complex user requirements in real time. However, traditional networking paradigms are characterized by a rigid decoupling of communication and computation, which often leads to significant inefficiencies in large-scale agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems. Emergent communication offers a novel solution by enabling autonomous agents that support task-specific signaling protocols for information exchange and collaborative coordination. In this paper, we consider a multi-agent emergent communication framework, tailored for semantic-aware AgentNet systems in which the user's semantic intent can be automatically detected, inferred, and linked to a set of sub-tasks to be assigned to a set of agents. We investigate how communication and signaling protocols can emerge among collaborative agents with computationally bounded intelligence under stringent bandwidth constraints. Our proposed framework, called SANEmerg, is designed to facilitate the emergence of communication for collaborative task fulfillment while adhering to the physical limits of AgentNet. SANEmerg incorporates a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter that dynamically prioritizes the transmission of higher-contribution message dimensions, ensuring robust performance in bandwidth-limited environments. Furthermore, SANEmerg integrates a complexity-regularizer grounded in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to facilitate the emergence of computationally bounded signaling. Evaluated via an AgentNet prototype and extensive experimentation, SANEmerg demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art solutions, achieving superior task accuracy while significantly reducing bandwidth and computational overhead.

</details>


### [469] [XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05866)
*Hanyu Gao,Bin Cao,Yunyue Su,Tong-Yi Zhang,Qiang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: XDecomposer is a prior-free deep learning framework that jointly decomposes and identifies multiple crystalline phases from multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns without needing candidate lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase count.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multiphase PXRD analysis is a major bottleneck because real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures, and existing methods assume single-phase inputs or require extensive prior knowledge (e.g., candidate phase lists), failing in multiphase settings.

Method: The authors formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, using a phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction to infer an unordered set of phase-resolved components, mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations.

Result: On both simulated and experimental datasets, XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, with strong generalization to unseen mixtures.

Conclusion: XDecomposer provides a practical, data-driven route toward source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis, reducing the long-standing dependence on prior-guided iterative phase matching.

Abstract: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

</details>


### [470] [Agentic, Context-Aware Risk Intelligence in the Internet of Value](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05878)
*Basel Magableh,OmniRisk Research*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a five-engine risk primitive architecture for the Internet of Value (IoV), combining prediction, decentralized verification (Bittensor), sentiment fusion, agentic action constraints, and API-risk scenario generation. Validated via two empirical experiments on Solana.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoV networks face composite marginal risks (route, sentiment, liquidity, policy commitment) rather than single-chain risks, requiring a more holistic risk primitive.

Method: A composition of five engines: (1) prediction engine for price/liquidity/volatility/route health, (2) Bittensor verification subnet for decentralized scoring, (3) sentiment-fusion engine over text/on-chain/grey-literature, (4) constitutional agentic engine with role-bound constraints, (5) API-risk scenario engine for pre-committed action programs via Monte-Carlo. Validated with two experiments: 27-hour liquidity stress test and 168-hour prediction-router calibration on Solana.

Result: The architecture demonstrates deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is formally stated and falsifiable. Two empirical artefacts support the approach.

Conclusion: The proposed five-engine composition is a suitable risk primitive for heterogeneous, partially-trusted IoV networks, with empirical backing from Solana-based experiments.

Abstract: The Internet of Value (IoV) is a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network in which the dominant marginal risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, and the policy a system is willing to commit to) rather than a property of any single chain. We argue that a risk primitive adequate for this regime is a composition of five engines: a prediction engine over price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; a Bittensor verification subnet that decentralises and economically scores prediction outputs; a sentiment-fusion engine over text, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; an agentic engine under constitutional, role-bound action constraints; and an API-risk and scenario engine that converts forecasts into pre-committed action programs in the sense of Monte-Carlo scenario generation. We anchor the architecture in two empirical artefacts: a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc reported with explicit class-imbalance honesty. The case study supports deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is stated formally and is falsifiable.

</details>


### [471] [Null Space Constrained Contrastive Visual Forgetting for MLLM Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05909)
*Yuhang Wang,Zhenxing Niu,Haoxuan Ji,Guangyu He,Linlin Zhang,Haichang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes an MLLM unlearning method that forgets target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual and all textual knowledge, using contrastive visual forgetting and null-space constrained fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The core challenge is balancing target knowledge removal with non-target knowledge retention in Multimodal LLMs, where visual and textual knowledge are tightly intertwined.

Method: (1) Freeze LLM backbone, fine-tune visual module; (2) Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) to separate target vs. retained visual knowledge; (3) Null-space constraint to prevent degradation of retained knowledge; (4) Extends to continual unlearning scenarios.

Result: Extensive experiments show a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention across diverse benchmarks.

Conclusion: The approach successfully handles both static and continual unlearning, achieving state-of-the-art trade-off between forgetting and retention in MLLMs.

Abstract: The core challenge of machine unlearning is to strike a balance between target knowledge removal and non-target knowledge retention. In the context of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), this challenge becomes even more pronounced, as knowledge is further divided into visual and textual modalities that are tightly intertwined. In this paper, we introduce an MLLM unlearning approach that aims to forget target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual knowledge and all textual knowledge. Specifically, we freeze the LLM backbone and achieve unlearning by fine-tuning the visual module. First, we propose a Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) mechanism to separate target visual knowledge from retained visual knowledge, guiding the representations of target visual concepts toward appropriate regions in the feature space. Second, we identify the null space associated with retained knowledge and constrain the unlearning process within this space, thereby significantly mitigating degradation in knowledge retention. Third, beyond static unlearning scenarios, we extend our approach to continual unlearning, where forgetting requests arrive sequentially. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention.

</details>


### [472] [PREFER: Personalized Review Summarization with Online Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05911)
*Millend Roy,Agostino Capponi,Vineet Goyal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an online learning framework that generates personalized product review summaries by iteratively learning user preferences from feedback on generated summaries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing summarization systems produce generic summaries that ignore varying user preferences and their dynamic nature.

Method: An online learning framework that refines user preference understanding by incorporating feedback from generated summaries over time.

Result: Controlled simulations on Amazon Reviews'23 dataset show improved alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.

Conclusion: Online preference learning effectively personalizes summaries to unknown latent user preferences without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Product reviews significantly influence purchasing decisions on e-commerce platforms. However, the sheer volume of reviews can overwhelm users, obscuring the information most relevant to their specific needs. Current e-commerce summarization systems typically produce generic, static summaries that fail to account for the fact that (i) different users care about different product characteristics, and (ii) these preferences may evolve with interactions. To address the challenge of unknown latent preferences, we propose an online learning framework that generates personalized summaries for each user. Our system iteratively refines its understanding of user preferences by incorporating feedback directly from the generated summaries over time. We provide a case study using the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset, showing in controlled simulations that online preference learning improves alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.

</details>


### [473] [Wisteria: A Unified Multi-Scale Feature Learning Framework for DNA Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05913)
*Weihua Wang,Haoji Li,Feilong Bao,Lei Yang,Guanglai Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Wisteria is a DNA language model integrating local motif capture (via gated dilated convolutions) and global dependency modeling (via gated MLPs and Fourier-based attention) within a Mamba-based architecture, achieving strong performance across short and long-range genomic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DNA language models focus on long-range token interactions but neglect the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies in genomic sequences.

Method: Wisteria augments Mamba-based architecture with (1) gated dilated convolutions for local motifs, (2) gated MLPs for global dependencies, and (3) Fourier-based attention for frequency-domain modeling and length generalization.

Result: Wisteria demonstrates strong performance across four experimental settings with both short and long-range dependencies, outperforming competitive DNA language model baselines.

Conclusion: Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi-scale genomic sequence analysis.

Abstract: DNA language model aims to decipher the regulatory grammar and semantic of genomes by capturing long range dependencies in DNA sequences. Existing methods emphasize long range token interactions but often ignore the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies. In this paper, we propose Wisteria, a genomic language model that integrates multi scale feature learning within a unified framework for DNA sequence. Specifically, Wisteria augments the Mamba based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs and regulatory patterns, while gated multilayer perceptrons refine global dependencies. We further introduce a Fourier based attention mechanism to support frequency domain modeling, periodic extension and length generalization. Across four experimental settings with both short and long range dependencies, Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines. These results indicate that Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi scale genomic sequence analysis.

</details>


### [474] [Intentmaking and Sensemaking: Human Interaction with AI-Guided Mathematical Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05921)
*Alex Bäuerle,Adam Connors,Alexander Novikov,Adam Zsolt Wagner,Ngân Vũ,Fernanda Viegas,Martin Wattenberg,Lucas Dixon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A user study with 11 expert mathematicians using an AI coding agent (AlphaEvolve) reveals a new workflow called "intentmaking"—iteratively defining and refining experimental goals through system interaction—which cycles with sensemaking, suggesting AI tools should be designed as collaborative instruments rather than question-answering black boxes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI tools for scientific discovery primarily operate on a question/answer paradigm, but how experts effectively interact with these systems to drive genuine discovery remains poorly understood.

Method: Formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians using AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to tackle advanced problems in their expertise domains.

Result: Identified and characterized "intentmaking"—the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining experimental goals through active system interaction, which cycles with sensemaking during investigations.

Conclusion: AI tools for scientific discovery should be designed as collaborative instruments that support the intentmaking-sensemaking cycle, moving beyond the question/answer model toward treating AI as collaborative partners rather than opaque assistants.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence offers powerful new tools for scientific discovery, but the interaction paradigms required to effectively harness these systems remain underexplored. In this paper, we present findings from a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians who used AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to tackle advanced problems in their fields of expertise. We identify and characterize a distinct workflow we term intentmaking, the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining one's experimental goals through active system interaction. We frame this as a natural extension to sensemaking, the cognitive process of building an understanding of complex or novel data. We suggest that users enter a cycle of intentmaking (defining and updating their experiment) and sensemaking (interpreting the results) which repeats many times during the course of an investigation. Our documentation of these themes suggests an approach to designing AI tools for scientific discovery that goes beyond the existing question/answer model of many current systems, treating them as collaborative instruments rather than opaque black-box assistants.

</details>


### [475] [Which Are the Low-Resource Languages of the Semantic Web?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05929)
*Ndeye-Emilie Mbengue,Pierre Monnin,Miguel Couceiro,Fabien Gandon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a methodology to quantitatively define low-, medium-, and high-resource languages in Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs, using DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata for categorization to aid cross-lingual transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Digital technologies are widening the Open Access Data gap between high- and low-resource languages, excluding many communities. A clear quantitative definition of low-resource languages in LOD KGs is missing, which is needed for effective cross-lingual transfer.

Method: The authors analyze language distribution across LOD KGs and propose a multi-level categorization based on DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata to formally define low-, medium-, and high-resource languages.

Result: A preliminary multi-level language categorization for LOD KGs is presented, providing a formal definition of resource levels that can later be used to select candidates for cross-lingual transfer.

Conclusion: The proposed categorization offers a foundation for quantitatively defining language resource levels in LOD KGs, which could help mitigate the digital divide by enabling better cross-lingual transfer strategies.

Abstract: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from the global digital transformation. Multilingual Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs (LOD KGs) could contribute to mitigating this divide through cross-lingual transfer; however, no clear quantitative definition of low-resource languages has yet been established in the context of LOD KGs. In this poster, we present a methodology to analyze the distribution of languages across LOD KGs and propose a preliminary multi-level categorization based on DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata. This categorization is leveraged to bring a formal definition of low-, high-, and medium-resource languages that could be later leveraged to select cross-lingual transfer candidates.

</details>


### [476] [In Data or Invisible: Toward a Better Digital Representation of Low-Resource Languages with Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05931)
*Ndeye-Emilie Mbengue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A PhD proposal addressing the language divide in Linked Open Data knowledge graphs by analyzing language distribution, studying cross-lingual transfer strategies, and exploring analogical reasoning to improve multilingual KG completion and enhance low-resource language coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Digital technologies are worsening the Open Access Data divide between high- and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from global digital transformation.

Method: 1) Identify and analyze key variables (Wikipedia article counts, language-tagged entities) across DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata. 2) Study cross-lingual transfer candidate selection based on linguistic proximity and curated alignments. 3) Explore analogical reasoning using (dis)similarities to identify cross-lingual correspondences.

Result: Not yet available (PhD proposal stage); expected outcomes include improved language coverage in LOD KGs and enhanced multilingual KG completion performance.

Conclusion: The proposed research aims to reduce the language gap in LOD by combining language distribution analysis, cross-lingual transfer learning, and analogical reasoning techniques.

Abstract: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from participating in the global digital transformation. In this PhD proposal, we aim to address this gap, focusing on the language coverage of Linked Open Data knowledge graphs (LOD KGs). First, we identify key variables that characterize language distribution in LOD, including the number of Wikipedia articles per language edition and the number of language-tagged entities in LOD KGs. These variables are analyzed across three major multilingual LOD KGs, DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata, providing insights into the representation and distribution of languages within LOD. Building on this analysis, we intend to study the impact of cross-lingual transfer candidate selection on the task of multilingual KG completion. In particular, we plan to investigate strategies based on linguistic proximity and the availability of curated annotated alignments between languages. Language proximity also motivates us to explore the benefits of analogical reasoning that relies on (dis)similarities and has not yet been investigated to identify correspondences across languages to improve KG completion performance and enhance language coverage in LOD.

</details>


### [477] [ICU-Bench:Benchmarking Continual Unlearning in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05938)
*Yuhang Wang,Wenjie Mei,Junkai Zhang,Guangyu He,Zhenxing Niu,Haichang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ICU-Bench is a continual multimodal unlearning benchmark for privacy-critical documents (medical reports and labor contracts), containing 1,000 profiles, 9,500 images, 16,000 QA pairs, and 100 forget tasks. Existing unlearning methods struggle in continual settings, highlighting the need for new approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on static or short-sequence settings, lacking support for evaluating continual privacy deletion requests in realistic MLLM deployments.

Method: Constructed a benchmark with 1,000 privacy-sensitive profiles from two domains (medical reports, labor contracts) with 9,500 images, 16,000 QA pairs, and 100 forget tasks. Introduces new continual unlearning metrics for comprehensive evaluation.

Result: Existing unlearning methods generally struggle in continual settings, showing clear limitations in balancing forgetting quality, utility preservation, and scalability over long task sequences.

Conclusion: There is a critical need for multimodal unlearning methods explicitly designed for continual privacy deletion.

Abstract: Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress across many domains, their training on large-scale multimodal datasets raises serious privacy concerns, making effective machine unlearning increasingly necessary. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on static or short-sequence settings, offering limited support for evaluating continual privacy deletion requests in realistic deployments. To bridge this gap, we introduce ICU-Bench, a continual multimodal unlearning benchmark built on privacy-critical document data. ICU-Bench contains 1,000 privacy-sensitive profiles from two document domains, medical reports and labor contracts, with 9,500 images, 16,000 question-answer pairs, and 100 forget tasks. Additionally, new continual unlearning metrics are introduced, facilitating a comprehensive analysis of forgetting effectiveness, historical forgetting preservation, retained utility, and stability throughout the continual unlearning process. Through extensive experiments with representative unlearning methods on ICU-Bench, we show that existing methods generally struggle in continual settings and exhibit clear limitations in balancing forgetting quality, utility preservation, and scalability over long task sequences. These findings highlight the need for multimodal unlearning methods explicitly designed for continual privacy deletion.

</details>


### [478] [MAS-Algorithm: A Workflow for Solving Algorithmic Programming Problems with a Multi-Agent System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05949)
*Yuliang Xu,Xiang Xu,Yao Wan,Hu Wei,Tong Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAS-Algorithm is a multi-agent workflow for algorithmic problem solving that decomposes the process into modular stages, achieving 6.48% improvement in acceptance rate across Qwen models, significantly outperforming fine-tuning (0.89%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches to algorithmic problem solving in AI are either model-centric (costly, limited interpretability) or fragmented (lack unified framework). The paper aims to provide a systematic, modular, and interpretable multi-agent framework inspired by human competitive programmers.

Method: A multi-agent workflow (MAS-Algorithm) that decomposes the algorithmic solving process into modular stages, enabling structured reasoning, tool integration, and flexible coordination among specialized agents.

Result: 6.48% average acceptance rate gain on Qwen models (vs 0.89% for fine-tuning), 4.72% gain on LiveCodeBench-Pro, and up to 27.7% improvement from individual agents in ablation studies.

Conclusion: MAS-Algorithm demonstrates strong potential for advancing AI-driven algorithmic reasoning through a systematic, modular multi-agent approach, with significant and consistent improvements over baseline methods.

Abstract: Algorithmic problem solving serves as a rigorous testbed for evaluating structured reasoning in AI coding systems, as it directly reflects a model's ability to perform structured reasoning in complex scenarios.Existing approaches predominantly rely on model-centric strategies, such as architectural modifications and data scaling, which are costly and offer limited interpretability. Alternative methods leveraging external tools or prompting techniques (e.g., chain-of-thought) are often fragmented and lack a unified framework. In this paper, we propose MAS-Algorithm, a systematic multi-agent workflow for algorithmic problem solving inspired by the practices of competitive programmers and algorithm engineers. Our framework decomposes the end-to-end solving process into modular stages, enabling structured reasoning, tool integration, and flexible coordination among agents. The design emphasizes both rigor and extensibility, allowing it to generalize across diverse problem types.Experimental results on a self-constructed benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements across multiple Qwen series models, achieving an average gain of 6.48% in acceptance rate. In contrast, parameter-efficient fine-tuning on the same data yields only a marginal improvement of 0.89%. We further observe a 4.72% gain on LiveCodeBench-Pro, along with consistent improvements across additional accuracy and efficiency metrics.Beyond performance gains, we conduct comprehensive analyses to better understand the reasoning process within the workflow, including error patterns and cross-scenario behaviors. We further perform customized replacement and ablation studies to explore the upper bound of the framework, showing that individual agents can contribute improvements of up to 27.7%. These results highlight the strong potential of MAS-Algorithm for advancing AI-driven algorithmic reasoning.

</details>


### [479] [HaM-World: Soft-Hamiltonian World Models with Selective Memory for Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05951)
*Haoyun Tang,Haodong Cui,Keyao Xu,Kun Wang,Zhandong Mei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HaM-World (HMW) is a structured world model that decomposes latent states into a canonical (q,p) subspace and context subspace c, using Mamba state-space memory and Hamiltonian dynamics to improve long-horizon planning stability and OOD generalization in model-based RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World models suffer from unstable imagined rollouts as planning horizon grows or dynamics distribution shifts. The authors argue this instability stems from missing history-conditioned memory for Markov completeness and missing geometric organization separating configuration, momentum, and task semantics.

Method: Decomposes latent state into (q,p) canonical subspace evolving via energy-derived Hamiltonian vector field plus learnable residuals, and context subspace c for semantic/dissipative factors. Uses Mamba selective state-space memory as history-conditioned input. Single latent state shared by all planning components.

Result: Best Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%), reduces long-horizon rollout error to 45% of baseline, wins 11/12 MSE cells. Achieves highest OOD return under all 12 perturbations, with gains of 10.2% (Finger Spin) and 13.6% (Reacher Easy). Diagnostics confirm bounded energy drift and structured energy transfer.

Conclusion: Incorporating Hamiltonian structure and selective memory into world model latents significantly improves planning stability and robustness, validating the Soft-Hamiltonian dynamics design.

Abstract: World models enable model-based planning through learned latent dynamics, but imagined rollouts become unstable as the planning horizon grows or the dynamics distribution shifts. We argue that this instability reflects two missing structures in planner-facing latents: history-conditioned memory for approximate Markov completeness, and geometric organization that separates configuration, momentum, and task semantics. We propose HaM-World (HMW), a structured world model that decomposes the latent state into a canonical (q, p) subspace and a context subspace c, while using Mamba selective state-space memory as the history-conditioned input to the same latent dynamics. Within this interface, (q, p) evolves through an energy-derived Hamiltonian vector field plus learnable residual/control dynamics, while c captures semantic, dissipative, and non-conservative factors. This gives the planner a single latent state shared by dynamics prediction, reward/value estimation, imagined rollouts, and CEM action search. On four DeepMind Control Suite tasks, HaM-World reaches the highest Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%), reduces long-horizon rollout error to 45% of a strong baseline model, and wins 11/12 k in {3,5,7} MSE cells. Under 12 OOD perturbations spanning dynamics shifts, action delay, and observation masking, HaM-World achieves the highest return in every condition, with average OOD-return gains of 10.2% on Finger Spin and 13.6% on Reacher Easy. Mechanism diagnostics further show bounded action-free Hamiltonian-energy drift, structured energy variation under policy rollouts, and coherent control-induced energy transfer, supporting the intended Soft-Hamiltonian dynamics design.

</details>


### [480] [Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust Learning for Debiased Knowledge Tracing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05958)
*Peilin Zhan,Wei Chen,Weilin Chen,Shuyi Pan,Ruichu Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes TSDR, a doubly robust framework with temporal smoothness regularization to address selection bias in Knowledge Tracing, achieving unbiased estimates and improved performance across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KT methods neglect selection bias caused by non-random exercise recommendations and student choices, leading to biased mastery estimates and poor recommendations.

Method: TSDR combines a doubly robust formulation (propensity model + error imputation model) with a temporal smoothness regularizer to reduce variance while maintaining unbiasedness guarantees.

Result: TSDR consistently improves various state-of-the-art KT backbones on multiple real-world benchmarks.

Conclusion: Principled bias correction is vital in KT, and the proposed TSDR effectively addresses selection bias with theoretical guarantees and empirical gains.

Abstract: Knowledge Tracing (KT) is fundamental to intelligent education systems, yet relies on educational logs that are selectively observed. The non-random nature of exercise recommendations and student choices inevitably induces severe selection bias. Most existing KT methods neglect this issue, training on observed logs using standard empirical risk, which yields biased mastery estimates and accumulates errors in subsequent recommendations. To address this, we introduce a doubly robust (DR) formulation for KT that integrates a propensity model with an error imputation model, theoretically guaranteeing unbiasedness if either model is accurate. Beyond unbiasedness, in the sequential setting of KT, we identify that the estimator's performance is compromised by variance-dependent stochastic deviations that accumulate over time, thereby causing training instability and limiting performance. To mitigate this, we derive a generalization bound that explicitly characterizes the impact of estimator variance and identifies temporal smoothness as a key factor in controlling it. Building on these theoretical insights, we propose the Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) framework. TSDR jointly optimizes the KT predictor and the imputation model with a smoothness regularizer, effectively reducing variance while preserving the unbiasedness guarantee of DR. Experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that TSDR consistently enhances various state-of-the-art KT backbones, underscoring the vital role of principled bias correction in KT.

</details>


### [481] [From Coordinate Matching to Structural Alignment: Rethinking Prototype Alignment in Heterogeneous Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05959)
*Xinghao Wu,Jianwei Niu,Guogang Zhu,Xuefeng Liu,Shaojie Tang,Jiayuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FedSAF shifts prototype alignment in heterogeneous federated learning from coordinate-based (forcing shared feature basis) to structural alignment (preserving inter-class relationships), improving performance by up to 3.52% over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prototype-based HtFL methods use MSE/cosine-based coordinate alignment that forces all clients into a global feature subspace. This is problematic under model heterogeneity because heterogeneous feature extractors have client-specific subspaces, and enforcing a shared basis unnecessarily suppresses learning capacity.

Method: FedSAF decouples inter-class semantic structure alignment from shared feature basis enforcement by shifting from absolute coordinate alignment to relational structure alignment between client prototypes and global prototypes.

Result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks show FedSAF outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52%.

Conclusion: Structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous federated learning settings, as it preserves beneficial semantic structure without imposing unnecessary shared feature subspaces.

Abstract: Heterogeneous federated learning (HtFL) aims to enable collaboration among clients that differ in both data distributions and model architectures. Prototype-based methods, which communicate class-level feature centers (prototypes) instead of full model parameters, have recently shown strong potential for HtFL. Existing prototype-based HtFL methods typically reuse the MSE-based or cosine-based alignment mechanism developed for homogeneous FL when aligning client-specific representations with global prototypes. These approaches are essentially coordinate alignment, where representations of clients are forced to match the global prototypes in the embedding space in an element-wise manner. Such alignment implicitly assumes that all clients should map their representations into the feature subspace defined by the global prototypes. This assumption is reasonable in homogeneous FL, where all clients share the same feature extractor. However, it becomes problematic in HtFL, since heterogeneous feature extractors naturally induce client-specific feature subspaces, and forcing all clients to optimize within a single global subspace unnecessarily suppresses their learning capacity. We observe that coordinate alignment implicitly couples two distinct objectives: aligning inter-class semantic structure, which is directly beneficial for classification, and enforcing a shared feature basis, which is unnecessary and even harmful under model heterogeneity. Building on this insight, we design FedSAF, which shifts the alignment objective from absolute coordinates to inter-class relational structure. We demonstrate that structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous settings. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our structural alignment outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52\%.

</details>


### [482] [TheraAgent: Self-Improving Therapeutic Agent for Precise and Comprehensive Treatment Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05963)
*Junkai Li,Yunghwei Lai,Tianyi Zhu,Zheng Long Lee,Weizhi Ma,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TheraAgent replaces one-shot LLM treatment planning with an iterative generate-judge-refine pipeline, achieving state-of-the-art results on HealthBench and 86% win rate against physicians in expert evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLMs produce one-shot treatment plans that are rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe, unlike the iterative refinement process used by human experts.

Method: An agentic framework (TheraAgent) with a generate-judge-refine pipeline, incorporating TheraJudge—a treatment-specific evaluation module for enforcing clinical standards during inference.

Result: State-of-the-art on HealthBench (Accuracy & Completeness); 86% win rate against physicians in expert evaluation; superior Targeting and Harm Control; high agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench.

Conclusion: Iterative generate-judge-refine framework significantly improves treatment plan quality and safety over one-shot generation, with reliable automated evaluation.

Abstract: Formulating a treatment plan is inherently a complex reasoning and refinement task rather than a simple generation problem. However, existing large language models (LLMs) mainly rely on one-shot output without explicit verification, which may result in rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe treatment plans. To address these limitations, we propose TheraAgent, an agentic framework that replaces one-shot generation with an iterative generate-judge-refine pipeline.
