By mirroring the actual reasoning process of human experts who iteratively revise treatment plans, our framework progressively transforms coarse and incomplete drafts into precise, comprehensive, and safer therapeutic regimens. To facilitate the critical judge component, we introduce TheraJudge, a treatment-specific evaluation module integrated into the inference loop to enforce clinical standards. Experiments show TheraAgent achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench, leading in Accuracy and Completeness. In expert evaluations, it attains an 86% win rate against physicians, with superior Targeting and Harm Control. Moreover, the highly agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations confirms the reliability of our framework.

</details>


### [483] [BehaviorGuard: Online Backdoor Defense for Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05977)
*Yinbo Yu,Xueyu Yin,Jiadai Wang,Chunwei Tian,Sai Xu,Qi Zhu,Daoqiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BehaviorGuard is a trigger-agnostic online defense framework that detects and mitigates backdoor attacks in DRL by identifying abnormal shifts in action distributions, outperforming prior methods across both single- and multi-agent settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor defenses rely on trigger reverse-engineering (vulnerable to complex triggers) and costly fine-tuning, limiting practical deployment. The authors seek a more robust, efficient, and trigger-agnostic defense approach.

Method: BehaviorGuard detects backdoor actions online by analyzing shifts in action distributions—specifically, consistent drift in high-quantile regions and distribution tails—without needing to identify triggers. It then suppresses these suspicious actions at runtime.

Result: Across diverse benchmarks with various backdoor attacks, BehaviorGuard consistently outperforms prior methods in both detection/mitigation efficacy and computational efficiency, and is the first online defense to work in both single- and multi-agent DRL settings.

Conclusion: Shifting focus from trigger-specific patterns to trigger-agnostic behavioral anomalies enables more robust, efficient, and practical online backdoor defense for DRL, with strong generalization across attack types and agent settings.

Abstract: Backdoor attacks pose a serious threat to deep reinforcement learning (DRL). Current defenses typically rely on reward anomalies to reverse-engineer triggers and model finetuning to remove backdoors. However, complex trigger patterns undermine their robustness, and fine-tuning entails high costs, limiting practical utility. Therefore, we shift defense concerns to trigger-agnostic backdoor output behaviors and propose BehaviorGuard, an online behavior-based backdoor detection and mitigation framework for DRL. Specifically, we find that regardless of attacks, backdoored policies induce consistent shifts in action distributions to ensure reliable activation, leaving detectable traces in high-quantile regions and distribution tails, even in the absence of triggers. Based on this, we design a novel metric that captures behavioral drift in action distributions to identify and suppress backdoor actions at runtime. To our knowledge, this is the first online backdoor defense that counters attacks both in single- and multi-agent DRL. Evaluated across diverse benchmarks with different backdoor attacks, BehaviorGuard consistently surpasses prior methods in both efficacy and efficiency.

</details>


### [484] [TACT: Mitigating Overthinking and Overacting in Coding Agents via Activation Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05980)
*Yuan Sui,Yulin Chen,Yibo Li,Xue Jiang,Yufei He,Yihong Dong,Xiaoxin He,Tianyu Gao,Bryan Hooi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TACT detects and mitigates "agent drift" (overthinking/overacting) in LLM agents by steering residual stream activations toward calibrated behavior, improving task resolution by ~5 pp and cutting steps by ~26%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language model agents degrade over long trajectories due to "agent drift"—specifically overthinking (repeated reasoning without new info) and overacting (issuing tool calls without integrating observations). Existing methods lack fine-grained detection and intervention at the representation level.

Method: TACT identifies two drift axes in the residual stream via linear separation of overthinking, overacting, and calibrated states (AUC~0.9). At test time, it projects each step's activation onto these axes and steers drifted activations back toward the calibrated region.

Result: On SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval, TACT outperforms unsteered baselines: +5.8 pp resolve rate on Qwen3.5-27B, +4.8 pp on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it, and reduces steps-to-resolve by up to 26%.

Conclusion: Agent drift is a steerable direction in the residual stream, and TACT provides a viable mechanism for building more reliable long-horizon language model agents.

Abstract: When language model agents tackle complex software engineering tasks, they often degrade over long trajectories, which we define as *agent drift*. We focus on two recurring failure modes *overthinking* and *overacting*, i.e., where the agent repeatedly reasons over information it already has, and where it issues tool calls without integrating recent observations or acquiring new evidence. In this paper, we introduce TACT (Think-Act Calibration via activation Steering), to detect and mitigate agent drift in the residual stream before it surfaces as a behavioral failure. In specific, we label trajectory steps as overthinking, overacting, or calibrated, and find that their hidden states can separate linearly along two *drift axes*, pointing from calibrated behavior toward each failure mode (AUC $\approx$ 0.9). To mitigate agent drift, we project each step's activation onto these axes at test time and pull drifted ones back toward the calibrated region. Experiments show that TACT outperforms unsteered baselines across SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval, lifting average resolve rate by $+5.8$ pp on Qwen3.5-27B and $+4.8$ pp on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it while cutting steps-to-resolve by up to $26\%$. These gains frame agent drift as a steerable direction in the residual stream, and position TACT as a viable handle for reliable long-horizon agents.

</details>


### [485] [BioResearcher: Scenario-Guided Multi-Agent for Translational Medicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05985)
*Remigiusz Kinas,Joanna Krawczyk,Rafał Powalski,Przemysław Pietrzak,Agnieszka Kowalewska,Krzysztof Kolmus,Maciej Sypetkowski,Łukasz Smoliński,Tomasz Jetka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ingenix BioResearcher is a scenario-guided multi-agent system for translational medicine that outperforms baselines across unit-level, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical benchmarks by using versioned playbooks, specialized subagents over 30+ tools, and claim-level multi-model reconciliation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Translational medicine requires evidence synthesis combining literature, trials, patents, and multi-omics data with provenance and uncertainty — tasks that general-purpose foundation models and tool-augmented systems cannot handle due to single-shot or open-ended outputs lacking auditability.

Method: A scenario-guided multi-agent system that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, delegates to specialized subagents over 30+ tools and ML endpoints, mixes structured DB access with sandboxed code for genome-scale analysis, and applies claim-level multi-model reconciliation before editorial assembly.

Result: Leads on 109 single-step tests (83.49% pass rate; 0.892 avg score), 89.33% on BixBench-Verified-50, top 0.758 mean score on BaisBench, and 74.7% positive hit rate / 96.8% negative clear rate on a 30-query clinical benchmark.

Conclusion: The system demonstrates broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical evaluations, showing that specialized multi-agent design with provenance tracking is effective for translational medicine.

Abstract: Translational medicine turns underspecified development goals into evidence synthesis that must combine literature, trials, patents, and quantitative multi-omics analysis while preserving identifiers, uncertainty, and retrievable provenance. General-purpose foundation models and off-the-shelf tool-augmented or multi-agent systems are not built for this: they tend to produce single-shot answers or run open-endedly, and fall short on the auditable, scenario-specific workflows that heterogeneous biomedical sources demand.
  This paper introduces Ingenix BioResearcher, a scenario-guided multi-agent system that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, delegates to specialized subagents over 30+ tools and machine-learning endpoints, mixes structured database access with sandboxed code for genome-scale analyses, and applies claim-level multi-model reconciliation before editorial assembly.
  We evaluate BioResearcher across unit-level capabilities, open-ended biomedical reasoning, and end-to-end clinical discovery. It leads evaluated baselines on 109 single-step tests (83.49% pass rate; 0.892 average score), achieves strong biomedical benchmark performance (89.33% on BixBench-Verified-50 and the top 0.758 mean score on BaisBench Scientific Discovery), and leads on a 30-query clinical end-to-end benchmark with the highest positive hit rate (74.7% $\pm$ 3.3%) and negative clear rate (96.8% $\pm$ 0.2%). These results show broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical evaluations.

</details>


### [486] [Strat-LLM: Stratified Strategy Alignment for LLM-based Stock Trading with Real-time Multi-Source Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06024)
*Wenliang Huang,Zengyi Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Strat-LLM is a framework for evaluating LLMs as trading agents, testing their alignment strategies (Free, Guided, Strict Modes) on live 2025 market data. Key findings: reasoning-heavy models excel in Free Mode; strict constraints help standard models but hurt ultra-large ones; mid-scale models (35B) perform best under strict rules; standard LLMs suffer a "high win-rate trap" optimizing small gains over total returns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM-based trading agents overlook the interplay between architectural reasoning and strategy consistency, and fail to eliminate look-ahead bias.

Method: Strat-LLM uses Stratified Strategy Alignment with three modes (Free, Guided, Strict) tested in a live-forward 2025 setting on A-share and U.S. markets, integrating sequential prices, real-time news, and annual reports.

Result: (1) Reasoning-heavy models peak in Free Mode; standard models need Strict Mode. (2) Alignment utility is regime-dependent. (3) Mid-scale models (35B) show optimal fidelity under strict constraints; ultra-large models (122B) suffer alignment tax under rigid rules but excel in Guided Mode. (4) Standard LLMs exhibit a "high win-rate trap."

Conclusion: LLM trading performance depends critically on aligning model scale, reasoning capability, and strategy constraints to market regimes, with deep reasoning or strict guardrails needed to avoid suboptimal trading behaviors.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are evolving into autonomous trading agents, yet existing benchmarks often overlook the interplay between architectural reasoning and strategy consistency. We propose Strat-LLM, a framework grounded in Stratified Strategy Alignment. Operating in a live-forward setting throughout 2025, it integrates heterogeneous data including sequential prices, real-time news, and annual reports to eliminate look-ahead bias. Extensive stress tests on A-share and U.S. markets reveal: (1) reasoning-heavy models achieve peak utility in Free Mode via internal logic, whereas standard models require Strict Mode as a vital risk anchor; (2) alignment utility is regime-dependent, with Free and Guided modes capturing momentum in uptrending markets, while Strict Mode mitigates drawdowns in downtrends; (3) mid-scale models (35B) show optimal fidelity under strict constraints, whereas ultra-large models (122B) suffer an alignment tax under rigid rules but gain a performance premium in Guided Mode; (4) standard LLMs often fall into a high win-rate trap, optimizing for small gains at the expense of total returns, which can only be mitigated through deep reasoning or strict external guardrails. Project details are available at https://Strat-LLM.github.io.

</details>


### [487] [Pathways to AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06029)
*Gordon Fletcher,Saomai Vu Khan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper critically examines five questions about AI development pathways, particularly around Generative AI and AGI, arguing that current trajectories are not inevitable but shaped by specific socio-political-economic conditions. It explores leverage points, dead ends, and alternative models, ultimately offering recommendations for more transparent, moderated, and sustainable AI development paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge assumptions about the inevitability of current AI trajectories by critically analyzing how commercial AI development is tightly linked to prevailing social, political, and economic circumstances, and to explore alternative pathways that were marginalized.

Method: A critical software studies perspective posing five structured research questions examining: (1) critical pathways to current generative AI dominance, (2) leverage nodes and dead ends, (3) differences across proprietary, open-weight, and domain-specific model trajectories, (4) alternative projects branching from key leverage points, and (5) plausible socio-technical programs toward AGI-adjacent capabilities with transparency and sustainability.

Result: The paper identifies specific leverage points where small changes had large downstream effects, dead ends representing lost alternative possibilities, and differing trajectories across proprietary, open-weight, and sovereign model development paths. It also catalogs alternative projects that succeeded, stalled, failed, or were absorbed.

Conclusion: AGI is conceptually and definitionally problematic, and conditioning views on this leads to specific conclusions about the future. The paper offers final recommendations for socio-technical development programs that could move toward AGI-adjacent capabilities while meeting requirements for transparency, moderation, wellbeing, and sustainable business models.

Abstract: Our focus are five related questions that stem from a critical software studies perspective. Underpinning this view is the acknowledged need to avoid assumptions regarding the inevitability of the current situation relating to AI. What we need to see is the closeness of the linkage between current commercial AI development and our prevailing social, political and economic circumstances. This does mean that the perspectives presented here are done so critically and conditionally. Most importantly, Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is seen as being problematic both conceptually and definitionally. This conditioning of any view regarding AGI does lead the discussion in specific directions and to certain conclusions regarding the future. However, adopting this perspective enables the work to offer some final recommendations. We set out to ask the following questions, 1. What are the critical pathways that produced the current dominant generative AI tools (capabilities, product forms, adoption patterns)? 2. Which decision points acted as leverage nodes (small changes that had large downstream effects), and which dead ends reveal alternative possibilities that did not become dominant? 3. How do pathways differ across three foundational-model trajectories such as the frontier proprietary models, open-weight models or specific domain and sovereign models? 4. Which alternative projects branched from key leverage nodes, what is their current state, and why did some succeed, stall, fail or become absorbed? 5. Based on this analysis, what socio-technical development programmes could plausibly move toward AGI-adjacent capability while meeting requirements for transparency, moderation, wellbeing and sustainable business models?

</details>


### [488] [Novelty-based Tree-of-Thought Search for LLM Reasoning and Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06040)
*Leon Hamm,Zlatan Ajanovic*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes using a novelty metric to prune branches in tree-of-thought reasoning, reducing token costs while maintaining performance on planning and reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs still struggle with reasoning and planning tasks despite improvements like chain-of-thought and tree-of-thought; they remain brittle, lack human-level performance, and incur high time/token costs.

Method: A measurable concept of novelty is introduced to describe the uniqueness of a new thought node compared to previously seen nodes in the search tree. Novelty is estimated by prompting an LLM using its pre-trained knowledge, and this metric is used to prune branches and reduce search scope.

Result: Although the method introduces more prompts per state, the overall token cost is reduced by pruning and reducing overall tree size. The approach was tested on several benchmarks in language-based planning and general reasoning.

Conclusion: Novelty-based pruning inspired by width-based search planning can effectively transfer to language domains, improving tree-of-thought reasoning efficiency by reducing token costs while maintaining performance.

Abstract: Although advances such as chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought or reinforcement learning have improved the performance of LLMs in reasoning and planning tasks, they are still brittle and have not achieved human-level performance in many domains, and often suffer from high time and token costs. Inspired by the success of width-based search in planning, we explore how the concept of novelty can be transferred to language domains and how it can improve tree-of-thought reasoning. A tree of thoughts relies on building possible "paths" of consecutive ideas or thoughts. These are generated by repeatedly prompting an LLM. In our paper, a measurable concept of novelty is proposed that describes the uniqueness of a new node (thought) in comparison to nodes previously seen in the search tree. Novelty is estimated by prompting an LLM and making use of embedded general knowledge from pre-training. This metric can then be used to prune branches and reduce the scope of the search. Although this method introduces more prompts per state, the overall token cost can be reduced by pruning and reducing the overall tree size. This procedure is tested and compared using several benchmarks in language-based planning and general reasoning.

</details>


### [489] [Visual Fingerprints for LLM Generation Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06054)
*Amal Alnouri,Andreas Hinterreiter,Christina Humer,Furui Cheng,Marc Streit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A method to visually compare LLM outputs across different generation conditions by extracting linguistic choices and representing them as distribution-level "visual fingerprints" for easier comparison.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM outputs vary based on generation conditions (prompts, system instructions, model parameters), but understanding these effects is challenging due to the stochastic and open-ended nature of text generation.

Method: Model LLM responses as collections of linguistic choices (content, expression, structure), extract them via NLP pipelines, represent distributions across repeated samples, and visualize as "visual fingerprints."

Result: Visual fingerprints reveal consistent patterns in LLM behavior across generation conditions that are difficult to observe through individual responses or aggregate metrics.

Conclusion: Distribution-level visual comparison of linguistic choices is an effective approach for understanding how generation conditions shape LLM outputs, aiding tasks like prompt design and model evaluation.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) outputs arise from complex interactions among prompts, system instructions, model parameters, and architecture. We refer to specific configurations of these factors as generation conditions, each of which can bias outputs in various ways. Understanding how different generation conditions shape model behaviors is essential for tasks such as prompt design and model evaluation, yet it remains challenging due to the stochastic and open-ended nature of text generation. We present an approach to visually compare LLM outputs across generation conditions by modeling responses as collections of linguistic choices, including content, expression, and structure. We extract these choices using natural language processing pipelines and represent their distributions across repeated samples. We then visualize these distributions as visual fingerprints, enabling direct, distribution-level comparison of condition-specific tendencies. Through four usage scenarios, we demonstrate how visual fingerprints reveal consistent patterns in LLM behavior that are difficult to observe through individual responses or aggregate metrics.

</details>


### [490] [VibeServe: Can AI Agents Build Bespoke LLM Serving Systems?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06068)
*Keisuke Kamahori,Shihang Li,Simon Peter,Baris Kasikci*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VibeServe is an agentic system that automatically generates bespoke LLM serving stacks for specific usage scenarios, rather than using a single general-purpose stack. It remains competitive with vLLM in standard settings and outperforms it in non-standard scenarios by exploiting specialization opportunities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional LLM serving systems use a single general-purpose stack optimized over many engineer-years. The authors propose the opposite approach: automatically generating customized serving systems for different usage scenarios, arguing that generation-time specialization can outperform runtime generality.

Method: VibeServe uses a multi-agent loop with two components: an outer loop that plans and tracks search over system designs, and an inner loop that implements candidates, checks correctness, and measures performance on target benchmarks.

Result: In standard deployment, VibeServe is competitive with vLLM. In non-standard scenarios (non-standard model architectures, workload knowledge, hardware-specific optimizations), it outperforms existing systems by exploiting opportunities generic systems miss.

Conclusion: The paper suggests a different design point for infrastructure software: favoring generation-time specialization over runtime generality for LLM serving systems.

Abstract: For years, we have built LLM serving systems like any other critical infrastructure: a single general-purpose stack, hand-tuned over many engineer-years, meant to support every model and workload. In this paper, we take the opposite bet: a multi-agent loop that automatically synthesizes bespoke serving systems for different usage scenarios. We propose VibeServe, the first agentic loop that generates entire LLM serving stacks end-to-end. VibeServe uses an outer loop to plan and track the search over system designs, and an inner loop to implement candidates, check correctness, and measure performance on the target benchmark. In the standard deployment setting, where existing stacks are highly optimized, VibeServe remains competitive with vLLM, showing that generation-time specialization need not come at the cost of performance. More interestingly, in non-standard scenarios, VibeServe outperforms existing systems by exploiting opportunities that generic systems miss in six scenarios involving non-standard model architectures, workload knowledge, and hardware-specific optimizations. Together, these results suggest a different point in the design space for infrastructure software: generation-time specialization rather than runtime generality. Code is available at https://github.com/uw-syfi/vibe-serve.

</details>


### [491] [Safety Certification is Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06087)
*Oliver Schön,Licio Romao,Sadegh Soudjani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a kernel embedding framework for safety certification that directly estimates T-step safety probabilities without recursion, avoiding compounding errors that plague DP-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DP-based safety certification methods suffer from compounding errors that lead to vacuous lower bounds for growing horizons T. Non-Markovian dynamics further challenge these recursive approaches.

Method: Formulates safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data using kernel embeddings, directly estimating T-step safety probability without recursion. Subsumes existing approaches like barrier certificates and robust Markov models.

Result: Direct estimators remain stable independent of certification horizon and work in non-Markovian settings, while DP-based certificates go unsound. Validated on a neural-controlled quadrotor simulation.

Conclusion: The kernel embedding framework bypasses compounding errors across horizons, enables certification for non-Markovian dynamics, and generalizes existing safety certification methods.

Abstract: The goal of this paper is certifying safety of dynamical systems subject to uncertainty. Existing approaches use trajectory data to estimate transition probabilities, and compute safety probabilities recursively via dynamic programming (DP). This recursion may lead to compounding errors in the certified safety probability, thus collapsing to a vacuous lower bound for growing horizons $T$. We propose a kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data, directly estimating the $T$-step safety probability without recursion. We show that the framework subsumes well-established approaches from the literature (e.g., barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases, and allows us to go beyond their limitations. As the main consequence, it bypasses compounding error across the horizon and enables certification for systems with non-Markovian dynamics. We demonstrate that direct estimators remain stable independent of the certification horizon and in the non-Markovian setting, whilst DP-based certificates silently go unsound -- confirmed in simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.

</details>


### [492] [Shallow Prefill, Deep Decoding: Efficient Long-Context Inference via Layer-Asymmetric KV Visibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06105)
*Jungsuk Oh,Hyeseo Jeon,Hyunjune Ji,Kyongmin Kong,Jay-Yoon Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPEED introduces a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy that restricts non-anchor prompt tokens to lower layers only, while keeping decode-phase tokens full-depth, achieving comparable benchmarks with significant efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference in decoder-only LMs is expensive due to full-depth KV caching of long prompts during both Prefill and Decode phases.

Method: Phase-asymmetric KV-visibility: non-anchor prompt tokens' KV states materialize only in lower layers (75% of layers), while decode tokens retain full-depth visibility.

Result: 51.2 vs 51.4 OLMES score (vs full-depth baseline); 33% TTFT improvement; 22% TPOT improvement; 25% reduction in active KV memory at 128K context.

Conclusion: Long-context prompt tokens do not need full-depth KV-cache persistence during decode when decode-phase tokens remain full-depth, enabling significant efficiency gains with minimal quality loss.

Abstract: Long-context inference in decoder-only language models is costly because long prompts are processed during Prefill, cached at every layer, and repeatedly attended to during autoregressive Decode. We introduce \emph{Shallow Prefill, dEEp Decode} (SPEED), a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy that materializes non-anchor prompt-token KV states only in lower layers while keeping Decode-phase tokens full-depth. Unlike previous approaches that make upper-layer prompt KV states cheaper to store or construct, SPEED removes prefill tokens from the upper-layer Decode visibility set altogether. With a minimal BoS anchor, this simple change preserves broad benchmark quality while reducing long-context cost. In a controlled Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning study, SPEED using only 75\% of layers for prefill tokens reaches 51.2 average score on OLMES-style benchmarks, compared with 51.4 for the full-depth baseline, while improving TTFT by 33\%, TPOT by 22\%, and reducing active KV memory by 25.0\% at 128K context. Layer-wise diagnostics suggest that this cutoff retains the main prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions of the full-depth model. These results show that long-context prompt tokens need not always persist as full-depth KV-cache objects when Decode-phase tokens remain full-depth.

</details>


### [493] [On Time, Within Budget: Constraint-Driven Online Resource Allocation for Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06110)
*Xinglin Wang,Zishen Liu,Shaoxiong Feng,Peiwen Yuan,Yiwei Li,Jiayi Shi,Yueqi Zhang,Chuyi Tan,Ji Zhang,Boyuan Pan,Yao Hu,Kan Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies constraint-driven online resource allocation for agentic workflows, proposing Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning (MCPP) to maximize the probability of completing workflows under explicit budget and deadline constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real deployments of agentic systems often face concrete budget and deadline requirements, shifting the goal from average efficiency optimization to maximizing the probability of successful completion under explicit constraints.

Method: Formulates the problem as a finite-horizon stochastic online allocation problem and proposes MCPP, a lightweight closed-loop planner that estimates constrained completion probability via simulated workflow executions and replans after observed outcomes.

Result: Experiments on CodeFlow and ProofFlow show MCPP consistently improves constrained completion probability over strong baselines across a wide range of budget-deadline constraints.

Conclusion: MCPP effectively addresses the practical need for constraint-driven resource allocation in agentic workflows, outperforming baselines under varying budget and deadline conditions.

Abstract: Agentic systems increasingly solve complex user requests by executing orchestrated workflows, where subtasks are assigned to specialized models or tools and coordinated according to their dependencies. While recent work improves agent efficiency by optimizing the performance--cost--latency frontier, real deployments often impose concrete requirements: a workflow must be completed within a specified budget and before a specified deadline. This shifts the goal from average efficiency optimization to maximizing the probability that the entire workflow completes successfully under explicit budget and deadline constraints. We study \emph{constraint-driven online resource allocation for agentic workflows}. Given a dependency-structured workflow and estimates of success rates and generation lengths for each subtask--model pair, the executor allocates models and parallel samples across simultaneously executable subtasks while managing the remaining budget and time. We formulate this setting as a finite-horizon stochastic online allocation problem and propose \emph{Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning} (MCPP), a lightweight closed-loop planner that directly estimates constrained completion probability through simulated workflow executions and replans after observed outcomes. Experiments on CodeFlow and ProofFlow demonstrate that MCPP consistently improves constrained completion probability over strong baselines across a wide range of budget--deadline constraints.

</details>


### [494] [CrossCult-KIBench: A Benchmark for Cross-Cultural Knowledge Insertion in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06115)
*Zhen Zeng,Leijiang Gu,Feng Li,Jing Yu,Zenglin Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces CrossCult-KIBench, a benchmark for evaluating cross-cultural knowledge insertion in MLLMs, and proposes MCKI as a baseline method that retrieves cultural knowledge from external memory using frozen MLLM representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs trained on English-centric data produce culturally inappropriate responses in cross-cultural settings, highlighting the need for methods to adapt models to specific cultural contexts.

Method: The authors propose Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI), which retrieves relevant cultural knowledge from an external memory using frozen MLLM representations and prepends matched entries as conditional prompts when applicable.

Result: Experiments show current approaches struggle to balance effective cultural adaptation with behavioral preservation, revealing a key challenge in developing culturally-aware MLLMs.

Conclusion: The work underscores an important research direction for developing more culturally adaptive and responsible MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), trained primarily on English-centric data, frequently generate culturally inappropriate or misaligned responses in cross-cultural settings. To mitigate this, we introduce the task of cross-cultural knowledge insertion, which focuses on adapting models to specific cultural contexts while preserving their original behavior in other cultures. To facilitate research in this area, we introduce CrossCult-KIBench, a comprehensive evaluation benchmark for assessing both the effectiveness of knowledge insertion and its unintended side effects on non-target cultures. The benchmark includes 9,800 image-grounded cases covering 49 culturally relevant visual scenarios across English, Chinese, and Arabic language-culture groups. It supports evaluation in both single-insert and sequential-insert settings. We also propose Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI) as a baseline method. MCKI retrieves relevant cultural knowledge from an external memory using frozen MLLM representations, prepending matched entries as conditional prompts when applicable. Extensive experiments on CrossCult-KIBench reveal that current approaches struggle to balance effective cultural adaptation with behavioral preservation, highlighting a key challenge in developing culturally-aware MLLMs. Our work thus underscores an important research direction for developing more culturally adaptive and responsible MLLMs.

</details>


### [495] [Policy-Guided Stepwise Model Routing for Cost-Effective Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06116)
*Wenwen Si,Insup Lee,Osbert Bastani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning-based method for stepwise model routing during chain-of-thought inference, improving the accuracy-cost tradeoff without needing large process reward models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time computation improves LLM reasoning but incurs high costs. Existing routing strategies are either handcrafted (limited performance) or rely on training large process reward models (infeasible in many applications).

Method: Formulates stepwise model routing as a constrained decision-making problem, solved by training a small control policy using reinforcement learning with threshold calibration to tune the performance-efficiency tradeoff.

Result: Validated on GSM8K, MATH500, and OmniMath benchmarks on both open and closed models; consistently improves accuracy-cost tradeoff over handcrafted approaches, achieving comparable tradeoff to methods using large process reward models.

Conclusion: RL-based routing with a small control policy offers an efficient and effective alternative to handcrafted rules or large reward models for stepwise model selection during CoT inference.

Abstract: Inference-time computation has greatly enhanced the performance of large language models (LLMs) on challenging reasoning tasks, but this strategy can incur high inference costs. One solution is to route intermediate chain-of-thought (CoT) states to language models of different sizes; however, existing approaches rely on handcrafted routing strategies that limit performance, or on training large process reward models that may be infeasible in many applications. We formulate stepwise model routing as a constrained decision-making problem, which we solve by training a small control policy using reinforcement learning in conjunction with threshold calibration to tune the performance-efficiency tradeoff. We validate our method on three math benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH500, and OmniMath) on both open and closed models. Our method consistently improves the accuracy-cost tradeoff compared to handcrafted approaches, while achieving a comparable tradeoff to methods that require training large process reward models.

</details>


### [496] [Back to the Beginning of Heuristic Design: Bridging Code and Knowledge with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06123)
*Nguyen Viet Tuan Kiet,Bui Dinh Pham,Dao Van Tung,Tran Cong Dao,Huynh Thi Thanh Binh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes shifting from a bottom-up (code-centric) to a top-down (knowledge-first) paradigm for automatic heuristic design (AHD) using LLMs, formalizing this via a statistical-learning view with a distortion-compression trade-off, and showing that knowledge-first search improves efficiency, transfer, and generalization across combinatorial optimization tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-driven AHD methods operate in a bottom-up paradigm where low-level program code is iteratively searched and refined, making learned insights implicit and tied to specific implementations. This limits reusability and generalization across problems. The authors argue this view is incomplete and motivate a complementary top-down perspective where explicit, interpretable knowledge becomes the primary search object.

Method: The authors formalize the shift from code-centric to knowledge-centric search through a statistical-learning view that reveals a distortion-compression trade-off. They instantiate this knowledge-first paradigm in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks, where heuristics are represented as interpretable knowledge structures rather than raw programs.

Result: Across combinatorial optimization tasks and beyond, the knowledge-first approach improves discovery efficiency, transferability across problems, and generalization compared to code-centric pipelines. Combining both bottom-up and top-down strategies yields further performance gains.

Conclusion: Progress in automatic heuristic design depends on iteratively constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory, suggesting that knowledge should be the core unit of search rather than code.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently advanced automatic heuristic design (AHD) for combinatorial optimization (CO), where candidate heuristics are iteratively proposed, evaluated, and refined. Most existing approaches search over executable programs and distill insights from execution feedback to guide later iterations. Because this process moves from low-level implementations to high-level principles, we refer to it as a bottom-up paradigm. We argue that this view is incomplete and introduce a complementary top-down perspective: knowledge becomes the primary search object and code merely instantiates and tests it, making what is learned explicit and reusable across problems and trajectories. We formalize this shift through a statistical-learning view that exposes a distortion--compression trade-off, and instantiate it in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks. Across CO and tasks beyond it, knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperforming code-centric pipelines, while combining both strategies yields further gains. Our results suggest that progress in AHD depends on iteratively constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory.

</details>


### [497] [P-Guide: Parameter-Efficient Prior Steering for Single-Pass CFG Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06124)
*Xin Peng,Ang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: P-Guide reduces CFG computational overhead by ~50% by modulating only the initial latent state for single-pass guidance in flow matching, achieving competitive results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) requires dual forward passes per sampling step, creating a significant computational bottleneck in flow matching. The authors aim to reduce this overhead while maintaining generation quality.

Method: P-Guide achieves guidance through a single inference pass by modulating only the initial latent state. Under a first-order approximation, it is equivalent to standard CFG. The framework supports both homoscedastic and heteroscedastic priors, jointly modeling mean and variance for adaptive loss attenuation.

Result: P-Guide reduces inference latency by approximately 50% while maintaining fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines.

Conclusion: P-Guide offers an efficient single-pass alternative to CFG for flow matching, cutting computation in half without sacrificing quality, with additional robustness benefits from heteroscedastic priors.

Abstract: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is essential for high-fidelity conditional generation in flow matching, yet it imposes significant computational overhead by requiring dual forward passes at each sampling step. In this work, we address this bottleneck by introducing \textbf{P-Guide}, a framework that achieves high-quality guidance through a single inference pass by modulating only the initial latent state. We further show that, under a first-order approximation, P-Guide is equivalent to CFG in the sense that it steers generation from the prior space, without requiring explicit velocity field extrapolation during sampling. We consider both homoscedastic and \textbf{heteroscedastic} priors, and find that jointly modeling the mean and variance enables adaptive loss attenuation and improved robustness to data uncertainty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that P-Guide reduces inference latency by approximately 50\% while maintaining fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines.

</details>


### [498] [Skill1: Unified Evolution of Skill-Augmented Agents via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06130)
*Yaorui Shi,Yuxin Chen,Zhengxi Lu,Yuchun Miao,Shugui Liu,Qi GU,Xunliang Cai,Xiang Wang,An Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Skill1 is a framework that trains a single policy to co-evolve skill selection, utilization, and distillation using a single task-outcome reward signal, outperforming prior methods on ALFWorld and WebShop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods optimize skill selection, utilization, and distillation in isolation or with separate reward sources, leading to partial and conflicting evolution of these capabilities.

Method: A single policy generates a query to search the skill library, re-ranks candidates to select one, solves the task conditioned on it, and distills a new skill from the trajectory. Learning uses a single task-outcome signal where low-frequency trends credit selection and high-frequency variations credit distillation.

Result: Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and RL baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop. Training dynamics confirm co-evolution of the three capabilities; ablations show removing any credit signal degrades evolution.

Conclusion: Co-evolving skill selection, utilization, and distillation with a unified reward signal is more effective than treating them separately.

Abstract: A persistent skill library allows language model agents to reuse successful strategies across tasks. Maintaining such a library requires three coupled capabilities. The agent selects a relevant skill, utilizes it during execution, and distills new skills from experience. Existing methods optimize these capabilities in isolation or with separate reward sources, resulting in partial and conflicting evolution. We propose Skill1, a framework that trains a single policy to co-evolve skill selection, utilization, and distillation toward a shared task-outcome objective. The policy generates a query to search the skill library, re-ranks candidates to select one, solves the task conditioned on it, and distills a new skill from the trajectory. All learning derives from a single task-outcome signal. Its low-frequency trend credits selection and its high-frequency variation credits distillation. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and reinforcement learning baselines. Training dynamics confirm the co-evolution of the three capabilities, and ablations show that removing any credit signal degrades the evolution.

</details>


### [499] [Graphlets as Building Blocks for Structural Vocabulary in Knowledge Graph Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06154)
*Kossi Amouzouvi,Robert Wardenga,Jens Lehmann,Sahar Vahdati*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes using graphlets (small connected graphs) as structural tokens for Knowledge Graph Foundation Models, enabling transferable representations across unseen KGs by treating graphlets as a universal vocabulary for relational patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current KGFMs struggle to transfer representations across unseen KGs because unlike language or vision, KGs lack a universal, fixed grid geometry - their entities and relations form irregular, non-Euclidean topologies without a universal token set.

Method: A model-agnostic framework that mines graphlets (closed/open 2- and 3-path, and star graphlets) between relations via pattern matching, treating them as structural tokens that recur across heterogeneous KGs.

Result: Evaluated on 51 KGs across diverse domains for zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction; adding simple graphlets to the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KGFMs.

Conclusion: Graphlets serve as effective structural tokens bridging the geometry gap between KGs and other discrete domains, enabling transferable representations in KGFMs without requiring a universal token set.

Abstract: Foundation models excel at language, where sentences become tokens, and vision, where images become pixels, because both reduce to discrete symbols on a shared, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs share the discreteness, but not the geometry. Their entities and relations are discrete symbols, yet their arrangement is relational and lacks a common, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs (KGs) share the discreteness, but not the geometry. They form irregular, non-Euclidean topologies whose local neighborhoods differ from graph to graph. Therefore, Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs) rely on identifying structural invariances to produce transferable representations. Without a universal token set, KGFMs are limited in their ability to transfer representations across unseen KGs. We close this gap by treating graphlets, small connected graphs, as structural tokens that recur in heterogeneous KGs. In this paper, We introduce a model-agnostic framework based on a vocabulary of graphlets that mines a KG between relations via pattern matching. In particular, we considered closed and open 2- and 3-path, and star graphlets, to obtain robust invariances. The framework is evaluated on 51 KGs from a wide range of domains, for zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction. Experiments show that adding simple graphlets to the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KGFMs.

</details>


### [500] [Beyond Accuracy: Policy Invariance as a Reliability Test for LLM Safety Judges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06161)
*Shihao Weng,Yang Feng,Xiaofei Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper challenges the assumption that LLM-as-a-Judge verdicts are reliable for agent safety evaluation. It proposes "policy invariance" as a necessary property for trustworthy judges, operationalized through three testable principles. Experiments reveal that current judges conflate agent behavior with evaluation phrasing, causing up to 9.1% verdict flips from content-preserving rewrites and 18-43% of flips on unambiguous cases. The authors contribute the Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card protocol to expose reliability differences invisible to accuracy leaderboards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks treat LLM-as-a-Judge verdicts as ground-truth proxies without checking whether those verdicts depend on the agent's actual behavior or merely on how the evaluation policy is worded. This oversight undermines the trustworthiness of safety evaluation pipelines.

Method: The authors define "policy invariance" as a basic property for trustworthy judges, operationalized as three principles: (1) rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, (2) rubric-threshold invariance under intentional strict-to-lenient shifts, and (3) ambiguity-aware calibration. They instantiate these as a stress-test protocol with four agent-class judges on trajectories from ASSEBench and R-Judge.

Result: Content-preserving policy rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts above baseline jitter. 18-43% of all observed flips occur on unambiguous cases under such rewrites. Judges respond to both meaningful normative shifts and meaningless structural rewrites with comparable strength, without distinguishing between them.

Conclusion: Existing safety scores conflate agent behavior with evaluator prompting. The proposed Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card protocol expose order-of-magnitude reliability differences invisible to accuracy-only leaderboards. The authors release their protocol and code for auditing evaluators rather than trusting them by default.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-Judge pipelines have become the de facto evaluator for agent safety, yet existing benchmarks treat their verdicts as ground-truth proxies without checking whether the verdicts depend on the agent's behavior or merely on how the evaluation policy happens to be worded. We argue that any trustworthy safety judge must satisfy a basic property we call policy invariance, and we operationalize it as three testable principles: rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, rubric-threshold invariance under intentional strict-to-lenient shifts, and ambiguity-aware calibration so that verdict instability concentrates on genuinely ambiguous cases. Instantiating these principles as a stress-test protocol with four agent-class judges on trajectories drawn from ASSEBench and R-Judge, we surface a previously unmeasured failure mode: today's judges respond to meaningful normative shifts and to meaningless structural rewrites with comparable strength, and cannot tell the two apart. Content-preserving policy rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts above baseline jitter, and 18-43% of all observed flips occur on unambiguous cases under such rewrites, so existing safety scores conflate what the agent did with how the evaluator was prompted. Beyond the diagnosis, we contribute the Policy Invariance Score and the Judge Card reporting protocol, which expose an order-of-magnitude spread in judge reliability that is invisible to accuracy-only leaderboards. We release the protocol and code so that future agent-safety benchmarks can audit their own evaluators rather than trust them by default.

</details>


### [501] [Post Reasoning: Improving the Performance of Non-Thinking Models at No Cost](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06165)
*Richmond Sin Jing Xuan,Rishabh Bhardwaj,Soujanya Poria*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Post-Reasoning is a method that makes LLMs generate their final answer first, then justify it afterward, improving performance (17.37% mean improvement) across 117 settings while avoiding extra inference latency and token costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Token consumption from intermediate reasoning traces increases latency and cost, yet many tasks don't need explicit reasoning and extra reasoning can even hurt performance. The authors aim to improve model performance without additional inference cost.

Method: Proposes Post-Reasoning: instruction-tuned models are conditioned to generate the final answer first, then provide a justification afterward. Also proposes supervised post-reason tuning to internalize this capability through training.

Result: Evaluated on 117 model-benchmark settings across 13 models, 4 model families, 9 benchmarks. Achieves 17.37% mean relative improvement, performing well in 88.19% of settings. Supervised tuning further improves 91.11% of settings with 8.01% gain over the prompt-based baseline.

Conclusion: Post-Reasoning establishes a new performance ceiling for direct-answer capabilities, effectively improving performance without added latency or token cost.

Abstract: As the widespread adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) accelerates, token consumption from intermediate reasoning traces increasingly contributes to inference latency and operational cost. Recent studies suggest that many real-world tasks require little to no explicit reasoning, with additional reasoning sometimes even degrading performance. In this work, we propose \textbf{Post-Reasoning}, a simple yet effective approach that improves instruction-tuned models by conditioning them to justify their answers after generating the final response. By design, it enables the final answer to be obtained without additional latency or token cost, while still improving performance through simple instruction augmentation. We evaluate Post-Reasoning across \(117\) model--benchmark settings spanning \(13\) open and proprietary models, \(4\) model families, and \(9\) diverse reasoning and knowledge-intensive benchmarks, including AMC, HMMT, GSM8K, GPQA, MMLU-Pro, and BIG-Bench Hard. Post-Reasoning improves performance in over \(88.19\%\) of evaluated settings, achieving a mean relative improvements of \(17.37\%\). Furthermore, we propose supervised post-reason tuning, which further improves performance in over \(91.11\%\) of evaluated settings, and exceeds the prompt-based post-reasoning baseline by an average of \(8.01\%\), demonstrating that post-reasoning can be effectively internalized through training. Ultimately, Post-Reasoning establishes a new performance ceiling for direct-answer capabilities.

</details>


### [502] [BioMedArena: An Open-source Toolkit for Building and Evaluating Biomedical Deep Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06177)
*Jinge Wu,Hongjian Zhou,Mingde Zeng,Jiayuan Zhu,Junde Wu,Jiazhen Pan,Sean Wu,Honghan Wu,Fenglin Liu,David A. Clifton*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BioMedArena is an open-source toolkit that decouples biomedical agent evaluation into six layers, providing 147 benchmarks and 75 tools, achieving SOTA results on 8 benchmarks with +15.03% average improvement while eliminating the "per-paper engineering tax" of model integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the "per-paper engineering tax"—the significant engineering overhead caused by inconsistent harnesses and tool registries that make it difficult to fairly compare different foundation models as deep research agents across papers.

Method: BioMedArena decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation (benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, scoring), exposes 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 tools across 9 families, and provides 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies. Adding new models/benchmarks/tools requires only a few-line provider adapter.

Result: Achieved state-of-the-art results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks with an average improvement of +15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA, with 12 backbones showing competitive research capabilities.

Conclusion: BioMedArena provides a standardized, modular platform for fair comparison of foundation models as deep research agents in biomedicine, significantly reducing engineering overhead while improving performance across diverse benchmarks.

Abstract: Building a deep research agent today is an exercise in glue code: the same backbone evaluated on the same benchmark can report different accuracies in different papers because harness and tool registry all differ, and integrating a new foundation model into a comparable evaluation surface costs weeks of model-specific engineering. We call this the per-paper engineering tax and release BioMedArena, an open-source toolkit that not only alleviates it but also provides an arena for fair comparison of different foundation models when evaluating them as deep-research agents. BioMedArena decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation -- benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, and scoring -- and exposes 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 biomedical tools across 9 functional families. Adding a new model, benchmark, or tool reduces to registering a few-line provider adapter. We further provide 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies, which provide 12 backbones with competitive research capabilities and significantly improved performance, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks, with an average lift of +15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA. The toolkit, configurations, and per-task traces are available at https://github.com/AI-in-Health/BioMedArena

</details>


### [503] [Rethinking Adapter Placement: A Dominant Adaptation Module Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06183)
*Suoxin Zhang,Run He,Di Fang,Xiang Tan,Kaixuan Chen,Huiping Zhuang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAGE identifies a single dominant adaptation module (shallow FFN down-projection) where gradient energy concentrates, and DomLoRA places just one LoRA adapter there, using ~0.7% of LoRA's parameters yet outperforming it across multiple tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate where to place a limited number of LoRA adapters to maximize performance, given that using fewer adapters can maintain or even improve performance.

Method: Introduces PAGE (Projected Adapter Gradient Energy), a gradient-based sensitivity probe to measure trainable gradient energy for each candidate LoRA adapter, and proposes DomLoRA which places a single adapter at the identified dominant adaptation module (shallow FFN down-projection).

Result: PAGE reveals gradient energy is highly concentrated on a single shallow FFN down-projection; DomLoRA with only ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA's trainable parameters outperforms it on instruction following, math reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn conversation tasks.

Conclusion: The dominant adaptation module perspective serves as a practical placement guideline, and DomLoRA improves both vanilla LoRA and other LoRA variants.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is a widely used parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that places trainable low-rank adapters into frozen pre-trained models. Recent studies show that using fewer LoRA adapters may still maintain or even improve performance, but existing methods still distribute adapters broadly, leaving where to place a limited number of adapters to maximize performance largely open. To investigate this, we introduce PAGE (Projected Adapter Gradient Energy), a gradient-based sensitivity probe that estimates the initial trainable gradient energy available to each candidate LoRA adapter. Surprisingly, we find that PAGE is highly concentrated on a single shallow FFN down-projection across two model families and four downstream tasks. We term this module the dominant adaptation module and show that its layer index is architecture-dependent but task-stable. Motivated by this finding, we propose DomLoRA, a placement method that places a single adapter at the dominant adaptation module. With only ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA's trainable parameters, DomLoRA outperforms it on average across various downstream tasks, including instruction following, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn conversation. This method also improves other LoRA variants, supporting the dominant adaptation module perspective as a practical placement guideline.

</details>


### [504] [Event-Causal RAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Long Video Reasoning in Complex Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06185)
*Peizheng Yan,Yu Zhao,Liang Xie,Juntong Qi,Mingming Wang,Erwei Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Event-Causal RAG is a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework that segments streaming videos into semantically coherent events, represents them as State-Event-State graphs in a dual-store memory, and uses bidirectional retrieval to enable infinite long-video reasoning with superior efficiency and causal inference capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing large vision-language models fail at ultra-long/infinite video reasoning due to O(n²) self-attention complexity and fragmented clip-level memory in RAG approaches, which lose temporal and causal structure while incurring high storage and inference costs.

Method: The framework segments streaming videos into semantically coherent events, represents each event as a State-Event-State (SES) graph capturing state transitions, merges them into a global Event Knowledge Graph stored in a dual-store memory (semantic + causal-topological), and employs bidirectional retrieval to identify relevant event causal chains for a video foundation model to generate answers.

Result: Consistently outperforms clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models on long-video understanding benchmarks, especially on multi-event integration and causal inference questions, while achieving improved memory efficiency and robust streaming performance.

Conclusion: Event-Causal RAG effectively addresses infinite long-video reasoning by preserving coherent causal structure through event-level segmentation and graph-based memory, outperforming existing methods on complex temporal reasoning tasks with greater efficiency.

Abstract: Recent large vision-language models have achieved strong performance on short- and medium-length video understanding, yet they remain inadequate for ultra-long or even infinite video reasoning, where models must preserve coherent memory over extended durations and infer causal dependencies across temporally distant events. Existing end-to-end video understanding methods are fundamentally limited by the $O(n^2)$ complexity of self-attention, while recent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches still suffer from fragmented clip-level memory, weak modeling of temporal and causal structure, and high storage and online inference costs. We present Event-Causal RAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for infinite long-video reasoning. Instead of indexing fixed-length clips, our method segments streaming videos into semantically coherent events and represents each event as a structured State-Event-State (SES) graph, capturing the event together with its surrounding state transitions. These graphs are merged into a global Event Knowledge Graph and stored in a dual-store memory that supports both semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval. On top of this memory, we design a bidirectional retrieval strategy to efficiently identify the most relevant event causal chains and provide them, together with the associated video evidence, to a backbone video foundation model for answer generation. Experiments on long-video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that Event-Causal RAG consistently outperforms strong clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models, particularly on questions requiring multi-event integration and causal inference across long temporal gaps, while also achieving improved memory efficiency and robust streaming performance.

</details>


### [505] [OPSD Compresses What RLVR Teaches: A Post-RL Compaction Stage for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06188)
*Jaehoon Kim,Dongha Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OPSD primarily acts as a compression mechanism in thinking-enabled math reasoning, not a correction mechanism; the paper proposes SFT→RLVR→OPSD pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPSD shows promise for RLVR alternatives but fails in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning; the authors aim to understand why and propose a fix.

Method: Applied OPSD separately to correct vs. incorrect rollout groups to isolate compression and correction effects in thinking-enabled math reasoning.

Result: OPSD on correct rollouts preserves accuracy and shortens responses; OPSD on incorrect rollouts damages accuracy. It acts reliably as compression, not correction.

Conclusion: Proposed a revised pipeline: SFT → RLVR → OPSD for thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning.

Abstract: On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) has recently emerged as an alternative to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), promising higher accuracy and shorter responses through token-level credit assignment from a self-teacher conditioned on privileged context. However, this promise does not carry over to thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, where reported accuracy gains shrink and sometimes turn negative. We hypothesize that hindsight supervision can specify better token-level alternatives in short thinking-disabled outputs, but in long thinking-enabled traces it more readily identifies redundancy than supplies better replacements. To test this, we applied OPSD separately to correct and incorrect rollout groups, so that compression and correction can be observed in isolation. Our results show that in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, OPSD behaves most reliably as a compression mechanism rather than a correction mechanism: training only on correct rollouts preserves accuracy while substantially shortening responses, whereas training only on incorrect rollouts damages accuracy. In light of these findings, we propose a revised post-training pipeline for thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning: SFT then RLVR then OPSD.

</details>


### [506] [Systematic Evaluation of Large Language Models for Post-Discharge Clinical Action Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06191)
*Shivali Dalmia,Ananya Mantravadi,Prasanna Desikan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates LLMs for safety-critical clinical action extraction from discharge notes, introduces a two-stage extraction framework, and shows LLMs match supervised models on binary tasks but lag on fine-grained classification. Key finding: performance is limited by annotation conventions rather than clinical reasoning, highlighting the need for reasoning-annotated datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess zero-shot and few-shot LLMs for extracting safety-critical clinical actions from discharge notes, particularly for transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety, and to understand limitations in current evaluation methods.

Method: A two-stage extraction framework using staged prompting to decompose narrative discharge notes into fine-grained actionable clinical tasks. Comparison of general-purpose LLMs vs. task-specific supervised BERT-based models on the CLIP dataset.

Result: LLMs match or exceed supervised models on binary actionability detection, but supervised baselines maintain an advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification. Many errors stem from misalignment between model reasoning and annotation conventions, especially for implicit clinical actions.

Conclusion: Reported performance reflects annotation convention mismatches rather than true clinical reasoning failures. The field needs reasoning-annotated datasets that document why spans are actionable, not just which spans are labeled.

Abstract: The work in this paper evaluates zero-shot and few-shot large language models (LLMs) for safety-critical clinical action extraction using the CLIP discharge-note dataset, with particular emphasis on transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety. To manage the complexity of clinical documentation, we introduce a two-stage extraction framework that decomposes discharge notes, that are written in narrative form, into fine-grained, explicitly actionable clinical tasks through a staged prompting strategy. Our contributions include a systematic assessment of generative LLMs for clinical action extraction, a detailed comparison between general-purpose LLMs and task-specific supervised BERT-based models, and an analysis of annotation inconsistencies across different action categories. We show that contemporary LLMs achieve performance comparable to or exceeding supervised models on binary actionability detection, while supervised baselines retain a meaningful advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification, despite the absence of task-specific fine-tuning and under strict data-privacy constraints. Qualitative error analysis reveals that many failures stem from misalignment between model reasoning and dataset annotation conventions, particularly in cases involving implicit clinical actions and rigid structural labeling rules. These results indicate that reported performance reflects model limitations due to lack of clinical reasoning, that is not captured by plain annotations. Labels without rationales make it impossible to distinguish clinical reasoning failures from annotation convention mismatches. Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why specific spans are actionable, not merely which spans were labeled, enabling proper evaluation of model clinical understanding.

</details>


### [507] [The Granularity Axis: A Micro-to-Macro Latent Direction for Social Roles in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06196)
*Chonghan Qin,Xiachong Feng,Ziyun Song,Xiaocheng Feng,Jing Xiong,Lingpeng Kong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs encode social role granularity (from individual to institutional) as the dominant geometric axis (PC1) in their representation space, accounting for 52.6% of variance, and this axis is causally manipulable via activation steering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs are routinely prompted to take on diverse social roles (individuals, institutions, nations), it is unknown whether their internal representations encode granularity—from micro-level individual experience to macro-level organizational reasoning.

Method: Defined a contrast-based Granularity Axis (difference between macro- and micro-role hidden states). Constructed 75 social roles across 5 granularity levels, collected 91,200 role-conditioned responses, extracted hidden states, and projected them onto the axis. Validated stability across layers, prompts, and models. Conducted activation steering experiments.

Result: The axis aligns with PC1 at cosine 0.972, accounting for 52.6% variance. Role projections increase monotonically across all 5 levels, remain stable across variations, and transfer to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Activation steering shifts response granularity (Llama from 2.00 to 3.17 on a 5-point macro scale).

Conclusion: Social role granularity is a structured, ordered, and causally manipulable latent direction in LLMs, not merely a stylistic surface feature. Models differ in controllability, suggesting dependence on each model's default operating regime.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are routinely prompted to take on social roles ranging from individuals to institutions, yet it remains unclear whether their internal representations encode the granularity of such roles, from micro-level individual experience to macro-level organizational, institutional, or national reasoning. We show that they do. We define a contrast-based Granularity Axis as the difference between mean macro- and micro-role hidden states. In Qwen3-8B, this axis aligns with the principal axis (PC1) of the role representation space at cosine 0.972 and accounts for 52.6% of its variance, indicating that granularity is the dominant geometric axis organizing prompted social roles. We construct 75 social roles across five granularity levels and collect 91,200 role-conditioned responses over shared questions and prompt variants, then extract role-level hidden states and project them onto the axis. Role projections increase monotonically across all five levels, remain stable across layers, prompt variants, endpoint definitions, held-out splits, and score-filtered subsets, and transfer to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. The axis is also causally relevant: activation steering along it shifts response granularity in the predicted direction, with Llama moving from 2.00 to 3.17 on a five-point macro scale under positive steering on prompts that admit local responses. The two models differ in controllability, suggesting that steering depends on each model's default operating regime. Overall, our findings suggest that social role granularity is not merely a stylistic surface feature, but a structured, ordered, and causally manipulable latent direction in role-conditioned language model behavior.

</details>


### [508] [Towards Annotation-Free Validation of MLLMs: A Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06201)
*Ying Gu,Mei Chee Leong,Hui Li Tan,Shangbo Mao,Liyuan Li,Nancy Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes VL-LCM, a new metric to evaluate vision-language logical consistency of MLLMs on cause-effect relations, without requiring ground-truth annotations. Experiments show logical consistency lags behind accuracy gains in MLLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional accuracy evaluation may reward unwarranted guessing from LLMs and cannot be applied to novel tasks lacking ground-truth annotations. The authors aim to address this gap.

Method: Based on basic logic principles, the authors define a Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric (VL-LCM) that evaluates both sufficient and necessary cause-effect relations on MC-VQA and NaturalBench tests without needing ground-truth annotations.

Result: Testing 11 recent open-source MLLMs across 4 families on MMMU and NaturalBench shows that while accuracy has improved significantly, logical consistency remains notably behind. Correlations with ground-truth metrics, reliability, and response distribution validate VL-LCM.

Conclusion: Beyond accuracy, logical consistency is important for both accuracy and reliability. VL-LCM can be used for model selection, validation, and answer justification in novel tasks without ground-truth annotation.

Abstract: Dominant accuracy evaluation might reward unwarranted guessing of Large Language Models, and it might not be applicable to novel tasks for model validation without ground-truth (gt) annotation. Based on basic logic principle, we propose a novel framework to evaluate the vision-language logical consistency of MLLMs on both sufficient and necessary cause-effect relations. We define Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric (VL-LCM) on traditional MC-VQA tests, and recent NaturalBench tests without the need for gt annotation. Through systematic experiments on representative VL benchmark MMMU and recent VL challenges like NaturalBench, we evaluated 11 recent open-source MLLMs from 4 frontier families. Our findings reveal that, despite significant progress of recent MLLMs on accuracy, logical consistency lags behind significantly. Extensive evaluations on the correlations of VL-LCM with metrics on gt, the reliability of LCM, and the relation of VL-LCM with response distribution justify the validity and applicability of VL-LCM even without gt annotation. Our findings suggest that, beyond accuracy, logical consistency could be employed for both accuracy and reliability. VL-LCM can also be employed for MLLM selection, validation, and reliable answer justification in novel tasks without gt annotation.

</details>


### [509] [Beyond Fixed Benchmarks and Worst-Case Attacks: Dynamic Boundary Evaluation for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06213)
*Haoxiang Wang,Da Yu,Huishuai Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Dynamic Boundary Evaluation (DBE), which finds the hardest prompts where a model has ~50% pass probability, providing a calibrated item bank, a search algorithm (SGBS), and an adaptive evaluation protocol to avoid ceiling/floor effects in LLM evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fixed benchmarks produce ceiling and floor effects that mask capability gaps in LLMs, because the same items are applied to any model regardless of ability level.

Method: DBE locates each model's boundary (where per-prompt pass probability ≈ 0.5) using Skill-Guided Boundary Search (SGBS) with only API-level query access, places models on a unified difficulty scale, and adaptively grows the evaluation set when needed.

Result: The method produces a calibrated item bank with difficulty labels from 9 reference LLMs, spans safety, capability, and truthfulness categories, and evaluates a broader model spectrum without saturation while remaining compatible with existing datasets.

Conclusion: Focusing evaluation on the boundary where models are uncertain (near 50% pass rate) yields more informative, discrimination-rich assessment of LLMs across diverse capabilities.

Abstract: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) today rests on fixed benchmarks that apply the same set of items to any model, producing ceiling and floor effects that mask capability gaps. We argue that the most informative evaluation signal lies at the boundary, where the per-prompt pass probability is near $0.5$ under random-sampling decoding, and propose Dynamic Boundary Evaluation (DBE), which actively locates each model's boundary and places it on a globally comparable difficulty scale. DBE delivers three artifacts: (i) a calibrated item bank covering safety, capability, and truthfulness, with per-item difficulty labels validated across $9$ reference LLMs; (ii) Skill-Guided Boundary Search (SGBS), a search algorithm that finds boundary items for a given target LLM using only API-level query access; and (iii) an evaluation protocol that places a new LLM on a unified ability scale and grows the evaluation set adaptively when the target falls outside the bank's coverage. We instantiate DBE on four categories spanning safety (harmful request refusal and over-refusal), capability (constrained instruction following), and truthfulness (multi-turn sycophancy resistance). The resulting evaluation covers a broader model spectrum without saturation while remaining compatible with existing datasets.

</details>


### [510] [Joint Consistency: A Unified Test-Time Aggregation Framework via Energy Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06219)
